AND SPECIAL REMEDIES AS FOLLOWS:

### --- Page 0012 ---

PREFACE.

Jurisdiction lies at the foundation of all legal proceedings. It must be present or the proceeding is void. It has been defined as “the power to decide.” The important question, however, remains of determining the existence of such power. Who is to determine it, and how and in what manner it is to be determined, are questions not free from difficulty. Every tribunal necessarily must in the first instance determine the question of its own jurisdiction. It is also a familiar rule that the decision which a court makes within its jurisdiction, whether right or wrong, cannot affect its power to decide. Hence if this rule was universally true, and the power to decide is the exercise of jurisdiction, then it would follow that the court could conclusively determine the question of its jurisdiction, which in effect would be to confer it. The rule, however, is only correct when applied to matters within the acknowledged jurisdiction of the court. To what extent it is applicable in determining the power of the court has been a fruitful source of conflict of judicial opinion. It has clothed the general subject with much of doubt and uncertainty. It has consequently made its treatment arduous and difficult. The effort has been for generations, through the operation of legal minds, to establish rules that are practicable and reasonably certain in their application, by which the distinction between want of power and mere error of judgment could be readily determined. The effort has not been a complete success. The general powers of a court have been made an important factor in the framing of definite rules or tests.

<br>

### --- Page 0013 ---

iv PREFACE,

Thus, in determining the question of jurisdiction we look at the general powers possessed, and determine whether the particular matter is within such power, rather than the manner in which such power has been exercised in the particular case. If the power is one specially conferred, the determination involves its extent, and if conditional the existence of the conditions prescribed. If the manner of its exercise is particularly directed, then the question is that of strict or substantial observance.

We often meet with the expression, “that if the court or tribunal has jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person then its authority is complete; that all else in the proceeding is but error.” This, however, is not universally true. An important qualification is that the court may not proceed beyond the power which is invoked in the particular case. Such and similar matters relate to the question of “excess of jurisdiction.” They involve the manner in which a power possessed is exercised; sometimes fatal to the judgment and sometimes not. The sentence of one convicted of crime to a term of imprisonment in excess of that prescribed by law is in excess of the power of the court; yet it is error of judgment, not fatal to the proceeding, but void merely as to the excess; while if the court should inflict a punishment different in kind than that prescribed for the offense, this also would be an excess of power, but fatal to the judgment. This important branch of the general subject is rarely discussed by text-writers, though its importance demands full consideration.

These matters are referred to here for the purpose of impressing upon the mind of the reader not only the importance of the subject of jurisdiction, but also the necessity of careful thought and study in ascertaining its principles in order to appreciate and understand and apply the distinctions, often somewhat refined, which permeate, so to speak, the entire subject.

<br>

### --- Page 0014 ---

PREFACE.

The plan upon which this work is constructed is one which I have confidence will meet the views of the profession. It is essentially a work on jurisdiction. I have taken that subject as the trunk of the tree, so to speak, and those remedies which are framed with special reference to jurisdiction as branches, and treated them all in connection as one continuous whole.

How and in what manner jurisdictional defects may be met is a part of the general subject. In fact, in no other way can the subject of jurisdiction be treated without much of unnecessary repetition. In this way much that is valuable is preserved, by reason of uninterrupted connection. It enables the whole subject to be placed in comparatively limited space, thus avoiding the expense of numerous works, each treating separately the distinctive branches of jurisdiction, and each necessarily containing a repetition of the general doctrine and the special rules and exceptions which apply to it.

The subject of contempt, involving the exercise of a special or summary power, is so closely allied to the question of jurisdiction as to make it proper to be considered in connection with it. The discussion is not confined to such contempts as are denominated “direct” or “constructive,” but embraces those matters commonly denominated “civil contempts.” The privilege of witnesses, both constitutional and at common law, is considered and discussed, as well as the question of privileged communications—such as between attorney and client, physician and patient, clergyman and layman, and husband and wife.

There are but few questions as to which the courts are agreed. They seem to diverge from the statement of the first proposition to the close. It would have been very unsatisfactory to have selected the opinions of those courts which most favorably meet the views of the writer and ignored the opinions of others. Therefore he has felt compelled, not only to

<br>

### --- Page 0015 ---

vi PREFACE.

give the views of different courts, but to a considerable extent discuss the premises assumed by each. This discussion has not been satisfactory. There is much left in doubt. Resort to the doctrine of particular states must be had in many cases, for the want of general rules, recognized by all.

W. F. Barey.  
Eau Claire, Wis.,  
August 25, 1899

<br>

### --- Page 0084 ---

JURISDICTION, IMPEACHMENT, ETC.

PART I.

JURISDICTION.

CHAPTER 1.

JURISDICTION IN GENERAL.

1. Definition — Requisites of.

2. Subject continued.

3. Subject continued.

4. Jurisdiction of the subject-matter defined.

5. Jurisdiction of the person defined.

6. Jurisdiction and judgment — Distinction.

7. Jurisdiction of the subject-matter must be actually obtained.

8. Subject illustrated.

9. Subject further illustrated.

10. Subject further illustrated.

11.

12. Subject further illustrated.

13. Subject discussed.

14. Subject further illustrated.

15. Unconstitutional law — Effect upon jurisdiction.

16. Subject continued.

17. Subject continued — Former jeopardy.

18. Subject further illustrated and discussed.

19. Subject continued — Indeterminate sentences — Effect on jurisdiction of invalid law authorizing.

20. Subject-matter further discussed — Effect of invalid laws.

Sufficiency of indictment or pleading a question of law.

Subject further discussed — Constitutional methods of procedure.

Sec. 1. Jurisdiction — Definition and requisites.— Jurisdiction is the power to hear and determine the subject-matter in controversies between parties to a suit; to adjudicate or exercise any judicial power over them. The question of jurisdiction must be considered and decided before any court can move one step further in the cause, as any movement is necessarily the exercise of jurisdiction. The real question is whether, in the case before a court, its action is judicial or extra-judicial.

<br>

### --- Page 0085 ---

2 JURISDICTION, [§§ 2, 3.

with or without the authority of law to render a judgment or decree upon the rights of the litigant parties. If the law confers the power to render a judgment or decree, then the court has jurisdiction. What shall be adjudged or decreed between the parties, and with which is the right of the case, is judicial action and judgment, by hearing and determining it. Bracton defines jurisdiction to be authority to judge or declare the law between parties brought into court. It was said by Ryan, C. J. (In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 454), that this definition had never been bettered, probably never would be, and is of universal authority.

Sec. 2. Jurisdiction — Definition — Requisites of, continued. — If a tribunal is authorized to act upon a certain state of facts, and also to try whether the facts exist, then, if they are properly alleged before it, and the parties are legally notified and have an opportunity to contest them, the finding of such a tribunal upon those questions would be the finding of a competent tribunal, and ought to be conclusive until reversed in a direct proceeding. The jurisdiction of the tribunal does not depend upon the actual facts alleged, but upon the authority to determine the existence or non-existence of such facts, and to render judgment according to its findings. If such authority exists, then the tribunal has jurisdiction of the subject-matter. The mere decision of a tribunal, however, that it has authority to try and determine a case, when no such power exists in the court, does not give it the power. Its judgment may be questioned anywhere for want of jurisdiction. The jurisdiction of a court can never depend upon its decision upon the merits of a case brought before it, but upon its right to hear and determine it at all.

Sec. 3. Jurisdiction — Definition, requisites of, continued. It is coram judice whenever a case is presented that brings the power to hear and determine a cause into action. But before this power can be affirmed to exist, it must be made to

1 Rhode Island v. Massachusetts, 12 Pet. 657; United States v. Arredondo, 6 Pet. 691; Wanzer v. Howland et al., 10 Wis. 87; Spoors v. Cohen, 44 Ohio St. 497, Ex parte Watkins, 7 Pet. 568, 572.

<br>

### --- Page 0086 ---

§ 4.] IN GENERAL,  
appear that the law has given the tribunal capacity to entertain the complaint against the person or thing sought to be charged or affected, that such complaint has actually been preferred, and that such person or thing has been properly brought before the tribunal to answer the charge therein contained. 

Note To Sec. 3.—In People v. Sturtevant, supra, the judge who wrote the opinion had occasion to say, after quoting the doctrine first herein given: “This, I apprehend, points to the true line of inquiry to determine the question of jurisdiction.” In that case a person had been adjudged guilty of contempt in not obeying an injunction. It was further stated in the opinion: “We are not called upon to say whether the court decided right or not in granting the injunction, but whether it became their duty to decide either that it should be granted or denied. If such was their duty, then they had jurisdiction, and their decision, be it correct or erroneous, is the law of the case until it shall be reversed upon appeal, and can only be questioned upon a direct proceeding to review it, and not collaterally.” 

Src. 4. Jurisdiction — Subject-matter defined.— The courts in treating the question of jurisdiction usually use the terms “of the subject-matter” and “of the person.” These expressions are generally well understood by the profession, and scarcely need to be specifically defined, yet a treatise would not be considered complete without it. It was stated by one court: “By jurisdiction of the subject-matter is meant the nature of the cause of action and of the relief sought, and this is conferred by the sovereign authority which organizes the court, and is to be sought for in the general nature of its powers, or in authority specially conferred. The power to render the judgment or decree which the court may undertake to make in the particular cause depends upon the nature and extent of the authority vested in it by law in regard to the subject-matter of the cause.” It was stated by another that “jurisdiction of the subject-matter is power to adjudge concerning the general question involved, and is not dependent upon the state of facts.”

'Sheldon v. Newton, 3 Ohio St. 494; State ex rel. Moreland et al. v. Whitford, 54 Wis. 150; Ex parte Walker, 25 Ala. 577; Jones v. Brown, 54 Iowa, 44; Succession of Weigel, 17 La. Ann. 70; Mills v. Commonwealth, 13 Pa. St. 627; Goodman v. Winter, 64 Ala. 410; Trumwell v. Town, 34 Ark. 105; Hickman v. O'Neill, 10 Cal. 292; Ex parte Bennett, 44 Cal. 84; Bush v. Hanson, 70 Ill. 480; Schroeder v. Insurance Co., 104 Ill. 71; Brownsville v. Basse, 43 Tex. 440; People v. Sturtevant, 9 N.Y. 263.

2 Couper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall 303.

<br>

### --- Page 0087 ---

4 JURISDICTION, [§§ 5, 6.

which may appear in a particular case, arising, or which is claimed to have arisen, under that general question. One court has jurisdiction in criminal cases, another in civil cases; each in its sphere has jurisdiction of the subject-matter; yet the facts, the acts of the party proceeded against, may be the same in a civil case as in a criminal case; as, for instance, in a civil action for false and fraudulent representations and deceit, and in a criminal action for obtaining property under false pretenses. We should not say that the court of civil powers had jurisdiction of the criminal action, nor even vice versa. So that there is a more general meaning to the phrase ‘subject-matter,’ in this connection, than power to act upon a particular state of facts. It is the power to act upon the general, and, so to speak, abstract question, and to determine and adjudge whether the particular facts presented call for the exercise of the abstract power. Jurisdiction of the subject-matter is the power lawfully conferred to deal with the subject involved in the action.¹

Sec. 5. Jurisdiction of the person defined.— Jurisdiction of the person (it has been stated) is where the citizen acted upon is before the judge, either constructively or in fact, by reason of the service upon him of some process known to the law, and which has been duly issued and executed.² This statement is not sufficiently comprehensive. It is more strictly accurate to state that jurisdiction of the person is where process has been issued and served, or where such party has voluntarily appeared in the progress of the cause.

Sec. 6. Jurisdiction and judgment — Distinction between. While the statement of the rule which determines jurisdiction is seemingly explicit, yet courts have sometimes found it difficult to avoid confounding judgment with jurisdiction, and, as we shall see, apparent and sometimes actual conflict has been the result of the application of the rule. It seems absolutely necessary that to some considerable extent there should be placed before the reader precedents which have been established by the courts, together with the attendant reasoning,

¹ Hunt v. Hunt, 72 N. Y. 217.  
² Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308.  
³ Lange v. Benedict, 73 N. Y. 196.

<br>

### --- Page 0088 ---

§7.] IN GENERAL. By

where error was distinguished from want of jurisdiction. This branch of the subject will be still more fully considered and illustrated under the special subjects of “Habeas Corpus,” “Certiorari” and “Prohibition.”

Sec. 7. Jurisdiction of the subject-matter must be actually obtained.—It is held by respectable authority that the court must actually have jurisdiction of the subject-matter. It is not sufficient that the subject-matter may be that which is conferred by law, and therefore may come within its jurisdiction. As was stated by a learned court with respect to the jurisdiction of the court in the matter of a decree directing the sale of lands, made by a probate court: “But had the probate court the same jurisdiction in such matters as the common pleas, it would avail nothing in this case, for the reason that no such jurisdiction was invoked by the petition of the administrator. There is no averment in the petition that any land had been fraudulently conveyed, mediately or immediately, to her.” The facts were that the plaintiff's husband in his lifetime conveyed a small tract of land to his son, who immediately conveyed the same to the wife and mother. The proceeding referred to was that of the administrator of the original grantor to sell the land so conveyed for the payment of the debts of his intestate. The land was sold pursuant to the order of the probate court, whereupon the widow brought suit against the purchaser to recover the lands. It will be observed that the proceedings in the probate court did not invoke a setting aside of such conveyances as fraudulent, nor was it so decreed. Continuing in the language of the court: “The only averment, as to her is (in the original proceeding) that she is entitled to dower in the lands for which an order of sale is asked, and the only relief asked as to her is that her dower may be set off and assigned to her. It is by no means intended to question or impair the principle that, when jurisdiction has been obtained over the subject-matter of a cause by a court competent to exercise it, its judgment, however erroneous, cannot be questioned in a collateral proceeding. But a judgment rendered by a court of competent jurisdiction in a case brought before it, however erroneously that jurisdiction may have been exercised, is one thing, and a

<br>

### --- Page 0089 ---

6 JURISDICTION. [§ 8.

Judgment entered by a court of like jurisdiction in a case not before it, is another and a different thing. In the one case its judgment may be erroneous. In the other it is void. To bring a case before a court competent to adjudicate it, it is not only necessary that the parties should be en jus vocatio, cited or summoned in the manner required by the law of procedure, but a case must also be made or stated affecting the party against whom relief is asked. It is not necessary that the statement of the claim should be so perfect in form and substance as to be free from objection of demurrer to confer jurisdiction upon the court to hear and determine it. If the case presented invoked the jurisdiction of the court, and could have been perfected by amendment, the judgment of the court thereon could not be treated as a nullity. But in order to permit a party to amend, there must be something to amend by. So, unless a case is presented that could be amended, there is no case upon which a judgment can be rendered. A judgment rendered where no case has been stated is as much a judgment upon a case coram non judice as a judgment upon a case, however perfectly stated, before a court not clothed with jurisdiction to hear and determine it.

§ 8. Subject further illustrated.—The distinction referred to is further aptly illustrated by another case, where the defendant in the action was the purchaser of lands in a foreclosure proceeding. At the time of such purchase the plaintiff held a prior mortgage by assignment from the mortgagee. The complaint in the proceeding to foreclose the subsequent mortgage averred that such prior mortgagee had or claimed some interest in the property, not stating its nature or whether it was a prior or subsequent lien. Such prior mortgagee did not answer, and a judgment was rendered, containing the usual clause, barring the defendants of all rights, etc. The plaintiff, who was assignee of such prior mortgagee, brought an action to foreclose such mortgage. The question was whether such action could be maintained by reason of the apparent bar created by such prior judgment. It was stated by the court that such prior mortgagee was not a necessary party to the first fore-

Spoors v. Coen, 44 Ohio St. 497,

<br>

### --- Page 0090 ---

§ 9.) IN GENERAL.

closure suit, and, if made a party without any allegation contesting his title, he had a right to assume that the proceeding was to be conducted on the theory that his claim was paramount and therefore not subject to it. There was no allegation against its validity which he was called upon to defend. Therefore the prior judgment was not a bar to the plaintiff's right to maintain his suit.¹

Sec. 9. Subject further illustrated.—In Zerwis v. Smith, 9 N.Y. 502, it was held that the widow was not divested of dower in the lands of her deceased husband by a decree to that effect, in a proceeding to which she was made a party, for the foreclosure of a mortgage which she had not signed, the bill containing the usual allegation that she claimed some interest in the premises subsequent to the mortgage or otherwise; that a suit to recover dower in lands was not barred by such decree; that the decree barred rights and interests in the equity of redemption, but not those which were paramount to the title of both mortgagor and mortgagee. It was said by Edwards, J.:

“The conclusion to which I have arrived is that the question of the plaintiff's right of dower was neither raised nor decided, nor was it the proper subject of adjudication in the foreclosure suit. The provision of the statute which declares that the master’s deed shall be a bar to all parties to the suit in which the decree of foreclosure and sale is made does not cut off a right in reference to which a person neither is, nor can be properly made, a party to the suit, although he may have been properly made such party, and may be barred as to some other interest.”

Note to Sec. 9.—It was not intended to decide in the original case that if a party claiming a title prior to the mortgage should be made a defendant and should answer and litigate the question, and should have a decree against him, it would not conclude him in a collateral action. In another case it was said the object of a foreclosure suit is to bar the mortgagor and those claiming subsequent to the mortgage from redemption, and not to try the titles alleged to be paramount to that of the mortgagor. One made a defendant set up in his answer such a paramount title. The plaintiff demurred, and it was held the demurrer should be sustained; that the plaint-

¹ Strobe v. Downer, 13 Wis. 11; Wicke v. Lake et al., 21 Wis. 416; Pelton v. Farmin et al., 18 Wis.; Williamson v. Probasco, 4 Halst. Ch.; Roche v. Knight et al., 21 Wis. 320; S7.

<br>

### --- Page 0091 ---

6 JURISDICTION. [§ 8.

Judgment entered by a court of like jurisdiction in a case not before it, is another and a different thing. In the one case its judgment may be erroneous. In the other it is void. To bring a case before a court competent to adjudicate it, it is not only necessary that the parties should be en jus vocatio, cited or summoned in the manner required by the law of procedure, but a case must also be made or stated affecting the party against whom relief is asked. It is not necessary that the statement of the claim should be so perfect in form and substance as to be free from objection of demurrer to confer jurisdiction upon the court to hear and determine it. If the case presented invoked the jurisdiction of the court, and could have been perfected by amendment, the judgment of the court thereon could not be treated as a nullity. But in order to permit a party to amend, there must be something to amend by. So, unless a case is presented that could be amended, there is no case upon which a judgment can be rendered. A judgment rendered where no case has been stated is as much a judgment upon a case coram non judice as a judgment upon a case, however perfectly stated, before a court not clothed with jurisdiction to hear and determine it.

Sec. 8. Subject farther illustrated.— The distinction referred to is further aptly illustrated by another case, where the defendant in the action was the purchaser of lands in a foreclosure proceeding. At the time of such purchase the plaintiff held a prior mortgage by assignment from the mortgagee. The complaint in the proceeding to foreclose the subsequent mortgage averred that such prior mortgagee had or claimed some interest in the property, not stating its nature or whether it was a prior or subsequent lien. Such prior mortgagee did not answer, and a judgment was rendered, containing the usual clause, barring the defendants of all rights, etc. The plaintiff, who was assignee of such prior mortgagee, brought an action to foreclose such mortgage. The question was whether such action could be maintained by reason of the apparent bar created by such prior judgment. It was stated by the court that such prior mortgagee was not a necessary party to the first fore-

1 Spoors v. Coen, 44 Ohio St. 497.

<br>

### --- Page 0092 ---

§ 9.) IN GENERAL.

closure suit, and, if made a party without any allegation contesting his title, he had a right to assume that the proceeding was to be conducted on the theory that his claim was paramount and therefore not subject to it. There was no allegation against its validity which he was called upon to defend. Therefore the prior judgment was not a bar to the plaintiff's right to maintain his suit.

Sec. 9. Subject further illustrated.—In Lewis v. Smith, 9 N. Y. 502, it was held that the widow was not divested of dower in the lands of her deceased husband by a decree to that effect, in a proceeding to which she was made a party, for the foreclosure of a mortgage which she had not signed, the bill containing the usual allegation that she claimed some interest in the premises subsequent to the mortgage or otherwise; that a suit to recover dower in lands was not barred by such decree; that the decree barred rights and interests in the equity of redemption, but not those which were paramount to the title of both mortgagor and mortgagee. It was said by Edwards, J.: “The conclusion to which I have arrived is that the question of the plaintiff's right of dower was neither raised nor decided, nor was it the proper subject of adjudication in the foreclosure suit. The provision of the statute which declares that the master's deed shall be a bar to all parties to the suit in which the decree of foreclosure and sale is made does not cut off a right in reference to which a person neither is, nor can be properly made, a party to the suit, although he may have been properly made such party, and may be barred as to some other interest.”

Note To Sec. 9.—It was not intended to decide in the original case that if a party claiming a title prior to the mortgage should be made a defendant and should answer and litigate the question, and should have a decree against him, it would not conclude him in a collateral action. In another case it was said the object of a foreclosure suit is to bar the mortgagor and those claiming subsequent to the mortgage from redemption, and not to try the titles alleged to be paramount to that of the mortgagor. One made a defendant set up in his answer such a paramount title. The plaintiff demurred, and it was held the demurrer should be sustained; that the plaint—

Strobe v. Downer, 13 Wis. 11; Wicke v. Lake et al, 21 Wis. 416; Pelton v. Farmin et al, 18 Wis 222; Williamson v. Probasco, 4 Halst. Ch. Roche v. Knight et al, 21 Wis. 329; S71,

<br>

### --- Page 0093 ---

2

8 JURISDICTION. [§ 10.

iff was not obliged to litigate such title in that suit. Pelton v. Farmin, 18 Wis. 222. In another, where a person was in possession of a part of the premises under a paramount title, and in connection with another person in possession of the remainder under a prior mortgage, and being made a party answered, setting up such prior right and interest, which was litigated and found to be prior, it was held that a judgment in the usual form, providing for a sale of the premises and that the purchaser be let into possession, which bars all the defendants, etc., of all rights, etc., was erroneous. Such a judgment cannot be made to perform the office of a suit in ejectment. Roche v. Knight, 21 Wis. 329. In another, where one made defendant in foreclosure as claiming a subsequent interest answers setting up possession under a paramount title, it was held that though plaintiff may not be compelled to litigate such title in that suit, yet the judgment should protect defendant's rights, and not leave him liable to be deprived of his possession by a writ of assistance, his title not having been litigated. Wicke v. Lake et al., 21 Wis. 410.

Sec. 10. Subject further illustrated.— Wall v. Wall, 123 Pa. St. 545, 10 Am. St. Rep. 549, was an action of ejectment. The plaintiff claimed title through and under an alleged will, and offered it in evidence. It had been admitted to probate, and though more than five years had elapsed since that time, there had been no appeal therefrom. In that state such a decree is held judicial, and after the lapse of five years without appeal is conclusive as to the real estate disposed of by it. It was said by the court: “If the court has no jurisdiction it is of no consequence that the proceedings have been formally conducted, for they are coram non judice. If such want of jurisdiction appear upon the record, it can be taken advantage of at any time and in any court where the conclusiveness of the judgment is the subject of judicial inquiry. The reason for this is found in the fact that the record of the judgment bears on its face the proof of its illegality, and shows the want of power in the tribunal to render it. When it is offered as a conclusive adjudication between the parties, an inspection shows it is not, because the court had no power to make an adjudication. The adjudication of the register was conferred by statute. Within these limits his decrees are conclusive. Outside of them he is without any authority to make a decree, and his decree if made is a nullity.” The particular point decided was that the writing, not having been signed, was not a will, and the register could not make a will out of it. He was therefore without jurisdic-

<br>

### --- Page 0094 ---

§11. IN GENERAL.

Substantially the same was held by the same court in another case. That was also an action of ejectment. The register had probated two papers attached to a formal will, as though they had been part of it. It was contended that the decree was conclusive as to the testamentary character of the attached papers. The reasoning was that probating did not make a will. That the register had no jurisdiction over these writings. They were merely advisory to the executor. Their character and his want of jurisdiction were apparent on the face of the record. His decree, therefore, was an absolute nullity.

NOTE TO SEC. 10.—It appears to the writer that the two cases last noticed are somewhat exceptional, to say the least, and it is doubtful if similar ones would receive the same conclusion in other courts. The very matter which was the subject of adjudication by the register was the validity and effect of the writings submitted. He undoubtedly had jurisdiction to determine the question whether they were what they purported to be or not. If he had decided that they were not what they purported to be, by reason of not being properly executed, it would not be questioned but that the register had jurisdiction to so decide, and that decision would be conclusive until reversed. It follows, and such is the law, that the character of the decision rendered by a tribunal, whether erroneous or proper, does not affect the question of the power to act upon or decide the question. The rule thus announced by the learned court, if followed, necessarily would lead to serious results and overturn much of the law that is now rightfully and firmly established. For instance, in an action upon contract, judgment is rendered upon the theory that the contract was properly executed. Though but evidence in the action, it is filed with or made a part of the record. Land is sold on execution based upon such judgment. It afterwards becomes essential to use such judgment to support a title or supply a link in the chain of title to such land. Would it be held in such collateral proceedings that the court would review the judgment of the former court as to the legal effect of such contract as evidenced by reason of its recitals, conditions or manner of execution; and if in the judgment of the latter court it was defective, or even void, declare for such reason that the court was without jurisdiction to render judgment, and its judgment rendered was and is a nullity? The probate of a will is a proceeding in rem; all persons who have rights or interests that may be affected are parties, and as such must proceed in the original proceeding.

Sec. 11. Subject further illustrated.—In another case it was held that a judgment and sale thereunder of real property, in an action where the jurisdiction was obtained in a proper

1 Bowlby v. Thunder, 105 Pa. St. 173.

<br>

### --- Page 0095 ---

10 | JURISDICTION, §§ 12, 13.

manner by publication and attachment against a non-resident who did not appear upon a cause of action stated, and the plaintiff thereafter, before judgment, amended his petition, setting forth an entirely new cause of action, upon which the judgment was based, was no bar to a subsequent action to try the title to the land. The principle was that the court had only acquired jurisdiction of the defendant as to the cause of action set forth in the pleadings on file when the citation was published, not the cause of action subsequently stated; therefore the judgment was void.

Sec. 12. Subject discussed.— The principle which I extract from the foregoing and other cases operating as a qualification of the general rule stated is that the general rule has application only when the acknowledged jurisdiction of the tribunal has been invoked over a particular subject; and where such jurisdiction has not thus been invoked as to such matter, the tribunal is powerless to act upon it or decree with respect to it; and the fact that its jurisdiction has been properly called into exercise with respect to another subject or another right does not carry with it the power to adjudicate upon any other, not properly incident thereto.

Sec. 13. Subject further illustrated.— The reasoning of the New York court of appeals in the famous Tweed case is very forcible and convincing in support of the general rule, and illustrates clearly the distinction between judgment and jurisdiction. The questions were as to the effect of joining in the same indictment several distinct offenses, and the refusal of the prosecuting officer to elect as to which he would prosecute, and the imposing of cumulative sentence upon the accused. It was urged that such joinder of distinct offenses was not only improper, but in violation of fundamental rights secured to the accused by the constitution: therefore the court was without jurisdiction. The reply to this argument was that the court had jurisdiction of the person of the accused, and of criminal offenses committed in the county of New York, and necessarily had jurisdiction to pass upon the form and sufficiency of the

1 Stuart et al. v. Anderson et al., 70 Tex. 588, 85 S.W. Rep. 293,

<br>

### --- Page 0096 ---

S§ 14, 15.] IN GENERAL. 11

indictment, and the order and course of the trial, and decide every question that arose in its progress; and whether the determinations of the court upon any or all questions were right or wrong did not affect the jurisdiction. In other words, the court had jurisdiction to make wrong as well as right decisions in all stages of the prosecution, and whether those made were right or wrong cannot be raised on habeas corpus.

Sec. 14. Sufficiency of indictment or pleading a question of law.—Whether an indictment or information sets forth in proper terms the offense which the statute describes and forbids, is in every case a question of law which must necessarily be decided by the court in which the case originates, and is therefore clearly within its jurisdiction. The same is the rule with respect to pleadings in civil actions. Their sufficiency as to statement of a cause of action is always a question of law, to be determined by the court in which the action is pending. If the decision is erroneous it is an error of judgment merely, which can be corrected on a writ of error or by appeal. The question of jurisdiction is not involved.

Sec. 15. Unconstitutional law—Effect upon jurisdiction. There is some controversy as to the effect upon the jurisdiction of a court where it proceeds to determine the rights of parties or the guilt of the accused of an offense under an act which in fact is in violation of a provision of the constitution. It is conceded as a general rule that a court of general jurisdiction has the power to decide all questions of law that may arise in the particular case. That the question of the validity of any law that may be applicable must be decided, when the question is raised, in the first instance by the court which is proceeding with the trial. It is purely a question of law, yet the courts quite generally hold the doctrine that proceedings under an unconstitutional law are absolutely void; that the act, being void, has no effect to confer jurisdiction; that the power to determine is wanting; therefore any decision the court may make is without the power to make it, and must be void. Thus—

1 People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N.Y. 500, 651; In re Blair, 4 W 2; State ex rel. Larkin, 70 Wis. 676; Reed v. Wright, 26 Gr. (Iowa), 15.

2 In Ex parte Yarbrough, 110 U.S. 651; Hauser v. State, 33 Wis. 673.

<br>

### --- Page 0097 ---

$16, 17.

12 JURISDICTION, [

trative of the rule was a case where a party was committed for non-compliance with an order of the court. The order was made without the court having previously adjudged the party to be in contempt. It was held that such commitment under the circumstances was imprisonment for debt, in violation of a provision of the constitution of the state; therefore the court was without authority to make the order. The question was not directly passed upon by the supreme court of Michigan. It was held, however, where the question was whether an act authorizing the confinement of one acquitted of the commission of a crime by reason of insanity in an insane asylum attached to the state prison was constitutional, that the question could not be raised on habeas corpus, but only upon writ of error or other appropriate proceeding. This would seem to imply that the question of jurisdiction was not involved.

Sec. 16. Subject continued.— This subject was discussed by the supreme court of the United States in two leading cases. In the former, speaking of the unconstitutionality of an act of congress, and a judgment passed under its authority, it was said: “If this position is well taken, it affects the foundation of the whole proceedings. An unconstitutional law is void. An offense created by it is not a crime. A conviction under it is not merely erroneous, but it is illegal and void, and cannot be a legal cause of imprisonment.” In the latter, the question was not exactly the same, but involved a plea of second jeopardy for the same offense, contrary to an express provision of the constitution. It was there stated: “It is difficult to see why a conviction and punishment under an unconstitutional law is more violative of a person's constitutional rights than an unconstitutional conviction and punishment under a valid law. In the first case, it is true, the court has no authority to take cognizance of the case, but in the other it has no authority to render judgment against the defendant.”

Sec. 17. Subject continued — Former jeopardy.— The force of the conclusion stated in the latter case is not weakened by

1 In re Blair, 4 Wis. 522.  
2 In Matter of Underwood, 30 Mich. 376; Hans Neilson, Petitioner, 131 U.S. 176.  
3 Ex parte Siebold, 100 U.S. 371. See In re Harris, 47 Mo. 164.

<br>

### --- Page 0098 ---

§ 17.) IN GENERAL, 13°

What was said in the former case, In re Bigelow, 113 U.S. 328, and in a later case, In re Belt, 159 U.S. 95. In the former, the facts were that fourteen indictments were pending against the petitioner for embezzlement, and it was ordered by the court that they be consolidated and tried together. A jury was impaneled and sworn, and the case opened by the district attorney, when the court took a recess. Upon reconvening, the court decided that the indictments could not be well tried together, and directed the jury to be discharged from further consideration of them, and rescinded the order of consolidation. The prisoner was thereupon tried before the same jury on one of the indictments and found guilty, all against his protest and without his consent. The contention in the federal supreme court was that he had been once placed in jeopardy by the proceedings and his trial and conviction were illegal. In the course of the opinion the court said: “The trial court had jurisdiction of the offense described in the indictment on which the prisoner was tried. It had jurisdiction of the prisoner, who was properly brought before the court. It had jurisdiction to hear the charge and the evidence against the prisoner. It had jurisdiction to hear and decide upon the defenses offered by him. The matter now presented was one of those defenses. Whether it was a sufficient defense was a matter of law, on which the court must pass so far as it was a matter of law, and on which the jury, under the instruction of the court, must pass, if we can suppose any of the facts were such as required submission to the jury. Clearly the court had not only jurisdiction to try and decide the question raised, but it was its imperative duty to do so. If the court makes a mistake on such a trial it is error, which may be corrected in the usual modes of correcting such errors; but that the court had jurisdiction to decide upon the matter raised by the plea, both as matter of law and of fact, cannot be doubted.” It is to be noted in In re Neilson, supra, that no reference is made by the court to this case, though apparently at least, it might seem the two cases are in conflict. The Bigelow case was an application direct to the Supreme Court of the United States for a writ of habeas corpus, which was denied. The Neilson case was an appeal from a final order of a district court refusing to issue a writ of habeas corpus. The Bigelow case was cited in the brief of the solicitor-general. The

<br>

### --- Page 0099 ---

14 JURISDICTION. [§ 1%.

court starts out with the statement of the question involved as follows: “The first question to be considered is whether, if the petitioner’s position was true, that he had been convicted twice for the same offense, and that the court erred in its decision, he could have relief by habeas corpus,” and it held that he was entitled to the writ. It is true that the facts which were relied upon as showing a former jeopardy were contained in the record in the plea of autrefois convict, which technically was admitted to be true by the demurrer. The same question was before the supreme court at a time intermediate the Bigelow and Neilson Cases (Ex parte Snow, 120 U.S. 274), and the same conclusion reached. In this case the Bigelow Case is referred to and distinguished. In the Bigelow Case is found this statement by the court: “In the present case no verdict nor judgment was rendered, no sentence enforced, and it remained with the trial court to decide whether the acts on which he relied were a defense to any trial at all.” In the Snow Case the accused was convicted and punished as for all three distinct offenses of the same character (which the federal supreme court held was but one continuous offense), and, after suffering the penalty imposed for the one, petitioned for the writ, to be released from the punishment imposed for the others. The court says: “Not only had the court which tried them no jurisdiction to inflict a punishment in respect of more than one of the convictions, but, as the want of jurisdiction appears on the face of the judgment, the objection may be taken by habeas corpus, when the sentence on more than one of the convictions is sought to be enforced. These considerations distinguish the case from that of Ex parte Bigelow, and bring it within the principle of such cases as Ex parte Milligan, 4 Wall. 2-181; Ex parte Lange, 18 Wall. 163-178, and Ex parte Wilson, 114 U.S. 417.” The exact questions thus decided would seem to be that the trial court has jurisdiction to decide whether matter alleged by way of defense as constituting a second jeopardy is such in fact or not, and a wrong decision in this respect is but error. But where it appears by the record that the accused has suffered the penalty imposed for the commission of the offense, and it is sought to enforce further punishment for the same offense, the court will protect, by the writ of habeas corpus, on the ground that such further punishment is in excess of the juris-

<br>

### --- Page 0100 ---

diction of the court. Where, however, no penalty has been imposed, or, if imposed, has not been satisfied, so that there is no punishment exacted beyond the jurisdiction of the court for the offense, then habeas corpus will not lie.

Sec. 18. Subject further illustrated.— The Belt Case, 159 U.S. 95, involved the question of waiver of a constitutional right or privilege. The accused was on trial for larceny. The record of a conviction for a similar offense was offered for the purpose of showing a second offense, upon conviction of the commission of which there might be an increased penalty. The objection to this record as evidence was that it appeared therefrom that the accused had waived a trial by jury (there being a statute permitting it), and that such statute was invalid, as the prisoner could not waive such right or privilege, and that the first conviction was necessarily void, and the second predicated thereon was likewise void. It was simply held that the court before which the trial was pending had jurisdiction and authority to determine the validity of the act which authorized the waiver of a jury, as to whether the record was legitimate proof of a first offense. See In re Staff, 63 Wis. 215, to the effect that such question is jurisdictional. This is far different from a case where the accused is held in custody under a void statute. The validity of a statute under which a prisoner is held in custody may be inquired into under a writ of habeas corpus as affecting the jurisdiction of the court which ordered his imprisonment, as laws are only valid where they are within the constitutional power of the body that enacts them.

Sec. 19. Subject continued — Indeterminate sentences — Effect on jurisdiction of invalid law authorizing.— In a case determined by one of the state courts the contention was that a provision of the statutes of the state which authorized general or indeterminate sentences was invalid. It was held that this question was not one affecting the jurisdiction of the court. The reasoning was that the trial court had jurisdiction to try the petitioner for the offense charged, and, on conviction—

In re Coy, 127 U.S. 731; Brown Case, 120 Mass. 118; Ex parte Rollins v. Duffus, 66 Iowa, 193; Whitcomb’s 80 Va. 314.

<br>

### --- Page 0101 ---

16 JURISDICTION. : [§ 20.

tion, to sentence him to imprisonment therefor. It also had jurisdiction of his person. On the theory that the general statute is invalid, the court merely entered and enforced the wrong judgment. These cases are very close to the border line. If the accused, as we have seen, is held in custody by virtue of an act that is void by reason of being contrary to constitutional provisions, then the power of the legislature to enact such law is a question proper to be inquired into upon habeas corpus. Will it do to say that because the court has jurisdiction of the offense and the person that it can impose any judgment it sees fit, indefinite as to time, when not so authorized? It is unlike those cases where there is power to imprison and the judgment is excessive as to the term. The judgment may be valid as to that part within the power, and void as to the excess, as we discuss later.

Sxc. 20. Subject further discussed — Effect of invalid law. In another case the contention was that the statute which provided for the trial of the issue of insanity first by a jury impaneled for such purpose, and, in case of a disagreement, then the court should proceed to the trial of all the issues, contravened provisions of the constitution, and a conviction under such proceedings was void. The reply of the court was, “that if it be conceded that the statute was invalid, still the court had jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person, and on his conviction of the offense charged it had jurisdiction to sentence him to imprisonment for life. The judgment, therefore, was not void, if erroneous.” It may be true, as stated in another case, the court had power to pass upon the validity of this statute, yet that is true in every case; true in respect to those upon which jurisdiction alone depends. This is no certain test. If the court decides that such a statute is valid and therefore gives it jurisdiction of the offense, such decision does not have the effect to clothe the court with jurisdiction if ultimately the statute is declared to be invalid. The serious question suggested by the foregoing decision and others is, to what extent may courts arbitrarily or in pursuance of void statutes ignore well-established constitutional methods in proceedings in criminal cases, so as to place

V In re Pikulik, 81 Wis 158; In re French, 81 Wis. 597. In re Schusler, 82 Wis. 610.

<br>

### --- Page 0102 ---

§ 21.) IN GENERAL, 17

the accused beyond the reach of habeas corpus. The constitution has wisely provided that certain safeguards thrown around one accused of crime must be observed by courts. The necessity for these provisions was suggested by experience. They were not to be considered mere idle ceremonies. They are as essential to his protection as the courts themselves. To say that, because a court has been clothed with power to hear and determine as to the guilt or innocence of one accused of crime, it may disregard every provision made by the fundamental law for the protection of the accused in his constitutional right to enjoy a fair trial, — that he may be condemned without being heard, — and this be only an error, is simply, as stated by a learned court, “a judicial suspension of the writ of habeas corpus.”

Sec. 21. Subject discussed — Proceedings must be in conformity to constitutional methods. — The true rule undoubtedly is that the court must proceed according to established methods; and where the record discloses that such methods have been ignored or violated, then the court is without jurisdiction to proceed to judgment, because it has no jurisdiction to proceed otherwise. This is in accord with an express declaration of the supreme court of the United States, as follows: “It is firmly established that if the court which rendered a judgment has not jurisdiction to render it, either because the proceedings or the law under which they are taken are unconstitutional, or for any other reason, the judgment is void and may be questioned collaterally, and a defendant who is imprisoned under and by virtue of it may be discharged from custody on habeas corpus.” In Jones v. Leobhins, 14 Mass. 329, there is an elaborate discussion of certain constitutional provisions and the effect of the bill of rights, which goes far to sustain the doctrine above stated, as being essential to that due process of law without which no man can be condemned. And in Cancemi v. People, 6 N.Y. 123, it was stated by the court: “When issue is joined upon the indictment, the trial must be by the tribunal and in the mode which the constitution and laws provide, without any essential change.” It was held in Wisconsin that habeas corpus would lie where it was alleged

1 Hans Neilson, Petitioner, 131 U. S. 176, 182.

<br>

### --- Page 0103 ---

18 JURISDICTION, [§ 21.

that the petitioner was convicted without a trial by jury; that the law authorizing such a waiver was not unconstitutional. It was held, further, that the law in that particular case was valid; but it was stated that if the prisoner could not effectually waive a trial by jury, the court had no jurisdiction to try him. So it is held that the declaration of article 5 of the amendments to the constitution, that “no person shall be held to answer for a capital or otherwise infamous crime unless on a presentment or indictment of a grand jury,” is jurisdictional, and no court of the United States has authority to try a prisoner without indictment or presentment in such cases. When such an indictment is changed by the court or prosecuting officer, though in immaterial matters, the instrument thus changed is no longer an indictment. There must be re-submission to the grand jury. Upon an indictment so changed the court can proceed no further. There is nothing for which the prisoner can be held to answer. A trial on such indictment is void. There is nothing to try. The federal courts exercise a discretion with respect to the issue of habeas corpus to determine in advance of the trial by or determination of a state court the unconstitutionality of the law by which the prisoner is held in custody.

1 In re Staff, 63 Wis. 285.
2 Ex parte Bain, 121 U.S.
3 Ex parte Royall, 117 U.S. 241.

<br>

EXCESS OF JURISDICTION.
