Sec. 22. General rule. — We have before stated a general rule, in substance, that where a court has jurisdiction it has the right to decide every question in the cause, and its judg-

<br>

### --- Page 0105 ---

20 JURISDICTION, [§ 93,

ment until reversed is binding on every court. To this rule, however, there are exceptions. A court must proceed and determine within the limits of the power conferred. If it renders a judgment or decree in an action or proceeding, where jurisdiction has attached, that it was not authorized or empowered to render at all, such judgment or decree is in excess of its jurisdiction, and for that reason a nullity. So, if it render a judgment or decree which is within its authority as to part only, but includes also that which is not within its power, the excess will be a nullity, and, if the valid and invalid parts are independent of each other, the whole will not be void, but only such part as is in excess of the powers of the court.

Src. 23. Procedure must conform to legal methods.—It was stated by the supreme court of the United States that the foregoing rule was only correct where the court proceeds, after acquiring jurisdiction of the cause, according to the established modes governing the class to which the case belongs, and does not transcend in the extent or character of its judgment the law which is applicable to it. The doctrine was stated in another case in that court as follows: “The jurisdiction having attached in the case, everything done within the power of that jurisdiction, when collaterally questioned, is held conclusive of the rights of the parties, unless impeached for fraud.” It was further said: “Though the court may possess jurisdiction of a cause, of the subject-matter and of the parties, it is still limited in its modes of procedure and in the extent and character of its judgments. It must act judicially in all things, and cannot transcend the power conferred by the law.” Hence, if an action be upon a money demand, the court would have no power to pass judgment or imprisonment. If for libel or personal tort, it could not order specific performance of a contract. If for possession of real property, the court could not admit in the case the probate of a will. The judgments in the supposed cases would be absolutely void, because the court, in rendering them, would transcend the limit of its authority in those cases.

1 Randall v. Howard, 2 Black, 585; 2 Windsor v. MeVeigh, 93 U.S. 274. Elliott v. Peirsol, 1 Pet. 328; Thompson v. Tolmie, 2 Pet. 157.  
3 Cornett v. Williams, 20 Wall 226.

<br>

### --- Page 0106 ---

§ 24. EXCESS.

Src. 24. Authority to render the particular judgment must exist.— The power of the court to render the particular judgment is a proper subject of inquiry in determining the question of jurisdiction. Thus, where a court had imposed a fine and imprisonment, where the statute only conferred power to punish by fine or imprisonment, and the fine had been paid, it was held the court could not, even during the same term, modify the judgment by imposing imprisonment instead of the former sentence. The power to punish was exhausted by the first judgment. The statement made by the court was: “It is no answer to this to say that the court had jurisdiction of the person of the prisoner and of the offense under the statute. It by no means follows that these two facts make valid, however erroneous it may be, any judgment the court may render in such a case. If a justice of the peace having jurisdiction to fine for a misdemeanor, and with the party properly charged before him, should render a judgment that he be hung, it would simply be void. Why void? Because he had no power to render such a judgment. Or if on an indictment for treason the court should render a judgment of attaint, whereby the heirs of the criminal could not inherit his property, which should by the judgment of the court be confiscated to the state, it would be void as to the attainder, because in excess of the authority of the court and forbidden by the constitution.” It appears that the court of appeals of New York were not of the same mind as the federal court. There, where the court exceeded its jurisdiction in sentencing one accused of an offense to the state prison, the court upon habeas corpus refused to discharge him, but remanded him to the sheriff for the trial court to pronounce such a sentence as was authorized by law. The Wisconsin court had occasion to discuss this question. There, upon an information for assault with intent to kill, the jury found a verdict of guilty of assault and battery, and the court sentenced the accused to six months’ imprisonment, to pay a fine of $500 and the costs of the action, and to be imprisoned until such fine and

People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 18 Wall 163; Ex parte Yarbrough, 60 N.Y. 550; Ex parte Page, 49 Mo. 110; U.S. 651; Ex parte Siebold, id. 291; Stewart et al v. Anderson, 70 S71. See sec. 7 et seq.

Tex. SYY 8 SL OW. Rep, 205; In re ex parte Lange, 18 Wall. 163. Pierce, 44 Wis. 411; Ex parte Lange, 2 People ex rel. Kelley, 87 N.Y. 212.

<br>

### --- Page 0107 ---

22 JURISDICTION, [§ 25.

costs were paid, such being the limit and extent of the punishment prescribed by law for the offense of assault and battery, but beyond that prescribed for assault—the provision in respect to the latter being imprisonment or fine. At the expiration of the term of imprisonment the defendant applied for his discharge upon habeas corpus, not having paid the fine and costs imposed, and being detained by virtue of the judgment in that respect. The court stated that if it were conceded that it was error to convict under the information for assault and battery, yet such error was not jurisdictional. The court had jurisdiction of offenses of that character. If there had been no authority in law for the circuit court to punish the petitioner by both fine and imprisonment, a clear case of excess of jurisdiction would have been presented. The ruling of the court thus appears to be that if the defendant had been convicted of assault and battery, which was beyond the power of the court in that case, and having imposed the penalty peculiar to that offense, this was mere error. This ruling is in conflict with the decisions of the federal court.

Sec. 25. Subject further illustrated.—The principle is well illustrated in another case decided by the federal supreme court. Under the confiscation acts of Congress, certain lands of French Forest had been condemned and sold, and one Bigelow became the holder of the title conveyed by these proceedings. After Forest’s death, his son and heir brought suit to recover the lands, contending that under the joint resolution of Congress, which declared that condemnation under that act should not be held to work a forfeiture of the lands of the offender beyond his natural life, the title of Bigelow terminated with the death of the elder Forest. The decree confiscating the property, in terms, ordered all the estate to be sold. It was argued in opposition that, though this part of the decree might be erroneous, it was not void. It was said by the court: “Here was a case of a proceeding in rem where the property was within the power of the court, and its authority to confiscate and sell beyond question. The extent of that power was limited by statute.” To the argument that such decree was conclusive it was said: “Doubtless a decree of a court having jurisdiction to make the

<br>

### --- Page 0108 ---

§ 26.) EXCESS. 23

Decree cannot be impeached collaterally, but under the act of congress the district court had no power to order a sale which should confer upon the purchaser rights outlasting the life of French Forest. Had it done so, it would have transcended its jurisdiction. In commenting upon this language, the same court, in Ex parte Lange, 18 Wall. 163, say: “But why could it not? Not because it wanted jurisdiction of the property or the offense, but because, in the very act of rendering a judgment of confiscation, it condemned more than it had authority to condemn. In other words, in a case where it had full jurisdiction to render one kind of judgment, operative upon the same property, it rendered one which included that which it had a right to render, and something more, and this excess was held simply void.” Another illustration of the exception to the general rule where the distinction is aptly recognized is found in a case decided by the supreme court of Wisconsin. There the mother of a child was committed to the county jail for refusing to comply with an order of the court in divorce proceedings commanding her to restore the child, which she had abducted, to the husband and father. It was held that under the provisions of the statute of that state such form of punishment was not authorized, therefore the order of commitment was void. It was conceded that the court had jurisdiction of the subject-matter as well as of the person of the defendant. It was devoid, however, of the power to exercise its jurisdiction in that manner. Justice Ryan makes a vigorous dissent, asserting that the court merely erred in its judgment of the law, that is, the proper construction of the effect of the statute, and likens it to every other case where the trial court mistakes the law, claiming that the order was the result of mere error of judgment and did not affect the jurisdiction of the court. This conflict of judicial opinion serves to illustrate that oftentimes the dividing line is close, and acts as an incentive to the courts to act with caution and deliberation if they would avoid a conflict of opinion.

Sec. 26. Subject further illustrated — Cumulative sentences.— In the Zweed case the accused was tried for a number of separate and distinct misdemeanors at the same time,

1 Bigelow v. Forest, 9 Wall. 339.  
2 In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.

<br>

### --- Page 0109 ---

24 JURISDICTION. [§ 27.

separately stated and charged in one indictment, and convicted. The court adjudged cumulative sentences, the total imprisonment exceeding by many years that for which he could have been sentenced upon conviction for any one. It was held that the punishment inflicted for more than one offense was in excess of the jurisdiction of the court and void. The court very aptly said: “A party held only by judgments thus pronounced, and therefore void for want of jurisdiction, or by reason of the excess of jurisdiction, is not put to his writ of error, but may be released by habeas corpus. It will not answer to say that a court having power to give a particular judgment can give any judgment, and that a judgment not authorized by law, and contrary to law, is merely voidable and not void, and must be corrected by error. This would be trifling with the law, the liberty of the citizen and the protection thrown about his person by the bill of rights and the constitution, and creating a judicial despotism. It would be to defeat justice and nullify the writ of habeas corpus by the merest technicality and the most artificial process of reasoning.” The question whether separate and distinct offenses may be charged in one indictment has been a subject of frequent discussion in the courts, and the conclusion reached that those of the same grade may be thus united. So it is uniformly held that the same act, constituting a single offense, may be charged in different ways in different counts in the same indictment. But whether, where several distinct offenses have been so separately stated, upon conviction there can be judgment and sentence in excess of the limit prescribed upon conviction for one such offense, is a grave question, some courts maintaining that there can be but one sentence, while others maintain that, under statutes authorizing it, cumulative sentences may be imposed.

Sec. 27. Trial for two distinct offenses at the same time.
There is much force in the arguments which are advanced to

1 People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N.Y. 559.

2 People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N.Y. 559; In re McCormick, 24 Wis. 492; People v. Forbes, 22 Cal. 135; State v. Smith, 5 Day. 175; Russell v. Rawle, 459; Brown v. Commonwealth, 4 Serg. & Rawle. 489.

3 Same cases, 459; People v. Forbes, 22 Cal. 135.

<br>

### --- Page 0110 ---

§ 28.] EXC

3. ' Lod

sustain the position that an accused ought not to be tried for two distinct offenses at the same time. Such a practice is fraught with danger to the accused, and can never be done except at great risk of doing him injustice. “Besides the confusion and embarrassment in which a trial at one time for many offenses would involve the accused, such a practice, if tolerated, would break down and utterly obliterate many principles of law that are not only well established, but essential to the safety of the citizen. Nothing is better settled than that the evil reputation of the accused shall not be offered to strengthen the proofs against him. That other misdeeds shall not be alleged, proved or attempted to be proven is equally well-known law. If the public prosecutor or a common informer in a penal action could put an unpopular person on trial for every delinquency imputed to him by common fame, such a one, however innocent, might often sink under the weight of unmerited opprobrium.” (Utterances of Chas. O’Conor in the Tweed case, approved by the court.) Again, the joinder of felonies is disallowed in England, because the prisoner's right of challenge would be reduced; and the same result follows here, not only as to felonies, but misdemeanors also, as the right of challenge in this country is secured in both cases, while not so in England. Prior to 1847 no such right existed in New York. The result of the rule which would permit the trial of distinct offenses at the same time would be that the accused should be entitled to the number of challenges permitted for each offense, multiplied by the number of offenses charged, or else he is deprived of the right of challenge in respect to all of such offenses except one. The doctrine quite generally prevails that distinct offenses, not of the same grade or class, not incident to the same act, cannot be united in the same indictment or information.

Sec. 28. Judgment in excess of that prescribed by law.—It is not, however, to be understood that every excess of the exercise of power is an excess of jurisdiction, such as to render the judgment pronounced an absolute nullity. A judgment sentencing a person to imprisonment for a longer term than the statute warrants is merely erroneous, and not void for want of jurisdiction. The reasoning which justifies this

<br>

### --- Page 0111 ---

26 JURISDICTION. [§ 29.

conclusion is, in effect, that the court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter or offense and of the person. The excess of punishment is merely a mistake of judgment. The New York court held where one convicted of an offense was sentenced to the state’s prison, the power of the court not extending to the imposing of such a sentence, that the sentence could not be carried into effect, on the ground that it was in excess of the jurisdiction of the court, and on a habeas corpus remanded the prisoner to the custody of the sheriff, that the court which sentenced him might deal with him according to law.

Sec. 29. Subject continued.—Conceding the application of the rule as above stated to be correct, it follows from what precedes it here, that a distinction is made between an excess of punishment in degree and kind. The reasoning upon which the distinction is based in the foregoing cases is not quite satisfactory. In either case the court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person. In either case its authority is limited by the provisions of the law. In either case the excess is the result of error of judgment. A more satisfactory reason would be that the excess in either case was beyond the jurisdiction of the court. In the former, as a part of the judgment was within the jurisdiction of the court, the judgment is only invalid as to the excess, and when the valid part is extinguished it necessarily extinguished all. In the latter no part of the judgment is within the jurisdiction of the court, and therefore the whole judgment is void. “No court can give a judgment valid for any purpose not authorized by law. A prisoner condemned for grand larceny, for which the statutory punishment is imprisonment in the state prison for a term not exceeding five years, and who is sentenced for ten years, is not, after the expiration of the first five years, held by due process of law, or the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction. No court is or can be competent to pronounce a sentence and give judgment in open and palpable violation of a positive statute, and a judgment thus given is simply void. If a court having jurisdiction of the person of the accused and of the offense with which he is charged may impose any sentence

In re Graham, In re McDonald, People v. Kelley, 97 N. Y. 212.

64 Wis. 450. See ch. 14, sec. 323.

<br>

### --- Page 0112 ---

§ 30.) EXCESS. 27

other than the legal statutory judgment, and deny the aggrieved party all relief except upon writ of error, it is but a judicial suspension of the writ of habeas corpus. That writ is alike a protection against encroachments upon the liberty of the citizen by the unauthorized acts of courts and judges as against any mere arbitrary arrest. "It matters not what the general powers and jurisdiction of a court may be, if it act without authority in the particular case its judgments and orders are mere nullities; not voidable, but simply void, protecting no one acting under them, and constituting no hindrance to the prosecution of any right."

Sec. 30. Jurisdiction of the person.—The question of jurisdiction of the person is very fully treated under the head of “Judgments,” in connection with the question of the impeachment of judgments on the ground of lack of jurisdiction. In personal actions, generally, in order to bind the party, there must be personal service of process where there is not voluntary appearance. In fact, it is held that such only, in these actions, is due process of law. In actions in rem, and those termed quasi in rem, jurisdiction may be obtained of the person so as to give jurisdiction of the res actually or constructively seized, and thus bind the party by the judgment of the court in respect to the res by a substituted service. The effect of such a service in such proceedings is also fully discussed in a subsequent chapter relating to judgments in rem. The purpose here is not to treat the question of service of process with reference to the effect of a valid service of a valid process, in concluding a party by a judgment in personam, but rather to discuss the requisites of process in order to be valid, and the character of the service that will meet the requirements of the law. The purpose is to discuss also the question of jurisdiction of the subject-matter as well as of the person, obtained by consent, either express or implied.

1 People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, Ex parte Milligan, 4 Wall. 2-131; Ex parte Wilson, 114 U. S. 417; In re Elliott v. Peirsol, 1 Pet. (U. S.) 328; Ex parte Kearney, 55 Cal. 212; Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714.

Ex parte Lange, 18 Wall 163; Ex

<br>

### --- Page 0113 ---

31-33.

23 JURISDICTION. [§ 7a

Sec. 31. Requisites of service of process.— With respect to personal service of process, the manner of service is usually regulated by statute, so that it need only be stated that the directions of the statute should be followed. Usually a return of the time, place and manner of service is required, but this is not jurisdictional. It is the fact of service, in superior courts, that gives jurisdiction. In inferior courts, such as courts of justices of the peace, the return confers jurisdiction, and not the fact of proper service. A return of a sheriff in an action in a superior court can therefore, before or after judgment, be amended so as to conform to the facts.¹

Sec. 32. Substituted service.—It is more with respect to substituted service of process that we have to deal. Statutes usually provide, in actions, for two methods of substituted service. One where the defendant cannot be found in the county, providing that the process may be served by leaving a copy of the same at his last and usual place of abode, with some member of the family, of suitable age and discretion, who shall be informed of its contents; the other by presenting to the court the existence of certain facts and conditions, and thereupon obtaining an order for the publication of the process or of notice. With respect to the former the return should show strict compliance with the requirements of the law as to the manner of service, and, if it fails, the judgment based thereon in case of default can be collaterally attacked for want of jurisdiction, though, as before stated, the return may be amended so as to conform to the facts.² Hence, the certificate or affidavit of service must contain an averment that the defendant could not be found, for the reason that upon this condition of things the substituted service rests. Also, it must show that the copy was left with some person, a member of the family, of suitable age and discretion.

Sec. 33. Subject further discussed — Process left with member of family.— There is some controversy as to whether

¹Pico v. Sunol, 6 Cal. 294; Lowe v. Alexander, 15 Cal. 297; Drake v. Du- rup v. Shepard, 23 Wis. 5 Cal. 455; Rowley v. How- Knox v. Miller, 18 Wis. 397; North- Cal. 402; Wilcox v. Mondy, 39 Cal.  
²Same cases, 4Rape et al. v. Ucaton, 9 Wis. 323

<br>

### --- Page 0114 ---

§ 34.] EXCESS. 29

such service is sufficient to confer jurisdiction upon the court to render a personal judgment. I do not consider that the question is definitely settled. It has been quite generally held that the laws of a state are binding upon citizens thereof, and that the state might provide for any method of service of process reasonably calculated to give notice, and that such a service would conclude the citizen who had subjected himself to the sovereignty of the state, but that as to non-residents, not actually within the state, its laws could have no extraterritorial force, so as to bind them. Hence we find that in most states it has been held, as before stated, that such service, if properly made, is sufficient to confer jurisdiction upon the court, while in some other jurisdictions the contrary appears to have been held. In the supreme court of the United States (Pennoyer v. Neff) it was decided that the "due process of law" referred to in the fourteenth amendment of the constitution required personal service of process in cases where it was sought to affect personal rights, and that a judgment in personam, not based upon such a service, was void for want of jurisdiction over the person of the defendant, in cases where such service had not been waived. It is true the case before the court was that of a judgment against a non-resident, where service had been made or attempted by publication, yet the language was general, equally applicable to a resident and non-resident, while the reason for the rule was also equally applicable. There can be nothing in the sovereignty of the state by which it can deprive its own citizens of the right or benefit of due process of law, and due process of law must be the same in the case of a citizen and one not a citizen of the state. This rule is expressed more fully in a subsequent chapter. This declaration of the law had the effect to induce the Massachusetts court to change its doctrine. What effect it has had directly upon other courts the writer has not ascertained.

Sec. 34. Service by publication.— Service of process by publication, as it is commonly designated, is ordinarily set on foot

1 Hunt v. Hunt, 72 N.Y. 217; Knox v. Miller, 18 Wis. 397; Harriman v. Roberts, 52 Md. 64; Fullerton v. Horton, 11 Vt. 425; Burns v. Belknapp, 22 Vt. 419.

2 Swift v. Meyers, 13 Saw. 583; Barrett v. Oppenheimer, 12 Heisk. (Tenn.) 298.

<br>

### --- Page 0115 ---

30 JURISDICTION. [§ 35.

by an affidavit, the requisites of which are prescribed by statute, on which is predicated an order of the court or a judge thereof, or one possessing the powers of a judge at chambers. The summons and complaint are to be filed and copies mailed, etc. Proof of the publication and mailing is to be made. In many of the states when the judicial officer issues his order of publication, it is held that he has passed upon the sufficiency of the affidavit, and it is not thereafter open to collateral attack. Where such rule prevails, in cases of default, the only papers which are subject to inspection to ascertain the question of jurisdiction of the person are the summons, the affidavit of the printer, and those relating to the deposit of the summons and complaint in the post-office, the complaint and the judgment, the affidavit for some purposes, and the order. In case of personal service outside of the state, proof of service in lieu of the affidavits of printer and deposit in the post-office. The California court hold, however, that under the statute of that state, the affidavit and order form no part of the judgment roll.

Sec. 35. The affidavit — Requisites of. — Where the affidavit prescribed by the statute as the basis of an order for publication of a summons becomes a part of the record or judgment roll, in the absence of a statute making an order issued thereon conclusive of the question of its sufficiency, the general rule is that the provisions of the statute must be strictly pursued, and that every condition therein required to be made to appear in the affidavit must appear in order that jurisdiction may be acquired by such constructive service. This rule has its qualifications, however, and one is that, when the affidavit presents some evidence tending to prove each jurisdictional fact, but of a character clearly too inclusive to justify an order of publication, such order is erroneous, and upon appeal a judgment that may be rendered where there has been no appearance will be reversed. Such judgment, however, will not be void. A question is then presented to the court or judge calling for his judgment.

1 Hahn v. Kelley, 34 Cal. 391. 3 Braley v. Seaman, 30 Cal. 611;
2 Hahn v. Kelley, 34 Cal. 391. See Forbes v. Hyde et al., 31 Cal. 342;
Belcher v. Chambers, 53 Cal. 635, Bradley v. Jamison, 46 Iowa, 63,
where the doctrine of this case as to presumptions is overruled.

<br>

### --- Page 0116 ---

§§ 36, 37.] EXCESS. 31

ment and determination, and if his mind is too easily satisfied and he makes a wrong decision, this is mere error. If there is a total want of evidence upon which to base the order, the judgment is void. In some of the states it is a sufficient compliance with the provisions of the statute to state conclusions in the language of the statute, but in most of the states the affidavit must contain a statement of the facts and circumstances which tend to prove the ultimate fact.

Sec. 36. The affidavit, requisites of, continued.—The affidavit must state the statutory grounds for making the order, and if it fail in any of these respects the order will be void and jurisdiction will not be acquired. In such a case, if the order recites that it was made upon the affidavit and complaint, referring to them, and that it appears to the satisfaction of the court therefrom that the plaintiff has a cause of action, etc., the affidavit may be referred to; and if it appear therefrom that it omits to state the statutory grounds required, there can be no presumption that the court had before it other sufficient proof; and the statute which makes such an order presumptive proof of the existence of all the facts required to exist to authorize the same to be made and conclusive in all collateral actions and proceedings can have no application. Hence, where an affidavit failed to show that the defendant had property in the state, or the cause of action arose therein, it was held the court did not acquire jurisdiction. It was said, if the order merely recited that it appeared to the satisfaction of the judge that the defendant had property in the state, there being nothing in the record to show what proof was made, the presumption would be that it was sufficient to authorize the order of publication.

Sec. 37. Affidavit must disclose the existence of a cause of action.—It must be kept in mind that the statutes in the several states are not uniform with respect to the statements that must appear in the affidavit and in other respects. As the

1 Forbes v. Hyde, 31 Cal. 342; Edson v. Richardson, 26 Cal. 149; Ples v. Fairchild, 8 Cos. 41; Matter of Faulkner v. Smith, 5 Minn. 367; Harrington v. Loomis, 10° Minn. 366;

2 Forbes v. Hyde, 31 Cal. 341; In re Manning v. Heady, 64 Wis. 630. Matter of Faulkner, 4 Hill, 598; Rick-

3 Manning v. Heady, 64 Wis. 630.

<br>

### --- Page 0117 ---

32 JURISDICTION. §§ 38, 39.

The whole proceeding is statutory; what may be compliance with the requirements of the law in one jurisdiction would not be so held in another. There are, however, certain general features which attend the subject that are practically the same in all jurisdictions. Those we may consider. In most of the states it is required that it shall appear by the affidavit that a cause of action exists against the defendant, and this is a jurisdictional fact.1 And it is not sufficient to state that the plaintiff has a good cause of action against the defendant, or that he is a necessary party, but the facts should be stated which show the existence of a good cause of action.2 Such, prior to the enactment of section 2639 of the Revised Statutes, was the rule in Wisconsin. This was made unnecessary by the change in the law thus made, which provides that the application for the order shall be based upon a complaint, etc. It is sufficient, therefore, that the cause of action is stated in the complaint.

Sec. 38. Affidavit must disclose the fact that defendant is a non-resident, has property within the state, or cause of action arose therein.—Statutes usually provide for such constructive method of service where the defendant is a non-resident of the state, or his residence is unknown, and that he has property within the state, or the cause of action arose therein. This must appear by the affidavit and is jurisdictional.

Sec. 39. Affidavit must disclose exercise of diligence in attempting service.—It is generally required that it must appear by the affidavit that the plaintiff is unable, with due diligence, to make service of the summons upon the defendant within the state. This is also jurisdictional. In some of the states it is held that the mere statement in the language of the statute that after due diligence the defendant cannot be found in the state is a sufficient compliance with the requirements of the law in this respect, and that the affidavit need not disclose what acts

1 Forbes v. Hyde, 31 Cal. 342; Ton v. Carleton, 85 N. Y. 313; Hafern v. Atkins, 9 Neb. 191; Holmes v. Davis, 10 Wis. 501; Witt v. Meyer, 15 Neb. 615; Manning v. Heady, 64 Wis. 595.

2 Ricketson v. Richardson, 26 Cal. 149; Fisk v. Anderson, 33 Barb. 71; Slocum v. Slocum, 17 Wis. 155; Carleton v. Carleton, 85 N. Y. 313.

3 Cummings v. Tabor, 61 Wis. 185; Sueterlee v. Sir, 25 Wis. 357.

4 People v. Huber, 20 Cal. 81; Carle-

<br>

### --- Page 0118 ---

constituting diligence were taken; while in other states the mere statement of such conclusion is insufficient, but the acts constituting due diligence should be stated. It was stated by the New York court: “It is not non-residence alone which constitutes the ground for making the order, and it therefore cannot be urged that proof of this fact furnishes sufficient reason for a conclusion of the judicial mind that due diligence had been employed to find the defendant within the state. It is manifest that the provision of the code cited contemplated that the defendant might, as in case of his doing business within the state, be found therein by an effort for that purpose, although his residence was in an adjacent or in another state. Cases may arise where the proof of residence in a distant state at the very time, and of an absolute location there, would be so strong as to render it entirely apparent that no act of diligence would be of any avail.”

Sec. 40. Departure from state to avoid service of process or concealment therein for like purpose.—Another ground which authorizes substituted service by publication, prevailing in some of the states, is “when the defendant, being a resident of the state, has departed therefrom with intent to defraud his creditors, or avoid the service of a summons, or keeps himself concealed therein with like intent.” It is quite generally recognized that it is sufficient if the allegation is in the language of the statute.

Sec. 41. Statement of facts or conclusions.—There can be no reason suggested why it is not as important that the facts should be stated in respect to this requirement as with respect to any other requirement of the law. In fact there are some reasons why it is more important. It seems that the intent with which the defendant departed constitutes the foundation of the proceedings; and to establish that intent it was stated by a court that it must appear that the defendant had property.

¹ Sueterlee v. Sir, 25 Wis. 356;  
² Carleton v. Carleton, 85 N.Y. 313.  
³ Young v. Schenck, 22 Wis. 556, 4 Storm v. Adams, 56 Wis. 137;  
⁴ Ricketson v. Richardson, 26 Cal. 400;  
⁵ Frisk et al. v. Reigelman, 75 Wis, 149;  
⁶ Gere v. Gunlach, 57 Barb. 18;  
⁷ Belmont v. Cornen et al, 82 N.Y. 256.  
⁸ Hafern v. Davis, 10 Wis. 501.

<br>

### --- Page 0119 ---

JURISDICTION, [§ 42.

subject to seizure, of which he has made or is about to make a disposition which would deprive his creditors of their rights; and to maintain the intent to avoid service of summons, it must appear that a summons has been issued or that the defendant believed that one was about to be issued. This provision has no application to one not a resident of the state, or who is absent therefrom temporarily, as on a visit or on business. The order when made, ordinarily, as has been stated, becomes final and conclusive between the parties in the state, to the extent of sustaining jurisdiction. It is granted ex parte, and it requires no stretch of imagination to foresee that if the facts are not stated in the affidavit such orders may be improvidently granted and injustice thereby result. It is true the intent with which an act is done is not always easy of proof, yet for this reason there should be something more than the mere conclusion of the party to satisfy the officer that such intent was present. There should be facts and circumstances in the line indicated above, from which the intent ought to be inferred. Whether the statement required to be made and contained in the affidavit should be stated positively, or may be stated on information and belief, there is some difference of opinion among the courts. Where the statements are thus made upon information and belief, and the officer has passed his judgment upon the sufficiency of the proof, it is generally held, in those states especially where such order is made conclusive as against collateral attack, that the order is valid.

Sec. 42. Statement of facts positively or upon information and belief.— Where such is not the law, then it seems, in many jurisdictions, that the facts must be averred positively and not upon information or belief; yet there are exceptions to the rule. Thus, in New York it is held that, when the place

1 Towsley v. McDonald, 32 Barb. 606. 6 Forbes v. Hyde, 31 Cal. 342; Hahn v. Kelley, 34 Cal. 391; Fontaine v. Houston, 58 Ind. 316; Harrison v. Beard, 30 Kan. 523; Hafern v. Davis, 10 Wis. 501; Evertston v. Thomas, 5 How. (N. Y.) 45. See Handley v. Quick, 47 How. 223; Wagoner v. Fogleman, 53 Ark. 181, 13 S. W. Rep. 729. 

2 Towsley v. McDonald, 32 Barb. 606. 3 Collins v. Campbell, 9 How. (N. Y.) 519. 4 Jones v. Derby, 1 Abb. 458. 5 Storm v. Adams, 56 Wis. 137; Frisk et al. v. Reigelman, 75 Wis. 199.

<br>

### --- Page 0120 ---

§§ 43, 44.] EXCESS. 35

of a person’s residence is unknown, information received from others is competent for the purpose of showing such residence, and allegations of this character may be made on information and belief. So, also, a sheriff’s certificate (if referred to in the affidavit), to the effect that the defendant cannot be found, is a basis upon which an allegation in the affidavit may properly be founded, as to the belief that the defendant has departed from the state. However, in some states it is held that the affidavit is sufficient if made on information and belief.

Sec. 43. Complaint must be verified and filed.— One of the essential requisites is that the complaint in the action must be duly verified. This requirement may not be exacted in all the states, but where it is a condition the law must be complied with in this respect. This is especially the rule where the application for the order is based on the complaint and the affidavit, and the affidavit need not state the facts in reference to the cause of action. And where a statute so requires the complaint must be filed before the order is made; and this provision is not complied with where the attorney simply procures the complaint, affidavit and order to be marked by and is withdrawn from the custody of the clerk. The filing of the affidavit is a condition precedent to an authorized publication, and if publication is made without such filing no jurisdiction is acquired. A judgment rendered is void, though the affidavit be afterwards filed.

Sec. 44. Rule in federal courts as to strict compliance with statutory requirements.— The federal courts declare the rule to be, independent of any statute, “that where a court of general jurisdiction is authorized in a proceeding, either statutory, or at law, or in equity, to bring in, by publication or other substituted service, non-resident defendants interested in

Howe Machine Co. v. Pettibone, 4 GAN. Y. 68. Manning v. Heady, 64 Wis. 630; Oel-
bermann v. Ide, 93 Wis. 669.
Colton v. Rupert, 60 Mich. 318; Easterbrook v. Easterbrook, 64 Barb. 421.
Pettiford v. Zoellner, 45 Mich. 353, See Storm v. Adams, 56 Wis. 107; Frisk v. Witt v. Meyer, 69 Wis. 595.
Voelz et ux. v. Voelz, 80 Wis. 501;
v. Reigelman, 75 Wis. 499.
Barber v. Morris, 37 Minn. 194

<br>

### --- Page 0121 ---

36 JURISDICTION, [§ 44.

or having a lien upon property lying within its territorial jurisdiction, but is not required to place the proof of service upon the record, and the court orders such substituted service, it will be presumed in favor of the jurisdiction that service was made as ordered, although no evidence thereof appears of record, and the judgment of the court, so far as it affects property, will be valid. A rule in most respects similar prevails in Iowa. There, if it appears that there was notice, though it be defective, or the service thereof was improper, neither being in strict compliance with the directions of the statute, and the court has determined in favor of the sufficiency of such notice and service, which thus appears upon the record, even though such determination was erroneous, the judgment of the court will not be held void in a collateral proceeding. A direct finding of the fact is not necessary. If it appear that the court, in rendering the judgment, necessarily determined the sufficiency of the service, this is enough. The record, however, must show that there was an affidavit before the court. A mere recital to the effect that an order of publication was made will not raise the presumption of a proper affidavit, though if it appear by the record that the court decided upon the question of its jurisdiction, or that the question was before it, this will be sufficient to raise such a presumption.

Referring again to the doctrine of the federal supreme court, and to avoid any misapprehension as to the rule, it must be said that, with respect to the question of substituted service, the rule there is that the courts act under a special power, and that in the exercise of that power, though courts of general jurisdiction, the presumption that that which the statute requires was properly done or made to appear does not prevail. On the contrary, the facts essential to the exercise of the special jurisdiction must appear in such cases upon record. It remains for the party relying upon the jurisdiction thus obtained to show that by the means provided by statute such

1 Applegate v. Lexington, etc. Min. Co., 117 U.S. 255.

2 Shawhan v. Loffer, 24 Iowa, 217; Fanning v. Krapfl, 68 Iowa, 244; Lee v. Wetmore, 58 Iowa, 170.

3 Fanning v. Krapfl, 68 Iowa, 244.

4 Bradley v. Jamison, 46 Iowa, 68.

The Iowa code, section 3669, provided “that proceedings of courts of limited or inferior jurisdiction shall, like those of courts of superior jurisdiction, have the presumption of being regular.”

<br>

### --- Page 0122 ---

§ 45.] EXCESS, 3y

jurisdiction was obtained. Therefore the order, which is the sole authority for the publication, and which by statute must prescribe the period and designate the paper in which the publication is to be made, should appear in the record, with proof of compliance with its directions, unless its absence is supplied by proper averment, and where the record shows no such order the service affirmatively appears to be invalid! There is some reason for assuming that the doctrine last above stated is in conflict with that stated in Ulpplegate v. Lexington, etc. Min. Co, supra. The court, however, in the latter case state there is no conflict, and make the following distinction: that the judgment set up on one side and attacked on the other, in Galpin v. Page, was rendered on service by publication. The law permitted service to be made by publication only where certain facts were made to appear to the satisfaction of the court, and the court by a precedent order, which must necessarily appear of record, authorized service to be made by publication. But the record showed no such order, and the publication was therefore the unauthorized act of the party, and appeared affirmatively to be invalid and ineffectual.

Sec. 45. Service outside of the state.— In some of the states the provision is, that at the plaintiff's option a copy of the summons and a copy of the complaint be delivered to the defendant personally without the state, which, when done, shall have the same effect as a completed publication and mailing. It is only when the plaintiff desires to exercise this privilege that the order is required to provide for its exercise. There must, however, in all cases be a valid order of publication. Service without such an order out of the state is a nullity and will not confer jurisdiction, though there be written admission of service. It has been held, however, that a written stipulation which acknowledges service of notice and pendency of the action, and provides for trying the cause at the next term of court, is a submission to the jurisdiction of the court. Where personal service is made, the necessity of publishing and mailing

MGalpin v. Page, 18 Wall. 350; Weatherbee v. Weatherbee, 20 Wis.
2 Weil v. Martin, 24 Hun, 645; O'Neil v. Bender, 30 Hun, 204; Jenkins v. Fahey, 73 N.Y. 355.
4 Weatherbee v. Weatherbee, 20 Wis. 490.
3 Manning v. Heady, 64 Wis. 630; Skeler v. Keeler, 24 Wis. 522

<br>

### --- Page 0123 ---

388 JURISDICTION. [§ 46.

the summons is superseded. There is a difference of opinion as to when the service is complete under an order permitting personal service without the state. The court of appeals of New York declare that such service is not complete until the expiration of at least six weeks from the time of such service. The Wisconsin supreme court, however, laying stress upon the language of the statute of that state, “that such delivery shall have the same effect as a completed publication and mailing,” hold that upon such delivery the service is complete, and that the defendant has but twenty days thereafter in which to plead.

Sec. 46, Construction of statute as to time when service is complete.— The construction that shall be placed upon the language of the statute, which requires publication once a week for six successive weeks, is not the same in the courts of New York and Wisconsin. In the former it is argued that the number of weeks is specified and not the number of times. That the publication must be made for a specified period of time, and when the statute provides for six weeks it is obvious that this period will not elapse prior to its expiration. The publication evidently means more than printing the notice. Its object is to give notice by means of the newspapers, and it cannot be claimed that such notice is given for six weeks before that time expires; and the court reached the conclusion that the law intended a full six-weeks publication and not six times in six different weeks. In that state the code declares (section 441) that the time shall be complete upon the day of the last publication, and another section (787) that the period of publication must be computed so as to include the day which completes the full period of publication. The argument of the Wisconsin court is that the requirement of the statute is satisfied where there are six publications on the first day of six successive weeks, but that the time to answer will not begin to run until the expiration of the last week. The example was, there was a publication commencing on October 8th, the last being on November 9th, and it was held that the requirements of the

1Pier v. Amory, 40 Wis. 571.  
2Brooklyn Trust Co. v. Bulmer, 49 N. Y. 84; Market Nat. Bank v. Pacific Nat. Bank, 89 N. Y. 397.  
3Wilmot v. Smith, 86 Wis. 299.  
4Market Nat. Bank v. Pacific Nat. Bank, 89 N. Y. 397.

S§ 47, 48.] EXCESS. 39  
statute were met, and that the service was complete at the end of the sixth week, i.e., November 16th. The statute of that state provided that “the first publication must be made within three months from date of such order, and service shall be deemed complete at the expiration of the full period prescribed by the order after the date of the first publication,” etc. In Minnesota and California the construction is that there must be six publications at least seven days apart, and that the proof must show that the publications were made once in each week.  

Src. 47. Order — Directions of.— The order must direct that service be made by mail, and specify the post office in which the papers are to be deposited, and require them to be mailed on or before the first day of publication, and designate the post office to which they are to be addressed. The copy must be addressed to the place designated in the order, in an envelope properly sealed, not bearing a request to return within a period less than the defendant is entitled to have in which to serve his answer or make his appearance in any other way.  

Src. 48. Voluntary appearance — Effect of.— Jurisdiction may be obtained of the person by the voluntary appearance of the party, though there may be wanting process upon him. Jurisdiction of the subject-matter cannot be thus conferred, as we discuss later. It is not every appearance, however, on the part of a party to the action that will thus confer jurisdiction. An appearance may be either special or general. It has been stated that it was general when the defendant asks the court to act on something besides the jurisdictional question raised, whether because of irregularity or substantial error. That it was special only when it is asked to do that which is consistent with the lack of jurisdiction. The effect of an appear-

§2 Wis. 141. (N.Y.) 248,  
2 Godfrey v. Valentine, 389 Minn.  
3 Smith v. Wells, 69 N.Y. 600, 336, 40 N. W. Rep. 163; Hernandez v.

His Creditors, 57 C 3; Gaffney v. Bigelow, 48 How.

New-

<br>

### --- Page 0125 ---

40 JURISDICTION. [§§ 49, 50.]

ance upon the jurisdiction of the court is discussed in the following sections, in connection with the doctrine of waiver and consent.

Sec. 49. Consent as affecting the question of jurisdiction.  
The effect upon jurisdiction of consent, express or implied, and of waiver of jurisdictional defects in the manner of acquiring jurisdiction and those that occur in the subsequent proceedings, present questions that are of great importance, and which are not entirely free from difficulty. The two are so closely connected that a more concise discussion of the subject can best be attained by treating them together. It is a general rule that consent cannot confer upon a court jurisdiction of the subject-matter. The authority of the court in judicial proceedings is conferred by law, and it may be such as is inherent in the court by its very constitution, or conferred by general laws enacted by the legislature. Neither an appearance nor pleading to the merits and going to trial will have the effect of conferring jurisdiction. The cases are so numerous and the doctrine so universal that nothing can be gained by special reference to many individual cases. Reference to a few, however, will be given, which illustrate the character and extent of the rule.

Sec. 50. Rule illustrated — Appeals. — Where an appellate court has no jurisdiction to review, by appeal or otherwise, the judgment of an inferior court, jurisdiction cannot be conferred on such court by the consent of the parties. This rule was applied in Indiana to the extent of excluding the supreme court of that state from deciding a matter in a case that was not passed upon by the lower court, but the case cited may be considered as extreme. The rule only goes to the extent that such court could not decide a matter not passed upon by the lower court and not involved in the issue before the court. Parties cannot consent that a verdict shall be found by a jury,

1 Detroit Safe Co. v. Kelley, 78 Wis.; Kelsey v. Forsyth, 21 How. (U.S.) 134; Stein v. Norton, 45 Wis. 412; Merrill v. Petty, 16 Wall.; Bonds v. Hickman, 29 Cal. 461; Coffin v. Mathie, 40 Wis. 120; Low v. Rice, 3 Caines, 129; Fleischman v. Walker, 91 Ill. 318; People v. Graice, 50 Cal. 447; Barnard v. Con., 25 Ind. 201; Smith v. Meyers, 109 Ind. 1; Davis v. Davis, 86 Ind. 160; Doctor v. Hartman, 74 Ind. 221.

<br>

### --- Page 0126 ---

§ 51, 52. EXCESS, 41

and that the entry of judgment shall be in favor of such party as the appellate court shall decide has the right of the controversy.

Sec. 51. Inferior courts without jurisdiction — Appellate court cannot gain. — The general rule is that, if the lower court had not jurisdiction of the subject-matter, the appellate court cannot gain it by appeal. Hence, where jurisdiction of a justice’s court was by law, in an action involving the investigation of an account, limited to an amount not exceeding $500, the circuit court, upon appeal, could not acquire jurisdiction of a cause involving a larger amount, even upon stipulation of the parties, though it had original jurisdiction of the subject-matter. Such a judgment is absolutely void. The language used in a case in the Iowa court is contrary to this rule, but there the action was upon the judgment of a sister state rendered in a county court upon an appeal from a judgment rendered before a justice of the peace, the defendant not appearing in such court, but appearing in the county court and contesting the merits. The want of jurisdiction urged was that the record did not show a formal judgment entered by the justice, and the contention was that the county court did not acquire jurisdiction by appeal. To the same effect upon different facts is a decision in Illinois, and also one by a divided court in Pennsylvania. — So in Wisconsin, where the statute provides that, in appeals from judgments rendered by a justice for a sum less than $15, the appeal shall be heard on the original papers and return of the justice, the circuit court cannot acquire jurisdiction by stipulation of the parties to try the case de novo.

Sec. 52. Statutory conditions — Compliance with upon appeal. — The jurisdiction of appellate courts as well as of courts generally in appeals is conferred ordinarily by statute and made

1 Ames v. Bolander, 1 Minn. 365; Feillett v. Engler, 8 Cal. 77. Guin et al. v. Rogers, 4 Gilm. (Ill.) 131; Danforth et al. v. Thompson, 34 Towa, 243.

2 Klaise v. State, 27 Wis. 462; Stringham v. Winnebago Co., 24 Wis. 504; Montgomery v. Heilman, 96 Pa. St. 44.

3 Hinckel v. Wheeler & Wilson Mfg. Co., 51 Wis. 363; Osgood v. Thurston, 23 Pick. 110; Randolph Co. v. Ralls, 18 Ill. 226; Bullard v. Kuhl, 54 Wis. 544.

<br>

### --- Page 0127 ---

42 JURISDICTION. — [§ 53. dependent upon certain prescribed acts or conditions. If these are wanting, the court obtains no jurisdiction, although there may have been a general appearance on the part of the appellee or appellant. Hence, it has been held that a failure to file an appeal bond is fatal to the jurisdiction of the court. Some courts have held otherwise, and that appeal bonds given as security for costs are not jurisdictional facts to be found by the court, but rather relate to the means of bringing a case within a pre-existing jurisdiction, and that an appearance waives this defect; while others hold that, under the statute permitting amendments to proceedings, a defective bond may be amended and an omission to file a proper bond may be supplied. So it was held that where, upon the organization of a court of appeals, the clerk of the county court was to transmit to such court all papers in civil cases of appeals, and thereafter they were to be determined by such former court, that until such transfer was made such court obtained no jurisdiction.

Sec. 53. Jurisdiction on appeal — Time and manner prescribed. — The appeal must be taken and perfected within the time and in the manner prescribed by the statute, and such time cannot be enlarged by waiver or consent. The Wisconsin court criticizes a case decided in the New York court of appeals (Saller v. Butler, 27 N.Y. 638), and some others decided in lower courts, apparently holding that the question of time might be waived, and most certainly its reasoning is sound. It states that the New York court had held that the court possesses no power directly to enlarge the time for appeal; that it appears to follow that if a court cannot restore its own jurisdiction, lost by statutory limit, the parties cannot restore it by consent, for it is not jurisdiction of the person but of the proceeding.

1. Santorn v. Ballard, 133 Mass. 464; Latham v. Edgerton, 9 Cow. 227; Clark v. Conn, 1 Munf. (Va.) 160; Troback v. Caro, 60 Cal. 301; Spaulding et al. v. Mil, LDS. & W. RR. Co, 57 Wis. 304.

2. Santorn v. Ballard, 133 Mass. 464; Latham v. Edgerton, 9 Cow.

3. Thompson v. Lea, 28 Ala. 454.

4. Gilbank v. Stephenson, 30 Wis. 155; Helden v. Helden, 9 Wis. 508; Falk v. Goldberg, 45 Wis. 94. See, however, Eaton v. Supervisors, 42 Wis. 317.

5. Troback v. Caro, 60 Cal. 301.

6. Herrick et al. v. Racine Warehouse & Dock Co., 43 Wis. 93; Spaulding v. Mil, LS. & W. R. Co., 57 Wis. oud.

7. Humphrey v. Chamberlain, 11 N.Y. 274; Wait v. Van Allen, 22 N.Y. 319.

8. Herrick et al. v. Racine Warehouse & Dock Co., 43 Wis. 93.

<br>

### --- Page 0128 ---

8§ 54, 55.) EXCESS. 43

It is necessary to the jurisdiction of the court of an appeal, that the record in the appellate court should show notice of appeal served both upon the respondent and the clerk of the court below, and a notice not signed is insufficient. The parties cannot confer jurisdiction by a stipulation waiving such proceeding. But it seems the parties may stipulate that such notice was filed, and upon this evidence it becomes a verity in the case that it was filed. In case of an appeal from a judgment rendered in justice’s court, it is essential to the acquiring of jurisdiction by the appellate court that the affidavit required in such cases, in effect that the appeal is made in good faith, that the jurat should be signed by the officer, and if his signature is wanting, though it is signed by the party, no jurisdiction will be acquired.

Sec. 54. Jurisdiction upon agreed statement of facts.— In many states it is provided by statute that parties to a question of difference which might be the subject of a civil action may agree upon a case containing the facts upon which the controversy depends, and present a submission of the same to any court which would have jurisdiction if an action had been brought. In such cases it must appear by affidavit usually, but in some manner, that the controversy is real and the proceeding in good faith to determine the rights of the parties, and this is essential to jurisdiction. Parties will not be allowed to agree upon facts not in the case, nor make up experimental cases on which to obtain the opinion of the court. Parties cannot by agreement divest courts of law or equity of their proper jurisdiction. Hence it was held that a court of equity, in the absence of statute, may interfere to correct fraud or mistake appearing on the face of the record in the matter of an award, though the parties may have stipulated not to appeal.

Sec. 55. Appearance in proceedings in rem — Effect of.— The effect of appearance upon the jurisdiction of the person.

1 Eaton v. Supervisors, 42 Wis. 317; 525; Manchester v. Dodge, 57 Ind. 
2 Bonds v. Hickman, 29 Cal. 461. 
3 Knappe v. Seyler, 87 Wis. 165. 
4 Plainfield v. Plainfield, 67 Wis. Ga. 511; Blair v. State Bank, 8 Ma. 313; Brennington v. Lowe, 1 Ind. 21; Nicholson v. Stephens, 47 Ind. 185, 
5 Muldrow v. Norris, 2 Cal. 74.

<br>

### --- Page 0129 ---

as stated is, ordinarily, to give it; but the effect of such an appearance in proceedings partaking in some respects of the nature of proceedings in rem is not ordinarily sufficient to confer jurisdiction over the proceedings or the res. Hence, where the contention was that an affidavit for garnishment in a proceeding in a justice’s court was lacking in substantial requirements, the question being raised after several trials upon appeal, it was in said respect to the effect of an appearance upon the jurisdiction of the court, that it could go no further than to cure defects in acquiring jurisdiction over the person. It cannot operate to give jurisdiction of a proceeding which the justice could not take without such appearance and submission. His jurisdiction of the subject-matter must come by statute, and if the statute makes his jurisdiction dependent on preliminaries or conditions precedent, the justice can take jurisdiction only by force of the statutory preliminaries or conditions precedent. This is well exemplified by the writ of attachment as mesne process. If the affidavit on which the attachment issues be materially defective, the defendant is entitled to have the action commenced by it dismissed in toto. But if the defendant appear and submit without objection, he cures the defect of process as a personal summons, but not as an attachment of property. The justice takes jurisdiction to render personal judgment against him, but not against the property attached. The statute in respect to actions by attachment and garnishment was, subsequent to the foregoing decision, amended so as to relieve parties from the effect of the rule therein declared, in providing that the affidavits therein might be amended at any time before trial by substituting a new affidavit. The same rule and reasoning was applied by the same court in a subsequent case, where the alleged want of jurisdiction was a defect in the affidavit in replevin (that it did not state it was made in behalf of the plaintiff). The objection was first raised in the supreme court after three trials had been had upon the merits. The statute in respect to replevin had not been amended. The same rule was applied in an action to enforce a lien upon logs, and also in an action to enforce a lien for

Stein v. Norton, 45 Wis. 412; 2 Detroit Safe Co. v. Kelley, 73 Wis.

Schindler v. Smith, 18 La. Ann. 476; 134.  
Phelps v. Boughton, 27 La. Ann. 592.  
Sheridan v. Ireland, 66 Me. 138

<br>

### --- Page 0130 ---

§ 56.) EXCESS. 45

labor on granite.’ In Illinois it seems to have been held that though the defects in such affidavits are jurisdictional, yet the want of jurisdiction is waived by going to trial in the appellate court upon the merits, without moving to dismiss the action for the alleged defect.

Sec. 56. Change of venue — Affidavits defective.— Another case aptly illustrating the rule was where the affidavits for removal of a cause, commenced before one justice, to another on account of the prejudice of the justice, was defective in stating that from prejudice or other cause the party could not have a fair trial, etc., no cause being stated. The papers were sent to the nearest justice, where the parties appeared. Judgment was subsequently rendered for the plaintiff upon proof. The defendant appealed therefrom. The appellate court, on motion, dismissed the case for want of jurisdiction, holding that consent could not confer jurisdiction of the subject-matter. The action was one of tort for damages. In a subsequent case, where it was claimed that the person upon whom the garnishee summons was served was not the managing agent of the corporation, and therefore such service was void, and it appeared that the garnishee defendant, after judgment by default, moved to set the judgment aside on such ground and on the ground that the garnishee was not indebted to the defendant, it was held that such an appearance was general in character and had the effect to waive the want of service of the summons and to cure any invalidity in the judgment. Yet such voluntary appearance by the garnishee without service of process does not give jurisdiction of the defendant in the principal action, so that the judgment in the garnishee action shall be binding on him. This latter proposition finds support in other cases.

Martin v. Darling, 78 Me. 78; Wickham v. South Shore Lumber Co., 80 Wis. 23.

Evans v. Bouton, 85 Ill. 579.

Hagar v. Falk, 82 Wis. 644. See Edler v. Hasche, 67 Wis. 653; also Plano Mfg. Co. v. Rasey, 60 Wis. 642; Hebel v. Amazon Ins. Co., 33 Mich. 246; Vogel v. City of Antigo, 81 Wis. 400.
