CHAPTER 3.

WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS OR PRIVILEGES.

Sec.
Statement of proposition. 69. Trial in county where crime committed.
58. Former jeopardy. 70. Accused as a witness against himself — Waiver.
59. Rule illustrated. 71. Presence of accused — Right to be heard by self and counsel.
60. Conviction of lesser offense. 72. Subject continued — Record must show presence.
61. Jeopardy — Nolle pros. — Effect of. 73. Meeting witnesses face to face.
62. Motion in arrest of judgment — Effect of. 74. Removal of accused for unseemly conduct.
63. Right to be heard by self and counsel and other rights or privileges. 75. Nature of accusation — Right to demand.
64. Trial by jury. 76. Speedy and public trials — Rights of accused in respect to.
65. Failure to challenge alien juror. 7. Exception — Where statute gives accused an option.
66. Trial by jury, continued. 68. Trial by jury — Different rule prevails in different states.

Sec. 57. Statement of proposition.— The constitution guarantees to one accused of crime certain constitutional rights, sometimes called privileges, and the question has frequently been before the courts, to what extent, if at all, these rights or privileges might be waived by the accused.

Sec. 58. Former jeopardy.— One of the most important of these is that provision which forbids the placing of a person twice in jeopardy for the same offense. It is almost universally conceded that this right may be waived by the accused, and is effectually waived, when, after verdict of guilty, he invokes the jurisdiction of the trial court for a new trial, either upon grounds of error in the proceedings or grounds going to the merits, and also when he invokes the jurisdiction of the appellate court by writ of error to reverse the judgment below.1

1 Stewart v. State, 15 Ohio St. 155; Monroe v. State, 5 Ga. 85; Sutcliffe v. King, 46 Ind. 132; Croy v. State, 18 Ohio, 469; Commonwealth v. State, 32 Ind. 384; People v. Webb, 383 Cal. 467; People v. McKay, 18 Wis. 589; In re Keenan, 7 Johns. 212; State v. Abram, 4 Ala. 272; Jackson v. Green, 17 Mass. 515.

<br>

### --- Page 0132 ---

§ 59.] WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETO. 47

is not our purpose to discuss at any length the proposition so often involved, when does a second jeopardy occur? nor what acts or conduct amount to a consent or waiver? Only a few illustrations will be given, which are deemed sufficient to clearly impress upon the mind the extent of the application of the rule.

Sec. 59. Illustrations of the rule.—In one case, after a jury had been impaneled and sworn, a juror of his own motion arose and made known the fact that he was a member of the grand jury which had found the indictment. The counsel for the accused, in response to an inquiry by the court, stated that he objected to proceeding in the trial with the jury then impaneled, at the same time declining to waive any of the defendant’s rights. The jury was therefore discharged and another jury was impaneled and the trial proceeded, the defendant objecting thereto. It was held by the supreme court that the first jury was discharged with his consent, as that was the result of his objection. At that stage of the case he had a right to demand such a disposition of the case, either by verdict of the jury or otherwise, as would bar another prosecution for the same crime. Not having done this, the discharge of the jury under the circumstances would not operate as an acquittal nor bar a further prosecution. In another case it was held, where a verdict of voluntary manslaughter was rendered during the accused's absence in jail, which on his motion was set aside, that there could be no further prosecution. Some courts hold, unless there has been an acquittal, that there can be no jeopardy unless there has been a legal trial and a verdict of guilty. That if reversible error intervened during the trial, the accused in fact was not in jeopardy, and therefore upon such reversal the former trial or verdict creates no bar to a second trial. In many others, and by far the larger number, jeopardy has been held to attach when the jury is sworn, and, as a consequence, that if the jury thereafter separate or are discharged without rendering a verdict, except by consent, or in case of such vio-

Stewart v. State, 15 Ohio St. 155. Walker, 134; Commonwealth v. Olds, 5 Litt. 140; State v. Stone, 2 Scam. 2 Nolan v. State, 55 Ga. 521. People v. Olwell, 28 Cal. 457; People v. March, 6 Cal. 546; People v. Goodwin, 18 Johns. 187; United States v. Perez, 9 Wheat. 326; Moore v. State, 1

<br>

### --- Page 0133 ---

48 JURISDICTION, [§§ 60, 61]

lent necessity as may be considered an act of God, such separation or discharge is a bar to all subsequent proceedings.

Sec. 60. Conviction of lesser offense.—Where one accused of a felony has been convicted of a lesser offense than that charged, or lesser in degree, he cannot again be put upon his trial for the greater. Such a conviction operates as an acquittal of the more serious and greater offense. If acquitted of murder and found guilty of manslaughter on the first trial, he cannot be convicted of murder on the second. Upon indictment for murder, a conviction of murder in the second degree is an acquittal of a higher crime.

Sec. 61. Jeopardy—Nolle pros.—Effect of.—The doctrine quite generally prevails, except in those courts which hold that there is no jeopardy in a trial where error has intervened, nor unless a verdict of guilty has been reached, that, after a jury has been impaneled and witnesses sworn, the district attorney cannot arrest the progress of the trial by entering a nolle, nor can the court for any other reason than that of imperative necessity. Whether or not, after conviction and a new trial ordered on the application of the accused, the district attorney, on the ground that he is not prepared for trial, or for any reason not presented to the court, can enter a nolle, and then file a new information, or have the accused arraigned and prosecuted on a new indictment charging substantially the same offense, may not be precisely clear. Upon principle and reason it ought to be manifest that he could not. The whole theory upon which the doctrine of waiver is held to rest is that the accused has invoked a new trial upon the charge as made, and the effect is, upon the granting of the order, that the indictment stands and the trial proceeds upon that alone. It is but a successive step in the same action. A jury has been

43 Wis. 395; Hilands v. Commonwealth, 111 Pa. St. 1.

*State v. Martin, 30 Wis. 216; State v. Hill, 30 Wis. 416; State v. Shear, 51 Wis. 460; State v. Rafts, 29 Mo. 32; Bishop, Crim. Law (11th ed.), sec. 849.

1Commonwealth v. Cook, 8 Serg. & Rawle, 577; Mahala v. State, 10 Serg. 532; Ned v. State, 7 Porter, 188; People v. Webb, 38 Cal. 467; State v. Calendine, 8 Iowa, 288; United States v. Shoemaker, 2 McLean, 114; 1 Chitty, Crim. Law, 657; People v. Jones, 48 Mich. 554; State v. Parish.

3State v. Martin, 30 Wis. 216.

4State v. Belden, 33 Wis. 120.

<br>

### --- Page 0134 ---

§ 62. WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETC. 

impaneled and witnesses sworn. The accused has been in jeopardy. That jeopardy has been waived only to the extent of the application on the part of the accused. He is entitled to and asks for a fair trial upon the charge made against him. When, therefore, the district attorney arbitrarily and without cause nolle prosequi the indictment, why is not the effect the same as though he had pursued the same course upon the first trial after a jury had been impaneled and witnesses sworn? If he may prosecute upon a new indictment at all, why may he not prosecute upon an indictment which charges a greater offense? The power can only exist upon the theory that all the proceedings had upon the former trial are discharged, and the status of the state and of the accused is the same as if no proceedings had been had, which cannot be true, as the indictment has not been affected by the waiver or the order of the court. With reference to the exercise of this power of entering a nolle prosequi, the learned judge who delivered the opinion in United States v. Shoemaker, 2 McLean, 114, has to say that if this power exists in the prosecutor it is subject to no limit and might be made the means of great vexation to persons charged with crime, and that it might be used experimentally, to draw out the testimony and see if it were sufficient or might be made stronger. He concedes that the attorney may enter a nolle prosequi before the trial is entered upon, and it will be no bar; but that after plea, and jury sworn, or after the evidence is in, he cannot do it. It has been decided, however, by one court that after granting a new trial on defendant’s motion and an entry of a nolle prosequi with consent of the court, the accused may be tried on a new information charging the same offense.

Sec. 62. Motion in arrest of judgment — Effect of. — The Wisconsin courts are firm in their position that a motion in arrest of judgment is not a waiver of the constitutional right against a second jeopardy. The argument is that the order in arrest of judgment on the first trial does not set aside the verdict, and while that stands the record shows defendant's former 

See also State v. Calendine, 82 Iowa, 288, 201. State v. Rust, 31 Kan, 509.

<br>

### --- Page 0135 ---

50, JURISDICTION, [§§ 63, 64.

jeopardy. The rule is otherwise in most jurisdictions? This court is also authority for the position that where the accused is convicted upon insufficient evidence, and the conviction is reversed, he cannot be tried again for the same offense.*

Sec. 63. Right to be heard by self and counsel and other rights or privileges.—Among the rights or privileges guaranteed by constitutional provisions are that: “In all criminal prosecutions the accused shall enjoy the right to be heard by himself and counsel, to demand the nature and the cause of the accusation against him, to meet the witnesses face to face, to have compulsory process to compel the attendance of witnesses in his behalf, and, in prosecutions by indictment or information, to a speedy public trial by an impartial jury of the county or district, which shall have been previously ascertained by law.” Whether these safeguards thrown around one accused of crime can be waived by him is a vexed question, and one which has frequently engaged the attention of courts, and as to which all have not reached the same conclusion.

Sec. 64. Trial by jury.—With reference to the provision relating to trial by jury there is direct conflict of opinion. One of the best considered cases where this question was involved arose in New York. There the defendant was on trial charged with murder. He consented that the trial might proceed with eleven jurors. He was convicted, and upon writ of error the court of appeals of that state held the conviction was unlawful, and that the accused could not waive this provision of the constitution, so as to authorize the tribunal to render a valid verdict and sentence. The reasoning was that the provision of the constitution relating to trial by jury prescribed the tribunal in which and before which criminal prosecutions must be tried, and that a jury is an essential part of such tribunal and cannot therefore be dispensed with; that neither the prosecuting officer nor the defendant has authority to consent to a change, or rather that such consent cannot have the effect to change the character of the tribunal and establish one different from that which the law has provided.

1 State v. Parish, 43 Wis. 395.  
2 State v. Moon, 41 Wis. 624.  
3 21 Whart. 591, and cases cited.  
4 Cancemi v. People, 18 N. Y. 123

<br>

### --- Page 0136 ---

§ 65.] WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETC. 51

Sec. 65. Failure to challenge — Alien juror.— In another case, where the question was whether neglect to challenge a juror who was an alien and therefore incompetent was a waiver of the right of trial by a constitutional jury, it was stated by the learned judge who delivered the opinion that “a criminal prosecution, in which the people in their sovereign capacity prosecute for a crime against the laws of the whole society and seek to subject the defendant to punishment, must to us, it seems, be considered as a proceeding in invitum against the will of the defendant throughout, so far as it relates to a question of this kind, or any question as to the legal constitution of the court or jury by which he is to be tried. It would be adding materially to the generally recognized force of the obligation of contracts to hold that a defendant charged with a crime might, without trial, enter into a binding contract with the prosecuting attorney to go to the penitentiary for a certain number of years in satisfaction of the offense, and yet it would approximate such a position to hold that he might be bound by a contract providing for a trial before a court or jury unknown to the constitution or the laws, the result of which trial might be to place him in the same penitentiary. The true theory, we think, is that the people in their political or sovereign capacity assume to provide by law the proper tribunals and modes of trial for offenses without consulting the wishes of the defendant as such, and upon them, therefore, devolves the responsibility not only of enacting such laws, but of carrying them into effect, by furnishing the tribunals, the panels of jurors and other safeguards for his trial, in accordance with the constitution which secures his rights. The government, the officers of the law, bring the jurors into the box; he has no control over the matter who shall be summoned or compose the panel upon which he may exercise the right of challenge; and the prosecution must see that electors only are placed there, as the law requires.” The judge, further considering the question in the light of the danger that accompanies an opposite view, says:

“Yet it once be settled that a defendant may thus waive this constitutional right, and no one can foresee the extent of the evils which might follow; but the whole judicial history of the past must admonish us that very serious evils should be apprehended, and that every step taken in that direction would tend

<br>

### --- Page 0137 ---

52 JURISDICTION. [§ 66.

to increase the danger. One act or neglect might be recognized as a waiver in one case and another in another, until the constitutional safeguards might be substantially frittered away. The only course is to meet the danger in limine, and prevent the first step in the wrong direction. It is the duty of courts to see that the constitutional rights of a defendant in a criminal case shall not be violated, however negligent he may be in raising the objection. It is in such cases emphatically that consent should not be allowed to give jurisdiction. 

Sec. 66. Subject continued.— The reasoning thus indulged in by the learned judge is so eminently sound, in accordance with the true spirit of the constitution, that I have felt it a duty to quote at much length from the opinion. A sense of justice should compel every man to protest against that doctrine which holds a man charged with crime to a responsibility for the manner in which the courts are constituted or trials conducted, where they are not organized and do not proceed according to the law of the land, simply because from ignorance or negligence he fails to raise his voice against it. It seems the supreme court of Wisconsin were not of the same mind as the Michigan court. It was there held that the alienage of a juror was ground of challenge, but not for setting aside the verdict in a criminal case not capital, though the fact was unknown to the accused when the jury was impaneled. It is broadly stated that there is no distinction to be observed with respect to the application of the doctrine of waiver in criminal and civil cases. Yet in a later case, in an opinion given by that most learned and able judge, Ryan, C. J., it was stated: “A plea of not guilty to an information or indictment for crime, whether felony or misdemeanor, puts the accused upon the country, and he can be tried by a jury only. The rule is universal as to felonies; not quite so as to misdemeanors. But the current of authority appears to apply it in both classes of crime, and this court holds it to be safer and better alike in principle and practice. The right of trial by jury, upon information or indictment for crime, is secured by the constitution upon a principle of public policy, and cannot be waived.” 

VHill v. People, 16 Mich. 351, State v. Lockwood, 43 Wis. 403. State v. Vogel, 22 Wis. 471. See also People v. O'Neil, 48 Cal. 257;

<br>

### --- Page 0138 ---

§ 67.) WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETC. 53

Sec. 67. Subject continued — Exception — Where statute gives accused an option.— We find, however, an important exception engrafted into the doctrine stated. It appears from an examination of the cases, however, that where the exception was applied was in cases of misdemeanor and in inferior courts. The exception thus referred to is that this doctrine of waiver is subject to legislative control, and where the legislature, by enactment, confers upon one accused of crime an option to exercise whether he shall be tried by a jury or not, and a power upon the court to proceed with such a trial if the accused so elect, then there is such a waiver of the right of trial by jury as does not infringe the defendant's constitutional right. Hence, under an act defining and regulating the practice of certain courts, providing that under a plea other than that of guilty, if the defendant do not demand a trial by jury, the probate judge shall proceed to try the issue, it was held one tried and convicted for selling intoxicated liquors, where the record showed he did not demand a jury, was lawfully convicted, and that the accused in not demanding a jury waived his constitutional right in this respect. In a subsequent case in the same state the same conclusion was reached, the offense charged being that of obtaining goods under false pretenses. The court adds that expressions of opinion under the former constitution (presumably contrary) arose not from any supposed infringement of the constitutional right, but because no law then existed conferring upon the court the power to try the issue upon the plea of not guilty. So in Michigan a statute provides that a justice may proceed to the trial of one accused of a misdemeanor, without a jury, if the accused so elects, or if no jury be demanded. One accused before such a justice of assault and battery, who so elected, was held to have waived the constitutional right of trial by jury. It was, however, stated that the mere failure to demand a jury would not have such effect. It was further stated that there was nothing in Hill v. People, supra, which conflicted with this conclusion. Also in Wis-

Carpenter v. State, 4 How. (Miss.) 163; Dailey v. State, 4 Ohio St. 57.
Bell v. State, 44 Ala. 203; Allen v. State, 54 Ind. 461; Dillingham v. State, 5 Ohio St. 280;
Williams v. State, 12 Ohio St.; Bond v. State, 17 Ark. 3; Ward v. People, 30 Mich. 116;
League v. State, 36 Ind. 269.

<br>

### --- Page 0139 ---

b4 JURISDICTION. [§§ 68, 69.

consin, it was held, under a similar statute, that where one was convicted and sentenced to state’s prison for larceny from the person by a municipal court, the record showing an express waiver of the right of trial by jury, the conviction and sentence was lawful. In this case the court apparently threw doubt upon the correctness of their decision in State v. Lockwood, supra, and seems to incline to the doctrine of those cases which hold that an accused may, independent of statute, waive his constitutional right of trial by jury.

Sec. 68. Subject continued — Contrary doctrine.— Another line of cases holds that it is competent for one accused of a felony not capital to waive a part or the whole of a jury independent of any statute upon the subject. The argument seems to be in effect that this and similar provisions of the constitution are in the nature of privileges conferred upon an accused, and that he is left to his option whether to avail himself of them or not. That it may often be to his interest, as he views it, to proceed with less than the constitutional number of jurors, either in order to avoid unnecessary delay or for other cogent reasons, and he ought not to be deprived of this right, as it is personal to himself. The Iowa court, however, is firm in its opinion that this waiver can only take place with the consent of the court.

Sec. 69. Trial in county where crime committed.— With respect to the clause of the constitution stated, relating to a trial in the county where the crime was committed, it is generally held that the accused may waive a strict compliance with its terms and upon his own motion. Sufficient grounds appearing therefor, the place of trial may be changed on account of the prejudice of the people of such county, and in some states upon the mere filing of an affidavit stating in terms that he cannot have a fair trial on account of the preju-

1 In re Staff, 63 Wis. 285. See also Commonwealth v. Dailey, 12 Cush.
State v. Warden, 46 Conn. 349; State ex rel. Murphy v. Commissioners, 1 Met.
v. Mansfield, 41 Mo. 470; Brown v. (Ky.) 365; Tyra v. Commonwealth, 2
State, 16 Ind. 496. Met. (Ky.) 1.

2 State v. Kaufman, 51 Iowa, 578; State v. Kaufman, 51 Iowa, 578

<br>

### --- Page 0140 ---

§ 70. WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETC.

Dice of the judge. The state, however, cannot have such change where there is objection on the part of the accused.

Sec. 70. Accused a witness against himself—Waiver.—So the accused may waive the right conferred by that clause of the constitution which provides that he shall not be compelled to be a witness against himself. Hence, where two persons were under arrest charged with the crime of murder, and one of them, upon the examination of the other before a magistrate, was called as a witness for the state, and testified without objection, and subsequently, on his trial for the same crime, the prosecution was permitted to give evidence of his testimony on such examination, it was held that such evidence was properly admitted. In another case, upon the examination of one charged with crime before a magistrate, the accused testified in his own behalf. Upon his trial upon the charge, the court, against his objection, permitted such magistrate to testify as to what the defendant then stated under oath. It was held that such evidence was properly admitted. It was stated, however, by the court, that such testimony was competent only by reason of the law which permitted persons accused of crime to testify in their own behalf. Before such statutes were enacted, it was the rule and the law that proof of statements made by one in arrest for a crime, before the examining magistrate or coroner, was not admissible in evidence against him. This rule was based upon the ground that such statements so made were deemed compulsory, not voluntary. The statute referred to, and providing further that the omission or refusal of the accused to testify shall create no presumption against him, removes from the testimony so given the element of compulsion. The testimony given by one afterwards charged with crime, before a coroner upon an inquest, is admissible against him.

Mooney, 6; 10 Iowa, 507; State v. Read, 49 Iowa, 85; Manly v. State, 52 Ind. 215; Wheeler v. State, 24 Wis. 52; Osborn v. State, 24 Ark. 629. State v. Glass, 40 Wis. 218; Dickerson v. State, 48 Wis. 259; People v. Me., 254; State v. Ober, 52 N. H. 59; Commonwealth v. Morgan, 107 Mass. 199; Connors v. People, 50 N.Y. 240; Dickerson v. State, 48 Wis. 288; State v. Glass, 50 Wis. 218; Hendrickson v. People, 10 N.Y. 13; Teachout v. People, 41 N.Y. 17;

### --- Page 0141 ---

56 JURISDICTION, [$ “1.

Sec. 71. Presence of accused — Right to be heard by self and counsel.— It is quite generally held that the accused may waive the benefit of that clause of the constitution which guarantees to him the right to appear and be present in court and be heard by himself and counsel. Yet the supreme court of the United States apparently are not disposed to accept this doctrine. It is true the question arose upon the construction of a statute which provided that, in cases of indictment for a felony, the accused must be personally present at the trial; yet, as will be observed from the language of the court, their construction was placed upon higher grounds, and not only had reference to the clause under consideration, but clauses similar in character, which pertain to rights guaranteed by the constitution. They state: “We are of opinion that it was not within the power of the accused or his counsel to dispense with the statutory requirement as to his personal presence at the trial. The argument to the contrary necessarily proceeds upon the ground that he alone is concerned as to the mode by which he may be deprived of his life or liberty, and that the chief object of the prosecution is to punish him for the crime charged. But this is a mistaken view, as well as of the relations which the accused holds to the public as the end of human punishment. ‘The natural life,’ says Blackstone, ‘cannot legally be disposed of or destroyed by any individual, neither by the person himself nor by any other of his fellow creatures, merely upon their own authority.’ The public has an interest in his life and liberty. Neither can be lawfully taken except in the mode prescribed by law. That which the law makes essential in proceedings involving the deprivation of life or liberty cannot be dispensed with or affected by the consent of the accused, much less by his mere failure, when on trial and in custody, to object to unauthorized methods. The great end of punishment is not the expiation or atonement of the offense committed, but the prevention of future offenses of the same kind. Such being the relation which the citizen holds to the public, and

Schoefier v. State, 3 Wis. 717; Dickerson v. State, 48 Wis. 285; McCorkle v. State, 14 Ind. 39; State v. Wamire, 16 Ind. 357. Contra, Salinger v. People, 102 Ill. 241; Prime v. Commonwealth, 18 Pa. St. 63 Wis. 285; Wilson v. State, 2 Ohio; Hill v. State, 17 Wis.; In re Staff, 103.

<br>

### --- Page 0142 ---

§ 72. WAIVER OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS, ETO.  
The object of public punishment for wrongs, the legislature has deemed it essential to the protection of one whose life or liberty is involved in a prosecution for felony that he shall personally be present at the trial; that is, at every stage of the trial when his substantial rights may be affected by the proceedings against him. If he be deprived of his life or liberty without being present, such deprivation would be without that due process of law required by the constitution.

Src. 72. Subject continued — Record must show presence.  
It is generally conceded that the record must show that the accused was present, and that no presumption will be indulged in that he was present. It was held otherwise in an early Wisconsin case; but in respect to the question under discussion that case was practically overruled by subsequent cases. With reference to this question, and also that of waiver of the constitutional right to be present, the language of the court in French v. State is very emphatic and forcible, as follows: “It was his (the accused's) constitutional right, that he may not waive, to be present during the whole trial, and meet the witnesses face to face, and to be confronted with the witnesses against him. This is not only the indispensable right of the accused, but the record must show that he had a legal and constitutional trial. In felonies the record must show the defendant to have been present at the arraignment and testing of the jurors. In capital cases, if not in all felonies, the record must show that the defendant was present at the trial, verdict and sentence.” The learned judge, referring to authorities briefed by the defendant's counsel, further states: “Many of the above authorities also hold that no presumptions will be indulged in to supply the record in such a case, and that there is no waiver of the right less than a positive and personal relinquishment of it, and this I understand to be the effect of our own decisions.” Again: “These great common-law rights have been made constitutional provisions in the various states.”

Hopt v. Utah, 110 U.S. 574, 579. v. State, 26 Mix 362; Bearden v. State, 44 Ark. 331; State v. Jones, 61 Mo. 232.

French v. State, 85 Wis 400; Prime v. Hill v. State, 17 Wis. 697. 

French v. State, 85 Wis. 400; Davis v. Commonwealth, 18 Pa St. 103; Hooker v. People, 13 Gratt. 763; Dyson v. State, 38 Wis. 439.

<br>

### --- Page 0143 ---

8 JURISDICTION, [§ 73.

and so made essential and paramount, and also indispensable, in trials for capital offenses and felonies. It is not too strict to hold that in all such cases the accused must be present in court to meet the witnesses face to face, and to test the jury, and when the verdict is rendered, and be asked if he has anything to say why the sentence should not be pronounced against him, and to meet his sentence; and also, still more important, that he have a trial by an impartial jury. These are great constitutional safeguards against oppression and injustice that must not be abridged or compromised.” It will be noticed the opinion first states that these great rights cannot be waived by the accused; later, that there can be no waiver of the right “less than a positive and personal relinquishment of it;” and finally, that they are indispensable, and must not be abridged or compromised. I confess I am in doubt whether the doctrine of this court is that such rights may be relinquished by a positive and personal relinquishment, or whether they cannot be relinquished at all. The language and conclusion are different from that of the court in In re Stuff, 63 Wis. 295, 204, where it is stated: “Section 7 of article I confers many rights upon a person accused of crime, every one of which he may waive without authority of statute, as has often been judicially determined, except the right to be tried by a jury. Such waiver may be express, or it may be by failure to make due objection and exception.”

Sec. 73. Meeting witnesses face to face.— It has been held, and the rule generally prevails, that the accused may consent to the reading of depositions of absent witnesses, and that the constitutional right is not thereby violated. It was said the constitution does not guarantee an accused person against the legitimate consequences of his own wrongful acts. It grants him the privilege of being confronted with the witnesses against him; but if he voluntarily keeps the witnesses away, he cannot insist on his privilege. If, therefore, when absent by his procurement, their evidence is supplied in some lawful way, he is in no condition to assert that his constitutional rights have been violated.

1 Williams v. State, 61 Wis. 281; In re Staff, 63 Wis. 283.

<br>

### --- Page 0144 ---

§ 74. Removal of accused for unseemly conduct.—The right of a prisoner to be present at his trial does not include the right to prevent a trial by unseemly disturbance. Courts have often been embarrassed by malicious conduct on the part of an accused upon trial. The conduct of Guiteau was an illustration. The court there, by the exercise of exceptional patience and diplomacy, was enabled finally to dispose of the case. In a recent number of the London Solicitor’s Journal reference is made to a case where it became necessary to remove the accused and proceed with the trial in his absence on account of his conduct. The prisoner attempted to attack the judge. He was overpowered and the cause continued until the next day. The next day, upon resuming the trial, the prisoner's conduct was such that it could not proceed, and he was removed. During the trial of a prisoner in one of the federal courts he persistently interrupted the district attorney in spite of the admonition of the court. He was ordered to be removed to an adjoining room, with liberty of access for his counsel. The trial then proceeded under the objection of his counsel. Afterwards he became composed and the trial proceeded without further disturbance. In the language of the judge presiding, “It does not lie in the mouth of the accused to complain of the order which was made necessary by his own misconduct, and which he could at any time have terminated by signifying his willingness to avoid creating a disturbance.”1

§ 75. Right to demand nature of accusation.—Another provision of the constitution is in substance that the accused may demand the nature of the accusation against him. This is embraced in the indictment or information, and is satisfied when the accused waives the reading of the indictment or information.

§ 76. Speedy and public trial.—Another provision is that the accused shall have a speedy and public trial; yet it is almost universally conceded that the accused may continue the trial for cause shown, and that such conduct on his part does not conflict with such provision. He may also demand

1 United States v. Davis, 6 Blatch. 404; In re Staff, 63 Wis. 285.

<br>

### --- Page 0145 ---

(7a)

that the public be excluded from attendance upon the trial, and if the court complies with such request it in no wise affects his constitutional right respecting a public trial. It requires but little power of discrimination to determine that these last-mentioned provisions are different in character from those before stated, and that while they may be properly classed as privileges the former are more in the nature of absolute rights, conferred not alone in the interest of the accused, but as essentials to be observed by the state, in order that substantial justice may be rendered.
Vin re Staff, 63 Wis. 285.

<br>

Sec. 77. General rule.— In the distribution of powers among courts it frequently happens that jurisdiction of the same subject-matter is given to different courts. Conflict and confusion would inevitably result unless some rule was adopted to pre-

<br>

### --- Page 0147 ---

62 JURISDICTION,

vent or avoid it. Therefore it has been wisely and uniformly determined that whichever court, of those having such jurisdiction, first obtains jurisdiction, or, as is sometimes said, possession of the cause, will retain it throughout to the exclusion of another; and this jurisdiction extends to the execution of the judgment. Hence, where a judgment is rendered in one of the courts of a state, no action of another court of coordinate jurisdiction can be maintained to set aside or open such judgment on the ground of partial payment or for other reasons, even though a transcript of such judgment has been filed and docketed. This rule, however, is subject to some limitation. In order to receive application it must appear that the suit is between the same parties or privies, with respect to the same relief or remedy, and the rule is confined to such matters as properly arise in the progress of the first suit. It does not extend to all matters which might have been involved in such suit. The rule is confined in its operation to the parties who are before the court, or who may, if they wish to do so, come before the court and have a hearing in the issue so to be decided. It is not true that a court, having obtained jurisdiction of the subject-matter of a suit and of parties before it, thereby excludes all other courts from the right to adjudicate upon other matters having a very close connection with those before the first court, and in some instances requiring the decision of exactly the same questions. In examining into the exclusive character of the jurisdiction of such cases, regard must be had to the nature of the remedies, the character of the relief sought, and the identity of the parties in the different suits.

Note To Sec. 77.— Jurisdiction — Concurrent.— In a recent case in the United States supreme court it was stated that “two propositions have been so firmly established by frequent decisions of this court as to require only to be stated: 1. When a state court has entered upon the trial of a criminal case under a statute not repugnant to the constitution of the

Ober v. Gallagher, 93 U.S. 199; Gould v. Hayes, 19 Ala. 438; Coon v. Seymour, 71 Wis. 340; Mail v. Maxwell, 107 Ill. 554; Miller v. County Commissioners, 119 Mass. 485; Powers v. City of Springfield, 116 Mass. 84; Anthony v. Dunlap, 8 Cal. 26; Freeman v. Howe, 24 How. 450.

Hawes v. Orr, 10 Bush (Ky.), 431; Coon v. Seymour, 71 Wis. 340.

Orient Ins. Co. v. Sloan, 70 Wis. 611; Coon v. Seymour et al., 71 Wis. 340; McLean v. Speed, 52 Mich. 257; Fenske v. Kluender, 61 Wis. 602.

Buck v. Colbath, 3 Wall. 334; Putnam v. New Albany, 4 Biss. (U.S.) 365.

<br>

### --- Page 0148 ---

§ 77. CONCURRENT. . 63  
United States, or to any law or treaty thereof, and when the state court has jurisdiction of the offense and of the accused, no mere error in the conduct of the trial can be made the basis of jurisdiction in a court of the United States to review the proceedings upon a writ of habeas corpus.  

2. When a state court and a court of the United States may each take jurisdiction of a matter, the tribunal whose jurisdiction first attaches holds it to the exclusion of the other, until its duty is fully performed and the jurisdiction involved is exhausted; and this rule applies alike in both civil and criminal cases. A court of equity, although having jurisdiction over person and property in a case pending before it, is not thereby vested with jurisdiction over crimes in dealing with such property by a party before the civil suit was brought, and cannot restrain by injunction proceedings regularly brought in a criminal court having jurisdiction of the crime and the accused. Harkrader v. Wadleigh, 172 U.S. 148.  

When there are courts of equal and concurrent jurisdiction, the court in which jurisdiction first attaches retains the case. The rule is not based, in a suit for an injunction, solely on the consideration that the injunction affects the parties and not the court. If one court can enjoin the suitors in another court, and compel them to litigate before it, it is the same thing in effect as restraining the other court. Merrill v. Lake, 16 Ohio, 405; Keating v. Spink, 3 Ohio St. 126.  

“It is a doctrine too long established to require the citation of authorities, that when the jurisdiction of the court, and the right of the plaintiff to prosecute his suit in it, has once attached, that right cannot be arrested or taken away by proceedings in another court. These rules have their foundation not merely in comity but necessity. For if one may enjoin, the other may retort by injunction, and then the parties be without remedy. Neither can one take property from the custody of the other by replevin or any other process, for this would produce a conflict extremely embarrassing to the administration of justice.” Peck v. Jenness, 7 How. 612.  

The powers which belong to a state are exercised independently. In its sphere of sovereignty it stands on an equality with the federal government and is not subject to its control. It would be as dangerous as humiliating to the rights of a state to hold that its legislative powers were exercised to any extent, and under any circumstances, subject to the paramount action of Congress. Such a doctrine would lead to serious and dangerous conflicts of power.” Justice McLean in Prigg v. Pennsylvania, 16 Pet. 663.  

Again it was said: “A most injudicious conflict of jurisdiction would be likely often to arise between the federal and the state courts, if the final process of the one could be levied on property which had been taken by the process of the other. No such case can exist. Property once levied upon remains in the custody of the law, and it is not liable to be taken by another execution in the hands of a different officer, and especially by an officer acting under a different jurisdiction.” Hagan v. Lucas, 10 Pet. 400.  

It is this principle which prevents money made on executions, in the hands of the sheriff, from being seized upon process. While the money remains in the hands of the sheriff, it is in the custody of the law. It does not become the property of the judgment creditor until it is paid over, and consequently it is not liable to be attached as his. The writ of attachment cannot supersede the execution or release the sheriff from a literal com-

<br>

### --- Page 0149 ---

G4 JURISDICTION. [§§ 78-80.

pliance with its command, which required him to bring the money into court so that it might be subject to their order.” Dawson v. Holcomb, 1 Ohio, 275. It is quite generally held by the state courts that a third person not a party may maintain replevin for property attached in the state courts. See sec. 228 et seq.

Sec. 78. Rule illustrated—Does not apply after suit is ended.—An illustration of this limitation is where an officer under a writ of replevin seizes certain property specified in the writ. The rule is familiar that his possession cannot be disturbed by the action of any other court. But after the litigation is ended, or the possession of the officer of the court is discharged, other courts are at liberty to deal with it according to the rights of the parties before them, whether those rights require them to take possession of the property or not.

Sec. 79. Rule not applicable where action is brought against officers who seize property upon attachment.—If, however, instead of the property being taken on a writ of replevin, it is taken upon a writ of attachment, a suit could be maintained in another court for trespass in seizing the property. In seizing property under attachment, the officer exercises a discretion and is legally responsible to any person for the consequences of any error or mistake in its exercise to the prejudice of such person. The proceeding in such a suit does not necessarily involve the question of title to the property attached. That is all the time a question between the officer or the purchaser at his sale on the one side and the adverse claimant on the other. There can be no pretense that anything more is involved in or concluded by the attachment proceedings than such title to the property as the defendant in attachment had when the levy was made. While trespass will thus lie against the officer, yet the property itself is within the custody of the law to such an extent that replevin will not lie against the officer to withdraw it.

Sec. 80. Rule does not apply where the court is unable to grant full relief on account of its jurisdiction.—This rule, like most others, has its exceptions and qualifications. One is,  

¹ Freeman v. Howe, 24 How. 450.  
² Buck v. Colbath, 3 Wall. 334. See sec. 228 et seq.  
³ Freeman v. Howe, 24 How. 450.

<br>

### --- Page 0150 ---

§§ 81, 82. CONCURRENT. 63

that where the court in which the action is pending is unable by reason of its jurisdiction to afford the relief sought, as where several fraudulent judgments are confessed in several courts, it would not be necessary for a creditor to bring a different suit in each different court.

Sec. 81. Bills of review.—Nor can the circuit court of one county review and reverse the judgment of the circuit court of another county on the ground of errors apparent on the face of the record or on that of newly-discovered evidence. A bill of review or a proceeding similar in character must always be filed in the court where the record is, and by which the decree was pronounced. Hence, it was held that a bill to revive a decree respecting land could not be filed in the county where the land then was, it having, by a change of county boundaries after the decree and before the filing of the bill, been transferred to another county.

Sec. 82. Federal or state courts cannot enjoin proceedings in each other.—Nor can the state courts enjoin proceedings in the United States courts, nor the courts of the latter enjoin proceedings in the state courts, except in proceedings under the bankrupt law. This restriction, however, does not apply so as to prevent a circuit court of the United States from enjoining a state officer from executing a state law in conflict with the constitution or a statute of the United States. Nor can one court restrain by injunction the proceedings of another court of co-ordinate jurisdiction, simply because the suit may bring in other parties not included in the action at law sought to be enjoined.

Note to Sec. 82.—Where a case is properly removed from the state court its jurisdiction ceases. Under the judiciary act a writ of injunction can—

Cal. 608, Riggs v. Johnson County, 6 Wall. 166;  
Parish v. Marvin et al., 15 Wis. 247; Dial v. Reynolds, 96 U.S. 340.  
Arnold v. Styles, 2 Blackf. 3801.  
Davis v. Gray, 16 Wall. 203;  
Arnold v. Styles, 2 Blackf. 301.  
Born v. Bank of United States, 7 McKim v. Voorhies, 7 Cranch, 279; Wheat. 738; Dodge v. Woolsey, 18 Weber v. Lee County, 6 Wall. 210; How.  #31; Allen v. Balt. & Ohio R. Co., 114 U.S. 311.

§ 83, St.

66 JURISDICTION.

Not be granted to stay proceedings in any court of a state. If the state court proceeds, the remedy is by a writ of error after final judgment from the highest court of the state, and not by prohibition or punishment for contempt. Chesapeake & O. R. Co. v. White, 111 U. S. 134; Kanouse v. Martin, 14 How. 23. If a party is forced to trial, he loses none of his rights by defending against the action in the state courts. National Steamship Co. v. Tugman, 106 U. S. 118; Meyer v. Delaware Const. Co., 100 U.S. 457. In French v. Hay, 22 Wall. 250, it was held, however, that where a plaintiff obtaining a judgment sends it into another state to be enforced, the circuit court of the United States into which the cause has been removed may enjoin the plaintiff from proceeding until it hears the case, and if after hearing it annuls the decree in the state court, and dismisses as wanting equity the bill in which the decree was made, the injunction may be made perpetual. The prohibition of the judiciary act was held to have no application. The prior jurisdiction of the circuit court took the case out of the operation of the statute.

Sec. 83. Rule does not prevent issue of habeas corpus in certain cases.—So the state courts may in all cases issue writs of habeas corpus, where it does not appear from the application that the prisoner is detained by virtue of writ or process emanating from a federal court. But when the court is apprised of the fact, by the return of the marshal or other federal officer, that the prisoner is in custody under federal authority, it can proceed no further, and if upon inquiry it does not appear that the prisoner is held by such paramount authority, the state court, if his imprisonment be illegal, may release him, notwithstanding such illegality may arise from a violation of the constitution and laws of the United States.

Sec. 84. Rule as to fugitives from justice.—A person duly appointed by the authorities of a state to receive a fugitive from justice is the agent of such state and not of the United States, though the subject is regulated by federal laws; and state courts have the authority to enforce obedience to its writ where such an agent has in custody a person charged as being unlawfully restrained of his liberty, and not only to inquire into the cause of his detention, but to discharge him if found to be illegally restrained of his liberty. If a prisoner is in the custody of a state court of competent jurisdiction, not illegally asserted, he

1 Ableman v. Booth, 21 How. 506; 2 Robb v. Connolly, 111 U. S. 624.

Tarble Case, 18 Wall. 897; Robb v. Connolly, 111 U.S. 624.

<br>

### --- Page 0152 ---

cannot be taken from that jurisdiction and discharged on habeas corpus, issued by a court of the United States, simply because he is not guilty of the offense for which he is held, though such defense may grow out of a provision in the constitution of the United States which may have been violated!

Sec. 85. Where accused may be tried in either one of two counties.— So where jurisdiction of a crime lies in either of two counties, the court which first obtains jurisdiction of the person of the accused retains it to the end, to the exclusion of the court of the other county, even though he may have been indicted first in such other county.

Sec. 86. Receivers— Actions by and against — Leave of court.— A court by which a receiver has been appointed to take possession of property has exclusive jurisdiction of proceedings which will affect his title or possession. Some courts hold, however, that an action for damages against a corporation, where a receiver has been appointed, may be prosecuted without first obtaining leave of the court appointing the receiver. The cases cited, with the exception of Allen v. Railroad Co. were actions against the receiver for damages accruing or injury inflicted while he was in charge of the affairs of the corporation as such receiver. It is said by the Iowa court in Allen v. Railroad Co., in referring to some of the cases cited: “If in such case it is not necessary to obtain authority to commence an action from the court appointing the receiver, a fortiori it is not necessary to obtain such authority when the action is against the railroad company itself.” Other courts hold in such cases that leave must be obtained from the court in which the receiver is appointed to bring an action for damages against the corporation, company or individual.

1Ex parte Crouch, 112 U. S. 178, 111 Mass. 108; Blumenthal v. Brain- erd, 38 Vt. 402; Chautauqua Co. Bank v. Risley, 19 N.Y. 369; Kenney v. Kinney v. Crocker, 18 Wis. 60; 96 Mich 617, Mil. & St. Paul R. R. Co. v. Mik & Minn. R. Co., 20 Wis 174.

7Ex parte Baldwin, 69 Iowa, 502, Carter v. Rodewald, 108 T.H. 351; Allen v. Central R. Co., 42 Iowa.

3Kinney v. Crocker, 18 Wis. 60; Paize v. Smith, 90 Mass. 395; Hills v. Parker, 94 Gest v. N.O., St. L. & Chi. R. Co., 30 La. Ann. 28; Angel v. Smith, 9 Vesey. See Wiswall v. Sampson, 62; Freeman v. Howe, 24 Low. 450.

### --- Page 0153 ---

63 JURISDICTION. [§ 87.

The New York court, in the case cited from that state, declined to follow the rule announced by the federal court, and permitted an action of ejectment to be brought under a claim of paramount title to land which had been conveyed in an equity suit to a receiver. Yet the Wisconsin court, notwithstanding its ruling in the case cited, with respect to an action for damages, held that an action to set aside a previous foreclosure and sale of premises ought not to be permitted while the premises are in possession of a receiver, because a judgment therein cannot be enforced without disturbing his possession. And also in another case it held that when property is lawfully in the possession of the receiver, it is the duty of the court to protect such possession, not only against violence, but also against suits at law.

Sec. 87. Receivers — Subject continued.— It was said by the Michigan court that a receiver of mortgaged property should see to it that he sells none but the property covered by the mortgage, under an order of court for its sale, and held that an action may be brought against him for the value of other property held by the mortgagor as bailee, and delivered to the receiver without demand and without leave of the court. A national banking association may be sued in any state, county or municipal court in the county or city where such association is located, having jurisdiction in similar cases, even though a receiver may have been appointed and is administering its concerns. Such a corporation does not lose its corporate existence by mere default in paying its circulating notes and upon the mere appointment of a receiver. The mere appointment by a circuit court of the United States of a receiver of a corporation organized under the laws of a state does not deprive a court of the state of jurisdiction to hear and determine an application for a mandamus directing a recorder of mortgages in the state to cancel and erase from the books of his office an inscription against property of the petitioner in favor of the corporation. In such case it is not essential to the jurisdiction.

§ 87.) CONCURRENT, (Gu)

of the court that leave be obtained from the court appointing the receiver, the action being against the corporation, and not against the receiver. The circuit court, on application of the receiver, might perhaps interfere to prevent the petitioner from proceeding in the state court. The state courts, however, have no jurisdiction to entertain proceedings in cases where the federal courts have appointed receivers, to deprive such receivers of property in their possession as such.1

The broad ground was taken by the supreme court of the United States that a receiver cannot be sued without the consent of the court of equity which appointed him on a money demand or for damages, though the latter arose out of his management of a railroad and through the negligence of his agents or servants. In that case the receiver was appointed by a state court of one jurisdiction, and the action was commenced in the courts of another jurisdiction. The act of March 3, 1887 (24 Stat. 554), in effect, and to a great extent, abrogated the rule declared by the supreme court, in so far only as it was applicable to receivers appointed by the federal courts. In Diddy v. Lafayette, 163 U.S. 456, the supreme court of the United States, in construing such act, stated that the second section thereof was intended to place receivers of railroads on the same plane with railroad companies, both as respects their liability to be sued for acts done while operating a railroad and as respects the mode of service.2 In a subsequent case, where an action was brought against a railroad company and its receiver to recover for personal injuries received while the road was in the hands of the receiver, but the receivership had been terminated before the commencement of the action, and the property had been transferred to the company, it was held that the company was liable in an action at law for the damages found by the jury, it appearing that the value of the road had been largely increased during the management by the receiver.3 One having a judgment lien upon land of his debtor, which is in the possession of a receiver appointed by a court of chancery under a bill by a creditor against the debtor and

1 Calhoun v. Lanoux, 127 U.S. 634.  
2 Texas & Pacific R. Co. v. Bloom, 164 U.S. 636, Barton v. Barbour, 104 U.S. 126.  
3 See also State v. Port Royal & N.R. Co., 84 Fed. Rep.

<br>

### --- Page 0155 ---

40 JURISDICTION. [§ 88.

a third person, to set aside a conveyance to the latter upon the ground of fraud against creditors, cannot proceed to levy his execution, if he have notice of the fact that the property is in the custody of the law. He must apply to the court of chancery, which will take care to protect his interest in making a sale or in distributing the proceeds. If the judgment creditor after making his levy does not apply to the court of chancery and pray that effect may be given to his title, and the court adjudges his title invalid, this is res adjudicata, and binds his title in an action of ejectment. One who claims the disposition or possession of property in the hands of receivers of a court must come to that court in that case and obtain leave to file a bill in the original cause.

Sec. 88. Suits pending in foreign jurisdictions.— Another exception to the general rule is that the pendency of a prior suit in personam, in a foreign court or in a court of a sister state, between the same parties and for the same cause, does not operate as a bar to the commencement and prosecution of a suit elsewhere. Such exception does not conflict with that rule which prevails in courts of equity, that the courts of one state may, where the parties are citizens of such state and the court has jurisdiction over such parties, enjoin them from proceeding in the courts of another state. As stated by Mr. Story: “Though the courts of one county have no authority to stay proceedings in the courts of another, they have an undoubted authority to control all persons and things within their own territorial limits.” “When the necessary parties are before a court of equity, it is immaterial that the res of the controversy, whether it be real or personal property, is beyond the territorial jurisdiction of the tribunal. It has the power to compel the defendant to do all things necessary, according to the lex loci rei sitae, which he could do voluntarily, to give full effect to the decree against him, without regard to the situation of the subject-matter. Such courts consider the equities between the

Wiswall v. Sampson, 14 How. 60. Seevers v. Clements, 28 Md. 426;

American Loan & Trust Co. v. Lowry v. Hall, 2 W. & S. (Pa.) 129; Vermont R. Co., 86 Fed. Rep. 390. Smith v. Lathrop, 44 Pa. St. 326.

White v. Whitman, 1 Curtis (U.S.), 4 Story’s Eq. Jur., secs. 899, 900, 494; Hatch v. Spoffard, 22 Conn. 455;

§ 89, 90.) CONCURRENT.

parties, and decree in personam according to those equities, and enforce obedience to their decrees by process in personam.*

Sec. 89. Exemptions of debtors — Suits to evade in other jurisdictions.— Hence it was held that, where the statutes of a state exempted the earnings for personal services of a debtor, who was the head of a family and a citizen of the state, the courts of that state had authority to restrain a citizen of the county in which the equity action was commenced from proceeding in another state to attach the earnings of such head of a family, with a view to evade the exemption laws of the state where the debtor resided, and to prevent the debtor from availing himself of the benefit of such law.? It was also held that an express company could maintain an action in New York to restrain the defendant, a resident of the state of New York, from prosecuting actions against the company in the District of Columbia, brought to avoid a decision of the court of appeals of New York, differing from the rule upon the same subject in the District of Columbia.?

Sec. 90. Application of the equity rule.— Where the plaintiff commenced a suit in Rhode Island upon the same cause of action, and after taking testimony in the suit pending in the New York court, and without concluding his case, proceeded in the suit in the Rhode Island court, the New York court refused to restrain him from prosecuting the suit pending in the Rhode Island court, but stayed proceedings in the suit pending in New York unless he agreed to proceed and conclude the suit pending there and stay proceedings in the other court. It was also held by the federal court that it was no violation of the provision of the constitution which requires that full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the judicial proceedings of every other state, if a court in one state (in which proceedings in insolvency have been commenced) enjoins a creditor of

1 Phelps v. McDonald, 99 U.S. 298; v. Joseph, 107 Ind. 490; Chaffee v. Quidnick Co., 13 R. I. 442; Great Falls Mfg. Co. v. Worster, 23 N. H. 308; Cole v. Cunningham, 133 U.S. 107, Erie Ry. Co. v. Ramsay, 45 N.Y. 462; Pickett v. Ferguson, 45 Ark. 177.

2 Snook v. Suetzer, 25 Ohio St. 516; Keyser v. Rice, 47 Md. 203; Zimmerman v. Franke, 34 Kan. 650; Wilson v. Hammond, 3 Sandf. 704.

3 Dinsmore v. Neresheimer, 32 Hun, 204.

### --- Page 0157 ---

72 JURISDICTION. [§§ 91, 92.]

the insolvent, a citizen of the state, and subject to the jurisdiction of the court, from proceeding to judgment and execution in a suit against the insolvent in another state, begun by attachment of property there after knowledge of the debtor's embarrassment and insolvency.

Sec. 91. Application of rule in federal court where question of title involved in state court.—The federal courts ordinarily will refuse to entertain jurisdiction of a question of title where that is pending in a state court. They say, "it is true that in an action of ejectment in a court of law the pending of another ejectment between the same parties might not afford a sufficient ground for a plea of autre action pendant; nor would the court have been bound even by comity to await the decision of the state court, or suffer the cause pending before them to be in any way affected by it. But a decree of a court of chancery, on a bill of peace, must necessarily operate by way of estoppel as to the title to the land, and conclude all the parties to it, because it should put an end to all litigation between them. If they have suits pending in other courts on the same question of title, they must cease. The bill acts by way of injunction on the party. No injunction ever goes to the court having concurrent jurisdiction of the question. The United States courts have no such power over suitors in a state court. But a decree on a bill of peace which does not put an end to litigation is a mere brutum fulmen. Unless a court can make a decree which it can execute, it is a sufficient reason for refusing to take cognizance of a case. It is a rule absolutely necessary to be observed by courts who have a concurrent jurisdiction, that in all cases where the jurisdiction of a court and the right of a plaintiff to prosecute his suit in it have once attached, that right cannot be arrested or taken away by proceedings in another court. This rule, it is said, has its foundation not merely in comity, but in necessity. If one may enjoin, the other may retort by injunction, and thus the parties be without remedy."

Sec. 92. Effect of fraud upon the rule.—Another exception to the general rule is that, under peculiar conditions, courts of

1 Cole v. Cunningham, 183 U.S. 107. 
2 Orton v. Smith, 18 How. 213

<br>

### --- Page 0158 ---

§ 93. CONCURRENT.

Equity will entertain jurisdiction to relieve against a judgment procured by fraud. In doing this they do not seek to control, supervise or annul the proceedings of the court rendering the judgment, but seek to give such relief as is consistent with the principles of equity. It is not universally true that the proceedings of courts having jurisdiction are so conclusive and binding that no other court can in any event interfere. The most solemn transactions and judgments may at the instance of the parties be set aside or rendered inoperative for fraud, and the courts of chancery are always open to hear complaints against it, whether committed ex parte or in judicial proceedings. In such case the court does not act as a court of review, nor does it inquire into any irregularities or errors of proceeding in another court, but it will scrutinize the conduct of the parties, and if it finds they have been guilty of fraud in obtaining a judgment or decree, it will deprive them of the benefit of it, and of any inequitable advantage which they have derived under it. Hence a circuit court of the United States has jurisdiction in equity to set aside a sale of an infant’s lands derived from a probate court, and to set aside fraudulent sales made by a testamentary executor under the orders of the probate court.

§ 93. Congress cannot confer judicial power upon state courts, yet the latter, unless restricted, may administer federal laws.— The state courts are independent of and beyond the power of Congress. Hence Congress cannot confer any judicial power upon a state court. Yet the statutes of the United States are as much the law of the land in any state as are those of the state; and although exclusive jurisdiction for their enforcement may be given to the federal courts, yet where it is not given, either expressly or by necessary implication, the state courts, having competent jurisdiction in other respects, may be resorted to. In such case the state courts do not exercise a new jurisdiction conferred upon them, but their ordinary jurisdiction derived from their constitution under the state law.

1 Arrowsmith v. Gleason, 120 U. S. 86; Johnson v. Waters, 111 U.S. 610.  
2 Arrowsmith v. Gleason, 129 U.S. 86.  
3 Johnson v. Waters, 111 U.S. 607.  
4 Claflin v. Houseman, 93 U.S. 130; Ex parte Knowles, 5 Cal. 301.  
5 Claflin v. Houseman, 93 U.S. 130.

<br>

### --- Page 0159 ---

Tt JURISDICTION. [§§ 94, 95.

State court, therefore, can exercise concurrent jurisdiction with the federal courts in cases arising under the constitution, laws and treaties of the United States, where it is not excluded by express provision or by incompatibility in its exercise arising from the nature of the particular case.

Sec. 94. Federal and state courts—Jurisdiction, when concurrent.—It was stated by Bradley, J., in Clafin v. Houseman, 93 U.S. 150: “The United States is not a foreign sovereignty as regards the several states, but is a concurrent, and within its jurisdiction paramount, sovereignty. Every citizen of a state is a subject of two distinct sovereignties having concurrent jurisdiction in the state,—concurrent as to place and persons, though distinct as to subject-matter. Legal or equitable rights acquired under either system of laws may be enforced in any court of either sovereignty competent to hear and determine such kind of rights and not restrained by its constitution in the exercise of such jurisdiction. Thus, a legal or equitable right acquired under state laws may be prosecuted in the state courts, and also, if the parties reside in different states, in the federal courts. So rights, whether legal or equitable, acquired under the laws of the United States may be prosecuted in the United States courts or in the state courts competent to decide rights of the like character and class, subject, however, to the qualification that, where a right arises under a law of the United States, congress may, if it see fit, give to the federal courts exclusive jurisdiction... The federal and state courts together form one system of jurisprudence, which constitutes the law of the land for the state; and the courts of the two jurisdictions are not foreign to each other, nor to be treated by each other as such, but as courts of the same country having jurisdiction partly different and partly concurrent.”

Sec. 95. Naturalization of aliens—Conferring power upon state courts.—The power to naturalize is judicial in character, and such power is vested exclusively in the congress of the United States. The question whether an alien so naturalized

1 Claflin v. Houseman, 93 U.S. 130, 150; Claflin v. Houseman, 93 U.S.

2 Ex parte Knowles, 5 Cal. 301; Houston v. Moore, 5 Wheat. 1;

3 Thurlow v. Massachusetts, 5 How.; Chirac v. Chirac, 2 Wheat. 269,

<br>

### --- Page 0160 ---

§ 90] CONCURRENT. vi)  
becomes a citizen of the United States, though often discussed, is not material here. The constitution provides that the citizens of each state shall be entitled to all the privileges and immunities of citizens in the several states. It has been held, however, that any state may confer the right of citizenship upon aliens, so far as to place them upon an equal footing with those who are citizens, but the exercise of such power does not extend to making them citizens of the United States. Yet it seems that congress has conferred upon state courts or the judges thereof the power of naturalization, which they uniformly exercise.

If it be a judicial power, then, without the sanction of the state, such jurisdiction could not be conferred. If, however, the state courts, not being restricted, have inherent jurisdiction, the same as they have in respect to legal or equitable rights acquired under the laws of the United States, then it is that jurisdiction is not conferred by the federal statutes?

Sec. 96. Power of the state legislatures over the federal courts.—It has been stated in a prior section that legal and equitable rights acquired under state laws may be prosecuted in the federal courts if the parties reside in different states. In so doing such courts are only administering the law of the land. State legislatures, however, cannot restrict the jurisdiction of the federal courts; nor can they impose conditions in any manner interfering with the right of suitors to remove causes commenced in the state courts into the federal courts;* nor prevent citizens of other states from bringing actions in the federal courts, where such right exists under federal laws. Neither can state courts confer jurisdiction on the federal courts, or provide for the removal of causes from state courts into the federal courts. State laws cannot have the force to regulate the proceedings of federal courts sitting within the state; hence the practice of state courts as to bills of exceptions and

1 In re Wehlitz, 16 Wis. 443, 84 Am. Dec. 700. See also Dred Scott v. Sandford, 19 How. 393.

2 Ex parte Knowles, 5 Cal. 301.

3 Insurance Co. v. Morse, 20 Wall. 445; Barron v. Burnside, 121 U. S. 186.

4 Suydam v. Broadnax, 14 Pet. 67; Bank v. Vaiden, 18 How. 502.

5 United States v. Peters, 5 Cranch, 115.

6 Greeley v. Townsend, 25 Cal. 604.

7 Guild v. Frontin, 18 How. 135; Kelsey v. Forsyth, 21 How. 85; Pomeroy v. State Bank, 68 U.S.; Gilman v. Telegraph Co., 91 U. S. 603.

### --- Page 0161 ---

6 JURISDICTION. [§ 97, 98.] Writs of error and charging of juries cannot affect proceedings in the federal courts.

Sec. 97. Equity jurisdiction of the federal courts cannot be affected.— The equity jurisdiction of the federal courts is the same as when the judiciary act of 1789 was adopted, and is and must be uniform throughout the several states, and hence is beyond all interference by state legislation. State statutes cannot enlarge such jurisdiction by provisions which attempt to confer jurisdiction to try common-law actions, nor to change the established rule in equity relating to the time when actions shall be commenced seeking equitable relief on the ground of fraud; but an equitable remedy provided by the statutes of a state, to be enforced by its courts, may be enforced by the federal courts.

Sec. 98. Personal actions against owners of vessels — Jurisdiction of federal and state courts.— The jurisdiction of the federal courts and the state courts is concurrent with respect to common-law actions in personam against the owners or proprietors of vessels upon navigable waters. The exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts conferred by act of Congress relates more particularly to proceedings in the nature of those in rem, and not those which involve personal contract liability, or liability for personal wrongs or torts. The character of the proceedings in general is the test. If the proceedings pursued in any case are those of admiralty, the state courts cannot acquire jurisdiction thereof. But if the action be at law or in chancery, although founded upon a subject-matter which, under proper proceedings, could have been brought before an admiralty court for adjudication, it is cognizable in the state court. Yet it was held that the true criterion to test a contract, whether maritime or otherwise, is the nature and the subject matter.

1. In re Chateaugay Ore & Iron Co., 128 U.S. 544.

2. St. Louis, I. M. & S. R. Co. v. Vickers, 122 U.S. 360.

5. Kirby v. Lake Shore & M. S. R. Co., 120 U.S. 150.

6. Broderick’s Will, 21 Wall. 503; United States v. Wilson, 118 U.S. 86.

7. The Hine v. Trevor, 4 Wall. 555.

8. Home Ins. Co. v. Northwestern Packet Co., 32 Iowa, 223.

CONCURRENT,

matter of the contract, having reference to maritime service or maritime transactions; but this does not have the effect to determine the question of the concurrent jurisdiction of federal and state courts. Thus it is held that with respect to contracts of affreightment between citizens of a state where a claim arises for damages for a breach of contract, it is a maritime contract, within the jurisdiction of the admiralty courts, yet such jurisdiction is not exclusive of the state courts. So a contract of insurance is a maritime contract, though proceedings to enforce it are not exclusively within the jurisdiction of the admiralty court. And a contract to transport a passenger from New York to San Francisco is such.

Sec. 99. Subject continued — Attachment of owner’s interest in vessel. — While the states cannot proceed in rem against a vessel, yet the court may in a proper case proceed by attachment and have the vessel seized and the interest liable for the debt or claim sold. In the language of the federal court, “the attaching creditor, if he succeeds in his suit, obtains recourse against the thing attached, just so far as his defendant had interest in it and no farther. The rights of third persons remain unaffected. The bottomry creditor, residing it may be in a foreign country, loses none of his rights, not being a party. His contract was with the thing, not with the owner, and it is therefore not embarrassed and cannot be by any question or contest of ownership. So, too, seamen. Whoever owns the vessel, or how often soever the ownership may be changed, wherever she may go, whatever may befall her, so long as a plank remains in her hull, the seamen are her first creditors, and she is privileged to them for their wages.” Hence it was held, where a vessel had been seized under a process of attachment in a state court, and was in actual custody of the sheriff, that no valid seizure could be made of her so as to give jurisdiction of proceedings in rem in the court of admiralty; and where the vessel was sold by the sheriff pursuant to such pro-

2 McKinley v. Morrish, 32 Iowa, 223; Home Ins Co. v. Northwestern Packet Co., 82 Iowa, 223; Morewood v. Euquist, 21 How. 343.

3 New England Marine Ins. Co. v. Dunham, 11 Wall. 1.

4 The Moses Taylor v. Hammond, 4 Wall. 411.

<br>

### --- Page 0163 ---

8 JURISDICTION. [§ 100.

Proceedings and possession, and also sold by the marshal under proceedings in rem, it was held that the title was in the purchaser under the attachment proceedings, and this upon the ground that the vessel was in the possession of a court of competent jurisdiction with which no other court could interfere, and that while such possession continued, the admiralty court could not get jurisdiction, because that could only follow the actual seizure of the vessel. Yet the proceedings against the vessel, the thing, the subject of the property, may still go on in admiralty. The sheriff's vendee of the ship may intervene there.

Sec. 100. Subject continued — Actions to enforce liens and for damages.— Where the cause of action is not a maritime tort, the courts of admiralty have no jurisdiction, and the state courts may proceed to enforce statutory liens authorized in such cases by a proceeding in personam, with an attachment to enforce the lien. Hence it was held that damages caused to property on land by fire communicated by a vessel not being a maritime tort, the remedy for the injury belonged to the courts of common law. Also that the remedy for damage to a building on land and contents, caused by negligent handling of a vessel, belonged to the courts of common law, and was not a subject of jurisdiction in admiralty. Where, however, the cause of action was a loss, by the burning of a vessel, of goods carried by her, this was held a subject of admiralty jurisdiction; the same where loss occurred by collision at sea, as well as where a suit was brought in rem to enforce an alleged lien for wharfage; while a contract for building a ship or supplying engines, timber or other materials for her construction is not a maritime contract. Nor was there a maritime lien for repairs and supplies furnished a vessel at her home port, prior

2 Same case. Ex parte Gordon, 104 U.S. 515.
3 The Plymouth, 3 Wall. 20; Ex parte Easton, 95 U.S. 68; Ex parte Phoenix Ins. Co., 118 U.S. 610. 8 Roach v. Chapman, 23 How. 129;
4 Johnson v. Pacific Elevator Co., 119 U.S. 388, 393.
5 People’s Ferry Co. v. Beers, 20 How.
