§ 101. CONCURRENT.

to the change in the twelfth admiralty rule; nor has the admiralty court jurisdiction to decree the sale of a ship on an unpaid mortgage.

Sec. 101. Criminal jurisdiction of federal courts.—Section 711 of the federal statutes provides that “the jurisdiction vested in the cases and proceedings hereinafter mentioned shall be exclusive of the courts of the several states: (1) Of all crimes and offenses cognizable under the authority of the United States. (2) In all suits for penalties, etc. (3) In all civil cases in admiralty. (4) Seizures under the laws of the United States. (5) Cases arising under the patent laws. (6) Of all cases arising in bankruptcy, and two other classes. This is substantially the law as it was enacted prior to the revision of 1878 with respect to the general clause that the jurisdiction shall be exclusive of the courts of the several states. The sixth clause, relating to cases arising in bankruptcy, was enacted March 2, 1867.

Under the general title of crimes, which declares statutory offenses, section 5328 thereof provides that “nothing in this title shall be held to take away or impair the jurisdiction of the several states under the laws thereof.” The federal courts have no common-law jurisdiction in criminal cases. The course of reasoning which leads to this conclusion is simple. The powers of the general government are made up from concessions from the several states. Whatever is not given to the former the latter expressly reserve. The judicial power of the United States is a constituent part of these concessions. That power is to be exercised by courts organized for that purpose, and brought into existence by an effort of the legislative power of the Union.

Of all the courts which the United States may under their powers constitute, only one, the supreme court, possesses jurisdiction derived immediately from the constitution, and of which the legislative power cannot deprive it. All other courts created by the general government possess no jurisdiction but what is given by the power that creates them, and can be vested with none but what the power ceded to the general government.

Rodd v. Heartt, 21 Wall. 558, 899; Schuchardt v. Babbage, 19 How.

Lottawanna, The, 21 Wall. 558, 239.

Bogart v. The John Jay, 17 How.

<br>

### --- Page 0165 ---

so JURISDICTION, [$ 102.

will authorize them to confer.” Again, the power to make municipal regulations for the restraint and punishment of crime, for the preservation of the health and morals of her citizens and the public peace, has never been surrendered by the states or restrained by the constitution of the United States. Every state has the power of defending officers and of punishing offenders against the laws.”

Sec. 102. Criminal jurisdiction of federal courts, continued.— The question is thus presented whether the statute, section 711, has the effect to deprive the several states of the power to punish offenders against their laws, where the general government has also declared by statute the same act to be an offense against its laws and prescribed a punishment therefor. It is not, strictly speaking, a question of exclusive jurisdiction in the federal courts, because they do not seek to assume jurisdiction to punish for offenses against the laws of a state; nor is it a question of concurrent jurisdiction, for the same reason. This question, but not exactly in the same form, has been presented to the federal courts several times; first, in For v. The State of Ohio, 5 How. 416. There the state courts had assumed jurisdiction of an action where the defendant was charged with passing counterfeit coin in the similitude of that of the United States. It was said “the punishment of a cheat or misdemeanor, practiced within a state, and against those whom she is bound to protect, is peculiarly and appropriately within her functions and duties, and it is difficult to imagine an interference with those duties and functions which would be regular and justifiable.” It was insisted that the statute of Ohio was repugnant to the fifth and sixth clauses of the eighth section of the first article of the constitution, which invest congress with the power to coin money and regulate the value thereof and of foreign coin, and to provide for the punishment of counterfeiting the current coin of the United States, contending that these clauses embrace not only what their language directly imports, but all other offenses which may be denominated offenses against the coin itself. On the

¹ United States v. Hudson & Goodwin, 7 Cranch, 32; Wheaton v. Peters, 8 Pet. 591, 658; Tennessee v. Davis, 100 U.S. 257.  
² Moore v. The People, 14 How. 13.

<br>

### --- Page 0166 ---

§ 103. CONCURRENT. 

On the other hand, the contention was that if any act of Congress should be construed as asserting the meaning as above stated in the Constitution, and as claiming for it the power contended for, it would not be a law passed in pursuance of the Constitution nor one deriving its authority regularly from that instrument. The court rejected the contention made on the part of the plaintiff in error. To the argument that as a result of such a conclusion a double punishment for the same act might be inflicted — one by the state and the other by the federal government — and which would be a violation of the fifth article of the amendment of the Constitution, it was said: “The prohibitions alluded to, as contained in the amendments to the Constitution, as well as others with which it is associated in these articles, were not designed as limits upon the state governments in reference to their own citizens. They are exclusively restrictions upon federal power, intended to prevent interference with the rights of the states and of their citizens.”

Sec. 103. Criminal jurisdiction of federal courts, continued. — In United States v. Marigold, 9 Mow. 261, it was said the stress of the argument of that case (Hox v. State, supra) was to show that the right of the state to punish for that cheat had not been taken from her by the express terms nor by any necessary implication of the Constitution. It was held that under the provision of the Constitution conferring upon Congress the power to coin money and to punish the offense of counterfeiting the same, it may also provide for the punishment of the offense of bringing into the United States from a foreign place forged, false and counterfeit coins, made in the similitude of the coins of the United States, and also punishment for the offense of uttering and passing the same. The court declared the doctrine announced in the case of Jor v. Ntute, supra, as sound in substance, that the act might constitute an offense against two sovereignties, punishable by each. In Moore v. The People, 14 How. 13, the question was as to the power of the state to enact laws which punish any person who shall harbor or secrete a fugitive negro slave, or which unlawfully prevent the owner thereof from arresting him, it being contended that such an act was in conflict with the federal Constitution and laws of Congress upon the same subject.

<br>

### --- Page 0167 ---

§2 JURISDICTION, [$ lod.

ject passed in pursuance thereof. It was held that such power existed in the state and was not in conflict with the constitution or laws of congress. It was said: “Every citizen of the United States is also a citizen of a state or territory. Te may be said to owe allegiance to two sovereigns, and may be liable for an infraction of the laws of either. The same act may be an offense or transgression of the laws of both. Thus, an assault upon the marshal of the United States and hindering him in the execution of a legal process is a high offense against the United States, for which the perpetrator is liable to punishment; and the same act may also be a breach of the peace of the state—a riot, assault or murder,—and subject the same person to a punishment under the state laws for a misdemeanor or felony.”

Sec. 104. Criminal jurisdiction of the federal courts, continued.— Again, in United States v. Cruikshank, 92 U. S. 542, it was said: “The people of the United States resident within any state are subject to two governments—one state, the other national; but there need be no conflict between the two. The powers which the one possesses the others do not. True, it may sometimes happen that a person is amenable to both jurisdictions for one and the same act.” After using the illustration of an assault upon a marshal and passing counterfeit coin, the court proceeds to say: “This does not, however, imply that the two governments possess powers in common or bring them into conflict with each other. It is the natural consequence of a citizenship which owes allegiance to two sovereigns and claims protection from both. He owes allegiance to two departments, so to speak, and within their respective spheres must pay the penalties which each exacts for the disobedience of its laws.” Again, in Cross v. North Carolina, 132 U.S., on page 139, it was said, in reply to the contention that the forging of a note payable at a national bank was exclusively an offense cognizable by the federal court: “Its (the federal government) legislature does not assume to restrict the authority which the states have always exercised, of punishing, in their own tribunals, the crime of forging promissory notes and other commercial securities executed by private persons and used for the purpose of private business. The forgery

<br>

### --- Page 0168 ---

§ 104.) CONCURRENT. 83

of such instruments is nonetheless injurious to the welfare of the people of the state because they happen to be made payable through or at a banking association which came into existence under the authority of the United States. The forgery may have been committed in order that the instrument forged might thereafter become the basis of false entries upon the books of the bank. But that circumstance cannot defeat the authority of the state, charged with the duty of protecting its own citizens, from punishing the forgery as in itself a distinct, separate offense, committed within its limits and against its laws. I have thus quoted at length from the expressions of the federal court for the purpose of demonstrating that in all the cases the idea has been paramount that the federal government has not the power to restrict the states in the exercise of their acknowledged right to punish offenders against their laws; that the jurisdictions of the state and federal authorities are entirely distinct and separate; that the former is invoked upon the ground of the exercise of the police power of the state, for the benefit and in behalf of the integrity of the state, the other for the integrity of the national government, and in some cases as well for the mere violation of the provisions of a federal statute. The former punishes for a crime against the people, the latter for an offense against the government. The former does not seek to enforce or vindicate the federal statute, but its own. The latter does not seek to enforce or vindicate the laws of the state, but its own. The jurisdictions are distinct and separate, and in no manner concurrent, though each is exacting penalties for the same act. The very conclusion thus reached answers incontrovertibly in the negative that other question involved, whether it was the intent of congress, in inserting the clause as to exclusive jurisdiction, to include more than that the federal court should exercise exclusive jurisdiction over the offense against the federal government. The very language is that such jurisdiction vested in the courts of the United States shall be exclusive of the courts of the several states: First. Of all crimes and offenses cognizable under the authority of the United States, which means that the state courts shall not exercise jurisdiction in such cases. It does not express or mean that the state courts cannot exercise jurisdiction of all offenses cognizable under the authority of the state.

<br>

### --- Page 0169 ---

St JURISDICTION, [§ 105.

if it happen that the same act constitutes an offense against each. If the doctrine of the federal court was that the same act can constitute but one offense, then it would be plausible to say the federal court had exclusive jurisdiction of that act, and consequently of the offense; but where the doctrine is that the act may constitute two offenses, one national and the other state in character, it necessarily follows that it was intended by the act of congress that the exclusive jurisdiction to punish for the offense was limited, not only by the powers of the government, but by the evident intent, to the offense against the government.

Sec. 105. Criminal jurisdiction of federal courts, continued — Embezzlement from national banks.—Thus far, from the authorities cited and the application of well-established principles, it would seem that our position was impregnable, and it would be so were it not for the conclusion reached by some courts, that the federal courts have exclusive jurisdiction of the offense of embezzlement by an officer or servant thereof from a national bank. The judiciary act of 1789 provided that the circuit court should have exclusive cognizance of all crimes and offenses and misdemeanors cognizable under the authority of the United States, except where this act otherwise provides or the laws of the United States otherwise direct. The act related entirely to the jurisdiction of the circuit courts, and it finally was amended so as to give the district court jurisdiction of certain offenses. Thereafter the district court was given jurisdiction of certain offenses and proceedings arising under the bankrupt act. The provision which is now 5328 of the Revised Statutes, under title 70, Of Crimes, chapter 1, General Provisions, provides that “nothing in this title shall be held to take away or impair the jurisdiction of the courts of the several states under the laws thereof.” The national banking act was enacted in 1863 and 1864. In the revision of the statutes afterwards, the offenses of embezzlement from and falsifying records of a national bank were placed in the chapter relating to crimes, and the provision heretofore stated, section 5328, was retained. The language of the act of 1789 was somewhat changed in the order of words, and numbered section 711, Other changes are

1 People v. Fonda, 62 Mich. 401; Commonwealth v. Felton, 101 Mass, 204

<br>

### --- Page 0170 ---

§ 106. CONCURRENT. 85

That section 711 is made general as to all courts, and uses the term “exclusive of the courts of the several states,” which is inserted in place of the expression “exclusive cognizance of all crimes,” etc.; and the proviso in the act of 1789, “except where this act otherwise provides, or the laws of the United States otherwise direct,” is dropped. Section 563 of the Revised Statutes confers jurisdiction upon the district court, in effect, of all crimes and offenses cognizable under the authority of the United States, committed within their respective districts or upon the high seas, the punishment of which is not capital, except in the cases mentioned in section 5412, title Crimes; and section 629 confers upon the circuit courts, in effect, “exclusive cognizance of all crimes and offenses cognizable under the authority of the United States, except where it is or may be otherwise provided by law, and concurrent jurisdiction with the district courts of crimes and offenses cognizable therein.”

It will thus be observed that it was no longer necessary (if it ever was) to retain, as part of section 711, the proviso omitted. That section applied to both courts and their jurisdiction specially defined. The interpolation of words, “of the courts of the several states,” did not add to the meaning of the law. It more concisely expressed it. It was exclusive before. It is therefore self-evident that the law as expressed in the revision of 1878 did not have nor was it intended to have any effect with respect to any change of jurisdiction on the part of the state courts, where such jurisdiction existed prior to such revision, and therefore section 5328 had the same force, effect and application after the revision as before. In New York v. Eno, 155 U.S. 89, the Supreme Court of the United States, in making a statement of the several provisions of law applicable to the crime of embezzlement from a national bank, inserts section 5328, as applicable in some manner to the propositions involved.

Sec. 106. Subject continued.— That no change was made in the law by the revision is exemplified by what was said in Cross v. North Carolina, supra, decided in 1889. The doctrine that an act may be an offense against two sovereignties is recognized and reiterated. It was there said: “If the punishment by the state of the crime of forgery of which the defendants were found guilty leaves them exposed to punishment by the United

<br>

### --- Page 0171 ---

86 JURISDICTION. [§ 166.

States for having made false entries upon the books of the bank of which they were officers, with the intent to deceive the agent appointed by the general government to examine its affairs, it results from the fact that they are amenable to the laws of the United States as well as of the state of North Carolina, and may be subjected to punishment for violating the laws of each government.” Again it was stated: “If it were competent for congress to give exclusive jurisdiction to the courts of the United States of the crime of falsely making or forging promissory notes, purporting to be executed by individuals and made payable to or at a national bank, or of the crime of uttering or publishing as true any such falsely-made or forged notes, it has not done so.” It will be further noticed that the provisions of section 711 apply as well to all matters and proceedings in bankruptcy proceedings as to crimes. The question was presented to the supreme court of the United States in 1876, whether the language of that section excluded action on the part of the state court. It was said “it is a mistake to suppose that the bankrupt law avoids of its own force all judicial proceedings in the state or other courts the instant one of the parties is adjudged a bankrupt. There is nothing in the act which sanctions such a proposition.” Again, “the debtor of a bankrupt, or the man who contests the right to real or personal property with him, loses none of those rights by the bankruptcy of his adversary. The same courts remain open to him in such contests, and the statute has not divested those courts of jurisdiction in such actions. If it has for certain classes of actions conferred a jurisdiction for the benefit of an assignee in the circuit courts of the United States, it is concurrent with and does not divest that of the state courts.” This case is also authority for the position that the state courts can exercise concurrent jurisdiction with the federal courts in cases arising under the constitution, laws and treaties of the United States, subject to the qualification that where a right arises under a law of the United States, congress may, if it see fit, give to the federal courts exclusive jurisdiction. The most that can be claimed with respect to the purpose of the change made by the revision was to take away from the state courts their concurrent jurisdiction.

¹Claflin v. Houseman, 93 U. S. 130, 134, citing many cases,

<br>

### --- Page 0172 ---

§ 107. Subject continued.— To complete the discussion it only remains to consider whether the act of embezzlement is such an offense or crime against the people of a state that, in the exercise of the police power, the state can define it to be a crime and declare the punishment for the commission thereof. Embezzlement is a crime unknown to the common law. It depends entirely upon statutory enactments and is sometimes called a statutory larceny. It is not an infamous crime within the meaning of the fifth amendment of the constitution, and hence may be tried without the intervention of a grand jury. Statutes relating thereto, both in this country and in England, had their origin in a design to supply a defect which was found to exist in the criminal law. The act of embezzlement has been made a crime in practically all of the states, and punishment prescribed, and the power of state legislatures to so enact has never been questioned. It certainly would not be questioned but that, in the absence of legislation on the part of the general government declaring embezzlement from a national bank a crime, the language of the state statutes in respect to declaring embezzlement a crime would include the act of embezzlement from a national bank. If this be conceded, then the question is simply whether, after the states have so declared, congress has the power to nullify those statutes and arrogate to itself jurisdiction to punish such an offense, where the party injured derives its corporate power or existence solely from the federal government. It must be conceded that the public interests are as much concerned in the punishment of offenders against such institutions as those against state institutions or private individuals. As well stated by Justice Harlan in delivering the opinion in Cross v. North Carolina, 132 U.S. 151-139, speaking with reference to notes forged by an officer of a national bank, and payable there: “The forgery of such instruments is none the less injurious to the welfare of the people of the state because they happen to be made payable to or at national banking associations which come into existence under the authority of the United States. The forgery.

TState v. Mason, 108 Ind. 48 Commonwealth v. Ketner, 62 Pa. St.

Uited States v. Reilly, 20 Fed. 372, 37 Am. Rep. 692,

Rep. 46; State v. Fuller, 34 Conn. 280; Commonwealth v. Hays, 80 Mass. 63.

<br>

### --- Page 0173 ---

83 JURISDICTION, [§ 107.

may have been committed in order that the instrument forged might thereafter become the basis of false entries upon the books of the bank. But that circumstance cannot defeat the authority of the state, charged with the duty of protecting its own citizens, from punishing the forgery as in itself a distinct, separate offense, committed within its limits and against its laws.” As well might it be urged that the general government could arrogate to itself the exclusive jurisdiction to try and punish an offender who should murder an officer of a national bank or of a railroad corporation deriving authority from that government, or an offender who should burglarize the vaults of any such corporation. Such offenses are none the less crimes against the laws of the state because the subject injured derives its corporate existence from federal sources, and no reason can be assigned why the offense of embezzlement should be made an exception, unless such offense is a fraud against the United States as such. The power of congress to declare the jurisdiction of the federal courts exclusive of crimes against the laws of a state, or to prohibit the state from enacting laws making acts that are essentially criminal, and which interrupt the peace and good order of her citizens, offenses against the state, may well be doubted. This question has been ably discussed in many cases, and the conclusion reached that no such grant of power was made by the constitution, but that the state courts, except in certain cases, retained their original jurisdiction, except as by that instrument made concurrent.
It has been shown that the act of forgery of a note payable at a national bank was not an offense under section 5418 of the federal statutes, upon the ground that the act was not committed for the purpose of defrauding the United States, but rather constituted a fraud upon the bank or its stockholders. It follows that if an act of embezzlement from such a bank is no more than a fraud upon the bank or its stockholders, certainly it is within the power of a state to provide for the punishment of such an offense, independent of what the federal government may provide as a punishment for violation of its statute. The doctrine so strenuously maintained by the supreme court of the

1 Teall v. Felton, 1 Comst. 537; Delafield v. Illinois, 2 Hill, 159; Teall v. Felton, 12 How. (U.S.) 136.
2 Cross v. North Carolina, 132 U. S. 131.

<br>

### --- Page 0174 ---

§ 107.] CONCURRENT. 39

United States, that the same criminal act may constitute a distinct offense against two sovereignties, and the guilty offender may be twice punished for the criminal act, was forced from the peculiar complex system of our form of government. The effect of such doctrine is abhorrent to all lovers of law and justice, and perhaps the only effect it was intended the declaration of it should have, was to settle a vexed question and prevent conflict between national and state authority. It was not intended, except in severe cases, that the double punishment would be enacted. It was said in Fux v. State, 5 Wow. 450: “It is almost certain that in the benignant spirit in which the institutions, both of state and federal systems, are administered, an offender who should have suffered the penalties denounced by the one would not be subjected a second time to punishment by the other, for acts essentially the same, unless, indeed, this might occur in instances of peculiar enormity, or where the public safety demanded extraordinary rigor.” And in another case it was said, as a reason for not assuming jurisdiction by a state court, “there would be the possible danger of subjecting an offender to double punishment, an enormity which no court would permit if it had the power to prevent it.” It ought to be mentioned here that the United States Supreme Court on two occasions have had the question discussed squarely before it and failed to decide it. In both cases the question arose upon habeas corpus, the former from Michigan, the latter from New York. In each the state authorities were prosecuting for embezzlement from national banks. The question presented in each case was that of the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts. In each the court refused to determine the question until after the highest court of the state had passed upon it. It was conceded the Supreme Court might, in the exercise of its discretion, determine the question in advance of action by the courts of last resort of the state. This is sufficient at least to warrant the impression that the doctrine is not settled in that court. In other cases they have, in advance of a trial, issued a writ of prohibition restraining admiralty courts from proceeding where the tort was not maritime in character, thus

1 Commonwealth v. Kitner, 92 Pa. 21 In Ex parte Fonda, 117 U.S. 516; St. 372. New York v. Eno, 155 U.S. 89.

<br>

### --- Page 0175 ---

90 JURISDICTION. [$§ 10%a, 1070.

determining in advance the question of the jurisdiction of the court over the subject-matter.

Src. 107a. Perjury.— The federal courts have exclusive jurisdiction to try and punish for perjury committed in proceedings before federal tribunals and officers. It is a crime against one sovereignty only; hence the state courts have no jurisdiction of such a crime committed in an examination before a commissioner under the United States bankrupt act; nor where committed before a commissioner of the circuit court of the United States; nor where committed in making an affidavit under the act of congress relating to the sale of the public lands. An act of congress designating certain officers or persons before whom depositions may be taken in contested election cases of members of the national house of representatives includes among others state judges, mayors, recorders of cities and notaries public. It was held that such officers, though appointed by or elected in the state, perform functions not under any authority derived from the state, but solely under the authority conferred upon them by congress, and in a matter concerning the government of the United States, and perjury in giving such testimony is punishable in the courts of the United States to the exclusion of the state courts. State courts have jurisdiction of perjury committed in a proceeding for naturalization before a court of the state under authority of congress. Such a proceeding is a judicial proceeding in a state court.

Sec. 1070. Illegal voting for federal officers.— The courts of the state, however, have jurisdiction of an indictment for illegal voting for electors of president and vice-president of the United States, and a person sentenced to imprisonment upon such an indictment cannot be discharged by writ of habeas corpus, although the indictment and sentence include illegal voting for a representative in congress. If it be conceded that the state

1 Ex parte Phenix Ins. Co. 118 U.S. 610. 2 People v. Kelley. 88 Cal. 145; State v. Kirkpatrick 
3 State v. Pike, 15 N. H. 83; In re Loney, 134 U.S. 372. 
4 In re Loney, 134 U.S. 372. 5 In re Loney, 134 U.S. 2; Rump v. Commonwealth, 30 Pa. St. 475; 
6 State v. Whittemore, 50 N. H. 245. 7 State v. Adams, 4 Tlackf. 146;

<br>

### --- Page 0176 ---

§ loth] CONCURRENT.

The court has not concurrent jurisdiction with the federal courts to punish fraudulent voting for representatives in congress (a question not determined, though the court state “may be it has”). It having such power in regard to votes for presidential electors, the including in one indictment and sentence of illegal voting both for a representative in congress and for presidential electors does not go to the jurisdiction of the state court, but is at most mere error, which cannot be inquired into by writ of habeas corpus.

Note TO CHAPTER 4

Jurisdiction of the separate departments of government over each other.— The constitution of the United States has restricted or limited the powers of the executive, legislative and judicial branches of government, and, as far as consistent with the great principles upon which the government is founded and carrying them into effect, each such department was intended to be independent of the others. This important question is to some extent referred to in a subsequent chapter. (Ch. 28, Mandamus.) The principal question which concerns us in the preparation of this work is the power of the judiciary to control or direct the other departments of government or officers thereof with respect to matters which are within the scope of the duties properly assigned to such departments. The character of the proceedings, where any may be resorted to, is such as is appropriate to compel action on the part of officers of such departments to confine them within the limit of their powers, and usually is by mandamus, and under that general head the question has been treated. The question of jurisdiction of the legislative department over the judicial department is incidentally discussed throughout the work where the question has been presented. The general rule is that congress cannot interfere with the express jurisdiction of the federal courts conferred by the constitution, nor can the legislature of a state interfere with such jurisdiction of the state courts. The whole question is discussed at length in the celebrated case of Marbury v. Madison, 1 Cranch, 137. The administrative department may regulate within certain limits the manner or method of procedure, but in no manner can it restrict the judicial department in the exercise of judicial functions. It is emphatically the province and duty of the judicial department to say what the law is. If an act of congress or of a legislature is void, as contravening constitutional provisions, the courts are in nowise bound to give it effect, but it is essentially their duty to declare it inoperative and void. To this extent, at least, the judicial may control the acts of the administrative department. So, if two laws conflict with each other, the courts must decide on the operation of each. The administrative department cannot assume to exercise judicial functions—for instance, granting a new trial. Lawson v. Jeffries, 47 Miss. 686. Yet they are not prohibited from exercising duties that to some extent require the exercise of functions in their nature judicial as distinguished from those that are ministerial.

VIn re Green, 134 U. S. 377.

<br>

CHAPTER 5.

JUDGMENTS.

Sec.
108. Classification of.

DOMESTIC JUDGMENTS OF SUPERIOR COURTS.

109. Presumption in favor of jurisdiction.

110. Rule illustrated.

111. Exception where record shows defendant lived beyond territorial limits.

112. Superior courts in the exercise of special jurisdiction.

113. Rule in such cases.

114. Doctrine of the California court.

Sec.

115. Subsequent proceedings — Rule.

116. Effect of record where it discloses a want of strict conformity to the requirements of the statute — Rule in federal courts.

117. Rule in state courts.

118. Summary proceedings in courts of general jurisdiction — Rule.

119. Subjects and proceedings that are summary.

Sec. 108. Judgments — Classification of.— For the purpose of logical treatment of the subject and the proper application of legal rules, it becomes necessary to classify judgments under appropriate heads as follows:

I. Domestic Judgments.

II. Foreign Judgments.

III. Judgments of Sister States.

IV. Judgments in Rem.

V. Judgments of Divorce.

And again to sub-classify those of the first class:

1. Those rendered by a constitutional court having original inherent jurisdiction.

2. Those rendered by such a court in the exercise of a statutory or power specially conferred.

3. Those rendered in proceedings summary in character.

4. Those rendered by a court of limited jurisdiction.

5. Those rendered by an inferior court.

Domestic Judgments of Superior Courts.

Sec. 109. Presumptions in favor of jurisdiction.— The general presumption of law which attaches to domestic judgments

<br>

### --- Page 0178 ---

Ys 110, 111.] JUDGMENTS, 93

of superior courts is that they have properly acquired jurisdiction of the person, proceeded regularly to judgment, and that the judgment was authorized in the law. The rule has been aptly stated by a learned court as follows: “A superior court of general jurisdiction, proceeding within the general scope of its powers, is presumed to have jurisdiction to give the judgment it renders until the contrary appears, and this presumption embraces jurisdiction not only of the cause or subject-matter of the action in which the judgment is given, but of the parties also. This presumption only arises with respect to jurisdictional facts concerning which the record is silent, and is limited to jurisdiction over persons within their territorial limits and over proceedings which are in accordance with the common law.” It is not necessary that the facts or evidence conferring jurisdiction should be set out in the record.

Sec. 110. Rule illustrated.— The application of the rule is well illustrated in the case of Zunger v. Barlow, 89 Iowa, 539. The plaintiff brought an action in equity to quiet title to certain real estate. The defendant claimed title by virtue of a decree in foreclosure of a tax deed. The records of the proceedings in that case had been lost or destroyed, save the decree itself, which was offered in evidence. It was held that it would be presumed that the proceedings were regular and that the court obtained jurisdiction over the persons and subject-matter. When it appears by the record that the defendants were served with process, the presumption will be, if the record is otherwise silent, that service was had within the territorial limits of the court, and if it should appear that service was had without such territorial limits, there being nothing in the record negativing an appearance, the presumption will attach that the defendant either appeared or did some act by which jurisdiction was conferred.

Sec. 111. Exception where record shows defendant lived beyond territorial limits.— There are cases which hold that

Galpin v. Page, 18 Wall. 350. Shumway v. Stillman, 4 Cowen, 204; Hale v. First National Bank, 50 Iowa, 612.  
Boker v. Chapline et al., 12 Iowa, 292.

<br>

### --- Page 0179 ---

OL JURISDICTION, [$ 112.

When it appears from the record that at the time of the alleged service the defendant lived beyond the territorial limits of the court, the presumption that the parties had appeared or conferred jurisdiction upon the court ceased, and that the burden of proving such facts was upon the party relying upon the judgment. It was held, where a judgment was taken pro confesso, that the record must affirmatively show service within the territorial limits of the court. So the general presumption in favor of such judgments will be overthrown when the record of the entire case discloses that there was no service of process, or no jurisdiction existing or acquired. The question of the effect of such presumptions, when conclusive and when may be overcome, is fully treated in subsequent chapters. See IMPERFECTION OF JUDGMENTS; COLLATERAL ATTACK.

Sec. 112. Superior courts in the exercise of special jurisdiction.— It is often urged that where a statute confers upon a court of general jurisdiction special or summary powers, wholly derived therefrom, which are not within the inherent jurisdiction of the court, or within its general powers as constituted, then its orders in respect to such matters are subject to the same rule in testing their validity as is applicable to courts of inferior or limited jurisdiction, and therefore every step essential to authorize it to make the order or judgment must appear upon the face of the record which the court makes of the transaction, and that everything will be presumed to be without the jurisdiction which does not distinctly appear to be within it. The jurisdiction which is now exercised by the common-law courts in this country is in a very large proportion dependent upon special statutes conferring it. Many of these statutes create for the first time the rights which the courts are called upon to enforce, and many of them prescribe with minuteness the mode in which these rights are to be pursued in the courts. Many of the powers thus granted to the courts are not only at variance with the common law, but often in derogation of that law.

¹ Gray v. Larimore, 22 Abb. (N.S.) 542.  
² Boker v. Chapline, 12 Iowa, 204.  
³ Gray v. Hawes et al., 8 Cal. 562.  
⁴ Allen v. Blunt, 1 Blatch. 480.  
⁵ Morse v. Presby, 5 Foster, 302.

<br>

### --- Page 0180 ---

§ 113.] JUDGMENTS. 95

Note To Sec. 112.— When the law confers upon courts an authority not after the course of the common law over property, whose owners are required to be before them as adversaries, they act as tribunals of special and limited jurisdiction. It is necessary that such tribunals show they act within the scope of their powers. But after jurisdiction is once acquired, however irregular or erroneous other proceedings may be, they cannot be collaterally impeached, and they conclude all persons unless annulled by certiorari or appeal. Adams v. Jeffries, 12 Ohio, 272, When the remedy pursued is given by statute, as in case of proceedings to charge the lands of a debtor with the payment of the judgment of a justice of the peace, it is necessary to follow the course which it prescribes in every material point. It is a general rule that every record must present a case apparently within the jurisdiction of the court, and when the jurisdiction is specially given by statute, and is to be resorted to only on the occurrence of particular facts, those facts must be shown; for however correct it may be to presume jurisdiction, when the want of it does not appear, in cases within the general jurisdiction of the court, yet when the jurisdiction is created by statute, and limited to particular cases, of which the court could not take cognizance without the statute, the jurisdiction cannot be presumed. If the facts on which it is made to depend are not averred, the party does not bring himself within the jurisdiction, and he cannot resort to the statute for aid because his record does not contain the case provided by the statute. Edmiston v. Edmiston, 2 Ohio, 251.

Sec. 113. Rule in such cases.— Many such statutes merely provide, as conditions precedent to the exercise of jurisdiction by such courts, certain preliminary steps or proceedings, and thereafter, either by inference or in express terms, the court is authorized to proceed in the ordinary manner to determine the matter in controversy. The rule has been very tersely stated to be that, “when the special powers conferred are brought into action, according to the course of the common law and chancery proceedings, by regular process and personal service where a personal judgment or decree is asked, or by seizure on attachment of the property where a judgment in rem is sought, the same presumption of jurisdiction will usually attend the judgment of the court as in cases falling within its general powers; but where the special powers conferred are exercised in a special manner, not according to the course of the common law, or where the general powers of the court are exercised over a class not within its ordinary jurisdiction, upon the performance of prescribed conditions, no such presumption of jurisdiction will usually attend the judgment of the court as in

<br>

### --- Page 0181 ---

96 JURISDICTION. ($$ 114, 115.

cases falling within its general powers. The facts essential to the exercise of jurisdiction must appear in such cases upon the record. When a power is given to a court of general jurisdiction over a special subject, which is not in the usual course of the common law, and a mode is prescribed for its exercise, such mode must be pursued, and sufficient must appear to show the case to be within the jurisdiction of the tribunal. If upon the face of the record sufficient appears to give the court jurisdiction, under the statute conferring the power, then the same presumption in favor of the validity of its proceedings attaches as when proceeding in the exercise of its general powers.

Sec. 114. Doctrine of the California court.— The rule in California was that the presumption of law in favor of the jurisdiction of courts of general jurisdiction and the regularity of their proceedings obtains equally, whether their proceedings be in the course of the common law or statute law, or be in the acquisition of jurisdiction of the person of a defendant by making either actual or constructive service of the summons upon him. This rule was subsequently changed in deference to the opinion of the supreme court of the United States. The doctrine of that court, however, is that, whenever a new right is created by statute, and the enforcement of that right is committed to a court of general jurisdiction, such court, quoad hoc, is an inferior court and must pursue the statute strictly.

Sec. 115. Subsequent proceedings— Rule.— The distinction sought to be maintained is between statutes relating to the remedy in enforcing a right and those statutes which confer the right itself and commit its enforcement in a specific manner to the court; and the rule generally prevailing may be said to be, with the possible exception of the federal courts, that

1 Harvey v. Tyler, 2 Wall. 328; Galpin v. Sunderland, 3 Iowa, 114; Morrow v. Lexing-ton, etc. Min. Co, 117 U.S. 255; 4 Belcher v. Chambers, 53 Cal. 685. 
2 Harvey v. Tyler, 2 Wall. 328; 5 Cohen v. Barrett et al., 5 Cal. 195; Embry v. Connor, 3 N. Y. 511; Cooper Hastings v. Cunningham, 39 Cal. 187; Hanscom v. Tower, 17 Cal. 518.

<br>

### --- Page 0182 ---

§ 116.] JUDGMENTS. 97

Where the statute prescribes certain things to be done or conclusions reached subsequent to those prescribed for the attainment of jurisdiction in the first instance, which are outside of the general course of proceedings in such courts, then the record must disclose that such subsequent proceedings were had, as well as those required, in order to support the jurisdiction of the court to render the particular judgment. Much of the discussion in the courts has been called for in the consideration of the effect of the record in proceedings where service was attempted by publication and in proceedings quasi in rem, where jurisdiction is to be acquired by some preliminary proceeding, such as the filing of an affidavit or petition, in which the subsequent proceedings are in the ordinary or general course of such courts. Generally speaking, they merely relate to the remedy, no new right or privilege being conferred, the enforcement of which is committed to the court.

Sec. 116. Effect of record—Where it discloses a want of strict conformity to the requirements of the statute.—The greatest difficulty to be met is with respect to the effect of the record when it appears therefrom that the preliminary proceeding was attempted, though it fail to show that there was a substantial compliance with the requirements of the statute either in respect to service or in the allegations contained in the affidavit, petition or other preliminary proceeding which is made the basis for calling into exercise the jurisdiction of the court. In proceedings other than those which are summary in character, the rule in the federal court seems to be that if the record discloses such preliminary proceeding to have been attempted, and that the proceeding thus taken was of such character as to call for the exercise of the judgment of the tribunal, and such judgment was exercised either directly or by proceeding to perform the act required, then it is that jurisdiction appears, though in fact such preliminary proceeding was faulty in not conforming to the requirements of the law. The act of the court in such case would be error of judgment merely.

VGrignon's Lessee v. Astor, 2 How. 319; Comstock v. Crawford, 3 Wall. 396; Moir v. Manierre, 101 U.S. 417.

<br>

### --- Page 0183 ---

93 JURISDICTION, [§§ 117-119]

Sec. 117. Rule in state courts.— In most of the state courts, however, the rule is otherwise and to the effect (with the exception in some of affidavits for the publication of summons) that the record must show a substantial compliance with all the statutory requirements that are made conditions precedent, and that when the record fails to show such conditions to have been substantially complied with in respect to statements in the preliminary proceeding of matters of substance required to be stated therein, then it is that it affirmatively appears that the judgment rendered was coram non judice. Authorities bearing upon this proposition will be found under heads, “Process,” “Attachment,” “Replevin,” and also under “Impeachment of Judgments” and “Collateral Attack.” It will there be found that the statement above is not of universal application.

Sec. 118. Summary proceedings in courts of general jurisdiction— Rule.— Confusion has again arisen in not observing a distinction between those cases where conditions are prescribed precedent to invoking the jurisdiction of courts of general powers, which thereafter proceed according to their usual course, and those cases where certain special powers are by statute imposed upon such courts, and the statute prescribes with particularity the manner in which such powers shall be exercised. Cases of the latter kind we designate summary proceedings; by which we mean to include those cases where the whole proceeding is without the ordinary course of such courts, with the exception of the method of hearing the parties. In such cases the rule is of almost general application that the record must show that the proceedings were in strict compliance with the requirements of the statute; that nothing therein required will be presumed to have been performed. With this distinction observed, most of the cases that have apparently been considered in conflict can be reconciled.

Sec. 119. Subjects and proceedings that are summary.— Proceedings in insolvency are special, and no intendment can be made in favor of the jurisdiction of the court. Everything

Bates v. Merchant, 8 Port. 99; Barry v. Patterson, 3 Humph. 312; Hamilton v. Burum, 3 Yerg. 365; Maples v. Tunis, 11 Humph. 103,

<br>

### --- Page 0184 ---

§ 120.] JUDGMENTS,

bearing upon that question must appear affirmatively. If the record fail to show publication of a required notice the requisite number of days, the proceedings are void. So are proceedings for the condemnation of land for public purposes. They are in the nature of a special inquisition, and are not to be considered by the general rules applicable to courts of general jurisdiction, but as to acts of a court sitting under a limited, special, statutory authority. So are proceedings in contested election cases, where they are of a special character and wholly distinct in form, and substantially different from the common-law remedy. The power of the court is limited by the provisions of the act, which must be strictly pursued, and the record must show such strict compliance. So are proceedings, under the statute of Maine, to recover annual compensation for the flowage of lands. So an execution by a public officer of a power to sell lands for the non-payment of taxes must be in strict conformity to the law under which it is made or no title is conveyed. It is absolutely essential that it appear upon the record that the required publications were made, and that the officer had returned that there were no goods or property of the delinquent proprietor out of which the taxes could be made.

Sec. 120, Distinction between special powers conferred and general powers extended.— Here again a distinction must be observed between conferring special powers upon a court of general jurisdiction and extending the general powers of such a court over a special matter. Thus, extending the jurisdiction of such a court over the foreclosure of tax deeds does not have the effect to make its jurisdiction special with respect to such proceedings. So the subject of foreclosure of mechanics’ liens is new, created by statute, and yet it is apprehended that this, and its process, too, is to be adjudicated upon the same rules that govern the ordinary power and proceedings of the court. An attachment proceeding, though exclusively statutory, is merely

1 McDonald v. Katz et al. 31 Cal.
2 Dorsey v. Barry, 24 Cal. 449; 167; Hanscom v. Tower, 17 Cal. 518; Saunders v. Haynes, 13 Cal.
3 Hastings v. Cunningham, 38 Cal. 137.
4 Prentiss v. Parks, 65 Me. 559.
5 Gilmer v. Lime Point, 1 Cal. 47; Thatcher v. Powell, 6 Wheat. 123.
6 Finbry v. Connors, 3 N.Y. 511; In re Grove Street, 61 Cal. 433.
7 Hunyer v. Barlow, 89 Iowa, 539.
8 Cooper v. Sunderland, 3 Iowa, LA.

### --- Page 0185 ---

100 JURISDICTION. [§ 120.

Auxiliary to other proceedings over which the jurisdiction of the court is in all cases general, and usually as antecedent to the attachment there must be an indebtedness, a cause of action, in which the jurisdiction of the court is not limited. The writ of attachment is issued in aid of that jurisdiction. It is not the object of the writ to curtail and limit that general power, but rather to augment and make it more secure. Without this auxiliary process the creditor could obtain a judgment and subsequently an execution. Therefore an attachment proceeding, after jurisdiction is obtained to seize the property, must have the same intendment in regard to the exercise of official duties as applies to any other cases of general jurisdiction.

1 Rowan v. Lamb, 4 G. Gr. (Iowa), 468; Jarvis v. Barrett, 14 Wis 591.

CHAPTER 6.

INFERIOR COURTS AND COURTS OF LIMITED JURISDICTION.

Bro. Src.

121. Presumptions with respect to jurisdiction.

122. In what manner want of jurisdiction may be shown.

123. Circuit and district courts of the United States are courts of limited but not inferior jurisdiction.

124. State courts — Certain, whether limited or inferior.

125. Probate courts — Status of.

126. Probate courts — Rule in.

128. County courts.

129. Inferior courts proper: (1) Statutory courts; (2) Courts of justices of the peace — Rule.

130. The essential facts conferring jurisdiction must appear.

131. Effect of record of subsequent proceedings.

132. Rule — Does not extend to every technical defect.

133. Rule applies to substantial requirements.

134. Controlling docket entries by extrinsic evidence.

135. Effect of such a judgment when docketed in a superior court.

Src. 121. Presumptions with respect to jurisdiction.—It will be observed as we proceed with the discussion that much confusion prevails among courts as to what courts are of limited jurisdiction with respect to the application of rules bearing upon the effect that shall be given to their judgments, whether conclusive or otherwise. It is therefore of the greatest importance that this question be settled as far as may be in advance. The general rule is, as we have seen, that every presumption not inconsistent with the record is to be indulged in favor of the jurisdiction of courts of general jurisdiction, and that their judgments, however erroneous, cannot be questioned when introduced collaterally, unless it be shown affirmatively that they had no jurisdiction of the case. Every act of such a court will be presumed to have been rightfully done until the contrary appears. Such, we say, is the general rule. The extent of its application, as well as qualifications thereof, including the effect of fraud in procuring the judg-

¹ Harvey v. Tyler, 2 Wall. 228; Voorhies v. Bank of United States, 10 Pet. 419.

<br>

### --- Page 0187 ---

102 JURISDICTION, [§§ 122, 123.]

ment, is discussed in subsequent chapters. This presumption, however, is not exercised in relation to the jurisdiction of a court inferior or limited, but its jurisdiction must appear or be shown.

Sec. 122. In what manner want of jurisdiction may be shown.—The question involved in the rule upon which the courts are not agreed is the manner in which the want of jurisdiction may be shown, and included in this is the effect that shall be given the record. In so far as domestic judgments are concerned, rendered in courts of general jurisdiction, the general rule is that the recitals in the judgment or the record cannot be impeached by extrinsic evidence only, upon direct attack, or, under the practice in most of the code states, that which is equivalent thereto, while in courts of limited jurisdiction and inferior courts the record as to jurisdiction may be prima facie only, and therefore subject to attack collaterally by extrinsic evidence, or by reason of jurisdiction not affirmatively appearing or the judgment being void upon its face.

This distinction is not recognized in some jurisdictions. See Cotwater Ral. ATTACK.

Sec. 128. Circuit and district courts of the United States are courts of limited but not inferior jurisdiction.—The courts of the United States, other than the supreme court, are in one sense courts of limited jurisdiction. In other words, while courts of record, they have no inherent powers emanating direct from the constitution. They are the creatures of congress, which alone confers and restricts their powers. The judgments of such courts have invariably been recognized as upon the same footing, so far as concerns the obligations created by them, with domestic judgments of the state courts wherever rendered and wherever sought to be enforced. They are record evidence of debt, or judgments of record, to be contested only in such way as judgments of record may be, and consequently are conclusive upon the defendant in every state, except for such causes as would be sufficient to set aside.

Embry v. Palmer, 107 U.S. 3-10, and cases cited.

Cooper v. Sunderland, 3 Iowa, 114; Denning v. Corwin, 11 Wend. 647; Roswell's Lessees v. Otis, 9 How.; Embry v. Connor, 3 Comst. 511.

<br>

### --- Page 0188 ---

§ 124. INFERIOR COURTS, ETC.

the judgment in the courts of the state in which it was rendered. The same presumption is indulged and the same effect is given to their records as is given to domestic judgments in courts of general jurisdiction. They are courts of limited but not of inferior jurisdiction.

Sec. 124. State courts — Certain, whether limited or inferior — Test.—With respect to state courts, the question here advanced has been the subject of much discussion, even to the extent that some courts have attempted to lay down an arbitrary rule or test which should determine it. Thus it has been held that the true line of distinction was between courts of record and those not of record. This subject was discussed at considerable length in Grignon's Lessee v. Astor, in reference to a decree rendered in a probate court, and the conclusion reached that its records were a verity to the same extent as those of courts of general jurisdiction. The rule declared was that all courts of record which have an original general jurisdiction over any particular subject are not courts of special or limited jurisdiction; they are not inferior courts in the technical sense of the term, because an appeal lies from their decision. That applies to courts of special and limited jurisdiction, which are created on such principles that their judgments taken alone are entirely disregarded, and their proceedings must show their jurisdiction. The New Jersey court undertakes to define what is meant by the use of the latter term. It does not predicate it upon what has previously been declared and recognized, but states simply the opinion of the judges of the court. First, they state the term is ambiguous, and again, that courts use it indiscriminately in place of special. Then the opinion is expressed that the true distinction between courts is such as possess a general and such as have only a special jurisdiction.

McEnoyle v. Cohen, 13 Pet. 312; Devaughn v. Devaughn, 10 Gratt.; Palmer, 107 U.S. 3-10; Grignon’s Lessee v. Astor, Ex’r, May, 6 Cranch, 2 How. (U.S.) 319; Beaubien v. Brink, 267; Ex parte Watkins, 3 Pet. 103; Kennedy v. Georgia State Bank, 8 Barron, 30 N. H. 78; Baldwin v. Hale, 17 Johns. Ch. 586; Chemung Bank v. Judson, 4 Seld. 254; Morse v. Presby, 5 Foster (N. H.), 299.

<br>

### --- Page 0189 ---

104 JURISDICTION, [$ 125.

for a particular purpose, or clothed with special powers for the performance of specific duties, beyond which they have no manner of authority; and these special powers to be exercised in a summary way, either by a tribunal already existing for general purposes, or by persons appointed or to be appointed in some definite form. They classify boards of commissioners and the like as such, which we have classified as inferior tribunals, and state that unless their proceedings on their face show a compliance with the directions required by the statute under which they act, it never could be known whether they acted within their jurisdiction or exceeded it. But they say the orphans’ court is not organized for a single purpose. It has a general jurisdiction as broad as the common pleas or circuit court, or perhaps the supreme court itself, exclusive of its appellate and superintending authority. . . . It confers no attribute of a special court for one purpose only, but a jurisdiction for the general administration of justice within certain departments. That its decrees are entitled to every presumption in favor of their regularity that the judgments of this court are entitled to, and such as must be considered legal till reversed by due course of law.' In California it was held that the superior court of San Francisco, though created and its power defined by the legislature, and its jurisdiction limited (though only in respect to territory and to subjects), but general in respect to matters conferred, was, in the sense which we are considering, a court of general jurisdiction? Apparently the rule thus declared by the federal court has not been acceptable to courts in many of the states, but the writer infers that, notwithstanding the verbal distinctions that have been attempted, there is in fact no substantial conflict.

Sec. 125. Probate courts — Status of.— Apparently there is much diversity of opinion in respect to the status of probate courts, sometimes designated differently. Thus in New York the surrogate court (a probate court) is held to be not an inferior court as sometimes stated, but a court of limited jurisdiction. And courts clothed with the same powers in some

Vassault v. Austin, 36 Cal. Merritt, 3 Barb. 341; People v. Hart-
Roderigas v. East River Savings man, 28 W. Rep. 576.

INFERIOR COURTS, FIC.

states have been so classed; as, in Maine, Massachusetts, Mississippi, Vermont and Wisconsin; while in Arkansas, California, Pennsylvania (by statute a court of record and consequently a superior court), Alabama, New Jersey and Texas, such courts are held to be superior. For the rule prevailing in other states and a discussion of the question of the effect of judgments and recitals, see section 180 et seq.

Sec. 126. Probate courts— Rule in.— The rule in every state, the writer concludes to be, that where such court, in the exercise of its general powers, acquires jurisdiction in the manner required by law, thereafter, as to subsequent proceedings, the presumption applies that the powers which are conferred upon the court are rightfully exercised. That therefore the presumption that is withheld from such courts is only that which is withheld from superior courts when jurisdiction is to be exercised in a special manner outside of the common law, or outside of the ordinary manner conferred, while in the exercise of its general jurisdiction. Conditions precedent are usually required to the exercise of such jurisdiction, and the rule of law is that presumptions of law will not prevail in favor of the existence of such precedent conditions, but on the contrary the record must disclose their existence properly performed, and the existence of those conditions or their proper performance is the subject of collateral attack, except in some courts, as well as in some cases where their existence has been a matter of judicial determination, and judgment has been exercised and declared in respect thereto. Such, in effect, is the declaration of the courts in New York, Iowa, New

106 JURISDICTION,

[§ 128.
Jersey, Massachusetts, Federal courts, Indiana and Maine? The rule may be somewhat different in Alabama. It appears to have been held in that state that if a probate court grants letters of administration, the presumption is indulged that it previously ascertained the existence of the necessary jurisdictional facts, and when the record asserts the jurisdictional facts the presumption is conclusive, though if the record is silent it may be that the want of jurisdiction may be shown; and in some of the states, as will appear, the same presumptions in favor of the existence of such jurisdictional facts is indulged as in other respects. See Collateral Attack. However, in respect to orphans, probate, and courts possessing similar powers, by whatever title designated, the rule is that, as their powers are purely statutory in respect to the sale of estates of decedents, the record must affirmatively show a compliance with the essential requisites of the statute, otherwise the sale is void and subject to collateral attack, and such has been held in Alabama. In Iowa a guardian’s sale was set aside for want of jurisdiction on the ground that the notice did not state the proper time when the application would be heard; yet this rule is not recognized in all jurisdictions. See Collateral Attack. When notice, however, is required and none given, the proceedings are void.

Sec. 128. County courts.— So it has been held that county courts, where not exercising probate jurisdiction, though of limited jurisdiction, are in the sense under discussion here courts of general jurisdiction, and this follows from the fact

§ 129. INFERIOR COURTS, ETC. 107  
that the power of such a court is to hear, try and determine according to law all actions local to the county where it sits and is a court of record.

Sec. 129. Inferior courts proper.—See section 183 et seq.  
(1) Statutory Courts.  
(2) Courts of Justice of the Peace.

With respect to statutory courts, the rule is that when a court is created by the legislature with particular powers expressly conferred, the record of their proceedings must affirmatively show a compliance with the statutory requirements, and that as to such matters favorable presumptions will not be indulged in; and the same rule applies to other tribunals exercising judicial powers, though not strictly courts of justice, such as commissioners of patents, commissioners of land office, municipal council in respect to proceedings authorized by statute, county commissioners in the exercise of such powers, town authorities in respect to laying roads or exercising statutory functions. The decisions of such bodies are, in respect to collateral attack, governed by the same rules as the judgments of inferior courts. Thus, an inferior board may determine conclusively its own jurisdiction or power by adjudicating the existence of facts upon which its jurisdiction or power depends; but when, however, the power depends not upon the existence or non-existence of matters en pare to be established by evidence, but upon allegations in a petition, a portion of the record, the question is not the same. In such

108 JURISDICTION. [§§ 130, 131.

case there must appear in the petition the statutory requisites.¹

Sec. 130. Essential facts conferring jurisdiction must appear.—The presumption that official duty was performed cannot be made. Hence, where the record of the common council of a city showed that a certain resolution in relation to taking property a/ invitum was adopted, and such record was silent as to the requisite number of votes cast in favor of the resolution, the charter requiring two-thirds, and no proof aliunde having been offered on that question, it was held that it could not be presumed that the resolution received the requisite two-thirds vote. Such proceedings were held void ab initio. No express adjudication that they were valid could make them so.² The rule as to judgments of inferior courts is, as we have seen, that the essential facts conferring jurisdiction must appear upon the face of the record.³

Sec. 131. Effect of record of subsequent proceedings.—The material question, in so far as it affects inferior courts proper, and especially courts of justices of the peace, is the presumption that attaches after jurisdiction is once obtained,—whether such presumption of regularity prevails with respect to subsequent steps, or whether the record must affirmatively show jurisdiction retained. Upon this question the courts of the several states are not agreed. In some states such courts are declared to be courts of record, and consequently superior courts with respect to the presumption that applies to their subsequent proceedings. Such is the rule in Vermont,⁵ Connecticut,⁶ Mississippi,⁷ Pennsylvania,⁸ Texas,⁹ and apparently Kansas.¹⁰ Though

§§ 132, 133.] INFERIOR COURTS, ETC. 109  
not classed strictly as superior courts or courts of general jurisdiction, the presumption applies that jurisdiction once appearing it continues until the contrary appears. That in this respect the rule is the same as applied to judgments of courts of general jurisdiction,* and such judgments are within the provisions of the constitution and act of congress relating to the faith and credit that shall be given to judgments of sister states;² while in others nothing can be assumed by way of intendment, and the record must affirmatively show, with respect to every step in the proceedings, that they were within a jurisdiction retained. That jurisdiction must appear, both as to person and subject matter, upon the face of their proceedings, and will not be presumed, is the rule, as we have intimated, in most of the states. There must be sufficient to show what the issue was, that it may thus appear that that which was adjudicated was within the jurisdiction of the justice.³  

§ 132. The rule does not extend to every technical defect.—The general rule, however, does not extend to every technical defect that may appear in the progress of the trial; but the rule is that every reasonable intendment from what appears to have been done should be made in support of the proceedings,⁴ but not to supply an omission. In Michigan and Iowa it was held that, where it appears that the parties were regularly brought into court, no presumption could be indulged that the justice lost jurisdiction by any misstep afterwards.⁵  

§ 133. Where statute requires an entry and none appears, no presumption that step was taken.—It is generally held, however, that when it appears from the record that any

110. JURISDICTION. [§§ 134, 135.

step was taken as to which the statute required an entry, and the record fails to disclose that such step was taken at the time and in the manner required, then no presumption will arise that it was taken at the proper time or in the proper manner. Hence, where the statute required that the justice enter upon his docket the time when and the place to which the cause is adjourned, and the record only discloses the time, no presumption arises that it was at his office. The record must state the place. So the record must show a service of the summons, and if it be silent as to service the judgment is void. While the rule is thus strict in respect to those matters which partake of the character of notice, there are other matters which the law will presume were regularly done at the proper time; among which is that the case was called at the proper time. It seems that the place or town or county at which the justice held his court need not affirmatively appear, nor at what hour he rendered his judgment, though it is essential that he render judgment immediately upon receiving the verdict, though it be a legal holiday, and this must affirmatively appear. It is held in New York, however, that the statute does not require such entry on Sunday or a legal holiday, and that the justice may perform the act on the succeeding day.

Sec. 134. Controlling docket entries by extrinsic evidence. The general rule is that parol proof is inadmissible to vary or explain a docket of a justice of the peace, so as to give him jurisdiction that does not appear upon its face.

Sec. 135. Effect of such a judgment when docketed in a superior court.— When a judgment of a justice of the peace has been docketed in a court of general jurisdiction, then it seems it is deemed to possess the elements of a judgment of such a court, in so far that the record is conclusive against collateral attack. It is generally held that judgments of such

§ 135.] INFERIOR COURTS, ETC.

courts, in those states where they are not classed as superior, are not within the act of congress providing for the certification of judgments from one state to another. The reason is that, not being courts of record, they cannot be properly authenticated. It is held, however, that they are included in the constitutional provision which requires full faith and credit to be given to the judgments of sister states.

Sec. 136. Distinction between void and voidable judgments.  
A judgment is not void in the legal sense unless its invalidity appears upon the record. It is voidable merely.  

1 Earle v. Earle, 91 Ind. 27.

### --- Page 0199 ---

114 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 137, 138.]

Sec. 137. Impeachment of judgments by the record.— It is proper to introduce the record of proceedings, wherein a judgment was the result, to impeach the judgment, whether the attack be direct or collateral. The presumption which the law implies in support of the judgments of superior courts of general jurisdiction only arises, as has before been stated, with respect to jurisdictional facts concerning which the record is silent. When the record states the evidence or makes an averment with reference to a jurisdictional fact, it will not be presumed that there was other or different evidence respecting the fact, or that the fact was otherwise than as averred. Or, differently stated, the recital in a judgment or decree is limited by and restricted to the evidence contained in the record, as the presumption is that such only was the evidence before the court, and the judgment stands or falls upon the sufficiency of the evidence. As was stated by the Iowa court, the judgment entry must be considered in the light of the entire record. No presumptions in support of the judgment are to be allowed in opposition to any statement in the record. If an act be stated in the roll as having been done in a specified manner, no presumption arises that at some future time the act was done in a better or more efficient manner. If it appears that process was served in a particular mode, no other or different service can be presumed. And this rule was applied in another state, where it was held that the recital in the judgment of service upon all the defendants would not control the evidence in the record to the effect that all were not served. There are cases, however, which hold that the presumption stated overcomes the evidence in the record. See Colrateran Attack.

Sec. 138. Exception where fact of jurisdiction is adjudged.

Some courts make an exception to the foregoing rule in cases where the court directly adjudges that it has jurisdiction (by

Gray v. Hawes, 8 Cal. 562.

Mickel v. Hicks, 19 Kan. 578;  
Miller v. Ewing, 88 S. & M. (Miss);  
Crow v. Myersieck, 88 Mo. 41;  
Mayfield v. Bennett, 48 Iowa, 194;  
Boker v. Chapline, 12 Iowa, 204;  
Gray v. Hawes, 3 Cal. 562.

Mayfield v. Bennett, 49 Iowa, 194;  
Boker v. Chapline, 12 Iowa, 204.

Coan v. Clow, 83 Ind. 417.
