### --- Page 0200 ---

§ 139.] JUDGMENTS. 115

which, the writer presumes, is meant if it judges the essential facts to exist which confer jurisdiction. Then such adjudication will control the evidence contained in the record, though insufficient, on the ground that a presumption attaches that there was other evidence not preserved that controlled the action of the court, or that jurisdiction was acquired in some other manner. The California and Iowa courts appear to have held the same, though such decisions appear to be inconsistent with other declarations.

Sec. 139. Subject explained.—The distinction the courts evidently intend to make is that, where a court has power to decide and actually exercises its judgment in adjudicating upon a state of facts, its decision, if erroneous, is merely a mistake of judgment, and that a wrong decision does not affect the question of its jurisdiction, and hence a mere recital of certain facts conferring jurisdiction relates to the record of those facts, while, where there is an express finding or adjudication upon the sufficiency of those facts, this is the exercise of jurisdiction. The distinction thus observed is important only in determining the effect of the record as evidence to overcome the adjudication, where one has been made. If the record is sufficient of itself to overcome a mere recital, it may not overcome an adjudication. But in case of a direct attack upon the judgment, and in some cases of collateral attack, the rule is, as we shall see, that the jurisdiction of the court is always open to inquiry, when there has been no appearance, and the record, though not always sufficient evidence, is proper in connection with other evidence, though extrinsic in character, to overcome the force of the adjudication. It cannot be properly said the court has jurisdiction because it has decided, simply, that it has jurisdiction to so decide, as well as to make a correct decision. Its decision is binding because it was authorized to make it, and because public policy and the respect due to the sovereignty it represents, at least in tribunals acting under the same sovereignty, require that the decision should be so regarded.

UBannon v. People, 1 Bradw. (Ill. App.) 496; Barnett v. Wolfe, 70 Ill. 76; Andrews v. Bernardi, 87 Ill. 365; Hemmer v. Wolfe, 124 Ill. 436; Gray v. West, 8 Cal. 562; May-Vassault v. Austin, 38 Cal. 691; Field v. Bennett, 48 Iowa, 104.

<br>

### --- Page 0201 ---

116 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 140, 141.]

while it remains on the record unimpeached and unreversed.  
The presumption of which we have spoken has for its basis that courts have rightfully exercised their authority in properly doing what they were required to do. Applying this presumption to the record, and it will appear that the officer whose duty it was to make up the record properly performed it, and included therein all that existed which was properly a part thereof. Further, that nothing was placed in the record but that which was properly a part thereof. At best we have two conflicting presumptions of equal weight and importance, or one presumption existing in favor of separate acts. Logically the result would be that the effect of the presumption would be neutralized, and, it affirmatively appearing there was a mistake on the part of some officer, either the judge in coming to a wrong conclusion as to the legal effect of the evidence before him, or of the officer of the court whose duty it was to prepare the record in making an incorrect record, consistency would seem to require that the whole record should be considered together, irrespective of the presumption, and the logical instead of the conjectural conclusion reached that the only evidence considered was that which the record shows to have been before the court.

Sec. 140. Methods of impeachment.—There are three methods of impeaching judgments in civil actions: 1. By proceedings taken in the action wherein the judgment was rendered, such as by motion to vacate, review by appeal or writ of error, and in some cases by the ancient writ of coram nobis or by certiorari to reverse the proceedings for want of jurisdiction or excess thereof. 2. By direct attack upon such judgment in other proceedings. 3. By collateral attack. The first method is foreign to the scope of this work, and therefore will be treated only in respect to proceedings to reverse by certiorari, which will be discussed under that head.

Sec. 141. Direct attack—Definition of direct attack and collateral attack.—At the outset we must determine what is embraced within the term “direct attack,” as distinguished from collateral attack. Definitions have been attempted, but

1 Callen v. Ellison et al., 13 Ohio St. 446

<br>

### --- Page 0202 ---

§ 142.] JUDGMENTS. 7

the courts, in language at least, have not confined themselves to the definition given. The Connecticut court defined a direct proceeding to be “a petition for a new trial,” a writ of error coram nobis, or, after the lapse of time during which these remedies are available, a suit in equity. The Indiana court has also defined a direct attack to be: “Whatever the form of the proceeding may be by which a party to a judgment is seeking to review it, or to obtain relief therefrom, if the proceeding is one for which provision is made by statute, and the statutory method is being pursued, or if it is an application which invokes the inherent power of the court for relief from an inequitable judgment taken against a party by fraud or mistake, so as to prevent a meritorious defense, which the unauthorized interference of another or the fraudulent conduct of his adversary prevented him from making, such proceeding and application, as a general rule, constitute a direct proceeding in the original case.” This court had previously declared that any proceeding to have a judgment declared void on account of matters not appearing on the face of the record was a collateral attack on the judgment. The definition thus given of a direct proceeding is not sufficiently clear to permit of the drawing of any line of distinction between a direct and a collateral attack. Nor does the writer comprehend whether a distinction was intended between direct attack and direct proceedings. A definition of a collateral proceeding is thus stated by a writer: 

“To impeach a judgment collaterally is to deny its effect as legal and binding, in some proceeding not instituted for the very purpose of annulling or modifying it, or of enjoining its execution, as in a suit upon the judgment.” It would seem that in Iowa any attack upon a judgment, the purpose of which is to have it declared invalid for any cause, whether in an affirmative proceeding or as a basis of defense, is not a collateral attack upon the judgment. For other definitions, see Cotrarrenar ATTACK.

Sec. 142. Action in equity.—Courts of equity have undoubted jurisdiction in proper cases to impeach judgments and decrees. Murlburt v. Thomas, 55 Conn. 131, 42 Colo. T. Times, 59, 3 Am. St. Rep. 43; Whetstone v. Whetstone, 31 Iowa, 7; Herman v. Moore, 112 Ind. 221, 276; Bank v. Ault, 102 Ind. 322.

<br>

### --- Page 0203 ---

118 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 143.

But to what extent and under what conditions this power may be exercised the courts are not agreed. Ordinarily, courts of equity will not assume this jurisdiction when the party has an adequate remedy at law. But in cases of fraud, accident or mistake, and perhaps in other cases, such courts will exercise their jurisdiction and open, vacate or modify judgments at law, stay proceedings therein, or direct an issue and trial upon the merits, though the same relief may be obtained by motion or other proceedings in the courts of law. It has been assumed by some courts that the court proceeds against the person to enjoin him from proceeding under the judgment, and does not presume to directly interfere with the action of the court which rendered it. And such was the ancient rule, the usual decree being that the party submit to a new trial or be perpetually enjoined from proceeding on his verdict. But the jurisdiction is now exercised against the judgment itself to the extent that the court may declare it vacated and set aside, the same as it could set aside or annul a contract (xfer partes?).

Sec. 143. For want of jurisdiction of the person.— That the court rendering the judgment did not acquire jurisdiction of the person of the defendant is a ground upon which a court of equity will exercise jurisdiction. This rule, however, does not prevail in all jurisdictions. In some courts it has been held that in such cases it must appear that the judgment rendered was unjust; while in others it is held that such an allegation is unnecessary. It would seem that such an allegation could serve no purpose,

§ 145-147. JUDGMENTS. 119

Jurisdiction is exercised is that the judgment is void, that the defendant has not had his day in court, and therefore no opportunity to test the justice or injustice of the plaintiff's demand, and certainly the equity court will not determine the merits of the controversy.

Sec. 145. Recitals of service or appearance—How far conclusive.—Hence it may be shown by extrinsic evidence that the recitals in the judgment are not true in respect to service of process or appearance by the defendant. This proposition is rejected by a number of courts. For a discussion of the question see CotnATerAL Attack, where the authorities are collected. And when it appears from the record in cases of personal judgment that the method of service was by publication, it may be shown that the party was a non-resident of the state, and did not appear in the action, and that no personal service was had.

Sec. 146. Due process of law requires personal service.—The due process of law embraced in the fourteenth amendment to the constitution of the United States demands that in order to bind a party by a personal judgment, there must be personal service of process or an appearance. And whenever it is made to appear in any proceeding that this requisite was not present, then it is that it appears that the judgment is void.

Sec. 147. Return of officer cannot be contradicted.—It was held, however, if the return of the officer showed a proper service, though in fact such return is false, that if the plaintiff was not in fault with respect to such return, redress could only be had in the court of law where the record was made, and, if relief could not be had there, the party injured must seek redress against the officer making the return. It is true that in the case cited there was an appearance and plea on the part of the defendant, yet the statement of the principle is independent of such fact, and seems to be general in its application. 

1. Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714.  
2. Arnold v. Hawley, 67 Iowa, Dunklin v. Wilson, 64 Ala. 162; Stone v. Skerry, 31 Iowa, 582; Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N.Y.; Johnson v. Coleman, 23 Wis. 452.  
3. Martin v. Parsons, 49 C.

<br>

### --- Page 0205 ---

120 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 140].

character. As will be noticed hereafter, some courts apply the same rule when there has been an unauthorized appearance by an attorney. If such is the law, it is unnecessarily harsh. Jurisdiction depends upon the fact of service of process, not upon the return of the officer: that is merely the evidence; and the writer assumes the rule in most jurisdictions to be that, when equity has taken jurisdiction, then on a direct attack in that manner the facts are open to inquiry to the same extent as upon motion in the original action to vacate and set aside the return of service of process when none in fact was made, though it must be conceded that many courts hold the contrary, while in some the doctrine is that the return may be impeached as to those matters not within the personal knowledge of the officer. The supreme court of the United States, in a more recent case, affirmed the doctrine of Walker v. Robbins (Knox County v. Harshman, 133 U.S. 152), and it may be considered as settled in the federal courts. The court makes no reference to the case of Knowles v. Gaslight and Coke Co., 19 Wall. 58, where it was distinctly held that in a collateral proceeding the sheriff's return of personal service upon the defendant might be contradicted by parol proof, within the rule of Thompson v. Whitman, 18 Wall. 457. That was, however, a judgment rendered in a sister state. The rule was stated by the supreme court of California (Lapham v. Campbell, 61 Cal. 296), that when judgment is taken without due process of law, or upon false proof of service upon a defendant, who was at the time of the alleged service beyond the territorial jurisdiction of the court, he is not chargeable with knowledge of the rendition of the judgment. No one is called upon to act in a judicial proceeding in which jurisdiction over his person has not been obtained; and, although he be a party named in the proceeding, yet, if jurisdiction over him be not obtained, he has no duty to perform in relation to the proceeding for the non-performance of which he is chargeable with mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect; and, as he is not chargeable with

1 Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N. Y.; Kingsbury v. Tousley (Ohio), 47 N. E. 253, and cases cited; Crafts v. Dexter, 8 Ala. 767; Hoskins v. Hottenbach et al. 14 Iowa, 314; Vaule v. Miller (Minn.), 72 N. W. Rep. 452;

2 Mastin v. Gray, 19 Kan. 453, Rep. 541; Cavanaugh v. Smith, 84 Ind. 280.

<br>

### --- Page 0206 ---

§ 148.] JUDGMENTS. 121

Any of these things, he is not called upon to avail himself of any of them as ground for motion to set aside the judgment. This was stated where the question arose upon demurrer, and there was an allegation that the false return made by affidavit was procured by the plaintiff. What was said, however, was general in its application, without reference to the act of the plaintiff.

Sec. 148. Grounds for relief in equity.—The grounds upon which a bill in equity would lie to grant relief from a judgment were enumerated by the supreme court of the United States¹ as follows: Where the unsuccessful party has been prevented from fully exhibiting his case, by fraud or deception practiced on him by his opponent, as by keeping him away from court, a false promise of a compromise; or where the defendant never had knowledge of the suit, being kept in ignorance by the acts of the plaintiff; or where an attorney fraudulently or without authority assumes to represent a party and connives at his defeat; or where the attorney regularly employed corruptly sells out his interest to the other side,—these and similar cases which show that there has never been a real contest in the trial or hearing of the case, are reasons for which a new suit may be sustained to set aside and annul the former judgment or decree and open the case for a new and fair hearing. In all these cases, and many others which have been examined, relief has been granted on the ground that, by some fraud practiced directly upon the party seeking relief against the judgment or decree, he has been prevented from presenting all of his case to the court. Apply this reasoning to a sheriff or marshal who makes a false return intentionally, though not directly by the procurement of the plaintiff, but possibly to excuse his neglect of duty, and the result is that the defendant has without fault on his part been deprived of his day in court. What principle is there involved which permits equity to grant relief against the acts of an unauthorized attorney, but not against the fraudulent acts of a sheriff or marshal, between whom and such party there is no privity. In another case it was held that an appearance for a party not served, by counsel, who has no authority to

¹ United States v. Throckmorton, 98 U.S. 61

<br>

### --- Page 0207 ---

429

22 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 149, 150]

waive process and defend the suit, does not bind the party, and the judgment and decree is a nullity; that equity would grant relief.

Sec. 149. Appearance by unauthorized attorney.— Another ground of jurisdiction exercised by courts of equity is when there has been an unauthorized appearance for a party not served, by an attorney, and jurisdiction to render judgment thus assured. The rule in New York, however, is that the fact of want of authority can only be shown in a direct proceeding, and that there must appear an allegation that the attorney is insolvent. The court makes a clear distinction between an unauthorized appearance and one falsely entered. In the latter case there is no appearance and consequently no jurisdiction, and this fact may be shown by extrinsic evidence. The doctrine of the New York court was approved by the supreme court of Michigan, limiting, however, the inquiry to direct proceedings; while in California the inquiry can be made in an action in equity, if the attorney is insolvent. If solvent, the only remedy is in an action for damages against the attorney. But in Vermont it is held in an action at law that an appearance by an attorney binds the party for whom he appears, whether he was employed by the party or not. If the party has any remedy for the unauthorized appearance of an attorney, it is by application directly to the court which rendered the judgment against him or by a writ of error, and not by audita querela.

Sec. 150. Equitable defense not available at law, or legal defense prevented by fraud or accident.— A court of equity

2 Shelton v. Tiffin, 6 How. 183; Critchfield v. Porter, 3 Ohio. 518; De Louis v. Meek, 2 Greene (Iowa), 55; Harshey v. Blackmar, 20 Iowa, 161; Carpentier v. City of Oakland, 30 Cal. 439; Anderson v. Hawke, 115 Ill. 33; Hess v. Cole, 3 Zab. (N.J.L.) 116; Price v. Ward, 25 N.J.L. 225; Parker v. Spencer, 64 Tex. 155.

3 Denton v. Noyes, 6 Johns. 296; Brown v. Nichols, 42 N.Y. 26; Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N.Y.

5 Carpentier v. City of Oakland, 30 Cal. 439.

### --- Page 0208 ---

§ 150.) JUDGMENTS. 123

will exercise its jurisdiction when the complainant has an equitable defense of which he could not avail himself at law because it did not amount to a legal defense, or had a good defense at law, of which he was prevented from availing himself by fraud or accident, unmixed with negligence of himself or agents. The application of this rule, in so far as questions of fraud are concerned, will be considered directly following the consideration of other questions which have been held to be embraced within it. An administrator brought his action in the supreme court of the District of Columbia to recover upon a claim for professional services rendered the defendants. The defendants appeared. Thereafter the plaintiff brought an action upon the judgment in the superior court of one of the counties in Connecticut. Thereupon the defendants filed their petition in equity in the same court, the object and prayer of which was to obtain a perpetual injunction against the enforcement and collection of such judgment, except a part thereof which they admitted was just. The grounds of relief which were stated in the petition were, in brief, that the claim as first presented was for only $2,296.29, based upon a special agreement, whereas judgment was taken upon a quantum meruit for more than $10,000; that one of the defendants was absent from the district when the case was tried, and the other was sick and unable to attend, and that letters material to the establishment of a defense as to the amount of the claim had been since discovered; and the defendants believing they had in law a defense to the entire claim, the amount which the plaintiff was entitled to recover was not litigated on their part, and they took it for granted that upon the merits judgment for a greater sum than the claim as first presented would not be asked for. The supreme court of errors granted the relief prayed for by the petitioners. The ground for such ruling seems to have been that the conduct of the plaintiff in the original action gave them every reason for thinking that he did not believe that he had any right to ask for a judgment for the larger sum, and would not, and that the defendants acted.

upon the belief, and therefore it was within the rule declared in Pearce v. Olney, 2 Conn, 544, that equitable jurisdiction will be exercised whenever a party having a good defense to an action at law has had no opportunity to make it, or has been prevented by the fraud or improper conduct of the other party from making it, and by reason thereof a judgment has been obtained which it is against conscience to enforce! The supreme court of the United States reviewed the decision of the Connecticut court, reversing it on the ground that it did not appear there was any fraud practiced, and applied the rule previously stated (Zlendrickson v. Hinckley, 17 How. 443), that “a court of equity does not interfere with judgments at law unless the complainant has an equitable defense of which he could not avail himself at law because it did not amount to a legal defense, or had a good defense at law which he was prevented from availing himself of by fraud or accident, unmixed with negligence of himself or his agents.”?

Sec. 151. Accident or surprise.— Where the defendant was absent, from sickness or other justifiable reason, and was thus prevented from making his defense;* where he was prevented from taking an appeal from the judgment by reason of mistake of the clerk, when he had performed all the conditions requisite on his part;# where he was prevented from making an application for a new trial by reason of the sudden adjournment of the court before the defendant or his counsel could arrive;> where he relied upon a general order continuing all cases until the next term, and judgment was taken at a subsequent day of the same term in his absence;® where, after being informed by the court that the case was dismissed, judgment was entered, and knowledge of such fact did not reach the defendant until after the time for appeal had expired,'—it was held that these circumstances and conditions were such as to invoke the jurisdiction of a court of equity to grant relief. So also in a case where the plaintiff! must have known that his claim was unjust,

1 Stanton v. Embry, 46 Conn. 595; same case, 46 Conn. 65.  
2 Embry v. Palmer, 107 U.S. 10.  
3 Mayor of New York v. Brady et al., 115 N.Y. 599.  
4 Oliver v. Pray, 4 Ohio, 175, 19 Am. Rep.  
5 Tarver v. McKay, 15 Ga. 550.  
6 Jones v. Kincaid, 5 Lea (Tenn.),  
7 Wagner v. Shank, 59 Md. 313.

JUDGMENTS, 125

and advantage was taken of the temporary inability on the part of the defendant to secure the proper evidence to resist such claim.

Sec. 152. Mistake.—Where the jury in bringing in a verdict make a mistake therein, or the judge or party make a mistake as to facts in an action resulting in an unjust judgment,—for instance, in erroneously computing the amount for which judgment should be rendered; where there is failure on the part of the attorney to appear in court and defend, caused by incorrect information as to the time of the sitting of the court,—it was held in each case that they were such mistakes as would warrant a court of equity to assume jurisdiction. The party, however, in such cases will be required to excuse any laches which otherwise will be apparent.

Sec. 153. Newly-discovered evidence.—Where important evidence has been discovered since the trial of the case, and it was not discovered or could not be procured in time to move for a new trial, a court of equity may assume jurisdiction to vacate the judgment and order a new trial; also when the judgment debtor had no personal knowledge of the facts and was merely acting in a representative character; also where he was ignorant of the facts constituting his defense; but in such cases it must appear that the party could not have acquired the information by diligent and careful labor in preparing his cause for trial; also when there has been discovery of a receipt of payment or release of the claim upon which the judgment

was founded; but if there was negligence in not discovering such evidence, or in the failure to present it at the trial, then equity will not interfere?

Src. 154. Grounds arising subsequent to the trial.— In general, when it is proper for a court of law to grant a new trial if the application is made while that court has power to do so, it is equally proper for a court of equity to grant a new trial, if the application be made on grounds arising after the court of law had ceased to have power to do so. Hence, if it appears that the judgment complained of is unjust, and that the party had in good faith used or attempted to use all the means given him by law to assert his rights, by active efforts on his part made in good faith, and to the extent that a party has it in his power to use, but has nevertheless been prevented from making his defense to the claim, equity should grant a new trial at law. This rule was applied where a party was surprised by unexpected evidence from a witness, who had assured the party that he would testify to a certain state of facts, which assurances were honestly relied upon, and he in fact testified to the contrary, and such party did not know of any other testimony at the time that could be procured to prove the facts so relied upon, but thereafter in subsequent proceedings in another action evidence was disclosed to prove the question relied upon in the first instance. Where, however, judgment was entered in an action commenced in a court of competent jurisdiction, and years afterwards an action in equity was commenced in another court of concurrent jurisdiction where a transcript had been filed, and where proceedings were pending supplementary to execution, the object and purpose of the subsequent action being to make available payments upon the original claim, one made before the entry of judgment and the

VKiser v. Winans, 20 Ind. 428; Mnuthrop v. Lane, 3 Des. (S. C.) 324.

Foster v. Wood, 6 Johns. Ch. (N. Y.) 87; Duncan v. Lyon, 3 Johns. Ch. (N. Y.) 351; Burton v. Harris, 76 Ind. 429; Barron v. Jones, 1 J. J. Marsh. (Ky.) 470.

Colyer v. Langfort’s Adm'r, 1 A. K. Marsh. 174; Deputy v. Tobias, 1 Blackf. 311; Hoskins v. Hottenbech et al., 14 Iowa, 314.

Ballance v. Loomis, 22 Ill. 82; Hoskins v. Hottenbech et al., 14 Iowa, 314; Story’s Eq. Jur., sec. 887.

Hoskins v. Hottenbech, 14 Iowa, 314. See also Sneed v. Town, 4 Eng. (Ark.) 536.

§§ 155, 156. JUDGMENTS. 127  
other after, it was held that the proper remedy was by motion in the original action. Also, that the court in which the latter action was commenced had no jurisdiction of the subject-matter, it being a court of concurrent jurisdiction. The rule includes such defenses as the party may have which have arisen since the judgment, such as payment, release or satisfaction of the judgment.

Sec. 155. Fraud.—The conclusiveness of a judgment depends upon the absence of fraud or collusion, and it is competent for the party injured to resort to a court of chancery for relief against such fraud or collusion. The rule was early stated to be that any fact which clearly proves it to be against conscience to execute a judgment, and of which the injured party could have availed himself in a court of law, but was prevented by fraud or accident, unmixed with any fault or negligence of himself or his agents, will justify an application to the court of chancery. It is not sufficient to authorize the interference of the court that it is shown that the claim upon which the judgment was obtained was unfounded, or that there was a good defense to the action, or that the court erroneously decided the law, or that the defendant omitted to avail himself of his defense, if before the judgment the facts were known, or might by the exercise of reasonable diligence have been ascertained by him. It is the duty of the defendant to make his defense, if he has any, when he is sued, and if he omits to do it he is in general concluded by the judgment.*

Sec. 156. Equity will relieve against a corrupt or fraudulent use of a judgment.—Not only will equity relieve against a judgment so procured, but will prevent a fraudulent, corrupt or inequitable use of a judgment at law, even though regularly obtained.

1 Coon v. Seymour, 71 Wis. 340, 439; Stowell v. Eldred, 26 Wis. 504;  
2 Savage v. Everman, 70 Pa. St.; Cole v. Cunningham, 133 U.S. 107;  
3 Barton v. Hasty, 6 Neb. 419; Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N.Y. 2 Am. Rep. 419; Amory v. Amory, 3 Biss. 266; Marine Ins. Co. v. Hodgson, 7 Cranch, 332.  
4 Conway et al. v. Duncan, 28 Ohio St. 102; Lockwood v. Mitchell, 19 Ohio, 448; Swibart v. Shaum, 24 Ohio St. 452; Merritt v. Baldwin, 6 Wis. 439; Stowell v. Eldred, 26 Wis. 504; Brown v. Parker, 28 Wis. 1.

128 IMPEACHMENT. [$ lov.

Sec. 157. Character of fraud from which the court will relieve.—The frauds, however, for which a court of equity will set aside or annul a judgment between the same parties are such as are extrinsic or collateral to the matter tried, and not fraud in the matter on which the decree was rendered. The maxim that fraud vitiates every proceeding must be taken, like other general maxims, to apply to cases where proof of fraud is admissible; but when the same matter has been actually tried, or so in issue that it might have been tried, it is not again admissible. The party is estopped to set up such fraud, because the judgment is the highest evidence and cannot be contradicted. As stated by the New York court: "It must be something that intervened in the proceedings by which the judgment was obtained, and it must have occurred in the very concoction or procurement of the judgment, and not have been known to the opposite party at the time, and for not knowing which he is not chargeable with neglect or inattention. It must consist in something of which the complaining party could not have availed himself in the court giving the judgment, or of which he was prevented from availing himself then, by fraud." It must be that only which is extrinsic to the merits of the case, and by which the court has been imposed upon or misled into a false judgment. The fraud in such cases is made up of the same constituents as is fraud in any other case, and the same state of facts must appear which is required in other cases. There must be fraudulent allegations and representations, designed and intended to mislead, with knowledge of falsity and resulting in damaging deception, or such conduct as in law amounts to fraudulent concealment. Where fraudulent concealment of a fact is relied upon, it must be an intentional concealment of a material and controlling fact, for the

1 United States v. Throckmorton, 98 U.S. 61, 68.

2 Stilwell v. Carpenter, 59 N.Y. 415, opinion on rehearing reported in 2 Abb. New Cases, 263. See also Koss v. Wood, 70 N. 8; Engstrom v. Sherburne, 137 Mass. 153; Wilhite v. Wilhite, 124 Ind. 226.

3 Amador C. & M. Co. v. Mitchell, 59 Cal. 169; Cavanaugh v. Smith, 84 Ind. 380.

4 State of Michigan v. Phœnix Bank, 33 N.Y. 9; Hunt v. Hunt, 72 N.Y. 217; Mayor, etc. of New York v. Brady et al., 115 N.Y. 599.

5 State of Michigan v. Phœnix Bank, 33 N.Y. 9; McDowell v. Morrell, 5 Lea (Tenn.), 278; United States v. Atherton, 102 U.S. 372; Ward v. Town of Southfield, 102 N.Y. 287, 203; Reed v. Harvey, 23 Ark. 44; Capital Bank v. Rutherford, 70 Ga. 57.

<br>

### --- Page 0214 ---

[§ 158, 159.] JUDGMENTS. 129

purpose of misleading and taking an undue advantage of the opposite party. This statement of the rule, however, must not be understood as extending to the failure to disclose every material fact known to the party. As was said by a learned court, “when there is no relation of confidence between the plaintiff and the defendant, the parties stand at arm’s-length. They come into court as adversaries, and neither party is bound to make any revelation of his case to the other. The plaintiff must be prepared to prove all the facts constituting his cause of action and to meet any defense which the defendant may interpose, and the defendant must be prepared to establish any defense which he may have. But neither party can mislead the other by any positive or actual fraud, nor can he, for the purpose of perpetrating a fraud upon the other party, conceal such facts as good faith and common honesty require him to reveal.”

Note To Sec. 157.— The concealment which was the subject of the action in Ward v. Town of Southfield, 102 N. Y. 287, was the failure to disclose on the part of the supervisor of the town, at the time he brought, in behalf of the town, the action against the collector of taxes to recover taxes uncollected by the latter, and when the judgment was obtained, information that owing to some defect in the assessment roll the taxes could not legally be collected. It appears that such supervisor had a copy of the assessment roll and was aware of its contents, but it did not appear that he knew that the defects in the affidavit were such as to furnish the collector a defense to the action, nor that the defects in the affidavit were unknown to such collector, nor did it appear that he fraudulently concealed the copy from the collector for the purpose of obtaining an undue advantage. It was held there was no relation of confidence between the parties and that the facts did not bring the case within the rule.

Sec. 158. Rule applies though there may be an adequate remedy at law.— The jurisdiction of equity is not restricted in cases of fraud by the rule that there must not exist an adequate remedy at law. Fraud in any case is a ground of equitable jurisdiction.

Sec. 159. General rule illustrated.— The case of Horner v. Fish, 1 Pick. 455, is an apt illustration of the rule, and goes

Ward v. Town of Southfield et al., 102 N. Y. 287; Wierch v. De Zoya, 2 Gilm. (Ill.) 385; Nelson v. Rockwell, 14 Ill. 375; Fleming v. Slocum, 18 Johns. 403; McLaren v. McLaren, 6 Wend. 537.

### --- Page 0215 ---

130 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 160.

as far as any other in upholding it in all its stringency. There the defendant had obtained a judgment against the plaintiff on a policy of insurance, which was paid on execution; afterwards the plaintiff discovered that the defendant caused the insurance to be made when he knew the ship had been lost, and so procured the insurance by fraud. The action was not brought directly to set aside the judgment, but sought in effect to impeach it, as it was for the recovery of the money paid. It was held that the former judgment was a bar to the action. To the argument that the question of the fraud was not litigated in the former action, the reply was that the nature of the action was such as to admit of such defense; that the judgment was conclusive as to all such matters until reversed. The conclusion in this case is hardly consistent with that reached in other cases, where it is stated that ignorance of the facts constituting a defense may be ground for relief in equity. 

Sec. 160. Fraud in procuring evidence of jurisdiction.—A court of equity has inherent power, in an original action commenced therein, to set aside a judgment when by reason of fraud of the adverse party jurisdiction of the person of the defendant was not actually though apparently acquired. Truth must be the basis of all judgments. The rule was applied where it appeared that judgment by default was entered against the defendant upon a false affidavit, made by a person qualified in law to serve a process, in effect that he had served the summons personally upon the defendant in the state, but in fact the summons was so served upon him in another state. It was said the defendant was not bound to resort to the remedy by motion to set aside a judgment taken against him in an action in which there was no service upon him or in which the affidavit of service of summons was false. Also where it was alleged that the defendant before obtaining the decree procured a man to make a false affidavit that the plaintiff in the action had gone beyond seas, upon which the defendant obtained

United States v. Throckmorton, 98 U.S. 68; v. Neeley, 82 Ill. 71; Cavanaugh v. Kiser v. Winans, 20 Ind. 423; Smith, 84 Ind. 380; Whetstone v. Whetstone, 31 Iowa, 276; Muthrop v. Lane, 3 Des (S.C.) 324; Gray v. Barton, 62 Mich. 185; Michigan v. Phoenix Bank, 33 N.Y.

Lapham v. Campbell, 61 Cal. 296; 22.

Weaver v. Poyer, 79 Ill. 417; Jones v. Lapham, 61 Cal. 206.

<br>

### --- Page 0216 ---

§ 161.] 131

an order from the court that service on the then defendant's clerk in court should be good service, whereas in fact the defendant was then living publicly in the next town, but on this false affidavit and the order made thereon the cause was heard ex parte, and confirmed absolutely, upon which decree the party so moving foreclosed and the estate was obtained. This rule so declared does not conflict with the doctrine announced by the federal court, in effect that a false return of an officer cannot be questioned. The distinction which that court recognizes is this: that for the misfeasance of such officer, not attributable to any act of the party, the remedy is solely against him; but when such misfeasance is procured by the act of a party, then both are liable, and the proceedings are so tainted with the fraud that the judgment is voidable at the election of the injured party.

Sec. 161. Upon the court or tribunal.—When the court or tribunal has been imposed upon by the fraudulent acts of others in respect to the exercise of its jurisdiction, then a proper case is presented for the interference of a court of equity to relieve against the results caused by such fraud. Hence, where the allegation was that the register and receiver of a United States land office had entered into a conspiracy to defraud the government by means of false entries, and a patent was issued, it was held that an action in equity would lie on the part of the United States to cancel the patent. A distinction was drawn between a decree obtained by means of false testimony or forged documents upon which a tribunal has acted, and the facts of the case as presented. That here officers constituting a special tribunal entered into a conspiracy, and the fraud consisted of documents which they had fabricated and presented with their judgment to those having appellate and supervisory power in such matters; and thus a fictitious proceeding was imposed upon the latter as one which had actually taken place. It was a fraud upon the jurisdiction of the department at Washington, and not the mere presentation to them of doubtful and disputed testimony. The decisions of such tribunals

Walker v. Robbins, 18 How. 584; Mott v. United States, 112 U.S. 24; Chester v. Miller, 13 Cal. 556,

<br>

### --- Page 0217 ---

152 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 162.

In respect to the matters specially conferred are final to the same extent as are those of other judicial or quasi-judicial tribunals, and in the absence of fraud are conclusive upon all questions of fact. Where a judgment after entry was altered so as to include a party not served with process, this was held a sufficient ground for the interposition of a court of equity to set aside the judgment.

Sec. 162. Where the unsuccessful party has been prevented from exhibiting fully his case by fraud or deception practiced on him by his opponent.—It is a ground for the interposition of a court of equity to relieve a party from the operation of a judgment procured by the opposite party, when by reason of fraud or deception practiced by the latter the former has been prevented from fully exhibiting his case; as, for instance, when he is prevented from defending by false and fraudulent promises or representations that the proceedings will be abandoned and not prosecuted further; or when his witnesses have been fraudulently tampered with or removed; or where judgment has been taken, in violation of the terms of an agreement between the parties that judgment should not be taken. Such an agreement, so existing, will be upheld, and its violation relieved against, though oral and made on Sunday, and notwithstanding the rules of the court provide that stipulations, to be binding, must be in writing. Equity will interfere also when the parties have settled the controversy prior to the final decree, and the plaintiff, notwithstanding such settlement, proceeds to judgment without notice to the defendant.

1 Vance v. Burbank, 101 U. S. 514. Co. v. Porter, 68 Cal. 369; Chambers also Babcock v. McDonald, 83 Ill. 214.

6 United States v. Throckmorton, 98 U. S. 65; California Beet Sugar Davis v. Headley, 22 N. J. Eq. 215.

### --- Page 0218 ---

§ 163. Service of process procured by fraud.— The general rule is that if a person be induced by fraud to go into another state for the purpose of obtaining service upon him, such fraud may be ground for setting aside the judgment resulting from such service.

§ 164. Perjury as a ground for relief.— There is some controversy in the courts whether allegations of perjury, or the use of forged documents as evidence in securing a judgment, are grounds for relief in an action in equity. The federal courts are very emphatic in declaring that they do not constitute a sufficient ground. In fact, such courts consistently hold that judgments are conclusive as to any matter which was actually presented and considered in the judgment assailed, and such appears to be the rule in many of the states. In some of the states, however, it has been held that a judgment based on perjury will be relieved against. In others that the rule first stated is not absolute, but under some circumstances perjury may constitute a ground for relief. Thus it was said: “It might be that a judgment at law might be so manifestly against conscience that a new trial would be granted in equity, as in a case where perjury was established by some instrument or document in writing, or where a witness for the prevailing party has been, subsequent to the trial, convicted of false swearing in the case. In such a case, if relief could not be had, it is manifest it would work a hardship upon the party thus injured.” However, the reasons for the rule, as stated by the federal supreme court, are so satisfactory that even the circumstances supposed ought not to be sufficient to destroy the rule. They say “that the mischief of retrying every case in which the judgment or decree rendered on false testimony, given by perjured witnesses, or on contracts or documents whose genuineness or validity was the

Lovengood, 2 Jones’ Eq.

2 United States v. Throckmorton, 98 (N.C.) 157; Peagram v. King, 2 Hawks U.S. 68; United States v. Moffat, M2 (N.C. 605, EE Am. Dec. 793.

U.S. Gray v. Barton, 62 Mich. 186, 197.

134 IMPEACHMENT. LS 165,

issue, and which are afterwards ascertained to be forged or fraudulent, would be greater by reason of the endless nature of the strife than any compensation arising from doing justice in individual cases.?

Src. 165. Matters involved in the issue — Errors of law.— It is a general principle that equity will not review matters litigated in the original action, or which were in issue, with the possible exception of those in which the party was ignorant of the facts, not due to negligence on his part; and further, that equity will not assume jurisdiction on account of errors at law, or irregularities in the proceedings, or on account of ignorance of or mistake in the law on the part of the tribunal or party, or his attorney, or the latter's unauthorized conduct of the case, if he was duly employed and authorized to conduct it.

United States v. Throckmorton, 98 U. S. 68.

### --- Page 0221 ---

136 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 165a.]

I. WHEN ACTION BROUGHT TO ENFORCE THE JUDGMENT, OR THE JUDGMENT IS MADE THE BASIS OF A RIGHT OF ACTION OR GROUND OF DEFENSE.

Sec. 165a. Collateral attack.— It is safe to say there is no proposition in the law that is involved in more doubt and affected with a greater variety of judicial opinion than the subject of collateral attack upon judicial proceedings. Difficulty is met at the very threshold in determining what is a collateral, as distinguished from a direct, attack; and as we proceed with the discussion of this important branch of our subject, it will appear that the courts are so widely apart that the almost universal use of the phrase “collateral attack” promotes confusion rather than certainty. Again, we find the expression oftentimes repeated, and by courts whose doctrines are directly in conflict, that a judgment rendered without jurisdiction is absolutely void and may be impeached whenever and wherever confronted, either in a direct or a collateral proceeding. The rule is universally true, but a mere general statement of the rule is often misleading, and is generally so where not qualified. When applied to judgments and decrees, where the want of jurisdiction is apparent upon the face of the record, the statement of the rule is correct. But when applied to judgments and decrees of domestic courts of general jurisdiction, where the record is silent as to the question of jurisdiction, then it is not the law in all jurisdictions; in fact it is the law in but comparatively few, and in those much depends upon the character of the proceeding and the manner in which the question is presented; and when applied to judgments or decrees where jurisdiction affirmatively appears either in the record or by recital, the question being presented of contradiction of such record or recitals, it will be found that there is an irreconcilable conflict of opinion, necessitating a resort to the expressed opinions of the courts of each particular jurisdiction to determine its rule with respect to the question. Even then, in many cases, it will be found difficult to extract any rule that will be of universal application there. The difficulty is increased by the fact that some courts are firm in their opinion that the question of jurisdiction of the person cannot be assailed where the record discloses apparent jurisdiction, even in

<br>

### --- Page 0222 ---

§ 166.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 137

an action in equity, to vacate and set aside the judgment on the ground that jurisdiction in fact was not obtained. Certain it is that the law writer ought to be excused for not making plain that which in the nature of things cannot be made so.

Src. 166. Rule in the code states — New York.— We had under discussion in a previous chapter the question of the conclusive effect of domestic judgments, and also in another chapter the causes for which they might be impeached in a direct proceeding and in equity. It now remains, to discuss to what extent and for what causes such judgments may be collaterally attacked in an action brought to enforce them, or when made the basis of a right of action or plea in defense, as well as when they are sought to be used as evidence in another proceeding.

Prior to the establishment of the code, it was generally considered that as to defects not apparent from the record (with the possible exception in some states of the defense of fraud), they could only be reached by a proceeding in chancery directly attacking the judgment. But now quite generally in those states which have adopted the code, and where law and equity are administered by the same judge, the rule is otherwise, and, to the extent that whatever is sufficient to impugn the judgment in equity by such direct proceeding, may be urged as matter of defense to an action brought to enforce the judgment.

This question was before the New York court of appeals for review in 1877. The question arose where an action was brought to foreclose a second mortgage. One of the defenses was that the rights of the plaintiff as mortgagee had been barred by a judgment of foreclosure of a prior mortgage; that he had appeared by an attorney therein, and therefore was conclusively bound by that decree. He contended that the signature to the notice of appearance on the part of said attorney was a forgery; that he was not authorized to appear for him, and sought thus to impeach the recital in the decree by extrinsic evidence—by the testimony of the attorney himself. The court frankly stated at the outset that they have been unable to find an authenticated adjudication of a court of last resort which sanctions the right or the practice of thus in a collateral

Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N.Y. 253,

<br>

### --- Page 0223 ---

15 IMPEACHMENT. [§ Les.

proceeding impeaching a domestic judgment, while as to judgments of other states such has become the rule. While there are many which have held the negative proposition, cases were found which seemingly sanction the affirmative view, but upon examination it was found that they related either (1) to judgments of inferior courts, or courts of limited jurisdiction; (2) to courts of general jurisdiction, acting in the exercise of special statutory powers; (3) to courts of sister states; (4) to cases where the want of jurisdiction appeared on the face of the record; (5) to cases of direct proceedings to reverse or set aside the judgment.

With the same frankness which characterizes their former statement they further state that, if the affirmative of the proposition can be sustained, it must rest upon the local law of the state, as it finds no support in adjudications elsewhere. In concluding that it may thus rest, the result is placed upon the ground that in that state law and equity are administered in the same forum. Equitable defenses are there allowed in actions at law; hence there is no reason why a defendant should not be permitted to set up by way of defense any matter which would be ground for relief in equity against the judgment. It was held that, in respect to the impeachment of judgments by extrinsic evidence relating to jurisdiction, there was no distinction between the effect of domestic and foreign judgments, and that the principle applied as well to the records of judgments of courts of general as of limited jurisdiction. The conclusion of the court cannot be otherwise than correct. It would be an idle ceremony to stay proceedings until an action could be brought on the equity side of such a court to set aside and vacate the judgment for want of jurisdiction, or fraud practiced in procuring it, before the same tribunal. Such a rule would practically ignore the statute in respect to equitable defenses in actions at law. It is true that the action where the rule was declared was equitable, for the foreclosure of a mortgage, but that fact does not affect the rule or the reasons upon which it is based. It would seem, notwithstanding the statement of the learned court, that the courts of that state had, prior to the decision referred to, announced the same doctrine, though perhaps the question was not presented in the same

<br>

### --- Page 0224 ---

§ 116Gh.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 109

manner. Thus in Warrington v. People, 6 Barb. 610, the opinion was expressed that the jurisdiction of a court of general or limited jurisdiction may be inquired into, although the record of the judgment states facts which apparently give it jurisdiction; and again, in Zurd v. Shipman, 6 Barb. 623, it was stated that the record is never conclusive as to the recital of a jurisdictional fact, and that the defendant may always show a want of jurisdiction, though the record shows to the contrary; that if the court has no jurisdiction it has no power to make the record. These expressions, however, were mere general statements of legal propositions, and were made independently of the question of the character of the proceeding. It was also stated in a court of last resort that jurisdiction is presumed in a court of general jurisdiction until the contrary appears, but that the want of jurisdiction may be shown by evidence upon collateral attack, with the exception where the question of jurisdiction has been judicially determined; and again, that it is undoubtedly true that the want of jurisdiction of the person is a good defense in an action on a judgment when set up for any purpose, and that such jurisdiction is open for inquiry. In other cases it has been held that the mere recitals showing jurisdiction were but prima facie evidence of the truth of the fact recited and were subject to impeachment by evidence.

Sec. 166. Recitals — Impeachment of.— The question whether the recitals in the record of a domestic judgment of a court of general jurisdiction can be impeached in an action brought to enforce such judgment or a suit in equity to set aside and vacate the judgment is one upon which courts are still at variance. In New York, as we have seen, and in the code states quite generally, the rule seems to be that, in an action brought to enforce the judgment, facts may be pleaded as an equitable defense and proved which tend to impeach the recitals as to service of process or appearance. It seems that in those states it is not intended to disturb the rule that such judgments are not subject to collateral attack, but the decisions go

MBloom v. Burdick, 1 Hill. 158; Bolton v. Jacks, 6 Robt. 198; Pendleton v. Weed, 17 Wend. 75. People v. Warden, etc., 100 N.Y. 21. Porter v. Bronson, 29 How. Pr.

<br>

### --- Page 0225 ---

140 IMPEACHMENT. [$160d.

upon the doctrine that such a defense is not a collateral attack, but rather a direct attack upon the judgment. If not this, that it is an attack directly against the judgment, of the same force and to the same extent as thus attacked by an action in equity. In fact the writer is of the opinion (though not conceded by many courts) that any attack which has for its object and purpose the setting aside and vacating of a judgment is a direct attack, whether in the same or an independent proceeding. Any attack the object and purpose of which is to avoid the judgment, leaving it to stand as the judgment of the court which pronounced it, is a collateral attack. Thus an action in equity to vacate and set aside a judgment is a direct attack: it directly attacks the judgment. An action to restrain proceedings under the judgment or to prevent the enforcement thereof is a collateral attack. An answer in an action to enforce a judgment asking affirmative relief, that the judgment be vacated and set aside, is a direct attack. But such a defense is permissible only in the code states (with perhaps an exception), where equitable defenses may be urged in actions at law. The writer understands such to be the doctrine of the New York, Wisconsin, Iowa, Minnesota, Ohio and Kansas courts and some others, as appears in subsequent pages. It would seem that courts of equity have frequently, in the exercise of their jurisdiction, permitted the contradiction or impeachment of recitals in a judgment. Thus, in Larl v. AeVeigh, 91 U. S. 503, the supreme court of the United States sustained a bill to set aside a judgment where it was alleged that the recital or the return, to the effect that the summons was posted on the front door of the debtor's residence, was untrue. It was said: “Argument to show that no person can be bound by a judgment or any proceeding conducive thereto to which he was never a party or privy is quite unnecessary, as no person can be considered in default with respect to that which it was never incumbent upon him to fulfill. Standard authorities lay down the rule that in order to give any binding effect to a judgment it is essential that the court should have jurisdiction of the person and the subject-matter; and it is equally clear that the want of jurisdiction may always be set up against a judgment when sought to be enforced or where any benefit is

<br>

### --- Page 0226 ---

§ 167] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 141

claimed under it, as the want of jurisdiction makes it utterly void and unavailable for any purpose.” Citing Borden v. Fitch, 15 Johns, 141.

Sec. 167. Rule in Kansas.—It was stated by the Kansas court (Mastin v. Gray, 19 Kan. 458, 27 Am. Rep. 149) that “proceedings instituted for the purpose of destroying, impairing or modifying the force or effect of a judgment for all cases, such as proceedings to reverse, vacate, set aside, declare void, suspend, modify or perpetually enjoin a judgment, are direct proceedings. But a proceeding instituted for some other purpose, and in which the question of the force or effect of the judgment arises only incidentally, is a collateral proceeding.” It was further stated that “a judgment rendered without jurisdiction is no judgment at all. A judgment cannot be rendered against any person until he has had his day in court, and until he has had an opportunity to be heard. To say that the record of a judgment can conclusively prove that any person was a party to the action in which it was rendered, and then to say that the judgment is conclusively valid because he was a party, is to reason illogically. It is begging the question. It is reasoning in a circle. That is, the judgment proves that he was a party, and being a party proves the validity of the judgment. Even a finding to the effect that one was a party could not have that effect, because a court cannot make a finding against a person until after the court has obtained jurisdiction of such person.” This court repudiates all distinctions between judgments of sister states and those of courts of limited jurisdiction and judgments of inferior courts in the respect named. It holds that a judgment rendered without jurisdiction is subject to collateral attack. That in an action of ejectment, the validity of a judgment under which either party claims title is just as much in issue as the validity of any judgment in any action can be, and that it may be shown that the return of the officer as to the service of process was false, especially as to those matters which do not come personally within the officer’s knowledge.

See also Harris v. Wardeman, 14 Pollard v. Baldwin, 22 Iowa, 328; 340; Tebbetts v. Tilton, 31 N.H.; Borden v. Fitch, 15 Johns. 828; See Sanborn v. Fellows, 22 N. H., also see, 142 et seq.

473; Elliott v. Peirsol, 1 Pet. 340; 2 Mastin v. Gray, 19 Kan. 458, Carlson v. Dockford, 13 Gray, 591;

<br>

### --- Page 0227 ---

142 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 168.

Sec. 168. Rule in California.—It is true that in many of the states the later proposition is not accepted, and it is held that in actions at law, in an action to enforce the judgment, the recitals as to jurisdictional facts are conclusive. This is the extent of the often-quoted decisions of the supreme court of California, and their ruling is based upon the theory that such method of attack is purely collateral. It was there held that a domestic judgment could be collaterally attacked only where want of jurisdiction affirmatively appeared by the judgment roll. That the record imparted absolute verity, and that extrinsic evidence could not be received to contradict it. The action was one to quiet title, based upon the decree sought to be impeached, and the statement of that court is that such a judgment when offered in evidence cannot be collaterally attacked. In a prior case the whole subject was reviewed. It was conceded that, under the code system of practice prevailing in that state, the defendant is allowed to set up as many defenses as he may have, regardless of the question whether they are of a legal or equitable nature. The action being upon a domestic judgment, it was said: “If there were any reasons founded either in law or equity why the plaintiff ought not to have the benefit of the judgment in suit, the defendant was at liberty to urge them in that action. That the simple question present was whether at common law, in an action of debt upon a judgment of a court of general jurisdiction, the defendant could show fraud or want of jurisdiction a//unde.” The court very emphatically held that the judgment could not be attacked collaterally on the ground of fraud, nor on the ground that the court had no jurisdiction, whether the supposed want of jurisdiction is alleged as an element of fraud or not, unless the want of jurisdiction appears upon the face of the record. The ground was that the judgment imports absolute verity, and its truth cannot be questioned either by showing otherwise than by the record itself that the court has no jurisdiction or that its jurisdiction was fraudulently procured. In view of the statement also expressed in the opinion, “Whether a party is to seek his remedy or avail himself of his defense in law or equity is no

Mitill v. City Cab, etc. Co., 79 Cal. 190, and cases cited.

Carpentier v. City of Oakland, 30 Cal. 439.

Hahn v. Kelley, 34 Cal. 391.

<br>

### --- Page 0228 ---

§ 163.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 143

longer a question, for we have but one forum, which combines all the powers of a court of law and a court of chancery, and exercises both through the same forum, and may exercise both in the same action,” it would seem that but one conclusion can follow from the statement of the law thus declared in the opinion, and that is that such a judgment or the recitals therein cannot be attacked by a direct suit in equity, for fraud or want of jurisdiction, when the evidence relied upon is extrinsic to the record. Yet we find in later cases the doctrine declared, that if a judgment is obtained by the wrongful act of a person, without service of a summons, it is against good conscience to allow him to profit by his own wrong, even if the judgment recites that it was served, and equity will interfere to prevent the party thus obtaining it from using it as an instrument of injustice. The action was one to quiet title under a decree. The defendant answered, setting up fraud in procuring the decree, and alleged that the recitals of service were false. The court held that it was proper to impeach the decree in that action by extrinsic evidence, valid in character, and stated that it would be a reproach to our jurisprudence if a court of equity could afford no relief in such a case. It is true the action was brought on the equity side of the court, but that fact was of no importance. Carpentier v. City of Oakland, supra, was also brought on that side of the court, and the decision there was not based upon the want of power of the court to grant the relief, but upon the ground that the matter stated was not ground for relief. If the doctrine of the California court is consistent in view of many subsequent decisions, it depends upon holding that in an action at law the judgment cannot be impeached by setting up the falsity of the recitals as an equitable defense, and it is hard to conclude in view of the language, with respect to defenses under the code, used in the Carpentier Case, that they are consistent even in this. It may be assumed that the element of fraud was the ground of distinction. The result and injury are no less damaging in cases of mistake than in cases of fraud. In Carpentier v. City of Oakland it was held that neither fraud nor failure of jurisdiction could be shown. 

4 Martin v. Parsons, 49 Cal. 94, 5 C., 50 Cal. 493; Lapham v. Campbell, 61 Cal. 296.

<br>

### --- Page 0229 ---

14t IMPEACHMENT. [$ 169.

Sec. 169. Rule in Maine, Michigan and Minnesota.— It has been stated that the doctrine of the supreme court of Maine is to the effect that the recitals in such a judgment are conclusive and can be impeached only by the record itself. I do not find such to be the rule of that court, but rather that such recitals cannot be impeached upon collateral attack. The error in classification here, as in most cases, is found in failure to make the proper distinction between direct and collateral attack. That court states in the case cited that “the records of domestic courts of general jurisdiction cannot be contradicted by plea and proof, the same as the records of courts of limited jurisdiction and of foreign courts, when the purpose is to show want of jurisdiction. It can only be done when the proceedings are instituted for the express purpose of setting them aside. The code system not prevailing in this state, the record or recital could not be attacked in an action brought upon the judgment, and to this extent it is in opposition to the rule of the federal and some other courts which permit a want of jurisdiction to be shown in such cases.” So it has been claimed that the decisions of the supreme court of Michigan were to the effect that the recitals of a judgment could not be impeached. In an early case (Wilcow et al. v. Cussick, 2 Mich. 165) it was held that the record of a judgment of a sister state could not be contradicted unless such plea or proof would be received in an action on the judgment in the court in which it was rendered. Such a rule is now discarded by practically all courts. In the later case of Whitworth v. Pelton, 81 Mich. 98, it was simply held that the defendant in the original suit could not, in a garnishee action based upon the judgment, impeach the record as to service of process and appearance. That such in effect would be collateral attack. The most recent declaration of this court is that a judgment cannot be impeached in a collateral action by extrinsic evidence to show in contradiction of the record that service was not in fact made on the defendant. It was made in an action of ejectment, the plaintiff being the purchaser of certain lands sold upon execution against one Pierson. The service upon the defendant in the original action and his appearance were evidenced by an indorsement

1 Penobscot R. Co. v. Weeks, 52 Me. 456. See Pratt v. Dow, 56 Me. 81.

<br>

### --- Page 0230 ---

§ 170.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 145

upon the summons “that the defendant hereby accepts service and will allow plaintiff to proceed with the case as though service had been made as commanded in said summons.” On the second trial of the case the defendant sought to show that this acceptance of service was not in fact signed by the defendant in the original action. The sole question was whether it was competent to make this appear by parol testimony, and it was answered in the negative. It has also been claimed that the supreme court of Minnesota was one of the courts which held to the doctrine that the recitals of such a judgment could not be impeached. The fact is that it was there held that an action to set aside a judgment upon the ground of jurisdictional defects is not a collateral but a direct attack upon it, and that such an action in equity may be maintained to set aside a judgment upon the ground that the summons was never served upon the defendant and the officer’s return of service shown to be false, where the rights of bona fide purchasers under a judgment valid upon its face are not involved. Whether such an action could be maintained against such a purchaser is not decided. Such being the case, the defendant has a right, when an action is brought upon such a judgment, to set up his alleged cause of action to have the judgment canceled in his answer by way of counter-claim.

Sec. 170. Rule in Ohio.—In Ohio it was said that in an action under the code to enforce a judgment a cross-petition may be filed and the judgment attacked for fraud in the same way that it might be by an original proceeding for that purpose. Hence, a judgment could be thus attacked for failure of jurisdiction. The inquiry, therefore, in that state should be directed to ascertain whether in an action in equity a bill would lie to set aside and vacate a domestic judgment of a court of general jurisdiction by reason of a lack of jurisdiction to render it, and the further question whether in such an action the recital of jurisdictional facts may be impeached. That such an action will lie is without question, but whether the recitals may be impeached is not precisely clear. We are met here.

Allured v. Voller, 70 N. W. Rep. 1037.
Conway et al. v. Duncan, 28 Ohio St. 102.
Vaule v. Miller, 72 N. W. Rep. 452.

<br>

### --- Page 0231 ---

143 IMPEACHMENT. [$170.]

again with the question of collateral attack, and the whole force of the decisions of this state depends upon that question. It is the settled law of that state in respect to such judgments that where it appears from the record that the court has positively found the facts on which its jurisdiction legally rests, the jurisdiction may not afterwards be collaterally questioned; that though the judgment may be or may have been voidable when attacked in a direct proceeding for that purpose, it is not and may not be shown collaterally void. There is an element here stated not involved in the cases before considered, to wit: that the court had judicially passed upon the jurisdictional facts. It appears, however, though perhaps negatively, that even in such a case the record could be impeached in a direct proceeding for that purpose. Such a direct proceeding must, it would seem, include an action in equity to set aside the judgment or a cross-bill praying directly for the same relief. In the last-cited cases the action was in equity, the one to restrain the defendant from taking possession of certain lands under a decree rendered in condemnation proceedings, and the other was an action at law to recover certain lands in which the defendant answered, setting up title under a judicial decree. It was held in each that such proceedings were collateral, and that the record, which contained an adjudication that the defendants were non-residents, which justified the order of publication, could not be impeached by plea or proof that at such time the parties were residents and were not personally served.

The whole subject has been very recently reviewed in a very able opinion. It became necessary to define the phrases “direct attack” and “collateral attack.” The former was defined to be one by which the judgment is directly assailed in some mode authorized by law, while a collateral attack is an attempt to defeat the operation of a judgment in a proceeding where some new right derived from or through the judgment is involved. The court proceeds to say that “two seemingly conflicting principles have given rise in their application to differences of judicial opinion concerning the effect of judicial records. The one accords to judgments of courts having apparent jurisdiction of

1 Callen v. Ellison, 13 Ohio St. 466; Railroad Co. v. Village of Bell Hammond v. Davenport, 16 Ohio St. Center, 48 Ohio St. 273.

<br>

### --- Page 0232 ---

$170.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 147

the subject-matter and parties in an action conclusive verity as to all matters purporting to have been adjudicated, and precludes a re-examination of them in subsequent litigation between the parties and their privies; and the other asserts the right to be heard before he can be condemned in his person or property. In accordance with the former, the rule generally prevails, and is nowhere more firmly established than in this state, that when it does not otherwise affirmatively appear from the record it will be conclusively presumed, whenever a domestic judgment of a court of general jurisdiction is drawn in question in any collateral way, that the court regularly acquired and lawfully exercised its jurisdiction over the parties; and the record of an inferior court imports absolute verity when it shows on its face that such jurisdiction was obtained, and neither the presumptions nor recitals in the record can be contradicted in such a proceeding by extrinsic evidence. Where the record discloses a want of jurisdiction the judgment is, of course, void everywhere and for every purpose. On the other hand, the other principle referred to is sometimes employed to justify the broad statement that in all cases, even when the judgment is collaterally assailed, the jurisdiction of the court rendering it may be inquired into, and a want of service, or the absence of other jurisdictional facts, be shown by parol in opposition to the recitals of the records; for while there is some conflict of the decisions as to whether, where there is a presumption of service and jurisdiction, or where these appear on the face of the record, it can be shown in a collateral proceeding that in fact no service was had, the authorities are substantially uniform to the effect that, when the judgment is directly attacked for want of jurisdiction, such want of service and jurisdiction may be shown, though in contradiction of the record. But here again the authorities differ as to what constitutes an attack of that nature. It is conceded that it includes proceedings in error, motions to vacate or modify the judgment, and in some cases it may be made by original action, as was formerly done by bill in chancery to vacate or set aside the judgment. The rule which forbids the collateral impeachment of judgments is founded on those considerations of public policy which require stability of judicial records for the protection of those who acquire rights and property in reliance upon their conclusive

<br>

### --- Page 0233 ---

148 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 171.  
effect and for the peace of society. It is obvious these reasons find no just application in an action brought upon the judgment in which the only relief is a new recovery on it, and where no rights of third persons have intervened or are involved. Nor in such case would there appear to be any substantial ground for denying to the defendant the right to show in defense to the action that the judgment was rendered without jurisdiction. Such a defense may, we think, be properly regarded as a direct attack upon the judgment, and not within the rule or its reason against collateral impeachment. In the particular case the defendant was a non-resident, and the court recognizes the binding force of the decision of the federal supreme court referred to (Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714; Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185). Notice will be taken that this case goes no further than to decide that, in an action upon a judgment, a want of jurisdiction to render it may be shown as a defense, and recitals are not conclusive. It does not hold that in proceedings to enforce rights which have accrued under such a judgment the record or the presumptions that attach can be impeached by extrinsic evidence.  

§ 171. Rule in Nebraska and Iowa.—In Nebraska, in the early case of Johnson v. Jones, 2 Neb. 126, it was held that such a judgment could not be impeached by extrinsic evidence. This was stated by another court to have been mere dictum, and bad dictum, too. Yet, subsequently and very recently, they have declared a contrary rule, and seem to have had before them the same state of facts that was presented to the Ohio court, and reached an opposite conclusion. Thus, where judgment of foreclosure was entered upon default, upon a substituted service by publication, the order being based upon an affidavit that the defendant was a non-resident, it was held, in an action by the heir of the mortgagor to redeem, that the judgment could be attacked by showing the averments of the affidavit to be false, and that the mortgagor was in fact, at the time of making such order, a resident of the state. So also, where property was seized and sold under an attachment based upon non-residence, constructive service only being made,  

1 Kingsbury v. Tousley, 47 N. E. Rep. 2 Eayrs v. Nason, 74 N. W. Rep. 408.

<br>

### --- Page 0234 ---

§ 171.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 149  
it was held that the adjudication made could be impeached in a subsequent proceeding between the same parties by proof of the fact that the debtor was a resident of the state at the time of such service, though the record in the attachment suit showed acts and facts sufficient to constitute jurisdiction.¹

“In Iowa the doctrine of the New York court, as herein stated, meets with approval, though the reasoning may not be precisely the same. The supreme court of that state say that they believe it to be a generally recognized doctrine, that a party whose rights are affected by a judgment void for any cause, or even erroneous, which he could not reverse or review upon appeal or writ of error, might show its invalidity or error in any action whenever it is brought into question.² They hold that such judgments may be attacked in proceedings to enforce them. That the attack in such cases is in the nature of a direct attack, and it is permissible to show that the court rendering the judgment had no jurisdiction, notwithstanding a recital in the judgment that the defendant was duly and legally served with notice of the pendency of the suit. That when, in such an action, the defendant by proper answer denies that he was served with notice, the defense is not a collateral but a direct attack upon the judgment.³ That such was the rule, where the action was brought directly to set aside the judgment, had been frequently declared in that state,⁴ and was approved in a very recent case. It seems that a section of the code of that state (see. §154, Code 1875) provides for moving to set aside a judgment for irregularity at the second term after rendered, and for want of jurisdiction or fraud within one year. It was held that a motion to vacate a judgment for the latter cause was a collateral attack (why, I know not), but that an adjudication that an affidavit had been filed (which was a condition prerequisite to jurisdiction) was subject to attack in such a proceeding to vacate the judgment.⁵ It was held in that state that where the answer set up fraud in procuring the preemption certificate, in an action to quiet title based thereon,

¹ Rolse v. Kautter, 75 N. W. Rep. 566; McGahen v. Carr, 6 Iowa, 331;  
² Bixby v. Adams County, 49 Iowa, 507; Hodson v. Tebbetts, 16 Iowa, 97; Newcombe v. Dewey, 27 Iowa, 341.  
³ Clark v. Little, 41 Iowa, 497; State & Day v. Goodwin, 104 Iowa, 374.  
⁴ Ina Co. v. Waterhouse et al., 43 N. W. Rep. 611.  
⁵ Priestman v. Priestman, 103 Iowa, 320.

<br>

### --- Page 0235 ---

150 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 172.

such answer was in the nature of a cross-petition, and was in fact and in law a direct attack upon the judgment of the county judge in issuing such certificate in a proceeding brought to establish its fraudulent character.) It was, however, held in another action that a domestic judgment could not be impeached collaterally for fraud, and in still another that it is not competent to collaterally attack a judgment on the ground of fraud in its procurement, when it is offered in another action as an instrument of evidence only. An action was brought in that state to procure a decree of divorce. The defendant pleaded in bar a decree of divorce obtained by him in the district court of that state in another county. The plaintiff filed a replication setting up the fact that the decree obtained by the defendant was procured by fraud, and without service or actual notice to her. The question was whether the decree could thus be impeached. The court at the outset made the distinction that it was not an attempt to impeach the judgment collaterally, but rather it was made the basis of the defendant’s defense. Then it proceeds to declare that there is no reason for the distinction recognized by some courts between foreign judgments and domestic judgments in respect to impeachment for fraud upon collateral attack, and concludes by stating that it was useless to require the plaintiff to go into the court where the defendant's decree was obtained, and there seek relief, either by an application in the original proceeding or by a direct proceeding in equity.

Src. 172. Rule in Wisconsin.—The supreme court of Wisconsin evidently incline to the view that whatever may be set up in an action to vacate or set aside a judgment may be set up by way of a defense in an action upon the judgment, as they state the latter is an equivalent proceeding.
