It is true the judgment under consideration was that of a sister state, but the language, in the connection in which it was used, makes it very evident that its literal sense as used was intended, to wit: That an action brought to impugn the judgment,

1 Bixby v. Adams County, 49 Iowa, 407.
2 Smith et al. v. Smith et al., 22 Iowa, 516.
3 Mason v. Messenger & Towa, 17 Iowa, 201.
4 Whetstone v. Whetstone, 31 Iowa, 276.
5 Brown v. Parker, 28 Wis. 21.

<br>

### --- Page 0236 ---

§ 1720] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 151

and an answer in an action brought to enforce the judgment, are in fact and in law equivalent proceedings, whether the judgment be a domestic or a foreign one, and that what would avail in the one case would be equally available in the other. Thus, in an action of ejectment, the plaintiff was permitted to show that the decree upon which the defendant's title was based was void for want of proper service of the summons, and it was held that, if it was necessary, the recitals in the decree could be impeached by extrinsic evidence. The court adopted the principles stated in the former case of Tape v. Eaton, 9 Wis. 328, in substance, that in all cases where a decree or judgment is relied upon as the foundation of a legal right, the court may inquire into the facts which by law are made necessary to the jurisdiction of the court or tribunal by which it was pronounced, and, if it appears that such facts did not exist, disregard such judgment or decree as unauthorized and void. In the able argument which follows this declaration, the doctrine and reasoning of the New York court in Starbuck v. Murray, 5 Wend. 148, meets with approval.

Sec. 172a. Rule in Indiana and Illinois.—In Indiana the question under consideration has been many times before its court, and I frankly confess that after much labor and research I have been unable to harmonize the varied expressions of that court. First, they define collateral attack, and state that “where a party to a judgment or decree seeks to impeach its validity and have it declared void, in a subsequent action, by the allegation of facts dehors the record of such judgment or decree and not apparent on its face, such an attack upon its validity is a collateral attack.” Second, they broadly state that it is settled by many decisions of that court that such an attack upon the validity of a judgment or decree cannot be made by a party to the record. It therefore logically follows that the record or recitals in a judgment are conclusive verities in all proceedings in equity to set aside and vacate the judgment; that the party is absolutely without remedy outside of the

1 Pollard v. Wegner, 13 Wis. 569.  
2 Exchange Bank v. Ault, 102 Ind. 322, and cases cited.  
3 Reid v. Mitchell, 93 Ind. 469;  
4 Roger v. Beauchamp, 102 Ind. 33.

<br>

### --- Page 0237 ---

152 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 172a.

action in which the judgment was obtained. They state in another case that it is settled law that a proceeding to enjoin the enforcement of a judgment or decree by execution or decretal order is a collateral attack upon the judgment, and cannot be maintained for mere error or irregularities, but only by showing that the judgment or decree, or the part thereof the enforcement of which is sought to be enjoined, is void. The presumption is that the part of the decree sought to be impeached, being rendered by a court of general jurisdiction, is valid, and unless the facts stated in the complaint are sufficient to overcome or exclude this presumption a demurrer thereto should be sustained. It might reasonably be inferred from such statement standing by itself that if a complaint should affirmatively show by statement of facts that the recitals in the judgment were untrue, and that no jurisdiction in fact was obtained, this would be sufficient to overcome the presumption which attaches to the record; yet such is not their view of the law as declared in recent cases. They say that where a party seeks by complaint to impeach the validity of a judgment rendered by a court of superior jurisdiction on the ground that he had no legal notice of the pendency of the action wherein it was rendered, he must allege what, if anything, is shown by the record of the cause in relation to the issue and service of process therein. Facts must be stated such as will overcome or exclude all presumption in favor of the jurisdiction; hence if the record recites the essential jurisdictional facts the action cannot be maintained.

We now reach a class of cases in which we contend the foregoing rule was disregarded and a rule recognized which is more in accord with well-settled law elsewhere. It should be stated here that this court is in accord with the courts of New York, Wisconsin and other states upon the proposition that what would be ground for relief in an original bill in equity may be set up by way of cross-bill or as an equitable defense. Thus, in an action to quiet title to real estate, any matter of defense,

1 Davis v. Clements, 47 N. E. Rep. 1056, and cases cited. Davis v. Clements, 47 N. E. Rep. 1
2 Same case. 
3 Hogg v. Link, 90 Ind. 346; Cavanaugh v. Smith, 84 Ind. 380.
4 Cassidy v. Miller, 106 Ind. 69.

<br>

### --- Page 0238 ---

§ 1720 COLLATERAL ATTACK, 155

either legal or equitable, may be given in evidence under a general denial, and fraud in obtaining a judgment is an equitable defense to such judgment. In an action based upon a judgment of that state, the defendant answered that the defendant in the original action was not a resident of the state of Indiana when the action was commenced against him, nor for two years prior to that time, and did not become a resident of the state until some years after the judgment was rendered; that he had no notice of the pendency of the action; that none was given by publication; that appellee knew that he was not a resident of the state; that with this knowledge she fraudulently procured the sheriff to leave a copy of the summons at the residence of one Henry McKendree, which was not at the time and never had been the residence of the appellant; that the appellee procured the sheriff to make a return that he had served the summons by leaving a copy at the last and usual place of residence of the appellant. It was said: “The answer does not attack the judgment set forth in the complaint on the ground of irregularity or of error, but on the ground that the court had no jurisdiction of the person of the defendant. It is true beyond all question that where it is made to appear in a proper manner that there was no jurisdiction of the person, the judgment will not be simply erroneous, but absolutely void. Where a judgment is erroneous it cannot be attacked collaterally, but where it is void a collateral attack will prevail." It will be observed that here the judgment was sought to be impeached by matters dehors the record in an action where the extrinsic matter was pleaded as a defense, and where also the record, if it had been pleaded, showed facts which gave the court jurisdiction, and which necessarily must be impeached by extrinsic evidence if the defensive matter was permitted to stand. Certain it is that the declarations of this court in the cases first cited are not in harmony with the adjudication here. The court proceeds to state, and speaking with reference to the conclusive effect of a sheriff's return: “If we regard the sheriff's return as conclusive, then, of course, the appellant has no right to make the question of jurisdiction or no jurisdiction. The case is, however, not the ordinary one of a collateral attack upon the sheriff's return."

MHogg v. Link, 90 Ind. 346 Cavanaugh et al. v. Smith, 84 Ind. 380.

<br>

### --- Page 0239 ---

for two important elements are here present which are absent from the cases holding the return conclusive. One is, that it affirmatively appears that the defendant was not a resident of the state, and the other that the return of service was procured by the fraudulent act of the plaintiff. But those things did not affirmatively appear from the record. They were merely affirmative allegations in the pleading. The court proceeds to state the effect of the allegation with respect to the procuring a false return, in substance, that such act, resulting in fraudulently creating the appearance of jurisdiction, takes the case out of the general rule. That it would be contrary to all principle to allow a plaintiff to insist that a false return secured by his own fraud should conclude his adversary from asserting the truth.

With respect to the method of attack they state: “It must be borne in mind that the defendant does not stop short of an assault upon the jurisdiction. If he did, then perhaps he would be driven to some other attack. If he establishes the position assumed, that there was no jurisdiction, then he proves the absolute nullity of the judgment, for it is settled that, where there is no jurisdiction of the person, no valid personal judgment can be rendered. We think it clear that one who shows that there was no jurisdiction of the person is not driven to direct attack, but may assail the judgment wherever it confronts him.”

In Llogg v. Link, supra, it is stated that “fraud in the procurement of a judgment is an extrinsic and collateral fact, and constitutes ground for a direct attack upon the judgment by a party thereto by an application corresponding to an original bill in equity.” “A party may impeach a judgment collaterally for fraud in obtaining it, under the code, which permits equitable defenses.” In an earlier case, decided in 1864, that court stated a rule as follows: “Where a judgment is recovered in a court of general jurisdiction against a defendant, and the record shows an attorney of the court appeared for the defendant and filed an answer, the jurisdiction of the court cannot be controverted unless it be by proof of fraud or that the defendant was not a citizen of the state, nor, during the pendency of the proceedings, within the jurisdiction of the court in which the judgment was rendered, and had neither been notified of the pendency of the suit, nor had given authority to the attorney

<br>

### --- Page 0240 ---

glia] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 155

to enter an appearance for him." The rule is stated to be in that state that a judgment is not subject to collateral attack unless its invalidity appears upon the face of the record. If it does not so appear, it is voidable merely,—a rule which is recognized in several jurisdictions, but is not so far reaching in any other, owing to the peculiar doctrine of Indiana respecting what constitutes collateral attack. It appears that in earlier cases that an action directly attacking a judgment on the ground that a defendant was not a resident of the township, even where the evidence was extrinsic, was held a direct attack upon the judgment, and that, upon such a direct attack, proof of the want of jurisdiction over the person and the nullity of the summons may be made, notwithstanding a recital in the record that the defendant was duly served with process. The distinction between collateral attack and direct proceedings to annul and vacate a judgment was stated to be: "If, according to the facts averred in the complaint, the judgments sought to be enjoined are void, the complaint must be held sufficient. If the judgments are merely irregular or erroneous, though reversible on appeal, the complaint must be held insufficient."

The rule in Illinois is to the effect that the record or recitals cannot be impeached in a collateral proceeding. "It is," they say, "a fundamental rule of evidence that the record of a court can never be contradicted, varied or explained by evidence beyond or outside the record itself. The record in one part may contradict another part, or one part may limit, qualify or explain another, but evidence dehors the record will never be received for the purpose. To permit such evidence would render records of no avail and would render judicial sentences of but little protection, and would unsettle rights and lead to unprecedented uncertainty and confusion. Hence all records must be tried and construed by themselves. Whilst the court may hear evidence in cases of service by publication, either by the certificate of the printer or by witnesses to prove that the court has jurisdiction and may rightfully proceed and adjudicate in the case, it would be unprecedented, where the record

Wiley v. Pratt, 23 Ind. 628; Brickley v. Heilbrunner, 7 Ind. 488;  
Indianapolis, etc. R. Co. v. Harm: Grass v. Hess, 87 Ind. 193,  
Earl v. Matheney, 60 Ind. 202;  
Johnson v. Ramsey, 61 Ind. 189; Johnson v. Ramsey, 61 Ind. 189.

<br>

### --- Page 0241 ---

156 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 119a.

Failed to show jurisdiction, to permit oral proof, or, for that matter, any other kind of evidence, to show in a collateral proceeding that the court in fact did have jurisdiction. No such precedent can be found, and if it could it would not be followed.

These reasons apply with all their force to prevent proof from being heard to contradict the finding of the court that it had jurisdiction. That such a record in a collateral proceeding imparts verity and cannot be contradicted is too well established to be questioned. Where the line is drawn in this state between a direct and collateral attack, I have not been able to discover. Where a creditor's bill was predicated upon a judgment, and the answer set up the want of jurisdiction of the person in defense, and also by cross-bill, and it was disclosed that an attorney appeared for the defendant and interposed a demurrer, and also that he had no authority to enter such an appearance, it was held sufficient to avoid the judgment on the ground that the court had not jurisdiction. It was stated by the court that it would be a dangerous precedent to hold a judgment valid where there had been no service, merely for the reason that an attorney had appeared on his own motion without authority and had filed some sort of a plea in the case. In Liggins v. Curtiss, 82 MI. 28, where a creditor’s bill was filed to enforce payment of a judgment of the county court allowing a claim against an estate, it was held that the administratrix, who was sole devisee, might show that the judgment was fraudulent and inequitable; and in Griggs v. Gear, 3 Gilm., 2, it was held that chancery would afford relief where the party’s appearance had been entered without authority. An action or suit in equity to set aside a deed founded upon a judicial decree as a cloud upon title was held to be a collateral attack and the recitals of the judgment could not be impeached. It was stated in Follansbee v. Scottish American Mort. Co., 7 Ill. App. 486: “It is a well established principle that a judgment rendered without jurisdiction is void and may be attacked collaterally in any proceedings in which its validity may be called in question. This principle is in no way affected by the fact that the defendant has a right to have such void judgment reversed on appeal or error. It is

1 Barnett v. Wolf, 70 Ill. 76; Harris v. Lester, 80 Ill. 307.  
2 Anderson v. Hawhe, 115 Ill. 33,  
3 Swift v. Yanaway, 153 Ill. 202.

<br>

### --- Page 0242 ---

§ 172b. COLLATERAL ATTACK, 107

Clearly within the jurisdiction of courts of equity to vacate or enjoin judgments at law which by reason of fraud, want of jurisdiction in the court before which they were rendered, or any other circumstance, it would be inequitable and against good conscience to have enforced.” Such general statements of correct legal propositions do not always meet the question. The question in this state is not alone whether equity will set aside a judgment on the ground of want of jurisdiction, but is also whether in such an action the recitals of the decree may be impeached or contradicted by extrinsic evidence. More particularly the question is whether it can be shown that the court was without power to make the recital or the decree. The supreme court, however, has stated that the court of chancery is entirely competent to grant relief against a judgment at law which has been rendered by a court not having jurisdiction over the person of the defendant, especially where the record of the court at law does not affirmatively show the facts which would confer such jurisdiction. That while a judgment is conclusive upon parties and privies and cannot be impeached, still in equity it may be vacated and set aside where it has been procured by collusion. The defense of fraud has been permitted in an action of assumpsit where by special plea the defendant set up the judgment.

Src. 1724. Rule in New Jersey, Massachusetts, Tennessee, South Carolina, Washington and Kentucky.—In New Jersey the rule stated by Judge Redfield in Dinich v. Brook, 21 Vt. STS, was approved as follows: “It is of the very essence of debt upon judgment, or upon any matter of record, that the obligation should result from the record itself. The record imparts absolute and complete verity. It is neither to be increased or diminished by averment out of or beyond the record. It is to the record as the law and the testimony which the pleader refers his claim.” This doctrine was applied only to excluding, in an action upon a judgment, the defense that the defendant was a non-resident of the state and was not personally served. It will be seen that such was the rule in Massachusetts subsequent to the decision of the federal court that 

Propst v. Meadows, 13 TH 157, Carr v. Miner, 42 IL 179, Vield et al. v. Flanders, 40 TL 470, Miller v. Dungan, 35 N. J. L. 389.

<br>

### --- Page 0243 ---

153 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 172%.

Judgments in personam rendered against a non-resident, without personal service, were void. Such service not being due process of law, the rule in that state was changed.

In Massachusetts, originally, the doctrine was asserted to be that a domestic judgment of a court of common-law jurisdiction, to which a writ of error will lie, is valid as between the parties until reversed, notwithstanding a failure to obtain, by proper process, jurisdiction of the person of the party against whom it was rendered; that such judgments could be impeached by parties thereto only by proceedings instituted directly for that purpose. Subsequently, however, the supreme court of the United States, in Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714, and in Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185, decided that a judgment 7a personam against a person who is not a resident of the state, who has not been personally served with process, and who has not appeared, is wholly void, and that no suit can be maintained upon it either in the same or in another court. The court has no jurisdiction and its judgment has no force. The Massachusetts court thereafter held that, in a suit upon such a judgment, the defendant may show by plea and proof that the judgment was invalid, that is, that he was a non-resident of the state, and no personal service of process was had. However, as the reason for the change of ruling by this court was the declaration of the federal court, it is not to be assumed that the general principle announced in prior cases as prevailing in that state is changed only with respect to cases of the character stated. It must follow that this rule must now prevail in all the states, the supreme court of the United States having thus declared authoritatively that substituted service in personal actions is not due process of law, without reference to the rule any particular state may have declared in respect to the subject. What effect such decision of the federal court necessarily has upon the peculiar rule declared in Wisconsin is perhaps not clear. There the court had decided that in proceedings by attachment it was not essential to jurisdiction that property be subjected to possession under the writ; that so

¹ Cook v. Darling, 18 Pick. 393; 3 Needham v. Thayer, 147 Mass. 536.

² Hendrick v. Wittemore, 105 Mass. 23.

³ Jarvis v. Barrett, 14 Wis. 591.

⁴ Hendrick v. Wittemore, 105 Mass. 23.

<br>

### --- Page 0244 ---

§ 172h.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 159

long as it appeared from the affidavit that a non-resident defendant had property in the state liable to attachment, this was sufficient to confer jurisdiction; and that thereafter in the proceeding the court could render a personal judgment, which might be satisfied out of property of the defendant in the state, though he in fact was not personally served with process and did not appear. The writer is of the opinion that any such judgment would be absolutely void, and its invalidity might be shown in any proceeding wherever the defendant was confronted with it.

The doctrine of the courts of Tennessee is perhaps as stringent as that pronounced by any court in favor of the proposition that such a judgment is not subject to attack. It assumes at the outset that an action in equity brought to set aside and vacate a prior judgment is a collateral attack, seemingly being of the same mind as the Indiana court as to what constitutes a collateral attack, to wit: That any attack grounded upon evidence dehors the record is a collateral attack, whether it directly attacks the judgment or merely seeks to avoid it. A bill was filed to impeach the decree of a court of general jurisdiction, directing the sale of land for delinquent taxes, on the ground of want of jurisdiction in the court. There was no question with respect to jurisdiction of the parties. It was rather a question of want of jurisdiction of the proceeding, it being urged that, by reason of taxes being delinquent also for a subsequent year, the proceedings were controlled by another provision of statute. The court stated that no such action will lie where no lack of jurisdiction is apparent in the record; that the decree of such a court cannot be collaterally questioned except for want of authority over the matter adjudicated upon, and this want of authority must be found in the record itself. And again,— citing many cases,— that it has been held settled law in that state, deemed indispensable to the sanctity of judicial proceedings necessary for the public welfare, that domestic judgments of courts of general jurisdiction cannot be collaterally attacked by evidence outside of the record itself. The court further held, that “though the existence of any jurisdictional fact may not be affirmed in the record, it will be presumed upon collateral attack that the court has acted correctly.

<br>

### --- Page 0245 ---

160 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 172d.

and with due authority. In this case it will be noticed that no contradiction of the record or recitals was involved. The evidence dehors the record proposed was as to the fact of subsequent delinquent taxes. The only question to be determined was one of law.

The South Carolina court holds to the doctrine that such a judgment cannot be attacked in any action unless the want of jurisdiction appears upon the face of the record. The law in that state is declared to be that the proper and only mode of attacking a judgment for any defect not appearing upon the face of the record is by motion in the cause and before the court in which the judgment was obtained and entered; and under this rule it cannot be shown in an independent action that the court was without jurisdiction by reason of the party not being a resident of the proper county.

In Washington it is the rule that where the judgment itself recites that the defendants were duly and regularly cited to appear, that they made default in that behalf, and that the default was duly and regularly entered, nothing appearing in the record to contradict the recital, it is an adjudication upon the point, as conclusive upon all parties and their privies as the determination of any other fact in the case. Hence it was held that in an action of foreclosure, where the answer set up an adverse title by purchase under a decree of sale of the land for taxes, the plaintiff could not impeach the judgment for matters not appearing upon the face of the record.

In Kentucky an action in equity will not lie to set aside and vacate a judgment on the ground that the defendant was not served with process, where the return of the officer is to the effect that service was made. The return of the officer in such a case being the subject of impeachment, the attack, even in such an action, is collateral, and the judgment cannot be impeached upon collateral attack by extrinsic evidence. If the false return had been procured by the defendants, or was with

¹ Reinhart v. Nealis, 46 S. W. Rep. 3; Kiser v. Canfield, 49 Pac. Rep. 446, 1064.

² Crocker v. Allen, 84 S. C. 452; Thomas et ux. v. Ireland et al, 11 S. W. Rep. 653, and cases cited. Drake v. Steadman, 46 S. C. 474; Reese v. Mutze, 29 S. E. Rep. 73.

<br>

### --- Page 0246 ---

§ 172c¢.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 161

their knowledge, equity would set aside the judgment, but the fraud of the husband and the sheriff was held not sufficient.

Sec. 172c. Rule in other states — Connecticut and Vermont.— In Connecticut the rule is that jurisdictional facts, such as the service of the writ and the like, are presumed, and conclusively presumed, in the case of a domestic court of general jurisdiction, unless the record itself shows to the contrary. Hence the record of recitals cannot be attacked or disproved. Such judgments can only be reviewed by proceedings instituted directly for the purpose and reversed on error, or by a new trial, and, in cases where the danger is imminent and special, relief may be obtained in equity.? It seems, however, that such special relief does not include a suit in equity to vacate and set aside the judgment, but extends to the prevention of an inequitable use of a judgment or the enforcement of a judgment that may thus be rendered inequitable.

In Vermont the record is an absolute verity and the recitals cannot be disproved. Yet it seems that, in a direct proceeding to set aside the judgment, any matters which would show the judgment to have been unfairly obtained, and coming under grounds generally for equitable relief, may be shown; but whether such a judgment could be set aside and vacated solely on the ground of want of service, to be established solely by extrinsic evidence in contradiction of the record, is a question which I do not find to have been decided.

Maryland rule.— With respect to the rule in Maryland I am much in doubt. The court thus states a general proposition: “That it is always competent for parties sought to be bound or affected by a judgment or decree of any regularly organized court professing to have jurisdiction, whether of that or a sister state, to allege and show that such judgment or decree was rendered without competent jurisdiction. It is not, however, to be assumed that there was want of jurisdiction. The presump-

1 Thomas v. Ireland et al, 118, W. 28 Conn. 556; Coit v. Haven, 30 Conn. Rep. 633. 191.

2 Coit v. Haven, 30 Conn. 191. 4 Kimball v. Newport, 47 Vt. 38; Pearce v. Olney, 20 Conn. 544; Mussey v. White, 58 Vt. 45.

Bridgeport Savings Bank v. Eldridge, Porter v. Gile, 47 Vt. 620; Kimball v. Newport, 47 Vt. 38.

<br>

### --- Page 0247 ---

142 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 168.]

Sec. 168. Rule in California.— It is true that in many of the states the later proposition is not accepted, and it is held that in actions at law, in an action to enforce the judgment, the recitals as to jurisdictional facts is conclusive. This is the extent of the often-quoted decisions of the supreme court of California, and their ruling is based upon the theory that such method of attack is purely collateral. It was there held that a domestic judgment could be collaterally attacked only where want of jurisdiction affirmatively appeared by the judgment roll. That the record imparted absolute verity, and that extrinsic evidence could not be received to contradict it. The action was one to quiet title, based upon the decree sought to be impeached, and the statement of that court is that such a judgment when offered in evidence cannot be collaterally attacked. In a prior case the whole subject was reviewed. It was conceded that, under the code system of practice prevailing in that state, the defendant is allowed to set up as many defenses as he may have, regardless of the question whether they are of a legal or equitable nature. The action being upon a domestic judgment, it was said: “If there were any reasons founded either in law or equity why the plaintiff ought not to have the benefit of the judgment in suit, the defendant was at liberty to urge them in that action. That the simple question present was whether at common law, in an action of debt upon a judgment of a court of general jurisdiction, the defendant could show fraud or want of jurisdiction aliiu[e].” The court very emphatically held that the judgment could not be attacked collaterally on the ground of fraud, nor on the ground that the court had no jurisdiction, whether the supposed want of jurisdiction is alleged as an element of fraud or not, unless the want of jurisdiction appears upon the face of the record. The ground

Was that the judgment imports absolute verity, and its truth cannot be questioned either by showing otherwise than by the record itself that the court has no jurisdiction or that its jurisdiction was fraudulently procured. In view of the statement also expressed in the opinion, “Whether a party is to seek his remedy or avail himself of his defense in law or equity is no

MHill v. City Cab, etc. Co. 79 Cal 190, and cases cited. Carpentier v. City of Oakland, 30 Cal. 439.

Hahn v. Kelley, 34 Cal 391.

<br>

### --- Page 0248 ---

§ 168.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 143

longer a question, for we have but one forum, which combines all the powers of a court of law and a court of chancery, and exercise both through the same forum, and may exercise both in the same action. It would seem that but one conclusion can follow from the statement of the law thus declared in the opinion, and that is that such a judgment or the recitals therein cannot be attacked by a direct suit in equity, for fraud or want of jurisdiction, when the evidence relied upon is extrinsic to the record. Yet we find in later cases the doctrine declared, that if a judgment is obtained by the wrongful act of a person, without service of a summons, it is against good conscience to allow him to profit by his own wrong, even if the judgment recites it was served, and equity will interfere to prevent the party thus obtaining it from using it as an instrument of injustice. The action was one to quiet title under a decree. The defendant answered, setting up fraud in procuring the decree, and alleged that the recitals of service were false. The court held that it was proper to impeach the decree in that action by extrinsic evidence, oral in character, and stated that it would be a reproach to our jurisprudence if a court of equity could afford no relief in such a case. It is true the action was brought on the equity side of the court, but that fact was of no importance. Carpentier v. City of Oakland, supra, was also brought on that side of the court, and the decision there was not based upon the want of power of the court to grant the relief, but upon the ground that the matter stated was not ground for relief. If the doctrine of the California court is consistent in view of many subsequent decisions, it depends upon holding that in an action at law the judgment cannot be impeached by setting up the falsity of the recitals as an equitable defense, and it is hard to conclude in view of the language, with respect to defenses under the code, used in the Carpentier Case, that they are consistent even in this. It may be assumed that the element of fraud was the ground of distinction. The result and injury are no less damaging in cases of mistake than in cases of fraud. In Carpentier v. City of Oakland it was held that neither fraud nor failure of jurisdiction could be shown.

{Martin v. Parsons, 49 Cal. 94; 50 Cal. 493; Lapham v. Campbell, 61 Cal. 206,}

<br>

### --- Page 0249 ---

144 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 169.]

Sec. 169. Rule in Maine, Michigan and Minnesota.— It has been stated that the doctrine of the supreme court of Maine is to the effect that the recitals in such a judgment are conclusive and can be impeached only by the record itself. I do not find such to be the rule of that court, but rather that such recitals cannot be impeached upon collateral attack. The error in classification here, as in most cases, is found in failure to make the proper distinction between direct and collateral attack. That court states in the case cited that “the records of domestic courts of general jurisdiction cannot be contradicted by plea and proof, the same as the records of courts of limited jurisdiction and of foreign courts, when the purpose is to show want of jurisdiction. It can only be done when the proceedings are instituted for the express purpose of setting them aside. The code system not prevailing in this state, the record or recital could not be attacked in an action brought upon the judgment, and to this extent it is in opposition to the rule of the federal and some other courts which permit a want of jurisdiction to be shown in such cases.” So it has been claimed that the decisions of the supreme court of Michigan were to the effect that the recitals of a judgment could not be impeached. In an early case (Wilcow et al. v. Cassick, 2 Mich. 165) it was held that the record of a judgment of a sister state could not be contradicted unless such plea or proof would be received in an action on the judgment in the court in which it was rendered. Such a rule is now discarded by practically all courts. In the later case of Whitworth v. Pelton, 81 Mich. 98, it was simply held that the defendant in the original suit could not, in a garnishee action based upon the judgment, impeach the record as to service of process and appearance. That such in effect would be collateral attack. The most recent declaration of this court is that a judgment cannot be impeached in a collateral action by extrinsic evidence to show in contradiction of the record that service was not in fact made on the defendant. It was made in an action of ejectment, the plaintiff being the purchaser of certain lands sold upon execution against one Pierson. The service upon the defendant in the original action and his appearance were evidenced by an indorsement.

1 Penobscot R. Co. v. Weeks, 52 Me. 456. See Pratt v. Dow, 56 Me. 81.

<br>

### --- Page 0250 ---

§ 170.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 145

upon the summons "that the defendant hereby accepts service and will allow plaintiff to proceed with the case as though service had been made as commanded in said summons." On the second trial of the case the defendant sought to show that this acceptance of service was not in fact signed by the defendant in the original action. The sole question was whether it was competent to make this appear by parol testimony, and it was answered in the negative. It has also been claimed that the supreme court of Minnesota was one of the courts which held to the doctrine that the recitals of such a judgment could not be impeached. The fact is that it was there held that an action to set aside a judgment upon the ground of jurisdictional defects is not a collateral but a direct attack upon it, and that such an action in equity may be maintained to set aside a judgment upon the ground that the summons was never served upon the defendant and the officer’s return of service shown to be false, where the rights of bona fide purchasers under a judgment valid upon its face are not involved. Whether such an action could be maintained against such a purchaser is not decided. Such being the case, the defendant has a right, when an action is brought upon such a judgment, to set up his alleged cause of action to have the judgment canceled in his answer by way of counter-claim?

Sec. 170. Rule in Ohio.—In Ohio it was said that in an action under the code to enforce a judgment a cross-petition may be filed and the judgment attacked for fraud in the same way that it might be by an original proceeding for that purpose. Hence, a judgment could be thus attacked for failure of jurisdiction. The inquiry, therefore, in that state should be directed to ascertain whether in an action in equity a bill would lie to set aside and vacate a domestic judgment of a court of general jurisdiction by reason of a lack of jurisdiction to render it, and the further question whether in such an action the recital of jurisdictional facts may be impeached. That such an action will lie is without question, but whether the recitals may be impeached is not precisely clear. We are met here

1Allured v. Voller, 70 N. W. Rep. 
2Conway et al. v. Duncan, 28 Ohio St. 1037.  
3Vaule v. Miller, 72 N. W. Rep. 452.

<br>

### --- Page 0251 ---

143 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 1v0.

again with the question of collateral attack, and the whole force of the decisions of this state depends upon that question. It is the settled law of that state in respect to such judgments that where it appears from the record that the court has positively found the facts on which its jurisdiction legally rests, the jurisdiction may not afterwards be collaterally questioned; that though the judgment may be or may have been voidable when attacked in a direct proceeding for that purpose, it is not and may not be shown collaterally void. There is an element here stated not involved in the cases before considered, to wit: that the court had judicially passed upon the jurisdictional facts. It appears, however, though perhaps negatively, that even in such a case the record could be impeached in a direct proceeding for that purpose. Such a direct proceeding must, it would seem, include an action in equity to set aside the judgment or a cross-bill praying directly for the same relief. In the last-cited cases the action was in equity, the one to restrain the defendant from taking possession of certain lands under a decree rendered in condemnation proceedings, and the other was an action at law to recover certain lands in which the defendant answered, setting up title under a judicial decree. It was held in each that such proceedings were collateral, and that the record, which contained an adjudication that the defendants were non-residents, which justified the order of publication, could not be impeached by plea or proof that at such time the parties were residents and were not personally served.

The whole subject has been very recently reviewed in a very able opinion. It became necessary to define the phrases “direct attack” and “collateral attack.” The former was defined to be one by which the judgment is directly assailed in some mode authorized by law, while a collateral attack is an attempt to defeat the operation of a judgment in a proceeding where some new right derived from or through the judgment is involved. The court proceeds to say that “two seemingly conflicting principles have given rise in their application to differences of judicial opinion concerning the effect of judicial records. The one accords to judgments of courts having apparent jurisdiction of

1 Callen v. Ellison, 13 Ohio St. 466; 178; Railroad Co. v. Village of Bell
Hammond v. Davenport, 16 Ohio St. Cent. 48 Ohio St. 278

<br>

### --- Page 0252 ---

§170.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 147

the subject-matter and parties in an action conclusive verity as to all matters purporting to have been adjudicated, and precludes a re-examination of them in subsequent litigation between the parties and their privies; and the other asserts the right to be heard before he can be condemned in his person or property. In accordance with the former, the rule generally prevails, and is nowhere more firmly established than in this state, that when it does not otherwise affirmatively appear from the record it will be conclusively presumed, whenever a domestic judgment of a court of general jurisdiction is drawn in question in any collateral way, that the court regularly acquired and lawfully exercised its jurisdiction over the parties; and the record of an inferior court imports absolute verity when it shows on its face that such jurisdiction was obtained, and neither the presumptions nor recitals in the record can be contradicted in such a proceeding by extrinsic evidence. Where the record discloses a want of jurisdiction the judgment is, of course, void everywhere and for every purpose. On the other hand, the other principle referred to is sometimes employed to justify the broad statement that in all cases, even when the judgment is collaterally assailed, the jurisdiction of the court rendering it may be inquired into, and a want of service, or the absence of other jurisdictional facts, be shown by parol in opposition to the recitals of the records; for while there is some conflict of the decisions as to whether, where there is a presumption of service and jurisdiction, or where these appear on the face of the record, it can be shown in a collateral proceeding that in fact no service was had, the authorities are substantially uniform to the effect that, when the judgment is directly attacked for want of jurisdiction, such want of service and jurisdiction may be shown, though in contradiction of the record. But here again the authorities differ as to what constitutes an attack of that nature. It is conceded that it includes proceedings in error, motions to vacate or modify the judgment, and in some cases it may be made by original action, as was formerly done by Dill in chancery to vacate or set aside the judgment. The rule which forbids the collateral impeachment of judgments is founded on those considerations of public policy which require stability of judicial records for the protection of those who acquire rights and property in reliance upon their conclusive.

<br>

### --- Page 0253 ---

148 IMPEACHMENT, - [§ 171.

effect and for the peace of society. It is obvious these reasons find no just application in an action brought upon the judgment in which the only relief is a new recovery on it, and where no rights of third persons have intervened or are involved. Nor in such case would there appear to be any substantial ground for denying to the defendant the right to show in defense to the action that the judgment was rendered without jurisdiction. Such a defense may, we think, be properly regarded as a direct attack upon the judgment, and not within the rule or its reason against collateral impeachment. In the particular case the defendant was a non-resident, and the court recognizes the binding force of the decision of the federal supreme court referred to (Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714; Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185). Notice will be taken that this case goes no further than to decide that, in an action upon a judgment, a want of jurisdiction to render it may be shown as a defense, and recitals are not conclusive. It does not hold that in proceedings to enforce rights which have accrued under such a judgment the record or the presumptions that attach can be impeached by extrinsic evidence.

Sec. 171. Rule in Nebraska and Iowa.—In Nebraska, in the early case of Johnson v. Jones, 2 Neb. 126, it was held that such a judgment could not be impeached by extrinsic evidence. This was stated by another court to have been mere dictum, and bad dictum, too. Yet, subsequently and very recently, they have declared a contrary rule, and seem to have had before them the same state of facts that was presented to the Ohio court, and reached an opposite conclusion. Thus, where judgment of foreclosure was entered upon default, upon a substituted service by publication, the order being based upon an affidavit that the defendant was a non-resident, it was held, in an action by the heir of the mortgagor to redeem, that the judgment could be attacked by showing the averments of the affidavit to be false, and that the mortgagor was in fact, at the time of making such order, a resident of the state. So also, where property was seized and sold under an attachment based upon non-residence, constructive service only being made,

Kingsbury v. Tousley, 47 N.E. Rep. 27; Eayrs v. Nason, 74 N.W. Rep. 403.

<br>

### --- Page 0254 ---

§ 171.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 149

It was held that the adjudication made could be impeached in a subsequent proceeding between the same parties by proof of the fact that the debtor was a resident of the state at the time of such service, though the record in the attachment suit showed acts and facts sufficient to constitute jurisdiction.

“In Iowa the doctrine of the New York court, as herein stated, meets with approval, though the reasoning may not be precisely the same. The supreme court of that state say that they believe it to be a generally recognized doctrine, that a party whose rights are affected by a judgment void for any cause, or even erroneous, which he could not reverse or review upon appeal or writ of error, might show its invalidity or error in any action whenever it is brought into question. They hold that such judgments may be attacked in proceedings to enforce them. That the attack in such cases is in the nature of a direct attack, and it is permissible to show that the court rendering the judgment had no jurisdiction, notwithstanding a recital in the judgment that the defendant was duly and legally served with notice of the pendency of the suit. That when, in such an action, the defendant by proper answer denies that he was served with notice, the defense is not a collateral but a direct attack upon the judgment. That such was the rule, where the action was brought directly to set aside the judgment, had been frequently declared in that state, and was approved in a very recent case. It seems that a section of the code of that state (sec. 8154, Code 1875) provides for moving to set aside a judgment for irregularity at the second term after rendered, and for want of jurisdiction or fraud within one year. It was held that a motion to vacate a judgment for the latter cause was a collateral attack (why, I know not), but that an adjudication that an affidavit had been filed (which was a condition prerequisite to jurisdiction) was subject to attack in such a proceeding to vacate the judgment. It was held in that state that where the answer set up fraud in procuring the preemption certificate, in an action to quiet title based thereon,

1 Bolse v. Kautter, 75 N. W. Rep. 566; McGahen v. Cart, 6 Iowa, 331;  
2 Bixby v. Adams County, 49 Iowa, 507. Hodson v. Tebbetts, 16 Iowa, 97; Newcombe v. Dewey, 27 Iowa, 381.  
3 Clark v. Little, 41 Iowa, 497; State v. Day, 104 Iowa, 374.  
4 Ina Co. v. Waterhouse et al., 43 N.W. Rep. 611.  
5 Priestman v. Priestman, 103 Iowa, 320.

<br>

### --- Page 0255 ---

150 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 172.

such answer was in the nature of a cross-petition, and was in fact and in law a direct attack upon the judgment of the county judge in issuing such certificate in a proceeding brought to establish its fraudulent character. It was, however, held in another action that a domestic judgment could not be impeached collaterally for fraud, and in still another that it is not competent to collaterally attack a judgment on the ground of fraud in its procurement, when it is offered in another action as an instrument of evidence only. An action was brought in that state to procure a decree of divorce. The defendant pleaded in bar a decree of divorce obtained by him in the district court of that state in another county. The plaintiff filed a replication setting up the fact that the decree obtained by the defendant was procured by fraud, and without service or actual notice to her. The question was whether the decree could thus be impeached. The court at the outset made the distinction that it was not an attempt to impeach the judgment collaterally, but rather it was made the basis of the defendant’s defense. Then it proceeds to declare that there is no reason for the distinction recognized by some courts between foreign judgments and domestic judgments in respect to impeachment for fraud upon collateral attack, and concludes by stating that it was useless to require the plaintiff to go into the court where the defendant's decree was obtained, and there seek relief, either by an application in the original proceeding or by a direct proceeding in equity.

§ 172. Rule in Wisconsin.—The supreme court of Wisconsin evidently incline to the view that whatever may be set up in an action to vacate or set aside a judgment may be set up by way of a defense in an action upon the judgment, as they state the latter is an equivalent proceeding. It is true the judgment under consideration was that of a sister state, but the language, in the connection in which it was used, makes it very evident that its literal sense as used was intended, to wit: That an action brought to impugn the judgment,

1 Bixby v. Adams County, 49 Iowa, 407. Smith et al. v. Smith et al., 22 Iowa, 516.
2 Mason v. Messenger & May, 17 Iowa, 261. 
4 Whetstone v. Whetstone, 31 Iowa, 276.
5 Brown v. Parker, 28 Wis. 21.

<br>

### --- Page 0256 ---

§ 1720.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 151

and an answer in an action brought to enforce the judgment, are in fact and in law equivalent proceedings, whether the judgment be a domestic or a foreign one, and that what would avail in the one case would be equally available in the other. Thus, in an action of ejectment, the plaintiff was permitted to show that the decree upon which the defendant's title was based was void for want of proper service of the summons, and it was held that, if it was necessary, the recitals in the decree could be impeached by extrinsic evidence. The court adopted the principles stated in the former case of Pape v. Heaton, 9 Wis. 328, in substance, that in all cases where a decree or judgment is relied upon as the foundation of a legal right, the court may inquire into the facts which by law are made necessary to the jurisdiction of the court or tribunal by which it was pronounced, and, if it appears that such facts did not exist, disregard such judgment or decree as unauthorized and void. In the able argument which follows this declaration, the doctrine and reasoning of the New York court in Starbuck v. Murray, 5 Wend. 148, meets with approval.

Sec. 172a. Rule in Indiana and Illinois.—In Indiana the question under consideration has been many times before its court, and I frankly confess that after much labor and research I have been unable to harmonize the varied expressions of that court. First, they define collateral attack, and state that “where a party to a judgment or decree seeks to impeach its validity and have it declared void, in a subsequent action, by the allegation of facts dehors the record of such judgment or decree and not apparent on its face, such an attack upon its validity is a collateral attack.” Second, they broadly state that it is settled by many decisions of that court that such an attack upon the validity of a judgment or decree cannot be made by a party to the record. It therefore logically follows that the record or recitals in a judgment are conclusive verities in all proceedings in equity to set aside and vacate the judgment; that the party is absolutely without remedy outside of the

Pollard v. Wegner, 13 Wis. 569.  
Exchange Bank v. Ault, 102 Ind. 322; Reid v. Mitchell, 93 Ind. 469;  
Roger v. Beauchamp, 102 Ind. 33.

<br>

### --- Page 0257 ---

102 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 192a.

action in which the judgment was obtained. They state in another case that it is settled law that a proceeding to enjoin the enforcement of a judgment or decree by execution or decretal order is a collateral attack upon the judgment, and cannot be maintained for mere error or irregularities, but only by showing that the judgment or decree, or the part thereof the enforcement of which is sought to be enjoined, is void. The presumption is that the part of the decree sought to be impeached, being rendered by a court of general jurisdiction, is valid, and unless the facts stated in the complaint are sufficient to overcome or exclude this presumption a demurrer thereto should be sustained. It might reasonably be inferred from such statement standing by itself that if a complaint should affirmatively show by statement of facts that the recitals in the judgment were untrue, and that no jurisdiction in fact was obtained, this would be sufficient to overcome the presumption which attaches to the record; yet such is not their view of the law as declared in recent cases. They say that where a party seeks by complaint to impeach the validity of a judgment rendered by a court of superior jurisdiction on the ground that he had no legal notice of the pendency of the action wherein it was rendered, he must allege what, if anything, is shown by the record of the cause in relation to the issue and service of process therein. Facts must be stated such as will overcome or exclude all presumption in favor of the jurisdiction; hence if the record recites the essential jurisdictional facts the action cannot be maintained.

We now reach a class of cases in which we contend the foregoing rule was disregarded and a rule recognized which is more in accord with well-settled law elsewhere. It should be stated here that this court is in accord with the courts of New York, Wisconsin and other states upon the proposition that what would be ground for relief in an original bill in equity may be set up by way of cross-bill or as an equitable defense. Thus, in an action to quiet title to real estate, any matter of defense,

1 Davis v. Clements, 47 N. E. Rep. 1056, and cases cited.  
2 Davis v. Clements, 47 N. E. Rep.  
3 Same case.  
4 Hogg v. Link, 90 Ind. 346; Cavanaugh v. Smith, 84 Ind. 380.  
5 Cassidy v. Miller, 106 Ind. 69.  
6 Cassidy v. Miller, 106 Ind. 69; Ex-

<br>

### --- Page 0258 ---

§ 1f2a.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 153

either legal or equitable, may be given in evidence under a general denial, and fraud in obtaining a judgment is an equitable defense to such judgment. In an action based upon a judgment of that state, the defendant answered that the defendant in the original action was not a resident of the state of Indiana when the action was commenced against him, nor for two years prior to that time, and did not become a resident of the state until some years after the judgment was rendered; that he had no notice of the pendency of the action; that none was given by publication; that appellee knew that he was not a resident of the state; that with this knowledge she fraudulently procured the sheriff to leave a copy of the summons at the residence of one Henry McKendree, which was not at the time and never had been the residence of the appellant; that the appellee procured the sheriff to make a return that he had served the summons by leaving a copy at the last and usual place of residence of the appellant. It was said: “The answer does not attack the judgment set forth in the complaint on the ground of irregularity or of error, but on the ground that the court had no jurisdiction of the person of the defendant. It is true beyond all question that where it is made to appear in a proper manner that there was no jurisdiction of the person, the judgment will not be simply erroneous, but absolutely void. Where a judgment is erroneous it cannot be attacked collaterally, but where it is void a collateral attack will prevail.” It will be observed that here the judgment was sought to be impeached by matters dehors the record in an action where the extrinsic matter was pleaded as a defense, and where also the record, if it had been pleaded, showed facts which gave the court jurisdiction, and which necessarily must be impeached by extrinsic evidence if the defensive matter was permitted to stand. Certain it is that the declarations of this court in the cases first cited are not in harmony with the adjudication here. The court proceeds to state, and speaking with reference to the conclusive effect of a sheriff's return: “If we regard the sheriff's return as conclusive, then, of course, the appellant has no right to make the question of jurisdiction or no jurisdiction. The case is, however, not the ordinary one of a collateral attack upon the sheriff's return,

M Hogg v. Link, 90 Ind. 346; Cavanaugh et al. v. Smith, 84 Ind. 380.

<br>

### --- Page 0259 ---

154 IMPEACHMENT, § 172.

for two important elements are here present which are absent from the cases holding the return conclusive. One is, that it affirmatively appears that the defendant was not a resident of the state, and the other that the return of service was procured by the fraudulent act of the plaintiff. But those things did not affirmatively appear from the record. They were merely affirmative allegations in the pleading. The court proceeds to state the effect of the allegation with respect to procuring a false return, in substance, that such act, resulting in fraudulently creating the appearance of jurisdiction, takes the case out of the general rule. That it would be contrary to all principle to allow a plaintiff to insist that a false return secured by his own fraud should conclude his adversary from asserting the truth. With respect to the method of attack they state: “It must be borne in mind that the defendant does not stop short of an assault upon the jurisdiction. If he did, then perhaps he would be driven to some other attack. If he establishes the position assumed, that there was no jurisdiction, then he proves the absolute nullity of the judgment, for it is settled that, where there is no jurisdiction of the person, no valid personal judgment can be rendered. We think it clear that one who shows that there was no jurisdiction of the person is not driven to direct attack, but may assail the judgment wherever it confronts him.”

In Logg v. Link, supra, it is stated that “fraud in the procurement of a judgment is an extrinsic and collateral fact, and constitutes ground for a direct attack upon the judgment by a party thereto by an application corresponding to an original bill in equity.” “A party may impeach a judgment collaterally for fraud in obtaining it, under the code, which permits equitable defenses.” In an earlier case, decided in 1864, that court stated a rule as follows: “Where a judgment is recovered in a court of general jurisdiction against a defendant, and the record shows an attorney of the court appeared for the defendant and filed an answer, the jurisdiction of the court cannot be controverted unless it be by proof of fraud or that the defendant was not a citizen of the state, nor, during the pendency of the proceedings, within the jurisdiction of the court in which the judgment was rendered, and had neither been notified of the pendency of the suit, nor had given authority to the attorney

<br>

### --- Page 0260 ---

§ 120] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 155  
to enter an appearance for him.” The rule is stated to be in that state that a judgment is not subject to collateral attack unless its invalidity appears upon the face of the record. If it does not so appear, it is voidable merely — a rule which is recognized in several jurisdictions, but is not so far reaching in any other, owing to the peculiar doctrine of Indiana respecting what constitutes collateral attack. It appears that in earlier cases that an action directly attacking a judgment on the ground that a defendant was not a resident of the township, even where the evidence was extrinsic, was held a direct attack upon the judgment, and that, upon such a direct attack, proof of the want of jurisdiction over the person and the nullity of the summons may be made, notwithstanding a recital in the record that the defendant was duly served with process. The distinction between collateral attack and direct proceedings to annul and vacate a judgment was stated to be: “If, according to the facts averred in the complaint, the judgments sought to be enjoined are void, the complaint must be held sufficient. If the judgments are merely irregular or erroneous, though reversible on appeal, the complaint must be held insufficient.”

The rule in Illinois is to the effect that the record or recitals cannot be impeached in a collateral proceeding. “It is,” they say, “a fundamental rule of evidence that the record of a court can never be contradicted, varied or explained by evidence beyond or outside the record itself. The record in one part may contradict another part, or one part may limit, qualify or explain another, but evidence dehors the record will never be received for the purpose. To permit such evidence would render records of no avail and would render judicial sentences of but little protection, and would unsettle rights and lead to unprecedented uncertainty and confusion. Hence all records must be tried and construed by themselves. Whilst the court may hear evidence in cases of service by publication, either by the certificate of the printer or by witnesses to prove that the court has jurisdiction and may rightfully proceed and adjudicate in the case, it would be unprecedented, where the record

Wiley v. Pratt, 23 Ind. 628; Brickley v. Heilbrunner, 7 Ind. 438;  
Indianapolis, etc. R. Co. v. Harm- Grass v. Hess, 37 Ind. 193,  
Tess. 124 Ind. 25; Earl v. Matheney, 60 Ind. 202;  
Johnson v. Ramsey, 91 Ind. 189; Johnson v. Ramsey, 91 Ind. 159.

<br>

### --- Page 0261 ---

156 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 12a.

failed to show jurisdiction, to permit oral proof, or, for that matter, any other kind of evidence, to show in a collateral proceeding that the court in fact did have jurisdiction. No such precedent can be found, and if it could it would not be followed. . . . These reasons apply with all their force to prevent proof from being heard to contradict the finding of the court that it had jurisdiction. That such a record in a collateral proceeding imparts verity and cannot be contradicted is too well established to be questioned.” Where the line is drawn in this state between a direct and collateral attack, I have not been able to discover. Where a creditor's bill was predicated upon a judgment, and the answer set up the want of jurisdiction of the person in defense, and also by cross-bill, and it was disclosed that an attorney appeared for the defendant and interposed a demurrer, and also that he had no authority to enter such an appearance, it was held sufficient to avoid the judgment on the ground that the court had not jurisdiction. It was stated by the court that it would be a dangerous precedent to hold a judgment valid where there had been no service, merely for the reason that an attorney had appeared on his own motion without authority and had filed some sort of a plea in the case. In Higgins v. Curtiss, 82 Ill. 28, where a creditor’s bill was filed to enforce payment of a judgment of the county court allowing a claim against an estate, it was held that the administratrix, who was sole devisee, might show that the judgment was fraudulent and inequitable; and in Griggs v. Gear, 3 Gilm. 2, it was held that chancery would afford relief where the party’s appearance had been entered without authority. An action or suit in equity to set aside a deed founded upon a judicial decree as a cloud upon title was held to be a collateral attack and the recitals of the judgment could not be impeached. It was stated in Follansbee v. Scottish American Mort. Co. 7 I. App. 486: “It is a well-established principle that a judgment rendered without jurisdiction is void and may be attacked collaterally in any proceedings in which its validity may be called in question. This principle is in no way affected by the fact that the defendant has a right to have such void judgment reversed on appeal or error. It is Barnett v. Wolf, 70 Ill. 76; Harris v. Lester, 80 Ill. 307; Anderson v. Hawhe, 115 Ill. 33; Swift v. Vanaway, 153 Ill. 202.

<br>

### --- Page 0262 ---

§ 1724.) COLLATERAL ATTACK, 157

clearly within the jurisdiction of courts of equity to vacate or enjoin judgments at law which by reason of fraud, want of jurisdiction in the court before which they were rendered, or any other circumstance, it would be inequitable and against good conscience to have enforced.” Such general statements of correct legal propositions do not always meet the question. The question in this state is not alone whether equity will set aside a judgment on the ground of want of jurisdiction, but is also whether in such an action the recitals of the decree may be impeached or contradicted by extrinsic evidence. More particularly the question is whether it can be shown that the court was without power to make the recital or the decree. The supreme court, however, has stated that the court of chancery is entirely competent to grant relief against a judgment at law which has been rendered by a court not having jurisdiction over the person of the defendant, especially where the record of the court at law does not affirmatively show the facts which would confer such jurisdiction. That while a judgment is conclusive upon parties and privies and cannot be impeached, still in equity it may be vacated and set aside where it has been procured by collusion. The defense of fraud has been permitted in an action of assumpsit where by special plea the defendant set up the judgment.

Sec. 172. Rule in New Jersey, Massachusetts, Tennessee, South Carolina, Washington and Kentucky.—In New Jersey the rule stated by Judge Redfield in Donich v. Brook, 21 Vt. 578, was approved as follows: “It is of the very essence of debt upon judgment, or upon any matter of record, that the obligation should result from the record itself. The record imparts absolute and complete verity. It is neither to be increased or diminished by averment out of or beyond the record. It is to the record as the law and the testimony which the pleader refers his claim.” This doctrine was applied only to excluding, in an action upon a judgment, the defense that the defendant was a non-resident of the state and was not personally served. It will be seen that such was the rule in Massachusetts subsequent to the decision of the federal court that

Propst v. Meadows, 13 Ill. 157. Carr v. Miner, 42 Ill. 179.  
Field et al. v. Flanders, 40 Ill. 470. Miller v. Dungan, 35 N.J. L. 389.

<br>

### --- Page 0263 ---

172.

(7A)

153 IMPEACHMENT, [

judgments 72 personam rendered against a non-resident, without personal service, were void. Such service not being due process of law, the rule in that state was changed.

In Massachusetts, originally, the doctrine was asserted to be that a domestic judgment of a court of common-law jurisdiction, to which a writ of error will lie, is valid as between the parties until reversed, notwithstanding a failure to obtain, by proper process, jurisdiction of the person of the party against whom it was rendered; that such judgments could be impeached by parties thereto only by proceedings instituted directly for that purpose. Subsequently, however, the supreme court of the United States, in Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714, and in Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185, decided that a judgment in personam against a person who is not a resident of the state, who has not been personally served with process, and who has not appeared, is wholly void, and that no suit can be maintained upon it either in the same or in another court. The court has no jurisdiction and its judgment has no force. The Massachusetts court thereafter held that, in a suit upon such a judgment, the defendant may show by plea and proof that the judgment was invalid, that is, that he was a non-resident of the state, and no personal service of process was had. However, as the reason for the change of ruling by this court was the declaration of the federal court, it is not to be assumed that the general principle announced in prior cases as prevailing in that state is changed only with respect to cases of the character stated. It must follow that this rule must now prevail in all the states, the supreme court of the United States having thus declared authoritatively that substituted service in personal actions is not due process of law, without reference to the rule any particular state may have declared in respect to the subject. What effect such decision of the federal court necessarily has upon the peculiar rule declared in Wisconsin is perhaps not clear. There the court had decided that in proceedings by attachment it was not essential to jurisdiction that property be subjected to possession under the writ; that so

1 Cook v. Darling, 18 Pick.
Hendrick v. Wittemore, 105 Mass.

* Hendrick v. Wittemore, 105 Mass.
23.

3 Needham v. Thayer, 147 Mass. 536.
4 Jarvis v. Barrett, 14 Wis. 591.

<br>

### --- Page 0264 ---

8 [12h] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 159

Long as it appeared from the affidavit that a non-resident defendant had property in the state liable to attachment, this was sufficient to confer jurisdiction; and that thereafter in the proceeding the court could render a personal judgment, which might be satisfied out of property of the defendant in the state, though he in fact was not personally served with process and did not appear. The writer is of the opinion that any such judgment would be absolutely void, and its invalidity might be shown in any proceeding wherever the defendant was confronted with it.

The doctrine of the courts of Tennessee is perhaps as stringent as that pronounced by any court in favor of the proposition that such a judgment is not subject to attack. It assumes at the outset that an action in equity brought to set aside and vacate a prior judgment is a collateral attack, seemingly being of the same mind as the Indiana court as to what constitutes a collateral attack, to wit: That any attack grounded upon evidence dehors the record is a collateral attack, whether it directly attacks the judgment or merely seeks to avoid it. A bill was filed to impeach the decree of a court of general jurisdiction, directing the sale of land for delinquent taxes, on the ground of want of jurisdiction in the court. There was no question with respect to jurisdiction of the parties. It was rather a question of want of jurisdiction of the proceeding, it being urged that, by reason of taxes being delinquent also for a subsequent year, the proceedings were controlled by another provision of statute. The court state that no such action will lie where no lack of jurisdiction is apparent in the record; that the decree of such a court cannot be collaterally questioned except for want of authority over the matter adjudicated upon, and this want of authority must be found in the record itself. And again, — citing many cases, — that it has been held settled law in that state, deemed indispensable to the sanctity of judicial proceedings necessary for the public welfare, that domestic judgments of courts of general jurisdiction cannot be collaterally attacked by evidence outside of the record itself. The court further held, that “though the existence of any jurisdictional fact may not be affirmed in the record, it will be presumed upon collateral attack that the court has acted correctly.

<br>

### --- Page 0265 ---

160 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 172d.

and with due authority. In this case it will be noticed that no contradiction of the record or recitals was involved. The evidence dehors the record proposed was as to the fact of subsequent delinquent taxes. The only question to be determined was one of law.

The South Carolina court holds to the doctrine that such a judgment cannot be attacked in any action unless the want of jurisdiction appears upon the face of the record. The law in that state is declared to be that the proper and only mode of attacking a judgment for any defect not appearing upon the face of the record is by motion in the cause and before the court in which the judgment was obtained and entered; and under this rule it cannot be shown in an independent action that the court was without jurisdiction by reason of the party not being a resident of the proper county.

In Washington it is the rule that where the judgment itself recites that the defendants were duly and regularly cited to appear, that they made default in that behalf, and that the default was duly and regularly entered, nothing appearing in the record to contradict the recital, it is an adjudication upon the point, as conclusive upon all parties and their privies as the determination of any other fact in the case. Hence it was held that in an action of foreclosure, where the answer set up an adverse title by purchase under a decree of sale of the land for taxes, the plaintiff could not impeach the judgment for matters not appearing upon the face of the record.

In Kentucky an action in equity will not lie to set aside and vacate a judgment on the ground that the defendant was not served with process, where the return of the officer is to the effect that service was made. The return of the officer in such a case being the subject of impeachment, the attack, even in such an action, is collateral, and the judgment cannot be impeached upon collateral attack by extrinsic evidence. If the false return had been procured by the defendants, or was with

1 Reinhart v. Nealis, 46 S. W. Rep. 3; Kiser v. Canfield, 49 Pac. Rep. 446. 1064.

2 Crocker v. Allen, 34 S. C. 452; Thomas et ux. v. Ireland et al, 11 Drake v. Steadman, 46 S. C. 474; S. W. Rep. 653, and cases cited. Reese v. Mutze, 29 S. E. Rep. 73.

<br>

### --- Page 0266 ---

§ 17 2c.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 161

their knowledge, equity would set aside the judgment, but the fraud of the husband and the sheriff was held not sufficient.

Sec. 172c. Rule in other states — Connecticut and Vermont. — In Connecticut the rule is that jurisdictional facts, such as the service of the writ and the like, are presumed, and conclusively presumed, in the case of a domestic court of general jurisdiction, unless the record itself shows to the contrary. Hence the record of recitals cannot be attacked or disproved. Such judgments can only be reviewed by proceedings instituted directly for the purpose and reversed on error, or by a new trial, and, in cases where the danger is imminent and special, relief may be obtained in equity. It seems, however, that such special relief does not include a suit in equity to vacate and set aside the judgment, but extends to the prevention of an inequitable use of a judgment or the enforcement of a judgment that may thus be rendered inequitable.

In Vermont the record is an absolute verity and the recitals cannot be disproved. Yet it seems that, in a direct proceeding to set aside the judgment, any matters which would show the judgment to have been unfairly obtained, and coming under grounds generally for equitable relief, may be shown; but whether such a judgment could be set aside and vacated solely on the ground of want of service, to be established solely by extrinsic evidence in contradiction of the record, is a question which I do not find to have been decided.

Maryland rule. — With respect to the rule in Maryland I am much in doubt. The court thus states a general proposition: “That it is always competent for parties sought to be bound or affected by a judgment or decree of any regularly organized court professing to have jurisdiction, whether of that or a sister state, to allege and show that such judgment or decree was rendered without competent jurisdiction. It is not, however, to be assumed that there was want of jurisdiction. The presumption—

1 Thomas v. Ireland et al. 11 S. W. 28 Conn. 556; Coit v. Haven, 30 Conn. Rep. 653, 191. 
2 Coit v. Haven, 30 Conn. 191. 
3 Kimball v. Newport, 47 Vt. 88; Pearce v. Olney, 20 Conn. 544; Mussey v. White, 58 Vt. 45. 
Bridgeport Savings Bank v. Eldridge, 
Porter v. Gile, 47 Vt. 620; Kimball v. Newport, 47 Vt. 388.

<br>

### --- Page 0267 ---

162 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 172e.

tion is the other way, and the party seeking to avoid the affect of the judgment or decree must allege and show affirmatively that it was rendered without jurisdiction, whether the want of power be in defect of process or otherwise. Other statements are that errors or defects in the proceedings where the court has jurisdiction must be corrected by some direct proceeding before it, or by appeal. That the general principle is recognized that a judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction, when coming incidentally in question or offered as evidence of title in any court, is conclusive upon the question decided, and cannot be impeached on the ground of informality of the proceedings or error or mistake of the court in the matter which has been adjudicated. Again, “A judgment rendered without jurisdiction is absolutely void and may be assailed at all times and in all proceedings by which it is sought to be enforced.” The courts of this state are among those which give in fact as well as in theory the same faith and credit to the judgments of sister states as is accorded to such a judgment in the state where rendered; and have found no case where the court has made any distinction between domestic judgments and those of sister states with respect to the method of impeachment or their conclusive effect. Where the judgment was that of a sister state, they state: “We think it is now clearly settled that such a judgment is utterly void where the court rendering it had acquired no jurisdiction over the person of the defendant, and that this objection may be set up either in a case where an action is brought upon the judgment itself, or where it is made the foundation of an attachment against the property of the defendant situated in the state of his residence.” Whatever has thus been stated by the court is equally consistent with the theory that such want of jurisdiction may be shown by extrinsic evidence, or that it cannot thus be shown. We have no definition of collateral attack. Perhaps the feature that indicates that the question of jurisdiction is open to inquiry and may be the subject of proof dehors the record is 

1 Glenn v. Williams, 60 Md. 93, 4 Hanley et al. v. Donaghue, 59 Md. 254. 
2 Manton et al. v. Hoyt et al. 43 Md. 289, 
3 Greshon et al. v. Thomas, 20 Md. 234. 
4 Grover & Baker Sewing Machine Co. v. Radcliff, 66 Md. 511. 
5 Grover & Baker Sewing Machine Co. v. Radcliff, 66 Md. 511.

<br>

### --- Page 0268 ---

§ 172¢.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 163

the statement in Manton v. Hoyt, supra, that errors and irregularities merely must be corrected by some direct proceeding when the court has jurisdiction. It can reasonably be inferred that a resort to such proceeding is not required where the question of jurisdiction is involved.

Missouri rule.— The rule which prevails in Missouri is stated to be that a domestic judgment rendered by a court of general jurisdiction cannot be impeached by the parties to it, merely because the record is silent as to the acquisition of jurisdiction. Such a judgment is equally as conclusive on the parties thereto, whether it recites or whether it fails to recite that jurisdiction has been acquired. That parol testimony is wholly inadmissible. The courts of this state also hold that an action in equity to set aside and vacate a judgment for want of jurisdiction is a collateral attack upon the judgment; and further, that where it appears upon the face of the proceedings that they are void, an action in equity will not lie, as the party has an adequate remedy in actions at law. It is, however, held that the recital in the judgment that the defendant was duly served with summons may be contradicted in a collateral proceeding by showing from the return itself that legal service was not made.
