The return of the sheriff is as much a part of the record as the judgment entry. The recital of the service contained in the judgment cannot impart greater verity than the return itself. Such recitals will be deemed to refer to the kind of service shown by other parts of the record.

Texas rule.— In Texas it is the rule that the same conclusive effect is given to judgments rendered in domestic courts of general jurisdiction upon constructive service as to those rendered upon personal service. That a like presumption will obtain in the one case as in the other. That in all such cases, in order for a collateral attack upon the jurisdiction of the court and the validity of the judgment to prevail, it must affirmatively appear that the facts essential to the jurisdiction did not in

McClanahan et al. v. West et al. 3 Hardy v. Beatty, 19 S.W. Rep. 13S. W. Rep. 674. Stewart v. Anderson, 70 Tex. Rep. 831, and cases cited; Cloud v. Pierce City, 86 Mo. 353.

Laney v. Garbee et al. 165 W. S90.

<br>

### --- Page 0269 ---

164 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 172.

fact exist. That the law presumes that an affidavit, upon which the order of citation was based, was made, and such presumption will exist until the contrary is affirmatively shown by something contained in the record. In fact, the court goes further, and holds that if the affidavit filed is so defective in its execution or otherwise as not to constitute an affidavit within the requirements of the statute, the presumption will attach that the court ascertained all facts necessary to the exercise of its jurisdiction. A recital in the judgment of such a court of due service of process does not impart absolute verity on a collateral attack where the judgment itself recites the facts upon which the conclusion of due service was predicated and such facts show want of such service. This is hardly consistent with what was held with respect to proceedings of a justice, in effect that, where the record showed that service could not have been had, it was insufficient to impeach a recital of due service, and also that a recital could not be impeached by the return of the citation.

Arkansas rule.— The question under consideration was ably reviewed in a recent case by the supreme court of Arkansas (Boyd v. Roane, 5 S.W. Rep. 704), and the conclusion reached that the weight of authority in case of collateral attack of a domestic judgment upon the question of notice was to the effect that it must be tried by the court upon an inspection of the record only. That extrinsic evidence was not permissible. That the presumption of service which is raised by the record is as conclusive as the recital of service and cannot be contradicted by evidence aliunde. An action in equity to set aside a decree of foreclosure upon the ground of want of service upon infant defendants was held to be a collateral attack.

Sec. 172d. Judgments of federal courts — Not domestic.— It was held by Deady, J., in Swit v. Myers et al., 37 Fed. Rep. 37, that the record of a court cannot be impeached or contradicted except by a suit in equity brought for the purpose of

1 Hardy v. Beatty, 19 S.W. Rep. 778; Williams v. Hays, 13 S.W. Rep. 1029.  
2 Trapwell v. Bank, 18 Ark. 53, See also Cato v. Stewart, 28 Ark. 146.  
5 Boyd v. Roane, 5 S.W. Rep. 704.

<br>

### --- Page 0270 ---

§ 172¢.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 165

setting aside a judgment, on the ground that in fact the court never acquired jurisdiction to give the same. This ruling is evidently in conflict with express language of the supreme court of the United States in the cases cited, though it may be in accord with the cases where the language has been applied. If it be conceded that such an action is a direct attack, then of course the decision is that such judgments are not subject to collateral attack on the ground of failure of jurisdiction where the defect is not apparent on the face of the record. It appears to be settled that the judgments of the federal courts rendered when sitting within a state are not to be considered as domestic judgments. As stated by Mr. Justice Field in Gulpin v. Page, 3 Sawy. 107: “Whilst the courts of the United States are not foreign courts in their relation to the state courts, they are courts of a different sovereignty, exercising a distinct and independent jurisdiction, and are bound to give to the judgments of the state courts only the same faith and credit which the courts of another state are bound to give them.” Such a judgment is neither foreign, nor domestic in every sense, in said court, but it is open to inquiry as to the jurisdiction of the court and notice to the defendant.

Sec. 172¢e. Rule where record silent.— We have to some extent stated the rule with respect to the presumptions that attach to domestic judgments rendered by courts of general jurisdiction. A question still remains, and that is whether such presumption can be overcome by extrinsic evidence. So far as those courts are concerned which hold the doctrine that the effect of recitals may be thus overcome, the question does not arise. In some courts, it may be stated, the presumption is of equal force with a recital, and cannot be overcome by extrinsic evidence, while in others it has been held that the presumption that there was service of process, which attaches where the record is silent, is rebuttable, and when rebutted by evidence the judgment is avoided. Generally speaking, in all

1 See Farl v. McVeigh, 91 U.S. 503, 
2 See also Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 732, 
3 Christmas v. Russell, 5 Wall. 305: 
4 Warring v. Hill, 80 Ind. 497, 
See also Propst v. Meadows, 13 IL 157;

<br>

### --- Page 0271 ---

166 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 1727.

In many jurisdictions it is the rule that a judgment entered by agreement cannot be collaterally attacked on the ground that such agreement was corrupt and unlawful; but this is in some states qualified by the condition that such agreement must not have operated as a fraud upon an innocent party, by which he was prevented from making his defense.

Sec. 1727. Adjudication, effect of.—Mere recitals have often been given the force of adjudication, and it is too often stated that where the court has decided, from the evidence before it, that it was sufficient to confer jurisdiction, then such decision or recital becomes conclusive. This question is discussed in subsequent pages (see sec. 177 et seq.). It will be mentioned here. How absurd it is to say that because a court recites in its judgment that due and personal service was made, etc., when the evidence upon which it was based is the affidavit of one who attempted to make the service, that such recital is equivalent to an adjudication. How can such recital become of greater force than the evidence upon which it is based? What necessity is there for an adjudication? It can and ought to determine whether, upon its face, it appears that the formal requisites were complied with as to manner, time and place, but nothing beyond that. How can it adjudicate that the person served is the identical person named, or that A. or B. is a member of his family, or that he resides at a certain place, or in fact is a resident of the state? Those questions are not litigated. No judgment is exercised with regard to them. Such a recital or finding, as stated by the New York court in Sibley v. Waffle, 16 N. Y. 180, is nothing more than a statement by the court that it had acquired jurisdiction of the parties and that the evidence of it was contained in the affidavit. It is far different where the court can only act upon conditions which must appear in a petition or affidavit. Necessarily it must determine whether the essential facts are stated; and though it may err, this does not affect the jurisdiction or the power to decide. Yet, I apprehend, if the petition were a forgery, it could not be claimed that it had adjudicated that it was valid. Root v. McFerrin, 37 Miss 45; Miller Bank of Wooster v. Stevens, 1 Ohio St. 253.  
Mannix v. The State, 115 Ind. 245;  
v. Ewing, & Smead & M. 421.

<br>

### --- Page 0272 ---

§ 172y. COLLATERAL ATTACK. 167  
8 Se]

and for that reason the finding was conclusive. Ordinarily the record recites that an attorney appeared for a party. It is only an adjudication that he appeared, not that he was authorized to appear. That is assumed from the fact of appearance. It has no other evidence upon which to make an adjudication. Hence, so far as domestic judgments of courts of general jurisdiction are concerned, when rendered in the exercise of their ordinary jurisdiction, the question of adjudication is not pertinent because not applicable. It only becomes applicable where such court is proceeding outside of its general course, and to proceedings of inferior courts and tribunals, and in some cases to courts of limited jurisdiction, and under this heading the doctrine is discussed.

Sec. 172g. Superior courts exercising special powers.—The Illinois court, in a series of many cases, have held to the rule that recitals in a judgment cannot be impeached by other parts of the record, and applied its rule to cases where the court of general jurisdiction was proceeding under special or statutory power. Thus, where a judgment confirming a special assessment recited that due notice as required by law had been given by publication, etc., and it appeared from the record itself that the notice was not published the requisite length of time, it was held that such recital could not be overcome by such record. That the presumption was conclusive that the court heard and acted upon other and sufficient evidence to sustain the finding. This court, however, makes a distinction where the service of process is by the sheriff. In such case, if his return contradicts the finding of the court, it will overcome the finding and prove the want of jurisdiction, even in a collateral proceeding. Parol evidence in such case is inadmissible to prove or aid the service. In case of service by publication, parol evidence may be received to prove that the notice was published. A certificate of publication found in the record insufficient to sustain the jurisdiction of the court will, however, not overcome the finding of the court, when it declares in its

is inadmissible

'Blythe & Fargo Co. v. Swenson, Barnett v. Wolf, 70 Ill. 76; Hertig v. People, 150 Ill. 257, 15 Utah, 345.  
Casey v. People, 46 N.E. Rep. 7;  
Casey v. People, 46 N.E. Rep. 7

<br>

### --- Page 0273 ---

168 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 172y.

decree that it had acquired jurisdiction by publication. It will be presumed in such case that the court acted on other and sufficient evidence to sustain its findings. The reason given for this distinction is, that in case of personal service the return of the sheriff is the only legitimate evidence of service; while in case of substituted service there may be different methods of proof. It would seem that the courts of West Virginia practically are of the same mind with respect to the judgments of superior courts, when in the exercise of special powers, as the court last stated. Thus, where a superior court of general jurisdiction was clothed with power in a proceeding administrative in character (not a suit between parties) with respect to forfeiture of entry of lands, it was said that thereby it was not made an inferior court, bound to establish by proof dehors the record that the land was in fact in the particular county (that being a jurisdictional question), whenever its action is involved in a collateral proceeding. Its procedure in the matter was judicial; its steps are so to be regarded. It is none the less a court of general jurisdiction as to its procedure because the general nature of its function in the case was administrative, because of the matter on which it acted. Notwithstanding the character of the subject-matter before it, the court had to ascertain whether the fact giving jurisdiction existed, and being a court of general jurisdiction, if it is not stated on its record that such fact exists, it would be presumed that it had been established to its satisfaction.

Judgments for taxes.— The Illinois court make distinction with reference to judgments entered for taxes unpaid. It concedes that such judgments stand on the same footing as judgments in other cases and are not subject to collateral attack, yet the general rule does not apply only in cases where there has been a trial on the merits, the court having jurisdiction of the person and subject-matter of the suit. Where the proper person does not appear and become a party to the proceedings in which such judgments are rendered, or make objections to their rendition as to him, they are of no binding force, and it is

1 Reedy v. Camfield, 46 N. E. Rep.
Graceland Cemetery Co. v. People, 92 Ill. 619.
2 Cecil v. Clark, 30 S. E. Rep. 216.

<br>

### --- Page 0274 ---

§ 173.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 169

open to him in a collateral proceeding to show that the property was not liable for the payment of such taxes.

§ 173. Collateral attack upon judgments of courts of limited jurisdiction. The doctrine quite generally prevails that the judgments of courts of limited jurisdiction can be collaterally attacked, as such judgments are an absolute nullity if the want of jurisdiction in fact exists. Many reasons have been given for this distinction; among others, that the law does not make the same conclusive presumption in their favor, and therefore, the jurisdiction not being presumed, it must depend upon actual facts, which are open to dispute and not concluded by the record. If the jurisdictional facts do not exist, the court in fact has no jurisdiction. It is perhaps of little concern whether the reason for the rule is well taken or not, so long as the courts in a great majority of jurisdictions firmly adhere to it; yet we cannot refrain from passing criticism with respect to the application of the rule to courts of limited jurisdiction with respect to personal service of process. That the record should show that the court was acting within the scope of its limited powers is quite generally conceded; that, having determined the question upon evidence before it, the adjudication is conclusive, is quite generally the law; but in the exercise of its general powers, or in respect to those matters which are common to all courts alike, such as depend upon service of process, no sufficient reason can be urged why the record, or the return of the officer, or the recital in the judgment, should not have the same effect, either conclusive or otherwise, in either case. As stated by the supreme court of Massachusetts: “In matters over which the jurisdiction of the court appears to extend, there can be no difference in this respect between a court of limited and a court of general jurisdiction.”

It seems strange sometimes that courts widely differ in their construction and application of an apparently plain and simple proposition of law. Thus the broad statement is made that the judgment of a court of limited jurisdiction is never conclusive of a jurisdictional question. Its jurisdiction may always

1 Belleville Nail Co. v. People, 102 Ill. 399.
2 Hendrick v. Wittemore, 105 Mass. 23.
3 Coit v. Haven, 30 Conn. 190.

<br>

### --- Page 0275 ---

IMPEACHMENT.

be controverted!’ Hence the adjudication by such a court upon the evidence before it, with respect to the residence of a person, such being a jurisdictional question, not involved, however, as a question of jurisdiction of the person, was held prima facie only, and subject to collateral attack? Yet, as we have stated, and as will further appear, the mere fact that such a judgment was rendered by a court of limited jurisdiction no more subjects it to collateral attack than a judgment of a court of general jurisdiction. Thus, where a county judge had acquired jurisdiction to determine the question as to whether the petition presented to him was signed by a majority of the taxpayers of the town, it was held that his decision thereon, so long as the same was not reversed, was conclusive, and could not be questioned in any collateral proceeding, such as an action by the town to have its bonds set aside or canceled. The court refers to Staples v. Furrchild, 3 N.Y. 41, hereinafter quoted, and to Poderigas v. East River Savings Institution, 63 N.Y. 460. In the latter case the rule is stated: “Where a statute prescribes that some fact must exist before jurisdiction can attach in any court, such fact must exist before there can be jurisdiction, and the court cannot acquire jurisdiction by erroneously deciding that the fact exists and that it has jurisdiction. But where general jurisdiction is given to a court over a subject, and that jurisdiction depends, in the particular case, upon facts which must be brought before the court for its determination upon evidence, and where it is required to act upon such evidence, its decision upon the question of its jurisdiction is conclusive until reversed, revoked, set aside or vacated, so far as to protect its officers and all other persons who act upon the faith of it.” If it were not for the doctrine of the other cases cited, we might assume that the apparent qualification in the last sentence rendered the rule stated inapplicable as between the parties. The conclusion reached in this case was disapproved by the United States Supreme Court. The jurisdiction of such a court is a matter, however, into

First Nat. Bank v. Baldwin, 35. 165. See also Lessee of Maxon v. 451; Culver's Appeal, 48 Conn. Sawyer, 12 Ohio, 195.

ears v. Terry, 26 Conn. 273. Town of Cherry Creek v. Becker et al., 136 N.Y. 161.

Conn. 351; Culver’s Appeal, 48 Conn.

<br>

### --- Page 0276 ---

§ 175a.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, VI

which inquiry may be made even in collateral proceedings, where the record contains no findings of facts expressly showing jurisdiction. When the record discloses that such jurisdictional facts were ascertained, then the existence of such facts can no more be collaterally impeached than the declaration of the finding of such facts by a superior court, or any other fact committed to the judgment of the tribunal, unless the evidence contained in the record is contrary; yet this rule does not extend so far, in most jurisdictions, as to include the mere action of the court upon the facts, in the absence of a declaration in some form of its judicial determination.

§ 173a. Extrinsic evidence to sustain or defeat.— We have heretofore considered the question of the effect to be given judgments of courts of limited jurisdiction and reached the conclusion that, after jurisdiction is acquired in the manner required by law in the particular proceeding, then thereafter, as to subsequent proceedings, the presumption in favor of the proceedings was the same practically as in courts of general jurisdiction. The question now to be considered, and frankly, it must be said, in view of conflicting decisions, the most difficult one, is to what extent and under what circumstances and conditions can such a judgment be impeached in a collateral action or proceeding. It is true that it is incumbent upon the party relying upon such judgment to affirmatively show, as it is often stated, that the court has acquired jurisdiction of the subject matter and of the person of the defendant, and it is equally true that this may be done by introducing the record itself; and if it appears that all the substantial requisites to jurisdiction were complied with, then prima facie the judgment or decree becomes admissible as record evidence. The question

[Footnotes]  
Scobey v. Gano, 35 Ohio St. 553; Stout, 73 Ind. 3; Muncey v. Joest, 74 Ind. 409; Stoddard v. Johnson, 75 Ind. 20; Quarl v. Abbott, 102 Ind. 510; Board of Com’rs v. Merkle, 460 Ind. 471. There are cases to the contrary: Sears v. Terry, 26 Conn. 273. See also COLLATERAL ATTACK.

Barclift v. Treece, 77 Ala. 528; Railroad Co. v. City, 1 Ind. 3; Little v. Sinnett, 7 Iowa, 32h; Kruse v. Wilson, 79 Ill.

Little v. Thompson, 24 Ind. 146; Porter v. Wyseatus Adm’r v. Rambo, 29 May 233; Oppenheim v. Railway Co., 85 Ill. 180; Winslow v. Barber, 12 Wend. 102.

<br>

### --- Page 0277 ---

172 IMPEACHMENT. (§§ 174, 175.

in the latter case is, how far, and to what extent, if at all, extrinsic evidence may be introduced to show compliance with the requirements of the law; and in the former, where the record discloses a substantial compliance with the requirements, to what extent can such record be contradicted by extrinsic evidence.

Sec. 174. Jurisdiction cannot be sustained by extrinsic evidence.— With respect to the first proposition it seems to be settled that the jurisdiction of such court can only be shown by the record, and that extrinsic evidence cannot be admitted to prove that to have been done as to which the record is silent. I find a statement in a case decided by the New York court of appeals, apparently in conflict, in substance that it is not essential that the record should show affirmatively and on its face that the court acquired jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person; that it is enough that they did exist and were shown to the court. In that case the objection was first raised upon the appeal, and it was said that if the objection had been made in time it might have been obviated by competent testimony. Yet it is not every fact essential to jurisdiction that must appear upon the record. The rule seems to be that those jurisdictional facts in support of judgments which are not in writing or required to be in writing or entered in the record may be proved by parol. So the fact that a defendant in an action in a justice’s court resided within a certain township, which was essential to jurisdiction, need not appear by the record, but may be proved by parol; also, that an alleged lunatic resided in the county where certain proceedings in respect to his estate were had in the surrogate’s court.

Sec. 175. Extrinsic evidence, when permissible to show lack of jurisdiction.— It is with respect to the second proposition that we find the difficulty spoken of. No good reason

1 Anderson v. Binford, 58 Tenn. 310; Jones v. Ritter, 56; Iron v. Dent, 17 S. C. 75; Lowe v. Alexander, 15 Cal. 296; Allen v. Blunt, 1 Blatchf. (U. S.) 480; Townsend v. Tallant, 33 Cal. 46; Adams v. Jeffries, 12 Ohio. 253; McKee v. McKee, 14 Pa. St. 231.

2 Van Deusen v. Sweet, 51 N. Y. 378.

3 Jolley v. Foltz, 34 Cal. 321; Van Deusen v. Sweet, 51 N. Y. 378.

4 Jolley v. Foltz, 34 Cal. 321.

5 Van Deusen v. Sweet, 51 N. Y. 378.

<br>

### --- Page 0278 ---

§ 176.) COLLATERAL ATTACK. 173

can be assigned why the records of a court of limited jurisdiction should not be as sacred as those of a court of general jurisdiction, or why, outside of the presumption that attaches to judgments of the latter kind, the same effect should not be given to their proceedings. It is a rule that a judgment, whether rendered by a court possessing one or the other jurisdiction, if acting within the scope of its lawful authority, duly observing all the prescribed modes of proceeding, is equally conclusive, not only against future litigation of the same matters, but in all other respects. That the judgment or decree of a court of competent jurisdiction is, as a general rule, final not only as to the subject-matter thereby actually determined, but as to every other matter which the parties might litigate in the cause and which might have been decided. It would seem to follow that judgments of the one ought not to be subject to collateral attack upon grounds that would not apply to the other, and when the best-considered cases are marshaled the writer is of the opinion that it may be gathered therefrom that such is the rule. True it is said that they are prima facie evidence only, but that relates to the question of acquiring jurisdiction, and it is equally true that the judgments of superior courts are in fact, as to the question of jurisdiction, only prima facie evidence; for, as we have seen supra, such judgments are open to direct attack in nearly all jurisdictions, and in most of the code states, and in some others where the reformed procedure does not prevail, they are open to attack in proceedings where the judgment is sought to be enforced. To what extent the record may be contradicted in such cases we have also considered. Some additional authorities which we cite in the present connection will also bear upon the question there considered.

Sec. 176. Impeaching record and recitals.—The proposition that upon collateral attack the defendant is estopped from asserting anything against what appears in the record, as it imparts verity, was vigorously resisted in an early leading case in this country. The reasoning of the court is so eminently sound that I deem it proper to give it place here. It was said: “It appears to me that this proposition assumes the very fact

¹ Embry v. Connor, 3 Comst. 511.  
² Embry v. Connor, 3 Comst. 511, and cases cited on page 522,

<br>

### --- Page 0279 ---

17+ IMPEACHMENT, (§177.

to be established, which is the only question in issue. For what purpose does the defendant question the jurisdiction of the court? Solely to show that its proceedings and judgment are void, and therefore the supposed record is in truth no record. If the defendant had not proper notice of and did not appear in the original action, all the state courts with one exception agree in opinion that the paper introduced as to him is no record; but if he cannot show even against the pretended record that fact on the alleged ground of the uncontrollable verity of the record, he is deprived of his defense by a process of reasoning to my mind a little less than sophistry. The plaintiffs in effect declare to the defendant the paper declared on is a record, because it says you appeared; and you appeared because the paper is a record. This is reasoning in a circle. The appearance makes the record an uncontrollable verity, and the record makes the appearance an unimpeachable fact. Unless a court has jurisdiction it can never make a record which imports uncontrollable verity to the party over whom it has usurped jurisdiction, and he ought not, therefore, to be estopped from proving any fact which goes to establish the truth of a plea alleging the want of jurisdiction.” This language was quoted with approval by the supreme court of the United States.

Sec. 177. Decision of court with respect to its own jurisdiction — Effect of.— There is, however, a clear distinction between a finding upon evidence that certain facts exist essential to jurisdiction of the subject-matter which the court has jurisdiction to determine, and a mere decision of a court that it has jurisdiction when it has none. The evidence upon which a court acts in regular proceedings being such only as the law prescribes, such as certificates or affidavits of service, or the mailing or service of notices which are designed to operate upon the person only, ought not to be within the rule of prior adjudication to the extent of prohibiting in a direct proceeding, or a proceeding collateral to the original action when the judgment is made the basis of a new recovery, proof of the actual facts in respect to personal or other substituted service. A finding that the statements contained in a petition or affidavit for

VStarbuck v. Murray, 5 Wend. 145; Harris v. Wardeman, 14 How. 334-341.

<br>

### --- Page 0280 ---

§ 178.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 105  
publication are in compliance with the requirements of the law may be sufficient to conclude the party from making a collateral attack, but those matters in the record which relate to the doing of acts which constitute service, as to which no special finding is made, ought not to have that effect. Of course, this conclusion is not the same as that of the federal court in respect to the return of an officer in case of a domestic judgment,¹ but is in accord with decisions of other courts, especially where the judgment is that of a sister state. The most comprehensive statement of the rule which has been observed by the writer, is that when certain facts are requisite to give an inferior court jurisdiction over the persons of parties, and it appears from the record of its judgment that there was evidence tending to prove such facts, and that such evidence was adjudged to be sufficient, such judgment cannot be collaterally impeached or contradicted.² This element of the rule was recognized in Reinach v. Railroad Co., 58 Fed. Rep. 33, where it is said: “There is a clear distinction between those facts which involve the jurisdiction of the court over the parties and the subject-matter and those quasi-judicial facts, without allegation of which the court cannot be set in motion, and without proof of which a decree should not be pronounced. The judgment of a court having no jurisdiction of the subject-matter or the parties is null and void, and may be impeached in collateral proceedings, and the record of the court showing such jurisdiction may be contradicted by parol evidence. But there are certain facts termed quasi-jurisdictional which must be alleged and proved, but when so proved are res adjudicata and binding in collateral proceedings.” The court gives many illustrations and cites authorities in their support.  

Sec. 178. Effect of recitals stated and illustrated.—The effect of recitals in an order or decree is well illustrated in Sidney v. Watte, 18 N.Y. 150. There were two questions there to be determined by the surrogate in a proceeding to sell land by an administrator for the payment of debts in order to acquire jurisdiction to sell the land: First, whether sufficient  

¹ Walker v. Robbins, supra.  
² Sheldon v. Wright, 47 Seld. 407. See also McGowan v. Lufburrow, 82 Ga.; Stoddard v. Johnson, 75 Ind. 20; Morrow v. Weed, 4 Iowa; Angell v. Robbins, 4 R.I. 403; Dollarhide v. Parker, 92 Mo. 178.

<br>

### --- Page 0281 ---

moncy could be raised for the payment of debts by mortgaging or leasing the land before an order of sale could be made. Second. In the particular case, whether the order to show cause was properly published and served so as to give jurisdiction of the persons of the widow, heirs and occupants of the land. The affidavit showing publication in the state paper stated that the publication commenced on a certain day, which was two days prior to the date of the order, and the affidavit of service upon three of the heirs stated a day of service which was two days less than the number of days required by the statute that it should be served prior to the day of hearing. With reference to the first question, the order authorizing the sale recited that the surrogate had inquired whether the money could be raised by mortgaging or leasing, and found that it could not. This was held to be conclusive evidence that the surrogate adjudicated on that question, and such adjudication was conclusive. With reference to the second question there was no evidence before the surrogate appearing from the record showing service upon the widow or two heirs residing in Ohio. It was held the proof of the publication was palpably defective. The order directing the sale recited that “on reading and filing satisfactory proof by affidavit of the due publication of said order and due service thereof on every person, etc., and the surrogate, upon due examination, being satisfied that the administrator fully complied with the requisitions of the statute,” etc., it was held that such recital was no evidence that the publication and service were at the time and in the manner prescribed by the statute. It was nothing more than a statement by the surrogate that he had acquired jurisdiction of the parties, and that the evidence of it was contained in affidavits filed with him, and it proved nothing without the affidavits. His adjudging that he had jurisdiction, without stating that he found from evidence that the facts existed which conferred it, was not sufficient. He could not acquire jurisdiction by deciding that he had it. It was incumbent on the plaintiff to show affirmatively that the surrogate had jurisdiction of the parties. It was said: “Had the order recited the publication and service, stating the time and manner in which they were done, perhaps such recital would have been evidence of the facts stated.” It seems that substantially such recital was held conclusive in an.

<br>

### --- Page 0282 ---

* €179.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 177

other case! The same court, in a later case, referred to the case of Sthley v. Waffles, and stated that it was intended there to decide that the recital of due publication did not overcome the proof that the order to show cause was published only five instead of six weeks, as required by the statute. After referring to the diversity of opinion upon the question as to the effect of recitals in an order as proof, it is stated that jurisdiction may be established prima facie by such recitals. That if such recitals are evidence of the performance of the acts necessary to be done to confer jurisdiction in one court, and for one purpose, they must be for all. That there cannot be one rule of evidence for superior and another for inferior courts; that the difference among the courts is principally upon the question whether the fact recited is conclusive or only prima facie. The controversy was with respect to an order appointing a receiver, and it was stated that the court making the order was a court of general jurisdiction, and had the power to so appoint conferred upon it by statute, and that when an order is made appointing such officer the presumption is that all things were done required by the statute to be done in order to authorize it to make the order. That in appointing a receiver in such cases the court acts as a court of general jurisdiction and not as exercising a special statutory power. The Iowa court followed a rule laid down in 1 Smith's Leading Cases, 527-534, that if there be a petition on the proper matter to call into action the power or jurisdiction of the court, its sufficiency cannot be called in question collaterally; if there be notice of publication its sufficiency cannot be questioned collaterally; and held that a recital in the record which finds that the court being satisfied from publication properly filed that the notice prescribed by law has been given is sufficient to give jurisdiction, at least until contradicted by proof, declining to decide whether proof could be received.

Src. 179. Statement of facts upon which jurisdiction depends may be refuted.— The New York courts hold that the allegation of facts showing jurisdiction may be defeated by MSheldon v. Wright. 1 Seld. 497, 3 Cooper v. Sutherland, 3 Iowa, 114. 2 Potter v. Merchants' Bank, 28 N.Y. 641-653.

<br>

### --- Page 0283 ---

178 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 180.]

showing in fact that jurisdiction did not attach, on the express ground that it is always admissible thus to attack the judgment of a court of limited jurisdiction. This was said, however, where, in an action upon a note, the defendant pleaded in bar his discharge in bankruptcy, it being held competent to show that the court did not have jurisdiction. The rule was attempted to be explained by another court, and a distinction drawn between proceedings of record and facts relating to jurisdiction; that the record could not collaterally be contradicted, but that the facts upon which jurisdiction was based might by extrinsic evidence be shown to be untrue; that it was proper to show that a person convicted, in fact was not served and did not appear; and yet they hold that if there was a return of an officer this would give jurisdiction, and it would not be competent to impeach it. This ruling was based upon what was decided in Putnam v. Man, 3 Wend. 202, which was in effect that the return of the officer gave the court jurisdiction, whether true or false, and if false, that the only remedy of the injured party was against him for a false return. If this reasoning is sound as to judgments of inferior courts, why not also as to judgments of sister states? We shall presently see that as to such judgments the want of jurisdiction is always open to inquiry, and upon this principle it is held that it is competent to disprove the return of the officer. Such return in inferior courts, proper in the state where rendered, gives the court jurisdiction. Proving the return to be false would not affect its jurisdiction. Again, we have seen that in superior courts it is not the return that gives the court jurisdiction, but the actual fact of personal service. Where is the reason, then, that prevents an inquiry as to such fact? See section 195 and cases cited.

Sec. 180. Impeachment of record as to service — Views of the Massachusetts court. — Upon investigation the writer ascertains that the reasoning indulged in is practically the same.

1 Barber v. Winslow, 12 Wend. 102.  
See also First Nat. Bank v. Balcolm, N. Y. 88; Potter v. Merchants’ Bank, 35 Conn. 351; Culver’s Appeal, 48 Conn. 165; Salladay v. Bainhill, 29 Iowa, 555; Mastin v. Gray, 19 Kan. 390; Clark v. Holmes, 1 Doug. (Mich.)

<br>

### --- Page 0284 ---

§ 180.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 179

as that stated by the supreme court of Massachusetts. In the first case cited the fact was that it appeared from the record of a domestic judgment rendered against a resident of another state that he had appeared in the action by an attorney, and the proof offered was that such attorney appeared by inadvertence or mistake. It was held competent to prove facts tending to show that the court had no jurisdiction of the cause, and therefore that it could make no record binding upon such party which would be conclusive. In the second, the action was upon the judgment of a court of another state. The conclusion of the court was that it was competent for the defendant to contradict the record as to personal service, and in coming to such conclusion they reason as follows: "We can perceive no distinction in principle between this case and many of the cases in which it was recited in the record that a party had appeared, that he had appeared by his attorney, and the like," but where he was still allowed to prove that in fact no attorney had appeared for him—that no attorney had been authorized to appear for him. "The acts and recitals of a court not having acquired jurisdiction cannot be conclusively binding on him; nor can acts and recitals be conclusive evidence of facts which would give them jurisdiction. No more force and effect can be given to the certificate of a person purporting to be a deputy sheriff of another state, in giving the court jurisdiction over an absent party, than to similar acts of attorneys."

Missouri rule.—In Missouri it is stated that the law is now well settled that the orders and judgments of probate courts are entitled to the same favorable presumptions and intendments that are accorded to the judgments of circuit courts. They are no more open to collateral attack than are the proceedings of any other court. Hence, where the party claiming under an order of sale made by such a court puts in evidence the order of sale and the deed based thereon, it is not necessary in the first instance to make any further or other proof. It will be presumed that all requisite antecedent steps

Bodurtha et al. v. Goodrich, 8 Ass'n, 145 S. W. Rep. 57; Sherwood v. Gray, 508; Carleton v. Bickford, 13 Baker et al, 168 W. Rep. 938; Camden v. Plain, 45 S. W. Rep. 86

Price v. Springfield Real Estate

<br>

### --- Page 0285 ---

180 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 180.

had been duly and timely taken until the contrary is made to appear. And the rule was applied where the question was whether a petition failed to show that the personal estate of the decedent was sufficient to pay his existing debts. The case was distinguished from another in that in the latter the order of sale made by the court was made without any petition being presented to the court. The cases which have been examined do not disclose whether extrinsic evidence may be received, or if, upon introduction of the whole record, it does not appear that such steps were taken, what the effect is — whether the taking of such steps will be conclusively presumed or whether extrinsic evidence can be received to show they were not taken. The language used, that it will be presumed they were taken in the first instance, until the contrary is made to appear, would indicate that the question was open to inquiry, the burden being upon the opposite party, but does not indicate whether the proof is confined to the record. Again, the broad declaration that the same conclusive presumption applies as in cases of judgments of superior courts, and the doctrine of that state being to the effect that extrinsic evidence is not admissible to impeach such judgments, even where the record is silent, would imply that impeaching proof was confined to the record.

New Jersey rule.—In New Jersey the orphans’ court is classed as a superior court of general jurisdiction, and its judgments and proceedings are entitled to the same presumptions in favor of their regularity as those of the highest courts of the common law, and while they remain in force are equally binding. It being the rule in that state that the judgments of courts of general jurisdiction cannot be collaterally attacked upon grounds not appearing of record, and that evidence will not be received to contradict either the recitals or the presumptions that attach to them, it follows that the same rule applies to the judgments and decrees of the orphans’ court. The conclusive presumption extends to the prerequisite conditions essential to jurisdiction, such as petitions, notices, and the like; and where the record is silent it will be conclusively presumed

Teverbaugh v. Hawkins, 82 Mo. 180.

<br>

### --- Page 0286 ---

§ 180.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 181

that the court had before it and passed upon all such matters. This court recognizes the rule that the records of inferior courts or tribunals of special or limited jurisdiction must necessarily affirmatively show everything essential to jurisdiction, and when the jurisdictional facts are not so set forth, the presumption of law is that the judgment pronounced is coram non judice.

Pennsylvania rule.—The Pennsylvania court does not extend the conclusive presumption of jurisdiction to the orphans’ court of that state. The rule there is that an unauthorized decree of an orphans’ court for the sale of lands will not stand until reversed in the regular course of appeal, but may be questioned in a collateral suit by or against a person claiming under that decree.* The court in reaching its conclusions does not consider the distinction so often made by other courts between courts of general and those of limited jurisdiction, but places its decision upon the broad ground that judgments rendered without jurisdiction are nullities. They say: “The conclusiveness of the judgment or decree depends upon the jurisdiction of the court pronouncing it, and the converse of the proposition is equally clear, that the decree of any court is a nullity if pronounced upon a subject over which the court has no jurisdiction. It is not indispensably necessary that the want of jurisdiction should appear affirmatively upon the record.” The court refers to the earlier case of Zorrance v. Torrance, 53 Pa. St. 503, in which the facts were that the executor presented his petition to the orphans’ court for leave to sell real estate, for the repayment to himself of money paid to a legatee, and for the payment to another legatee of a judgment recovered by him against the executor for a balance due him upon his legacy. The legacies had been charged by the will of the testator on certain real estate. The court without inquiry directed the sale, and subsequently made a decree of confirmation, and the deed was delivered. In an action of ejectment the supreme court held the decree void. The court under the will had no

¹ Clark v. Costello, 86 Atl. Rep. 271; Rees v. Wildman, 35 Atl. Rep. J. L. 220, Plume v. Savings Institution, 46 N. J. L. 1047; Sager v. Mead, 164 Pa. St. 125; Torrance v. Torrance, 53 Pa. St. 505.

*Clark v. Costello, 86 Atl. Rep. 271.

<br>

### --- Page 0287 ---

IMPEACHMENT. [§ 150.

He

jurisdiction over the land, and therefore its decree was without conclusiveness and void. None of the facts that avoided the sale appeared in the record except the fact that the sale was sought in order to pay legacies. The terms of the will and the want of statutory power to sell for such a purpose had to be sought outside the files of the court and the recitals upon its docket. In the case first cited, the principal ground of attack was that the debt for which the land was sold was barred by the statute of limitations, and the land was relieved from the lien of the debt by the expiration of five years from the death of the testator. With respect to the effect of such an attack upon the purchaser, it was stated that such a sale passes only the decedent’s title. That the rule applicable to all judicial sales is caveat emptor as to the title acquired. The statute of 1852 in that state provides: “The orphans’ court is hereby declared to be a court of record, with all the qualities and incidents of a court of record at common law. Its proceedings and decrees in all matters within its jurisdiction shall not be reversed or avoided collaterally in other courts, but they shall be liable to reversal or modification or alteration on appeal to the supreme court as hereinafter directed.” It was held by the supreme court of the United States in Erron v. Dater, 120 U. S. 464, that though it appeared upon the face of the petition for the sale of real estate of the decedent to pay debts that such debts were barred by the statute of limitations, that was not sufficient upon collateral attack to impeach the decree. This was evidently upon the ground that the jurisdiction of the court was not thus affected.

North Carolina rule.— It seems that in North Carolina the judgments and decrees of probate courts are classed with those of courts of general jurisdiction, though not expressly so declared. This is assumed from a distinction referred to in the cases hereinafter cited. Thus it is held in common with all jurisdictions, that the rule is not universally true that the judgments and decrees of probate courts cannot be collaterally impeached. The notable exception which is referred to is that administration on the estate of a person who is in fact alive is

See also McPherson v. Conliff, 11 S. & R. 432, where the whole question is elaborately discussed.

<br>

### --- Page 0288 ---

§ 180.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 183

absolutely void for want of jurisdiction; and the fact of being alive may at all times, in any proceeding, be shown. A great number of cases are collected from which quotations are made, in all of which the same conclusion was reached. It is conceded that the determination of the fact of life or death is one properly before the court to be ascertained as a jurisdictional fact, the same as other questions, such as the existence of debts where land is directed to be sold, or residence in the particular county; yet as jurisdiction of the subject-matter is conferred by law and cannot be by consent, such fact is open to inquiry. There is no reasoning that would make such a question an exception, except the apparent absurdity in a judgment disposing of the estate of a live man as though he were dead, and dealing with it upon that presumption only, when he is known to be alive and may be actually present.

Texas rule.— The supreme court of Texas accords to the judgments and orders of probate courts the same conclusive presumption that it accords to judgments of superior courts. If the record of such a court shows that the steps necessary to clothe it with power to act in the given case were taken, or if the record be silent upon the subject, then its judgment must be held conclusive in any other court of the same sovereignty when collaterally called in question. Many cases in that court are referred to where such decrees and judgments have been held null and void, and some of them for reasons that must have been shown by extrinsic evidence. It was said, however, that when in a collateral attack it is stated that an administration is a nullity, unless some facts be then shown, etc., the word “null” or “void” is used in the sense of voidable; for if there be then a fact, proof of which would make the administration valid, it cannot be void, and the legal presumption is that the very fact which would give it validity was proved. It must be said that the doctrine of this court is extreme, and but few, if any, others have applied the doctrine of conclusive presumption to the essential steps required elsewhere as condi- 

1 Springer et al. v. Shavender, 23° mons, 45 Wis. 334; Scott v. McNeal, 154 U.S. 47,  
Patterson, 27 Tex. 497; Melia v. Sim-

<br>

### --- Page 0289 ---

1581 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 150.

tions precedent to the exercise of jurisdiction. It was held in that state that when land of a decedent has been sold under an order of court, an action of trespass to try title cannot be maintained by the heirs against the purchaser to recover the land, on the ground that the order of sale was obtained by the fraud of the purchaser and the administrator, since it is a collateral attack on such order. The rule was applied that a purchaser need not look beyond the application and orders of the court to ascertain whether or not the court had jurisdiction to make the order of sale. Where, however, it is sought also to set aside the deed executed by the administrator to the ancestor of the defendants, executed pursuant to such proceedings, on the ground of fraud, to which such ancestor was a party, then such an attack is a direct attack upon the deed, and an action would lie, though the order of sale was not disturbed.

Arkansas rule.—It appears that the supreme court of Arkansas makes no distinction with respect to courts of limited jurisdiction as to the conclusive effect that attaches to their records and recitals. Thus it is held that a probate court is a superior court under their law, whose jurisdiction is obtained over the estate of a decedent, as well as the person of the administrator, by the grant of letters of administration; although previous notice of some of the steps to be taken in the course of administration is required by the statute, that neglect to observe these requirements does not render the judgments or orders of the court void, but only voidable. It is not necessary that jurisdictional facts should appear of record in such a court, for the reason stated, to wit: That such court is a court of general jurisdiction, and hence, evidence outside of the record cannot be heard to contradict the presumption which attaches to the silent record.

California rule.—In California the same presumption and construction is to be given to the proceedings of probate court: that is accorded to courts of general jurisdiction; hence wel

1 Martin v. Robinson, 3 S. W. Rep. 550. See Lyon et al. v. Sanford et al., 19 S. W. Rep. 847.
2 Boyd v. Roane, 5 S. W. Rep. 704; Borden v. State, 11 Ark. 519.
3 Broadrib v. Tibbetts et al., 63 Cal. 852 (Tex. Civ. App.); MeCampbell v. Durst, 73 Tex. 420.
4 Storer v. Lane, 20 S. W. Rep. 79; Burroughs v. De Couts, 70 Cal. 36L

<br>

### --- Page 0290 ---

§ 180.] COLLATERAL ATTACK, 185

must look to the doctrine in that state with respect to courts of general jurisdiction to ascertain whether a want of jurisdiction can be shown by extrinsic evidence. What the presumption is with respect to the observance of prerequisite conditions I have not found to have been determined.

Wisconsin rule.—The doctrine of the Wisconsin court is that such courts are courts of limited jurisdiction when exercising statutory powers, and that in such cases not only must the statute be strictly followed in order to confer jurisdiction, but full compliance with the statute must appear on the face of the proceeding. Thus, in a proceeding for the appointment of a guardian for an insane person, where the statute requires a verified petition, such a petition must affirmatively appear, and if not so appearing the court is without jurisdiction and the proceedings are a nullity. Upon appeal therefrom to the circuit court that court will not obtain jurisdiction though the whole matter is tried de novo. The same rule is applied in attachment proceedings, also in cases of sales made by administrators, executors or guardians; and is, in substance, that any omission of a matter required by law avoids the proceeding in the absence of a statute curing the defect. In addition to cases in that court, numerous cases are cited from the court of appeals of New York, among others People v. Spencer, 55 N.Y. 1. The quotation from the decision in that case is: “If any facts required to be stated are omitted, all the subsequent proceedings are fatally defective. It does not aid the proceeding that the facts exist, or that they are in some other way or at any other stage of the proceedings brought to the knowledge of the officer, or that the statement of them may seem unnecessary in view of the inquiry and adjudication which he is authorized to make. The statute prescribes the proof which is to be presented to the county judge. It is material because the statute requires it. It must be presented in the form and at the time required, or the officer acquires no jurisdiction.”

Vermont rule.—In Vermont probate courts are classed as courts of limited and special jurisdiction. If they proceed in a manner prohibited or not authorized by law their proceedings are void. If they exceed their authority their judgments

<br>

### --- Page 0291 ---

186 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 181, 182.]

may be treated as nullities. Yet if the record shows an exercise of power within their jurisdiction and in the manner required by the statute, it cannot be avoided or impeached by extrinsic evidence.

Sec. 181. Rule in Iowa.—It was held in Iowa that an action in equity would lie to set aside a judgment at law, where the proof of service stated that the person upon whom service was made was the agent of the defendant corporation, when such was not the fact; that the return of the officer might be contradicted to this extent; and also held in that state that a recital in a decree “that it appeared that the defendant had been served with notice as required by law did not preclude the party from showing in a direct proceeding that the copies of the petition and notice were never directed to him as required by the code.” In such state the doctrine is, as we have seen, that in cases where a judgment is used as the basis of a new recovery, the defense interposed of a want of jurisdiction is in fact a direct attack upon the judgment.

Sec. 182. Distinction between void notice and defective notice.—A distinction is sometimes drawn between a defective notice and where no notice has been given; that in cases of the latter kind the judgment and proceeding is absolutely void and may be attacked collaterally as well as directly; that a notice deficient in substantial requirements is not merely irregular but void. In fact the language used by the very learned judge who delivered the opinion in the case is applicable to judgments of superior courts as well. It is equally true that for a mere defect in the notice or service the judgment will not be held void when collaterally attacked. The distinction between a notice that is void and one defective merely is stated to be, “that where some essential fact or requirement of the

1 Hendrick v. Cleveland, 2 Vt. 329. 4 Dunlap v. Cody, 31 Iowa, 260;

Farmers’ Ins. Co. v. Highsmith, 44 Iowa, 338. 5 Lyon v. Vannatta, 35 Iowa, 521;

3 McGahen v. Carr, 6 Iowa, 331; Newcomb v. Dewey, 27 Iowa, 381.

Hodson v. Tibbetts, 16 Iowa, 97. See 6 Kittsmiller v. Kitchen, 24 Iowa, 163; also Bridges v. Arnold, 37 Iowa, 221. See, however, Lee v. Wetmore, 58 Iowa, 170.

<br>

### --- Page 0292 ---

§ 183.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 1st

law which goes to make up and constitute notice to the party is omitted, so that practically the notice required by the law has not been given, then there is such a fatal defect in the substance of the notice as that no jurisdiction is conferred thereby. And on the other hand, where the party has been served with a notice which informs him of the substance of the remedy sought, and the time and place when and where he is required to appear, proceedings had in conformity with such notice will not be held void in a collateral proceeding, though there are technical defects in the notice or service."

Sec. 183. Inferior courts.—In the preceding chapter the rule with respect to the presumptions that attach to the judgments of inferior courts proper, such as those of a justice of the peace, and the effect of docket entries, as well as what should appear therein, was somewhat considered. The rule applicable, as there stated, was strictly that the record must show every essential step required by the statute to have been duly taken, and no presumption could be indulged in favor of the jurisdiction where it did not thus affirmatively appear.

Docket entries in justices’ courts — How far conclusive.

It has frequently been decided that the entries made in his docket by a justice cannot be contradicted; hence, where the entry as made by the justice was that the cause was continued by agreement of the parties, it was held that extrinsic evidence was not permissible to show that in fact no agreement was made. It was stated, however, in the decision of another court, that, as far as the authorities had come under the observation of the writer of the opinion, it would seem that the jurisdiction of special inferior tribunals at least may be inquired into in respect to their authority over the person as well as the subject-matter, and the want of jurisdiction may be shown by evidence, even when it tends to contradict the minutes or dockets which those tribunals may keep as records of their proceedings. The Wisconsin court holds that such entries cannot be impeached, on certiorari, by contradictory state-

Lyon v. Vannatta, 35 Iowa, 521.
Compare Mastin v. Gray, 19 Kan. 458,
Coughlin v. Blake, 55 Iowa, 634.
See also White v. Thompson, 3 Oreg. 390,
Clark v. Holmes, 1 Doug. (Mich.)

<br>

### --- Page 0293 ---

188 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 183.

ments made by the justice in his return, but does not hold that this may not be done by other proceedings. The recital in the docket, based upon the return of the officer alone, as to sufficient service, will not avail to give jurisdiction when the return fails to show sufficient service. The return of the officer is as much a part of the record as the docket itself. Such a recital amounts to nothing more than the opinion of the justice as to the legal sufficiency of the return. It neither embraces, or professes to embrace, the determination of any question of fact. Where the entry was merely summons served, this was held insufficient to support a judgment by default.*

Texas rule.— The rule recognized in Texas is that justices’ courts, being created by the constitution, are within their defined limits tribunals of general jurisdiction, and as such all reasonable presumptions should be indulged in support of the validity of their judgments. Hence, if the recitals or record show that a defendant was duly cited, extrinsic evidence is wholly inadmissible on the trial of a collateral action to contradict or vary such recital. Yet I do not understand that the same presumption applies as is given to judgments of superior courts. The record must affirmatively show jurisdiction, and in determining whether it is thus shown or not, the entire judgment must be looked into and considered; and if that portion of it relating to the question discloses there was no service, or service so defective that a judgment by default thereon would be void and not voidable merely, and the remainder of the record is silent, and there appears no finding of the court from which it may be inferred there was other service or an appearance, then the absence of jurisdiction would affirmatively appear. If, however, other parts of the record, and particularly the judgment,— which is the final act of the court, entered upon full examination and consideration of all the necessary facts,— should recite the due service of process or other facts which would give the court jurisdiction of the person, then this would be a case in which it would affirmatively appear that the jurisdiction had in fact attached, and the general rule

*Cassiady v. Millerick, 52 Wis. 379; Lowe v. Alexander, 15 Cal. 297. State ex rel. Cameron v. Roberts, 87 Wis. 292; Kane v. Desmond, 63 Cal. 464.

<br>

### --- Page 0294 ---

§ 183.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 189

would apply that in a collateral proceeding this recital imports absolute verity and cannot be contradicted. The rule herein stated was carried to the extent that even where a part of the record—the citation and its return—show that service could not have been had, still the judgment of the justice reciting that the defendant wholly made default and that he was duly served with process could not be thus impeached. Nor can evidence of fraud a l tunde the record be heard to dispute the judgment, even where the fraud is in obtaining jurisdiction. Nor can such a recital be disputed by the return of the citation. That court, however, entertained a bill to enjoin the collection of a judgment where it was alleged that there was no service of process, and that an appearance in the defendant's name on certiorari to reverse the judgment was unauthorized. It was said that this was a direct proceeding, and that other cases referred to were collateral proceedings, and the doctrine stated only applied to the latter. In examining a subsequent decision of that court, I find that the position which I assumed with respect to its rule where the record was silent was in accord with the doctrine which it announced. That court states that the general rule with respect to presumptions only applies to parties and privies, and not to strangers; and further, that where the record of the justice does not show whether a defendant against whom judgment was rendered by default had been served with process, it may be shown by parol evidence that he was not served. The reasoning of the court in the attempt to distinguish former decisions is quite interesting, and illustrates the great difficulty under which courts oftentimes labor in an attempt to sustain an extreme position once declared. It would be far better to recede at once.

Arkansas and Iowa rule.—The Arkansas and Iowa courts apparently make a distinction between want of service and a defective service in justices’ courts. In the one case there is no jurisdiction, in the other it constitutes error or irregularity merely. In the argument indulged in by the former court,

1 Heck v. Martin, 138 S. W. Rep. 51.  
2 Williams v. Hays, 138 S. W. Rep. 51.  
3 Williams v. Hays, 138 S. W. Rep. 5.  
4 Wilkerson v. Schoonmaker et al., 145 S. W. Rep. 222.  

Glass v. Smith, 66 Tex. 519.

<br>

### --- Page 0295 ---

190 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 18382.

Statements made by the Mississippi court, applied to courts of general jurisdiction, are quoted, where this distinction is recognized. The rule was applied by the Iowa court where it appeared from the record that the notice given was only four days instead of five, as was required by the statute, and the process was served by the justice himself; and it was held, as there was notice, but so defective as to justify reversal upon appeal, that the defendant nor his assignee could question the validity of the judgment or claim to have it treated as void in a collateral proceeding. The Arkansas court applied it in case of publication of a summons or warning order under their system. It was said the publication operated as constructive notice. The proof of publication filled the place of a return upon the summons. That if the warning order was properly published, the failure to make the proof prescribed by law was an irregularity merely, and could not be urged in a collateral proceeding.

Ohio rule.—In an early case in Ohio (State v. Dailey, 14 Ohio, 91) the doctrine was announced that records of judgments of a justice of the peace were no more liable to collateral attack than judgments of superior courts; and later, that the jurisdiction of a justice must affirmatively appear from his record, but when fairly appearing it is as impregnable against collateral attack as the judgment of any other court.

West Virginia rule.—In West Virginia the statement is made that where the jurisdiction of an inferior court is dependent upon a fact which the court is required to ascertain and settle, its decision is held conclusive. Further, that it has been claimed that this principle is not confined to determinations of a judicial character.

Sec. 183c. Inferior tribunals.—The more delicate question relates to the decision of inferior bodies or tribunals upon a state of facts presented for their determination. We have here,

1 Harrington v. Wofford, 46 Miss. 41; Smith v. Bradley, 6 Smedes & M. St. 78. For other cases relating to the jurisdiction of a justice and the presumptions that apply, see sec. 129 et seq.

2 McCurdy v. Baughman, 43 Ohio 492.

3 Ballinger v. Tarball, 16 Iowa, 492.

4 Webster v. Daniel et al., 14 S. W. Rep. 530.

5 Cecil v. Clark, 30 S. E. Rep. 216.

<br>

### --- Page 0296 ---

§ 183a.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 

as in many other cases, general statements to the effect that such a decision is conclusive against collateral attack. Is such a rule one of general application? We have seen (sec. 173) that the judgment and decision of such a tribunal is never conclusive upon the question of jurisdiction is the doctrine of some courts, and that the contrary is the doctrine of others. It is elsewhere shown that upon mandamus even errors of law committed by such tribunals are subject to review and correction. Cases also may be found to the effect that the decision of taxing officers is conclusive upon collateral attack, and others that such a decision is only prima facie evidence. That their mistakes of judgment in some cases may be reviewed in an action in equity. It is almost universally true that the decision of a board of election canvassers is only prima facie evidence, and that their decision can be collaterally attacked by extrinsic evidence, such as proceedings in quo warranto to determine the right to an office, and by mandamus the location of a county seat. In fact, in cases of the latter class, mandamus to compel an officer to hold his office at the county seat is the appropriate method to determine its proper location. Such a proceeding is collateral. The New York court, it is true, held that the decision of a county judge that a petition was signed by the requisite number of freeholders, the question being upon the issue of bonds, could not be reviewed. This was held by a divided court, while other courts have held that a question presented as to the residence of a person, such residence being essential in a proceeding in probate, though controverted, was not conclusive; that it was the fact of such residence, and not the determination of it in courts of limited jurisdiction, or by inferior courts or tribunals, that conferred the jurisdiction, and its decision could in this respect be impeached by extrinsic evidence (sec. 173). The profession is left without any certain guide or test. It may be said, and I think it has been held, that the decisions of canvassing boards constituted an exception to the general rule. If this be true it is probably placed upon the ground that, there being no right of appeal, mandamus lies to review their proceedings as the only adequate remedy. With respect to the proceedings of boards exercising judicial

1 See cases cited, sec. 129.

<br>

### --- Page 0297 ---

192 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 183a.

Power, such as drainage and highway commissioners, the supreme court of Indiana accords to their records and proceedings the same conclusiveness that is accorded to the proceedings of courts with respect to matters affirmatively appearing therein. Thus, they say that if the board had jurisdiction, the record of its proceedings and orders import absolute verity and cannot be collaterally impeached or contradicted. That in the case of assessments to pay the expenses of a macadamized road, a suit in equity will not lie to enjoin the collection thereof, such being a collateral attack, and the records or recitals of such a board as to jurisdictional facts cannot be contradicted. The court holds in such cases that as jurisdiction of such matters is conferred upon such tribunals by statute, the petition which the statute requires, when substantially conforming thereto, clothes such tribunals with jurisdiction in the particular case. Any action taken by the board on the hearing, however erroneous, within the limits of the petition, the purview and general scope of the act under which the proceedings were taken, is binding upon the parties affected over whom jurisdiction has been obtained by the giving of the requisite notice when collaterally attacked; and generally the doctrine of this court with respect to the general rule is, that when statutory powers are conferred upon a court of inferior jurisdiction, and a mode of exercising these powers is prescribed, the course pointed out must be substantially followed or the acts and judgments of the court are coram non judice and void; that in so far as the rule requires that the jurisdiction must appear from its own record, it meets with their approval; but in so far as such rule negatives presumptions in favor of the proceedings of such courts after jurisdiction has been acquired, and to the effect that their proceedings are void unless in strict compliance with the law, it is disapproved, and previous cases in that court apparently giving it approval are overruled. It is further the rule in that state that when an inferior tribunal is required to ascertain and decide upon facts essential to its jurisdiction, its determination thereon is conclusive against col-

1 Bowen v. Hester, 41 N. E. Rep. 330.

2 Bowen v. Hester, 41 N. E. Rep. 330; Stoddard v. Johnson, 75 Ind. 20.

<br>

### --- Page 0298 ---

§ 184, 185.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 193

lateral attack, and that in proceedings like those above referred to, the filing or presentation of the petition calls into exercise the jurisdiction of the board, and authorizes that body to determine not only whether the petition is properly signed by the requisite land-owners, but every other fact necessary to the granting of the prayer of the petition; and it is not necessary that the record of the board shall show an express finding upon those facts. Such finding will be presumed in support of the proceedings if the record shows an order granting the petition or for the taking of the steps necessary to the accomplishment of the end desired.1

II. WHEN A JUDGMENT IS RELIED UPON AS EVIDENCE IN ANOTHER PROCEEDING.

Sec. 184. Judgment relied upon as evidence in an action cannot be attacked by extrinsic evidence.—Thus far we have considered the subject of collateral attack upon judgments upon the ground of want of jurisdiction, and it has appeared throughout the discussion that much is made to depend upon what is considered strictly a collateral attack. That while some courts hold that an action upon a judgment is collateral, others hold the contrary. That while some hold that an action to enforce a right under a judgment, when it is used as the evidence of that right, is collateral, others hold that the defense of want of jurisdiction in such case is not collateral, but direct, as the basis of the defense, and it must be confessed that much of the apparent confusion is due to a failure to express the proper distinction. When a judgment is relied upon simply as evidence in an action, not as the basis of a right, the rule is general in the several jurisdictions that it cannot be attacked by extrinsic evidence.2

Sec. 185. Entire record may be resorted to, to show judgment void on its face.—The entire record in a cause is com-

1 Bowen v. Hester, 41 N.E. Rep. 330; Stoddard v. Johnson, 75 Ind. 20; Kelley v. Mise, 3 Sneed (Tenn.), 5; Million v. Board of Commissioners, 89 Ind. 13; Otterson v. Middleton, 102 Pa. St. 78; Murchison v. White, 4 Tex. 78; Alderson v. Bell, 9 Cal. 816; Smith v.

2 Weiss v. Guerineau, 109 Ind. 438; Smith et al, 22 Iowa, 516.

<br>

### --- Page 0299 ---

194 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 186, 187.]

petent when the action involves proof of a judgment therein; and when the judgment is thus brought collaterally before the court as evidence, it may be shown to be void upon its face, if the record discloses its invalidity. But ordinarily extrinsic evidence cannot be resorted to for such purpose. The court cannot try such collateral issue. The party must seek his remedy in the original action or by some proceeding direct in its character. Where such defenses have been sustained it has been upon the ground that in the particular proceeding it was either a direct or in the nature of a direct attack upon the judgment, and in this respect there can be no distinction between judgments of courts of general jurisdiction and those of limited jurisdiction or of inferior courts. There is dicta to the contrary. Thus in Cort v. Haven, 30 Conn. 199, the language used is broad enough to indicate that in a purely collateral proceeding in an inferior court the facts recited in the record are open to dispute.

§ 186. Rule extracted from the decisions.—The rule which the writer extracts from the decisions is that in respect to judgments of inferior courts, the extent of the doctrine is that when such a judgment comes directly in issue as the basis of a right, whether offered by the plaintiff or defendant, extrinsic evidence may be given for the purpose of proving that the judgment is a nullity for want of jurisdiction to render it. Many courts do not go so far, but hold the record conclusive, making no distinction in this respect between courts of general jurisdiction and inferior courts. That none of the courts go so far as to hold that, when such a judgment comes incidentally in question between parties and privies purely as evidence, it can be attacked upon grounds not appearing of record,

§ 187. Fraud—To what extent it may be shown.—The next question to be considered is, Can the judgments of courts of limited jurisdiction or of inferior courts be collaterally impeached for fraud? The proper distinction, which we have discussed in a previous chapter, between the proceedings of courts of general jurisdiction when exercising special powers must be

Smith v. Smith et al, 22 Iowa, 2; Webster v. Reed, 11 How. 437.

Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N.Y. 253.

<br>

### --- Page 0300 ---

§ 187.] COLLATERAL ATTACK. 195

carefully observed, as well as the distinction between courts which are designated as of limited jurisdiction, but whose judgments after jurisdiction obtained have ordinarily attached to them the same presumption with respect to conclusiveness as courts of general jurisdiction. Also the distinction heretofore made by different courts with respect to what may constitute a direct as distinguished from a collateral attack. We have considered the question of fraud in a prior chapter with respect to domestic judgments in courts of general jurisdiction. When fraud would prevail in such cases it would prevail in cases of judgments rendered by courts of limited jurisdiction or by inferior courts. But the mere fact that the court in which the judgment was rendered was one of limited jurisdiction or an inferior court does not subject the judgment to collateral attack on the ground of fraud. In Indiana, however, an attack upon a judgment for fraud in its procurement is regarded as a direct attack, which is permitted notwithstanding the decree or judgment questioned may appear upon its face in all respects regular and valid.

NOTE TO CHAPTER 8

I have felt compelled to give the foregoing extended review of the authorities in order to convey to the profession the extent and limit of the application of general rules. In no other way could it be done. It would be misleading, as well as presumptuous, to merely state the construction of the rules stated, given by one court, as being the correct interpretation, or to state that other courts were of a contrary opinion. If the courts were arranged only on two sides of the proposition this might be done; but, as appears, the question seems to have many sides, and comparatively few in number of the courts are in accord upon any one side or feature. It is universally conceded that a judgment or decree of any court cannot be collaterally, incidentally or indirectly attacked for errors or irregularities that do not reach the jurisdiction of the court when fraud is not alleged, and the rule is most often stated with reference to such errors. In some jurisdictions judgments may be collaterally attacked for want of jurisdiction; hence it is deemed important to make the distinction as clear as possible between jurisdiction and judgment. In all cases it must first be determined whether the defect is error merely, or whether it is fatal to the judgment, disclosing a want of jurisdiction. In most jurisdictions the whole record may be examined, and may be found to be such as to impeach the recitals in, or presumptions from, the judgment. This may arise in a collateral pro-

Ferguson v. Crawford, 70 N. Y. 253. Cotterell v. Koon et al., 51 N. E. Rep. 285, and cases cited.

<br>

### --- Page 0301 ---

196 IMPEACHMENT. [$ 187.

ceeding, yet quite generally it is sufficient to defeat the judgment. Again, the record and the recitals may be sufficient, or the record may be silent. Here we find the greatest difficulty and the greatest conflict. Mere statements that the judgment may be collaterally assailed for want of jurisdiction to render it are of but little force. The great question is not alone of the right of collateral attack, but whether upon such an attack the want of jurisdiction can be shown otherwise than by the record. No classification could give any intelligent statement of the diverse opinions upon this question. The books must be consulted with reference to the particular or peculiar doctrine of each state. It has become immaterial which is the better rule. The courts have taken issue and adhere firmly to the position first taken. It is of great importance to ascertain what remedies are classed as collateral as distinguished from direct attack. As we have seen, the courts will not even agree upon this apparently very simple proposition. The whole subject appears to be in as much confusion as when the courts of this country were first organized, and, judging from the past, will remain so until some action is taken looking to a more uniform code of laws among the several states.

<br>

### --- Page 0302 ---

CHAPTER 9.

JUDGMENTS OF SISTER STATES AND FOREIGN JUDGMENTS.

I. JUDGMENTS OF SISTER STATES.
