231. Mechanics’ and laborers’ liens—Proceedings to enforce.  

Sec. 212. Judgments in rem defined—Effect of. — Judgments in rem are binding on all the world if the court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter. In rem is a technical term used to designate proceedings or actions instituted against the thing, in contradistinction to personal actions, which are said to be in personam. “Proceedings in rem include not only those instituted to obtain decrees and judgments against property as forfeited in the admiralty or English exchequer or as prize, but also suits against property to enforce a lien or privilege in the admiralty courts, and suits to obtain the sentence, judgment or decree of other courts upon the personal status or relation of

<br>

### --- Page 0329 ---

22k IMPEACHMENT. [§ 212.]

the party, such as marriage, divorce, bastardy, settlement or the like. A judgment in rem is an adjudication pronounced upon the status of some particular matter by a tribunal having competent authority for that purpose. It differs from a judgment in personam in this: that the latter judgment is, in form and substance, between the parties claiming the right, and that it is so inter partes appears by the record itself. A judgment in rem is founded on a proceeding instituted not against the person as such, but against or upon the thing or subject-matter itself whose state or condition is to be determined. It is a proceeding to determine the state or condition of the thing.

An attachment of property where the court has jurisdiction of the property, but not of the person of the defendant, and a sale under it on execution, is in the nature of a proceeding in rem. The judgment, if the defendant have no notice, will be treated as a nullity out of the jurisdiction so far as the person of the defendant is concerned, though it would be held binding as between the parties so far as regarded the property as a proceeding in rem. Judgments in personam are adjudications of the individual rights pronounced against particular persons, upon whom alone they are binding. Judgments in rem are adjudications pronounced upon the status of some particular matter by a tribunal having competent authority for the purpose. They decide and determine a status or make a final and conclusive transfer of property.

Actions in rem, strictly considered, are proceedings against property alone, treated as responsible for the claims asserted by the libelants or plaintiffs. The property itself is in such actions the defendant, and, except in cases arising during the war for its hostile character, its forfeiture or sale is sought for the wrong in the commission of which it has been the instrument, or for debts or obligations for which by operation of law it is liable. The court acquires jurisdiction over property in such cases by its seizure, and of the subsequent proceedings by public citation to the world, of which the owner is at liberty to avail himself by appearing as a claimant in the case.

1 Bouv. Law Dict. 692.  
4 State v. Railroad Co., 10 Nev. 47;  
2 Woodruff v. Taylor, 20 Vt. 65. Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185.  
3 Best's Evidence, sec. 539,

<br>

### --- Page 0330 ---

§§ 213, 214.] JUDGMENTS IN REM. 225

Sec. 213. Quasi-actions in rem.—There is, however, a large class of cases which are not strictly actions in rem, but are frequently spoken of as actions quasi in rem, because, though brought against persons, they only seek to subject certain property of those persons to the discharge of the claims asserted. Such are actions in which property of non-residents is attached and held for the discharge of debts due by them to citizens of the state, and actions for the enforcement of mortgages and other liens. Indeed, all proceedings having for their sole object the sale or other disposition of the property of the defendant to satisfy the demands of the plaintiff are, in a general way, thus designated. But they differ, among other things, from actions which are strictly in rem, in that the interest of the defendant is alone sought to be affected, that citation to him is required, and that judgment therein is only conclusive between the parties. Decisions in proceedings in rem are binding and conclusive, not only upon the parties actually litigating in the cases, but upon all others. Every one who can possibly be affected by the decision has a right to appear and assert his own rights by becoming an actual party to the proceedings. Strictly speaking, that only is a proceeding in rem in which the process is to be served upon the thing itself.

Sec. 214. Wills — Probate of.— A familiar instance of a judgment in rem is the probate of a will. The whole proceeding, both in form and substance, is upon the will itself. The only question to be determined is whether it is the will of the testator. It is true that the determination of the question incidentally affects rights of individuals to property, but the direct question is the status of the subject-matter, and when properly determined the adjudication is conclusive as to the world.

Note To Sec. 214.—It was said: “The proceeding is in form and substance upon the will itself. No process is issued against any one; but all persons interested in determining the state or condition of the instrument are constructively notified by a newspaper publication to appear and contest the probate, and the judgment is, not that this or that person shall.

11 Greenk Ev., sec. 525. 3 Woodruff v. Taylor, 20 Vt. 63, 2 Cole v. Cunningham, 133 U.S. 107.

<br>

### --- Page 0331 ---

226 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 214.

pay a sum of money, or do any particular act, but that the instrument is or is not the will of the testator. It determines the status of the subject-matter of the proceeding. The judgment is upon the thing itself; and when the proper steps required by law are taken, the judgment is conclusive, and makes the instrument as to all the world, at least so far as the property of the testator within this state is concerned, just what the judgment declares it to be. This is the instance of a proceeding upon a written instrument to determine its state or condition: and that determination in its consequences involves and incidentally determines the rights of individuals to property affected by it.” Woollruff v. Taylor, 20 Vt. 635. In another case (Newman v. Waterman, 63 Wis. 612) it is stated by Cassoday, Justice, that at common law the probate of a will was conclusive as to the personal property, but was no evidence as to the execution or validity of the will so far as it affected real property. That at common law and as to real estate, the will itself, on being duly proved in an action of ejectment or other suit affecting the title to realty, became a muniment of title. But that has been changed by statute in England and in several of the states, including Wisconsin. That in such state the probate of a will is made conclusive as to its due execution. It was stated in Brigham v. Fairweather, 140 Mass. 411, that “a judgment in rem is an act of the sovereign power; and as such its effects cannot be disputed, at least within the jurisdiction. If a competent court declares a vessel forfeited, or orders it sold free of all claims, or divorces a couple, or establishes a will under statutes like ours (Pub. Stats, ch. 127, sec. 7), a paramount title is passed, the couple is divorced, the will is established as against all the world, whether parties or not, because the sovereign has said that it shall be so. . . . It does not follow, nor is it true, that the judgment, because conclusive on all the world in what we may call its legislative effect, is equally conclusive upon all as an adjudication of the facts upon which it was grounded. On the contrary these judgments, such as sentences of prize courts, to which the greatest effect has been given in collateral proceedings, are said to be conclusive evidence of the facts upon which they proceed, only against parties who were entitled to be heard before they were rendered. (Citing The Mary, 9 Cranch, 126-146; Salem v. Eastern Railroad, 93 Mass. 431; Baxter v. New England Ins. Co., 6 Mass. 277; Whiting v. Walsh, 1 Cush. 29.) We may lay on one side, then, any argument based on the misleading expression that all the world are parties to a proceeding in rem. This does not mean that all the world are entitled to be heard; and as strangers in interest are not entitled to be heard, there is no reason why they should be bound by the findings of fact, although bound to admit the title or status which the judgment establishes. Still the cases last cited show that some judgments in rem are conclusive evidence of the facts adjudicated in favor of a stranger as against a party.” It was held that the probate of a will was no evidence of the testator’s mental capacity in a collateral issue; nor is the issue of letters of administration prima facie evidence of death. It was said that such holding was entirely consistent with the cases of Jeffers v. Radcliff, 10 N. H. 242, and Tisdale v. Insurance Co., 26 Iowa. 170. Mr. Bigelow in his work on Estoppel (4th ed. 237) states that the decrees

<br>

### --- Page 0332 ---

§ 215. JUDGMENTS IN REM. 227

of foreign probate courts within their jurisdiction are conclusive upon the admissibility of the instrument to probate and of the title derived from the issuance of letters testamentary and of administration. It was stated in Wall v. Wall, 128 Pa. St. 545, that the power of adjudication of the register was conferred by statute. Within these limits his decrees are conclusive. Outside of them he has no authority to make a decree. And it was held that the decree of the register admitting a will to probate was a nullity, where the will in fact had not been signed; that he was without jurisdiction.

Src. 215. Executors, administrators and guardians — Settlement of their accounts and other proceedings.— The settlements of the accounts of administrators, executors or guardians are classed as proceedings in rem, and the final order or judgment is conclusive as to the matters adjudicated as against a collateral attack, either on the part of the parties, privies or strangers. So with respect to the appointment of administrators or guardians by probate courts, not alone in the former on the ground that the judgment operates upon the res, nor alone in the latter, that it determines the status or relation of such persons, but on the ground that jurisdiction in such matters is vested, generally, exclusively in the first instance in such courts, and their judgments in these respects can only be questioned by some proceeding before that court in the first instance, which can be revised only by an appellate court in the manner provided by law. To illustrate: If one appointed by such a court as guardian of a minor commences a proceeding with respect to the rights of his ward, the question of the legality of his appointment cannot be put in issue; or if one be appointed as administrator in a state other than that of the decedent’s residence, when none had been appointed there, and should commence proceedings in respect to personal property that belonged to the deceased in his lifetime, though such appointment might be illegal, the question of the legality of his appointment could not be raised in such action. So, also, in an action by a devisee to recover land devised, which has been sold by the executor under license to pay debts, the court will not examine the finding of the probate court as to the ex-

1 Farrar v. Olinstead et al., 24 Vt. 123; Driggs v. Abbott, 27 Vt. 580.
2 Abbott v. Coburn, 28 Vt. 663;

<br>

### --- Page 0333 ---

228 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 215a.]

sistence of debts against the estate or the amount of them. So, also, where the records of the orphans’ court are offered in evidence to show a sale of land in dispute under a license for the payment of debts, and it appeared from the face of the petition that the debts were outlawed, the proceedings cannot be collaterally attacked. The settlement of an executor’s or administrator’s account is conclusive upon every subject adjudicated. The heirs, legatees or creditors cannot, in a suit on the probate bond, show as a breach that the sale of the real estate was fraudulent and in fact sold to the executor or administrator for less than its value. These matters can only be contested before the probate court at the time of the examination of his account. However, such settlement does not preclude such parties from proving that other property came into his hands for which he failed to account.

Sec. 215a. Impeachment for want of jurisdiction.— It is unnecessary here to repeat what has been stated in other chapters with respect to the impeachment of judgments. It may have been inferred by some from the strong language sometimes used, in substance that judgments in rem are conclusive upon all the world, and that, the court having jurisdiction of the res, the question of personal notice is immaterial, and like assertions, that a different rule might prevail with respect to their impeachment, either upon a direct or collateral attack.

But such is not the fact or the law. The rule is broadly stated to be: “That the jurisdiction of any court exercising authority over a subject may be inquired into in every other court, when the proceedings in the former are relied upon and brought before the latter by a party claiming the benefit of such proceedings; and the rule prevails whether the decree or judgment has been given in a court of admiralty, chancery, ecclesiastical court or court of common law, or whether the point ruled has arisen under the laws of nations, the practice in chancery, or the municipal laws of states.” The fact is, in the great leading

Norman v. Olney, 64 Mich. 553, 234. See also Holden v. Lathrop, 65 Mich. 652, 32 N. W. Rep. 879.

Herron v. Dater, 120 U. S. 464. Williamson v. Berry, 8 How. 540; Probate Court v. Merriam, 8 Vt. Thompson v. Whitman, 18 Wall. 457,

<br>

### --- Page 0334 ---

§ 215a. JUDGMENTS IN REM.

case in this country where the doctrine of the federal court was pronounced with respect to the question of impeachment of judgments (Thompson v. Whitman, supra), that the judgment collaterally assailed was one strictly in rem, and in that case it was distinctly affirmed that the jurisdiction of the court by which a judgment is rendered, whether in rem or in personam, in any state, may be questioned in a collateral proceeding in another state; and further, that evidence may be admitted to contradict the record as to jurisdictional facts asserted therein, and especially as to facts stated to have been passed upon by the court. In determining the force of a foreign judgment in rem, the court where it is propounded may look into the power under which the foreign tribunal acted to see that it had jurisdiction of the subject-matter. Says Mr. Justice Story: “It must appear that there have been regular proceedings to found the judgment or decree, and that the parties in interest in rem have had notice or an opportunity to appear and defend their interests before it was pronounced.” The question is, when a judgment comes in question, did the forum proceed according to its own municipal law in pronouncing such judgment or decree? The party contesting its validity should be allowed to show that it was procured by fraud, or that it is void on its face, or void by the local law, for res judicata? It is always competent to determine whether the judgment or decree is in excess of the jurisdiction of the court rendering it, and for such purpose the court in which the judgment is produced may inquire and determine whether such judgment was justified by the local law; that is, whether upon the case made the court had authority to render the particular judgment. Thus, when the supreme court of New York, proceeding under a special statute, decreed the dissolution of a corporation, it was held competent in another state to examine the statute under which the court proceeded and determine from that whether such a judgment was authorized, even though the New York court had construed the statute as giving it such power. It is equally competent for a court to determine.

1. Rose v. Himely, 4 Cranch, 260, Marshall, C. J. 126.  
2. Story's Conflict of Laws, sec. 592.  
3. Monroe v. Douglas, 4 Sandf. Ch.  
4. Folger v. Columbian Ins. Co., 99 Mass. 267.

<br>

### --- Page 0335 ---

230 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 216.

termine from the statutes of a particular state whether the proceedings taken thereunder, which resulted in a judgment, were in compliance with such statutes in essential matters, and whether its judgment rendered in the particular case was such as was authorized by the special statute. This must follow upon the authority of the case last cited, and as to jurisdictional facts is supported by the decisions of the federal court also cited.

Sec. 216. Distinction between judgments in rem and those quasi in rem — Probate judgments.— It is not surprising that members of the profession become confused, in wading through disconnected arguments and writings, upon the question of the conclusive effect of judgments, where no distinction is made as to the character of the proceedings in which the question becomes involved, a simple statement merely being made that such judgments are conclusive against collateral attack. Take, for instance, a treatise upon the question of the conclusive effect of judgments in rem. We find that those strictly such, and those not, are blended together, and general principles applied to each. In one sentence it is stated without qualification that the judgments of probate courts in respect to the sale of lands, or other disposition of property, are conclusive upon all the world; that they cannot be collaterally attacked. This is true in one sense, that they are not subject to attack by parties, privies or strangers when they come incidentally in question. If, however, the court has no jurisdiction over the subject-matter, and this appears, such judgments are nullities. Again, most proceedings in such courts are founded upon certain prerequisite conditions, usually by petition in some form, presenting as existing certain facts which call into exercise the power or jurisdiction of the court. If a purchaser brings an action in respect to the title, or rights growing out of the title to such property, relying upon such a judgment, it is beyond question that it is competent for the other party to show from the record either that such prerequisites were not observed, or, if observed, that either the petition or the notice required was deficient in essential requirements, and thus destroy the force of the judgment, though the proceeding is a collateral one. The writer has al-

<br>

### --- Page 0336 ---

§ 217.) JUDGMENTS IN REM. 231

ways been of the opinion that with respect to judgments of courts of limited jurisdiction, such as probate courts, where their functions are exercised in a manner particularly directed, less confusion would exist if attacks upon their judgments, when made in a similar manner to that last above stated, were designated as being in the nature of a direct attack in a collateral proceeding, and designating such attacks as first mentioned, that is, when they come incidentally into question, as collateral attacks in a collateral proceeding. Again, such judgments determine conclusively the title to the property only as against those who claim title under the deceased, while in proceedings strictly in rem, such as prize, forfeiture, and the like, the judgment is conclusive as to the title against all the world; and of like effect in general are judgments determining the status or relation of persons. Probate proceedings only have the characteristics of proceedings in rem, to the extent that what has been actually adjudicated, where the tribunal had jurisdiction of the subject-matter, is not open to be again reviewed by strangers. It is conclusive as an adjudication upon every one except where review is provided by law.

Sec. 217. Rule in federal court the same.— It may be said, however, that the decision of the federal supreme court is adverse to the position here assumed. It was there stated on a proceeding to sell the real estate of an indebted intestate, “there are no adversary parties; the proceeding is in rem; the administrator represents the land. They are analogous to proceedings in the admiralty, where the only question of jurisdiction is the power of the court over the thing, the subject-matter before them, without regard to the persons who may have an interest in it; all the world are parties. In the orphans’ court and all courts which have power to sell the estates of intestates, their action operates on the estate, not on the heirs of the estate. A purchaser claims not their title, but one paramount. The sale is a proceeding in rem, to which all claiming under the intestate are parties, which directs the title of the deceased.” The only expressions that could be so considered are those first marked in italics: “that the administrator represents the land;” “that

Grignon's Lessee v. Astor, 2 How. 319.

<br>

### --- Page 0337 ---

252 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 218.

they (the proceedings) are analogous to proceedings in admiralty.” etc. The expression may be inaccurate. Perhaps it would have been more accurate to have said the administrator represents the title or interest the deceased had in the land. That this was intended is plainly evident from what follows: “Their action (the courts’) operates on the estate;” “a purchaser claims not their (the heirs’) title, but one paramount;” “the sale directs the title of the deceased.” The second expression is used by way of illustration, and the learned judge did not say, nor did he intend to say, that all the world were parties to such a proceeding in probate courts, but that they were such as to proceedings in rem in admiralty. In many of the states such deeds are made prima facie evidence of title.

Sec. 218. Notice — Effect of failure to give when required. The question of notice, where required, is not of the character to obtain jurisdiction of the person, so as to bind him personally by the judgment, but is a step provided in respect to acquiring jurisdiction over the subject-matter, sometimes essential, sometimes not, and is designed more for the purpose of giving the persons interested an opportunity to be heard, at their option. Their appearance or non-appearance does not affect the manner of procedure. The whole matter must be investigated in the one case as well as the other, and determined, and nothing can be assumed or taken to his disadvantage against one, though regularly noticed, who does not appear. The whole proceeding is with respect to the right or title held by the deceased or ward, as the case may be, and not with respect, as is often stated, to the property itself. The judgment is in rem only with respect to the disposition of the title or interest held by the deceased at the time of his death. The supreme court of Wisconsin (Leth et al. v. Oberbranner, 40 Wis. 238), however, held that notice of the assignment of property devised under a will, by the probate court, was essential to jurisdiction, and that when no such notice was given to an heir he was not bound by the judgment and could maintain an action of ejectment against such devisee to recover land thus assigned to him, and

1See Robb v. Lessee of Irwin, 15 Ohio, 689. 2Chase v. Whitney, 30 Wis. 544.

<br>

### --- Page 0338 ---

§ 218a.] JUDGMENTS IN REM. 233.

that in such legal action the court could consider the matters adjudicated by the probate court, not only with respect to its final decree assigning the estate, but also the decision of the court as to the validity of the provisions of the will devising the estate in trust for charitable purposes. It was conceded that the proceedings were regular, with respect to such probate, in the essentials expressed by statute. That with respect to distribution they were also regular, with the possible exception of failure to give notice to the plaintiffs of the final order of distribution. As to this, however, notice such as expressed by the statute was given by publication. In Lobb v. Lessees of Irwin, 15 Ohio, 639, the question of the effect of probate decrees where land had been sold under license to pay debts was very ably and fully considered. There the statute provided that the heir should be made a party, and it was argued that the proceeding was adversary for this reason, and his interest in the land could not be diverted, but only upon personal notice. The court held otherwise, also, that the proceeding was in rem, and the judgment of the court conclusive against collateral attack. In Perkins v. Luirfield, 11 Mass. 227, it was also held that a title under a sale by administrators, by virtue of a license, was good against the heirs of the intestate, although the license was granted by the judge of probate, not authorized by the circumstances of the estate. These cases and many more which might be cited to the same effect proceed upon the principle that the petition gives the court jurisdiction, the proceedings are against the title, and whatever is determined by the court becomes conclusive against collateral attack.

§ 218a. Pedigree.— A judgment or decree of any court, foreign or domestic, having jurisdiction, establishing pedigree, is in rem, and is evidence of the facts adjudicated against all the world. However, it is so conclusive only with respect to the particular person in favor of or against whom the decree was rendered, and those claiming by or through or under him. In other words, it is conclusive upon the question of his pedigree. It in no manner affects the status of other children by

1 Ennis v. Smith, 14 How. 400.

<br>

### --- Page 0339 ---

204 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 218-220.

the same father and mother. Thus, where a decree was rendered declaring that a person was not the legitimate child of a deceased testate, and was based upon a finding that his father and mother were never lawfully married, it was held that such decree was not admissible as evidence, in an action brought by other children by the same connection, to prove their illegitimacy. Such decree operated as an estoppel only as to the person against whom it was rendered.1

Sec. 2180. Decree of sale of estate of lunatic.—Decrees of sale of estates of lunatics stand upon the same footing as other probate decrees heretofore considered. They are proceedings quasi in rem, and in some jurisdictions are binding on strangers to the proceedings.2

Sec. 218¢. Boundaries.—Proceedings under statutes to determine the question of boundary between towns have been held to be in rem, and conclusive upon all persons.3

Sec. 219. Actions of ejectment.—Actions of ejectment are classed as quasi in rem. The subject-matter is the title to the real estate involved. It is a proceeding in rem to the extent that the subject-matter is realty, not a personal demand. The judgment in such case is conclusive, however, only upon the parties and privies. It has no force as to strangers. It differs from probate proceedings of the character mentioned in this: that there are adversary parties as such. It is what may be termed an adversary proceeding. It is purely a personal action, having for its subject-matter real property.4

Sec. 220. Attachment and replevin.—The same is true with respect to an action of replevin, and also with respect to proceedings by attachment. In such proceedings the only question upon which the courts differ is as to what presumptions should apply with respect to compliance with conditions precedent, and their effect upon the parties and those claiming rights.

1 Kearney v. Denn, 15 Wall. 51.
2 Pitman v. Albany, 34 N. H. 577; Wyman v. Campbell, 6 Port. (Ala.) 219; Latham v. Wiswall, 2 Ired. Eq. 280.
3 Chirac v. Reinecker, 11 Wheat. 204.

<br>

### --- Page 0340 ---

§ 220. JUDGMENTS IN REM. 235

under them. Such proceedings do not affect strangers, except to prevent a purely collateral attack in a collateral proceeding. There is a difference of opinion as to presumptions, whether conclusive or otherwise, where the record is silent with respect to substantial compliance with precedent conditions. The rule generally prevailing is that seizure of the property, either actual or constructive, is an essential element to jurisdiction. If the writ is the lawful writ of the court, issued in proper form under the seal of the court, and it is by the proper officer levied upon property liable to the attachment, when such a writ is returned into the court, the power of the court over the res is established, though there may not have been personal service of process on the defendant or personal appearance by him. The elements of a proceeding in rem that are incident to an attachment are, that the subject-matter is res, and that the proceeding is against the res to the extent that personal service is not essential to jurisdiction to render the judgment. If there be no personal service, no personal judgment can be had. The judgment acts upon the right or interest of the party only, and cannot affect the right or interest of those not parties. The writ usually, in superior courts, is founded upon an affidavit, which must recite as existing certain conditions which the law makes essential to the issuing of the writ. In some jurisdictions this is the preliminary step to the commencement of the proceedings, while in others the action is commenced by summons or precept, and the attachment is auxiliary to the principal action. In the federal court the affidavit, though preliminary to the issuing of the writ, has served its purpose when the writ is issued, and though it may be defective this does not affect the jurisdiction of the court over the res; and if the record is silent as to the existence of such an affidavit, the order of sale and confirmation becomes the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction on all the acts preceding the sale, affirming their validity in the same manner as their judgment affirms the existence of the debt, and therefore the conclusive presumption attaches that all prerequisites were rightfully performed.

1 Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308, 10 Pet. 449; Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308  
2 Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308  
3 Voorhees v. Bank of United States

<br>

### --- Page 0341 ---

236 IMPEACHMENT. CS 221.

Sec. 221. Proceedings to subject property to the discharge of debts.—The doctrine was stated in Freeman v. Alderson, 119 U.S. 185, in substance to be, that actions which are brought against persons to subject certain property of those persons to the discharge of the claims asserted are not strictly actions in rem, but are sometimes classed as quasi in rem. Such are actions in which property of non-residents is attached and held for the discharge of debts due by them to citizens of the state, and actions for the enforcement of mortgages and other liens. In fact, all proceedings having for their sole object the sale or other disposition of the property of the defendant to satisfy the demands of the plaintiff are in a general way thus designated. But they differ, among other things, from actions which are strictly in rem, in that the interest of the defendant is alone sought to be affected, that citation to him is required, and that judgment therein is only conclusive over property within the territorial limits of the court. Quoting from the opinion in another case: "If the defendant appears the cause becomes one mainly a suit in personam, with the added incident that the property attached remains liable, under the control of the court, to answer any demand which may be established against the defendant by the final judgment of the court. But if there is no appearance by the defendant and no service of process on him, the case becomes in its essential nature a proceeding in rem, the only effect of which is to subject the property attached to the payment of the demand which the court may find to be due the plaintiff. That such is the nature of the proceeding in this latter class of cases is clearly evidenced by two well-established principles: First, the judgment of the court, though in form a personal judgment against the defendant, has no effect beyond the property attached in the suit. No general execution can be issued for any balance unpaid after the attached property is exhausted. No suit can be maintained upon the same judgment in the same court or any other court, nor can it be used as evidence in any other proceeding not affecting the attached property, nor could the costs in that proceeding be collected of defendant out of any other property than that attached in the suit. Second, the

1 Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308.

<br>

### --- Page 0342 ---

§ 2u2. JUDGMENTS IN REM. 237

court in such a suit cannot proceed unless the officer finds some property of defendant on which to levy the writ of attachment. A return that none can be found is the end of the case, and deprives the court of further jurisdiction, though the publication may have been duly made and proven in court. While the costs may be properly satisfied out of the property attached or otherwise brought under the control of the court, no personal liability for them can be created against the absent or non-resident defendant. The action was to recover an undivided interest in the property, and then to obtain a partition of it, and have that interest set apart in severalty to the plaintiffs. Costs were rendered by the court below, execution issued upon the judgment, the property sold to satisfy the judgment, and a deed issued to the plaintiff. It was classed as an action of trespass to try the title. It was further said that such action was not strictly in rem, though dealing entirely with realty. It was an action against the parties named, and the relief sought did not change its character as a personal action. The service by publication might be sufficient for the exercise of jurisdiction over the property to grant the relief prayed for, but it could not authorize the creation of any personal demand against the defendant, not even for costs, which could be satisfied out of his other property. A judgment for costs would be a judgment in personam.

Sec. 222. Substituted service — Effect of.— In another case (Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714) the subject was also under consideration by the federal court, and the rule declared that, “except in cases affecting the personal status of the plaintiff and in those wherein that ‘mode of service’ (publication) may be considered to have been assented to in advance, the substituted service of process by publication, usually provided for in the several states when actions are brought against non-residents, is effectual only where, in connection with process against the person for commencing the action, property in the state is brought under the control of the court and subjected to its disposition by process adapted to that purpose, or where the judgment is sought as a means of reaching such property or affecting some interest therein; in other words, where the action is in

<br>

### --- Page 0343 ---

253 IMPEACHMENT. LS 223.

the nature of a proceeding zz rem.” The supreme court of Wisconsin (Juris a. Barrett, 14 Wis. 51) held a doctrine in conflict with that announced by the federal court, in terms that by the service of a summons by publication pursuant to the statute, without a seizure of property by attachment, the courts of record of that state might acquire jurisdiction in an action upon contract so as to pronounce a judgment against a non-resident defendant which would be effectual and binding as against his property which was within the state at the time the order of publication was made, and which is not removed from the state or sold to a purchaser in good faith before the judgment is rendered. That the essential fact upon which such jurisdiction is made to depend is property in the state, and not its seizure upon attachment. This proposition was under consideration in Pennoyer v. Neff, supra, and there answered by the statement that the jurisdiction of the court to inquire into and determine the obligations of the defendant at all is only incident to its jurisdiction over the property; and further, that its jurisdiction in that respect cannot be made to depend upon the facts to be ascertained after it has tried the cause and rendered judgment. The court went further in that case and declared that personal judgments rendered against non-residents, where there was not personal service within the state, were in violation of the fourteenth amendment to the federal constitution as being rendered without due process of law.

Sec. 223. Notice — Failure to publish, when required — Effect on proceedings in federal courts.— So with respect to the publication of the notice, where it is required when there has not been personal service, it is the duty of the court to order such publication and to see that it has been properly made; and if there be no publication, a court of errors may reverse the judgment. But when the writ has been issued and the property condemned and sold, it cannot be held that the court has no jurisdiction for want of sufficient publication of notice. This rule is applied in such court to affidavits of publication. Defects in such an affidavit cannot be considered after

Jarvis v. Barrett, 14 Wis. 591; 2 Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 308, Miner v. Fitzgerald, 19 Wis. 393. 3 Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 714.

<br>

### --- Page 0344 ---

§ 224.) JUDGMENTS IN REM. 239

the court has acted upon it. The federal court admits that there may be cases where the rule above stated is not applicable, where the judgment is to have effect on personal rights, as in divorce suits or in proceedings to compel personal conveyance or other personal acts, in which the legislature has properly made the jurisdiction to depend upon the publication of notice or in bringing the suit to the notice of the party in some other manner when he is not within the territorial jurisdiction.

Sec. 224. Rule in state courts — Effect of affidavit for attachment defective in essential particulars.— The doctrine of the federal court, stated with respect to the conclusive effect of the affidavit in attachment proceedings upon the question of jurisdiction, does not prevail in most of the state courts. The rule prevailing is that the essentials required must be positively stated, and if they are omitted or otherwise stated this is fatal to the jurisdiction and to a judgment rendered in the action, where there has not been personal service, and in the absence of an appearance on the part of the defendant. In some states it is sufficient if the grounds be alleged in the language of the statute, while in others facts must be stated, and not merely conclusions. The affidavit must state a sufficient cause of action in order to sustain an attachment. It must state facts upon which the cause of action arose; a mere recital of them is not sufficient. If the affidavit states circumstances which have a legal tendency to make out the essential statutory facts required to be shown, and fairly call upon the judge to exercise his judgment upon the sufficiency of the evidence, it is enough to confer jurisdiction. There must be some evidence, however. Where an affidavit upon which an attachment

Cooper v. Reynolds, 10 Wall. 314; Miller v. Munson et al., 34 Wis. 319; Streissguth v. Reigelman, 75 Wis. 212; Emmett v. Yeigh, 12 Ohio St. 579; Streissguth v. Reigelman, 75 Wis. 335; Eureka S. H. Co. v. Slote, 4 Pomeroy v. Ricketts, 27 Hun, 242; Manton v. Poole, 4 Hun, 639; Haston v. Malavazi, 7 Daly, 147; Steuben Co. Bank v. Alberzer, 78 N.Y. 252; Ruppert v. Haug, 87 N.Y. 141; Thorrington v. Merrick et al., 101 N.Y. 5; Matthews v. Densmore, 43 Mich. 461; Van Norman v. Jackson, Judge, 49 Mich. 205; Roberts v. Landecker, 9 Cal. 262; Hisler v. Carr, 34 Cal. 646.

<br>

### --- Page 0345 ---

240 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 225.

was issued contained the following averment as to plaintiff's claim: “That Christian Haug, the above-mentioned defendant, is justly and truly indebted to the deponent in the sum of $1,017.54,—$768.54 for goods, wares and merchandise heretofore sold and delivered by the deponent to the defendant, and $250 on a promissory note made by defendant and to the order of deponent,—and deponent believes that he is justly entitled to recover said sum,” it was held this was insufficient to give jurisdiction to issue the warrant. The particular defect was that it did not appear in any manner that a counterclaim did not exist. In the supreme court of New York it was held that an attachment issued upon a defective affidavit is not void; that it binds the property until set aside on motion. If the law thus would deny the right of collateral attack, even on the ground of want of jurisdiction appearing upon the face of the record, it is not probable that the rule even in that state extends further than to affidavits that are merely defective as distinguished from those which omit statements of matter of substance.

Sec. 225. Effect of proceedings in attachment upon the property in certain cases.—An attachment only operates upon the interest of the defendant in the property. With respect to personal property the officer obtains a special interest therein, and can maintain either trover or trespass. Where, however, personal property thus seized is ordered to be sold as perishable, the sale of the thing itself is conclusive upon all the world. The lien in such case is transferred to the purchase money. A sale of a ship made by a master in case of necessity will bind all who have or claim to have an interest in the property. In case of replevin, however, the judgment cannot affect the independent title or interests of third persons. The writ, however, protects the officer.

1 Ruppert v. Haug, 87 N. Y. 141. 5 Woodruff v. Taylor, 20 Vt. 65.  
2 Carr v. Van Hoesen, 26 Hun, 316. 6 Wade on Attachment, sec. 136.  
3 Drake on Attachment (6th ed.), 7 Herman on Estoppel, sec. 324.  
sec. 245. § Shaeffer v. Marienthal et al, 17 Ohio St. 183: Spencer v. McGowen, Newton v. Adams, 4 Vt. 437. 13 Wend. 256.

<br>

### --- Page 0346 ---

€§ 226, 227.) JUDGMENTS IN REM. 241

Src. 226. Garnishment proceedings.—Proceedings by garnishment come within the same rule, and in their effect upon property are substantially the same as proceedings in attachment. The only distinction between the two is that the former reaches for effects or property that cannot be seized as in case of attachment, by a peculiar process. The right or title of third persons to the property itself is not affected. In some of the states the garnishee may protect himself against liability from other persons by paying the money into court or to a receiver, or submitting the question of his liability directly to the court. These proceedings are purely statutory, the statute usually prescribing certain essential matters of substance to be made to appear by petition or affidavit as preliminary to the issuing of the process, and the rule is substantially the same as in proceedings by attachment with respect to the effect upon the jurisdiction of the court to entertain the proceedings.

Src. 227. Replevin.—In replevin the preliminary proceedings are prescribed by statute. Usually an affidavit is required setting forth that the plaintiff is the owner of the property, or has a special interest therein, and entitled to the possession thereof, that the same is unlawfully detained by the defendant (when unlawfully taken, an allegation to that effect), with a statement also of the value of the property; also quite generally that the property has not been taken on execution, etc. In some of the states in courts of record the plaintiff's attorney directs the officer to take the property specified in the affidavit, by an indorsement on the affidavit signed by him. This has the effect of, and is a substitute for, a warrant. In those cases where the purpose of the proceeding is to obtain the actual delivery of the property in advance of the trial, such an affidavit is an essential prerequisite, and must contain statements substantially in compliance with the requirements of the statute. The affidavit only becomes a condition precedent when a delivery of the property is requested in advance. It does not

1 Kimball v. McComber, 50 Mich. 362. Bridges v. Layman, 31 Ind. 884; Adams v. Davis, 100 Ind. 10; Carlson v. Small, 82 Minn. 492; Phoenix v. Clarke, 2 Mich. 327.

<br>

### --- Page 0347 ---

242 IMPEACHMENT, [§ 228.]  
affect the jurisdiction of the court to proceed and determine the issues of title and right of possession in the action.

Sec. 228. Property seized upon attachment or execution by process out of federal court—Vice versa—Not subject of replevin.—There has been much discussion of the proposition whether property in possession of a United States marshal by virtue of process, judgment or order issued out of the federal court was the subject of replevin in proceedings in the state courts, and vice versa, whether property in possession of a sheriff by virtue of similar authority from the state courts was the subject of replevin in proceedings in the federal courts. The United States supreme court has determined the question, denying the right or jurisdiction of the state court to maintain such proceeding in cases first mentioned, as well as the right or jurisdiction of the federal court to maintain such proceedings in cases last mentioned, applying a principle which they state to be that, whenever property has been seized by an officer of the court by virtue of its process, the property is to be considered as in the custody of the court, and under its control for the time being; and that no other court has a right to interfere with that possession, unless it be some court which may have a direct supervisory control over the court whose process has first taken possession, or some superior jurisdiction in the premises. The same court maintains that there can be no distinction in the respect stated between process in rem and an attachment issued out of a common-law court, although the latter is not the foundation of the jurisdiction, and no lien attaches only as the result of seizure upon the writ. The reason is that the property has been seized under process issued by the federal court, which must determine the right without interference by any other court. Nor can property seized upon execution be so taken. Property levied on by attachment or taken on execution is brought by the writ within the scope of the jurisdiction of the court whose process it is, and as long

¹ Jarmin v. Ward, 67 N.C. 32; ² Buck v. Colbath, 8 Wall 334; Batchelor v. Walburne, 23 Kan. 733; Covell v. Heyman, 111 U.S. 176; Lamont v. Williams, 48 Kan. 558; Jarmin v. Howe, 24 How. 450.

³ Eads v. Stephens, 63 Mo. 90; Catterlin v. Mitchell, 27 Ind. 298; Taylor v. Carryll, 20 How. 583; Freeman v. Howe, 24 How. 450.

<br>

### --- Page 0348 ---

§ 229. JUDGMENTS IN REM. 243

as it remains in the possession of the officer it is in the custody of the law. It is the bare fact of that possession under claim and color of that authority, without respect to the ultimate right to be asserted otherwise and elsewhere, that furnishes to the officer complete immunity from the process of every other jurisdiction that attempts to dispossess him.

Sec. 229, Remedy of party where property has been wrongfully taken.—The remedy of a party, where property has been wrongfully taken and withheld, in respect to a recovery of the specific property so taken, is by an ancillary proceeding in the court where its process has been made the instrument of the wrong. For instance, if the original proceeding is in equity, the remedy is by a petition pro interesse suo, or by ancillary bill, or by summary motion, or by proceeding in the nature of an interpleader, or if proceedings authorized by statutes of the state in which the cause is pending afford an adequate remedy, by adopting them as part of the practice of the court. This principle is that a bill filed on the equity side of the court to restrain or regulate judgments or suits at law in the same court, and thereby prevent injustice or an inequitable advantage under mesne or final process, is not an original suit, but ancillary and dependent, supplementary merely to an original suit out of which it has arisen, and is maintained without reference to the citizenship or residence of the parties. Any party may file the bill whose interests are affected by the suit at law, whether parties to the suit or not. A person whose property has been wrongfully taken is not debarred from his remedy against the officer committing the wrong. An officer is protected against an action by such a party only in that class of cases where he could justify under process or order of a court, directing expressly the very act alleged to be wrongful, and not in that other class where the writ or order, such as a writ of attachment or other mesne process, and the final process of execution upon a judgment, commands the seizure of property described, not specifically, but only generally, as the property of the party named in the writ. In the latter the officer acts at his peril.

Covell v. Heyman, 111 U. S. 176. Freeman v. Howe, 24 How. 450;  
Krippendorf v. Hyde, 110 U.S. 276. Krippendorf v. Hyde, 110 U.S. 276,

<br>

### --- Page 0349 ---

244 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 229.

and is responsible for damages to the party injured for the consequences of any error or mistake in the exercise of his discretion in the attempt to enforce the writ. In the former, as he has no discretion, it is the court itself which acts, and the officer is protected in his obedience to its command. At first the rule declared by the federal court was not the same as that which had been declared by some of the state courts. The case of Heyman v. Covell, 44 Mich. 332, was a case that was reversed by the United States Supreme Court. The Wisconsin court declared that a doctrine which would prevent the state court from assuming jurisdiction in an action against a United States marshal who had wrongfully seized upon attachment issued out of the federal court the property of a third person, brought by such a person to recover it, was absurd. The court subsequently recognized the doctrine of the federal court, and held that the state court had no jurisdiction to entertain an action of replevin for property seized by a United States marshal to try the title or right of possession, but could, when the property had been taken by its process, order its return. That court also held that an action against such an officer would lie in the state court for trespass in seizing under an attachment the property of a third person.

NOTE TO SEC. 229.—The state courts, however, in many jurisdictions, hold that, where the property of a third person has been seized upon attachment or execution, replevin will lie against the officer. Gallagher v. Bishop, 15 Wis. 276; Williams v. Morgan, 50 Wis. 548; Libby v. Murray, 51 Wis. 371; Sewell v. Nichols, 34 Me. 582; Morse v. Hurd, 12 N. W. Rep. 246. It is true that the cases cited in the federal court involved the question of the right of an officer to take, upon replevin issued out of a state court, property in the possession of a United States marshal, either held upon execution or by levy upon an attachment. It will be observed, however, that one principle involved and declared is, that the property was in the custody of the court and no other court could interfere with its possession, and the reasoning indulged is applicable to property held under process by a state court. The Iowa court make a distinction where the property is in

1 Covell v. Heyman, 111 U. S. 176; 3 Gilman v. Williams, 7 Wis. 329; 7 Rio Grande R. Co. v. Gomiller, 132 Am. Dec. 219.
2 U.S. 478. 4 Booth v. Ableman, 16 Wis. 485;
3 Heyman v. Covell, 44 Mich. 332; S.C. 18 Wis. 495; Ward v. Henry, 19 Cooper v. Tompkins, 43 Mich. 406; Wis. 76
Gilman v. Williams, 7 Wis. 329, 76 Am. Dec. 219.

<br>

### --- Page 0350 ---

§ 230.) JUDGMENTS IN REM. 245

the possession of an officer of one of the state courts. They state that the district and circuit courts of that state derive their powers from the same source and are instituted for the determination of the same class of cases, neither affording any advantage over the other; therefore the same jealousy does not exist as between a federal and state court. Yet they concede that interference as between two co-ordinate courts cannot be allowed to extend to any question drawn directly in issue; that that one of two coordinate courts which first obtains possession of a controversy must be allowed to dispose of it finally. They hold, however, that a person not a party to an action aided by attachment in the circuit court may maintain replevin for the attached property in the district court; that no question is thus put in issue which is in issue in the former action. Seaton v. Higgins, 50 Iowa, 305.

In fact, the same has been held by the United States supreme court. A distinction is recognized where property is seized upon attachment instead of upon a writ of replevin or other process where specific property is directed to be seized. In the latter, say the court: “The officer has no discretion to use, no judgment to exercise, no duty to perform but to seize the property described. That it follows as a rule of law of universal application that if the court issuing the process had jurisdiction in the case before it to issue that process, and it was a valid process when placed in the officer’s hands, and in the execution of such process he kept himself strictly within the mandatory clause of the process, then such writ or process is a complete protection to him, not only in the court which issued it, but in all other courts. In the former class of writs the officer has a very large and important field for the exercise of his judgment and discretion. First, in ascertaining that the property on which he proposes to levy is the property of the person against whom the writ is directed; secondly, that it is property which by law is subject to be taken under the writ; thirdly, as to the quantity of such property necessary to be seized in the case in hand. In all these particulars he is bound to exercise his own judgment, and is legally responsible to any person for the consequences of any error or mistake in its exercise to his prejudice. He is so liable to plaintiff, to defendant, or to any third person, whom his erroneous action in the premises may injure. The court can afford him no protection against the parties so injured, for the court is in no wise responsible for the manner in which he exercises that discretion which the law reposes in him and in no one else.” Buck v. Colbath, 3 Wall. 334. The doctrine is carried to the extent that property seized upon execution issued out of the federal court, though upon a judgment entered upon confession, will not be delivered to an assignee of the debtor, though the entry of assignment was made within sixty days after the entry of such judgment, and under the statutes of the state was void as against existing creditors. It was held that the right of the creditor to the usual remedies in the federal court could not be affected by state legislation, Nelson Bros. v. Brochan, U. S. Cir. Ct. West. Dist. Wis, Feb. 9, 1898.

§ 230, Judgments strictly in rem — Familiar instances — Admiralty — Condemnation of land, etc. — Familiar instances of judgments strictly in rem are those in courts of

<br>

### --- Page 0351 ---

246 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 230.

admiralty relating to a prize or to condemnation of vessels; also those in other courts where confiscation or forfeiture for violation of the revenue or excise laws is decreed; those also which establish the personal status or relations of a party; those which operate upon the land directly, such as in proceedings to sell the land for the non-payment of taxes, or condemning it under the right of eminent domain (Stewart v. Board, etc., 8 Cush. 479), and the like. In such cases the law usually provides for constructive service upon absent or unknown parties; and where such notice is properly given the judgment becomes conclusive on all the world. The nature and effect of such judgments, together with the reasons upon which their conclusiveness is based, was elaborately stated by Mr. Justice Story in Gelston v. Hoyt, 3 Wheat. 246. First, speaking with reference to judgments of forfeiture, he says: “All persons having an interest in the subject-matter, whether as seizing officers or informers or claimants, are parties or may be parties to such suits as far as their interest extends. The decree of the court acts upon the thing in controversy and settles the title of the property itself, the right of seizure and the question of forfeiture.” Second, with respect to judgments of condemnation, he continues: “Nothing can be better settled than that a sentence of condemnation is, in an action of trespass for the property seized, conclusive evidence against the title of the plaintiff; . . . and upon principle where is there to be found a substantial difference between a sentence of condemnation and of acquittal en vem? If the former ascertains and fixes the forfeiture and therefore is conclusive, the latter no less ascertains that there is no forfeiture and therefore restores the property to the claimant. It cannot be pretended that a new seizure might, after an acquittal, be made for the same supposed offense; or, if made, that the former sentence would not, as evidence, be conclusive, and, as a bar, be peremptory against the second suit ex rem; and, if conclusive either way, it must be because the acquittal ascertains the fact that there was no forfeiture; and if the fact be found, it is strange that it cannot be evidence for the party if found one way, and yet can be evidence against him if found the other way. When property is seized and libeled as forfeited to the government, the sole object of the suit is to ascertain whether the seizure be right-

<br>

### --- Page 0352 ---

§ 231.) JUDGMENTS IN REM. 247

ful and the forfeiture incurred or not. The decree of the court in such case acts upon the thing itself and binds the interest of all the world, whether any party actually appears or not. If it is condemned, the title of the property is completely changed, and the new title acquired by the forfeiture travels with the thing in all its future progress. If, on the other hand, it is acquitted, the taint of forfeiture is completely removed and cannot be re-annexed to it. The original owner stands upon his title discharged of any latent claims with which the supposed forfeiture may have previously infected it.” Judgments of condemnation are conclusive evidence against the insured to falsify his warranty of neutrality in any action that may arise between the owners of the vessel and the insurers! So are judgments in proceedings for a libel, filed for repairs upon, or supplies furnished to, a vessel, conclusive upon the owners, though not parties.

Sec. 231. Mechanics’ and laborers’ liens—Proceedings to enforce.—Proceedings to enforce mechanics’ liens and laborers’ liens upon logs are in the nature of proceedings in rem. They affect the property, but are not conclusive upon strangers, in most jurisdictions at least. It is held that the materials supplied or labor performed must, in order to entitle the party who furnishes the one or performs the other to recover, have been furnished or performed at the request, either express or implied, of the owner. In Maine, however, it was stated with reference to proceedings to enforce a laborer's lien upon logs that the action was a proceeding in rem as well as in personam; and that in such a suit judgment could not be rendered for the plaintiff until notice of its pendency is given, such as in contemplation of law is notice to all the world. Notice to the personal defendant (the debtor) is not sufficient. Nor is an appearance by him or by persons claiming to be the owners of logs sufficient. It cannot be known that there are not others still who have an interest in the property and a right to be heard. Hence such notice of the pendency of the suit must be

1 Croudson v. Leonard, 4 Cranch, 434, Ohio St. 26,  
2 Thompson v. Steamboat Martin, 2  
3 Shaw et al. v. Allen, 24 Wis., 563.

<br>

### --- Page 0353 ---

248 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 231.]  
given as in contemplation of law is notice to all the world. The judgment affects only the interests of the parties to the record in the property, and if they have no interest therein there can be no lien decreed. The rights of third parties cannot be affected; nor those having rights and interests which accrued prior to the attaching of the lien. The proceedings are usually purely statutory, and the requirements of the statute must be strictly followed, otherwise the right and remedy will be lost. In many of the states, if the petitioner fails to establish his lien, he may have judgment in personam against the party primarily liable for the debt. The provisions of the statute have no application where a lien is sought to be enforced against a municipality.

3 Grosbeck v. Ferguson, 43 Iowa, 432.

Scott, 46 Tex. 1; Gould v. Harrison, 48 Ill. 258; Brugman v. McGuire, 32 Ark. 733; Van Winkle v. Stow, 23 Cal. 458; Bedsole v. Peters, 79 Ala. 133; More v. Ruggles, 15 Wis. 275; Everett v. Avery, 19 Md. 136.
