Sro. 232. Parties and privies defined.— Thus far the question discussed has been considered with reference to the effect of judgments upon, and the rights and remedies of, parties and privies to the proceedings. It may not in all cases be easy to determine the question of privity, that is, whether a person stands in such relation to the judgment, or whether he is a stranger. With respect to the conclusive effect of judgments upon privies, the term “privity” denotes mutual or successive relationship to the same rights of property. “But it should be noticed,” says Bigelow in his work on Estoppel (4th ed., 135),  

1Greenleaf’s Ev., sec. 189,

<br>

### --- Page 0355 ---

250 IMPEACHMENT, § 233.

“that the ground of privity is property, and not personal relation. To make a man privy to an action he must have acquired an interest in the subject-matter of the action, either by inheritance, succession or purchase from a party, subsequently to the action.” Yet it has been held that persons who are directly interested in the suit, who have knowledge of its pendency, and who neglect to appear, may be as effectually concluded as if they were parties to the record. This, perhaps, is qualified by limitation to such as had a right to appear and control the defense, and then only in certain classes of actions equitable in their nature. There may be cases where the doctrine of estoppel might apply in other actions, but it is not proper to digress and specifically refer to them here.

Note to Sec. 232.— As a general rule, a judgment concludes parties and privies, but not strangers. And by parties is meant all those who have had a right to make defense or to control the proceeding and to appeal from the judgment. Persons not having these rights are regarded as strangers. And by privies is meant such persons as are privies in estate, as donor and donee, lessor and lessee, and joint tenants; or privies in blood, as heir and ancestor; or privies in representation, as executor and testator, or administrator and intestate; or privies in law, when the law, without privity in blood or estate, casts land upon another by escheat. Buckingham v. Ludlum, 37 N. J. Eq. 137.

Sec. 233. Third persons — When they may impeach judgments.— A third person who is affected by a judgment, not being either a party or privy thereto, may avoid such judgment in a collateral proceeding on the ground of want of jurisdiction in the court to render it, or of fraud and collusion practiced by the parties in procuring it, as well as on the ground of errors and defects in the proceedings, which make the judgment erroneous. This may be done by plea and proof. The particular ground upon which this rule is made to rest is that, as such persons cannot resort to appeal or writ of error to review or reverse the judgment, having a right to impeach it, they are not to be precluded from a remedy. It must not be

1 Albert v. Hamilton, 76 Ind. 304; Weare, 142 Mass. 231; Granger v. Jackson, 6 Mass. 244; Safferd v. Parr v. State, 71 Md. 220. 
2 McKenzie v. B. & O. R. Co., 28 Md. 316; Cecil v. Cecil, 19 Md. 72. 
3 Downs v. Fuller, 2 Met. 135; Pearce v. Clark, 22 Me. 128; Bergman v. Hutchinson, 60 Miss. 872; Griswold v. Stewart, 4 Cow. 457.

<br>

### --- Page 0356 ---

§ 254. BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS.

understood that the right extends to all who may be strangers, but only to such who will be prejudiced by the judgment if it be given full faith and credit in respect to some pre-existing right.

Sec. 234. Judgments ordinarily not admissible in evidence against a stranger—Exceptions.—As a rule a judgment is not admissible in evidence for or against a stranger to the action. It is, however, admissible for certain purposes, such as proving the fact of its rendition, or for the purpose of establishing a claim of title, or showing the character or extent of the possession of a party, or to prove the existence of some other collateral fact. No interest which the person may have who is not a party to the record is ordinarily, in proceedings other than those en rem, concluded or affected by the judgment or decree; but where it is introduced in evidence merely to establish a link in the chain of title, or to show the character in which a party holds title to land, it imports absolute verity, and the objection of res inter alios acta cannot avail to exclude it. Says Mr. Greenleaf: “There is a material difference between proving the existence of the record and its tenor and using the record as the medium of proof of the matters of fact recited in it. In the former case the record can never be considered as res inter alios acta, the judgment being a public transaction, rendered by public authority, and being presumed to be faithfully recorded. It is therefore the only proper legal evidence of itself, and is conclusive evidence of the fact of the rendition of the judgment, and of all the legal consequences resulting from that fact, whoever may be the parties to the suit in which it is offered in evidence.

1 Wilcher v. Robinson, 78 Va. 602.

2 Mayo v. Wood, 50 Cal. 171; McDonald & Co. v. Gregory, 41 Iowa, 513; Stoddard v. Burton, 41 Iowa, 582; Dooley v. Patten, 140 Mass. 49; Hill v. Stevenson, 63 Me. 364; Davis Machine Co. v. Barnard, 43 Mich. 379; Meltzer v. Doll, 91 N.Y. 865; Buckingham v. Ludlom, 37 N.J. Eq. 137; Chandler's Appeal, 100 Pa. St. 262; Williams v. Williams, 63 Wis. 58; Goodman v. Niblack, 102 U.S. 556.

3 Williams v. McGrade, 130 Minn. 46; Spencer v. Dearth, 43 Vt. 98; Harrison v. Harrison, 39 Ala. 449; 1 Greenleaf’s Ev., sec. 506.

4 Lessee of Buckingham v. Hanna, 2 Ohio St. 551.

5 King v. Chase, 15 N.H. 9.

6 Lessee of Buckingham v. Hanna, 2 Ohio St. 551.

<br>

### --- Page 0357 ---

252 IMPEACHMENT. [3 235.

for an assault and battery has been acquitted, and sues the prosecutor for malicious prosecution, the record of acquittal is evidence for the plaintiff to establish that fact, notwithstanding the parties are not the same. But if he were convicted of the offense, and then is sued in trespass for the assault, the record in the former case would not be evidence to establish the fact of the assault, for, as to the matters involved in the issue, it is res judicata.*

Note to Sec. 234.—The supreme court of Maine evidently limit the application of the rule. They say: “While it is generally true that an erroneous judgment cannot be avoided by writ of error, the books abound in cases where manifest injustice would be done to parties who have no right to reverse a judgment by writ of error, unless they had the right to impeach it collaterally. Hence the rule of law has been so far relaxed in such cases as to allow parties to impeach a judgment by plea and proof, where the court had no jurisdiction, or it had been obtained by fraud or collusion or erroneously and unlawfully entered up. Beyond this the rules of law do not authorize parties to proceed in the collateral impeachment of judgments: and where a judgment in a personal action is not liable to either of these objections, whether rendered in default or after contestation, it is conclusive as to the relation of debtor and creditor between the parties, and cannot be collaterally impeached by third parties in a subsequent suit where such relation and indebtedness are called in question.” It was held, the action being ejectment, the plaintiff's title depending upon the levy of an execution in favor of the plaintiff against the defendant's grantor, made subsequent to the conveyance, that all testimony tending to show that the note constituting the foundation of the judgment was invalid was inadmissible; that a stranger could not be permitted to retry an issue of fact already determined between the parties. Sidensparker v. Sidensparker, 52 Me. 481. This court had previously held that, in an action to compel conveyance of land held in alleged fraud of the plaintiff as a judgment creditor, the grantee might impeach such judgment on the ground that it was in excess of the amount which by law he was entitled to recover (Sargent v. Salmond, 27 Me. 541),—making the distinction in the case first cited between error of fact and error of law. It would seem that under somewhat similar state of facts the supreme court of Massachusetts arrived at a different conclusion. They state: “The tenant was not a party or privy to the suit, and is not concluded by the judgment, but may show that the note upon which it was founded was not a valid debt, or was paid, or that the administrator with the will annexed could not maintain an action upon it against the maker.” Tarbell v. Jewett, 129 Mass. 457.

Sec. 235. Illustrations of the rule and exceptions.—The learned author notes several illustrations of the distinction no—

*11 Greenl. Ev., sec. 538.

<br>

### --- Page 0358 ---

§ 235.] BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS, 253

ticed, mentioning, among others, that of a judgment against a sheriff for the misconduct of his deputy being evidence against the deputy of the fact that the sheriff has been compelled to pay the amount awarded and for the cause alleged, but not evidence of the fact of misconduct of the deputy, unless he was notified of the suit and required to defend it. So where the record is matter of inducement or necessarily introductory to other evidence; as in an action against the sheriff for neglect in regard to an execution, or to show the evidence of a witness upon a former trial, or where the judgment constitutes one of the muniments of the party's title to an estate, as where a deed was made under a decree of chancery or a sale was made by a sheriff upon an execution. A record may also be admitted as showing a solemn declaration or admission of a fact upon that ground alone and not as evidence tending to establish the fact. Speaking with reference to the admissibility of a decree in evidence upon the question of proving a claim of title, it was said by the supreme court of the United States: “To reject the proof of the decree would be in effect to declare that no title derived under a decree in chancery was of any validity except in a suit between parties and privies, so that in a suit by or against a stranger it would be mere nullity. It might with as much propriety be argued that the plaintiff was not at liberty to prove any other title deeds in a suit because they were res inter alios acta.”

It was further stated: “It is true that in general judgments and decrees are evidence only in suits between parties and privies; but the doctrine is wholly inapplicable to a case like the present, where the decree is not introduced as per se binding upon any rights of the other party, but as an introductory fact to a link in the chain of plaintiff's title and constituting a part of the muniment of his estate. Without establishing the existence of the decree it would be impossible to establish the legal validity of the deed from Johnson to the lessors of the plaintiff, which was made under the authority of that decree.”

A judgment in replevin against an officer holding goods under an attachment does not estop the same or prevent any other officer from levying execution on the same goods in a

11 Greenl. Ev., sec. 539; 3 Barr v. Gratz, 4 Wheat. 213; Parsons v. Copeland, 33 Me. 370.

<br>

### --- Page 0359 ---

254 IMPEACHMENT, [$ 2385.

different suit brought by another creditor of the party against whom the attachment was issued, where such creditor had no interest in the replevin suit, nor right to interfere therein. Nor would it be conclusive of the title as against a purchaser at the execution sale. A sheriff in making levy and sale merely exercises a power in the interest of a party whose writ is delivered to him for execution, and a purchaser at the execution sale does not stand in the sheriff's right, but as the privy of the party under whose execution the sale is made."

Note To Sec. 235.—Judgment of foreclosure against the mortgagor, where the latter has, prior to the commencement of the action, parted with his equity of redemption, is not conclusive upon such purchaser of the amount due upon the mortgage in an action by the latter to redeem. Dooley v. Patten, 140 Mass. 49. Judgment in ejectment rendered against one who holds the demised premises as the tenant of another will not operate as an estoppel against a third person who afterwards takes possession of such premises without any privity with such tenant or his landlord. Mayo v. Wood, 50 Cal. 171. Judgment against an administrator on his administration bond for neglect to include in his inventory money deposited in a savings bank, though subsequently he so inventoried such money and collected it, was held not a bar to a recovery of the same money from him by persons who claimed and proved that the deceased in her lifetime had donated such money to them. The latter not being parties to the suit, and not being heard, their rights were not affected by the judgment. Hill et al. v. Stevenson, 63 Me. 364. See also Gardner v. Merritt, 32 Ind. 78. A judgment against the surviving member of a firm does not conclude the representatives of the deceased partner. They are strangers to the judgment. Buckingham v. Ludlam, 37 N. J. Eq. 137; Smith v. Ballantyne, 10 Paige, 101. In an action upon one of four promissory notes executed in payment of a threshing machine, it appeared that one of the notes had been transferred, and that the same defense of breach of warranty and want of consideration as pleaded in the present action had therein been adjudged without merit. It was held that the former adjudication was not conclusive upon the parties to the case at bar and could not be pleaded therein. McDonald & Co. v. Gregory, 41 Iowa, 513. A judgment canceling a contract between the plaintiff and the defendant in that action, and enjoining the collection of one of two notes given as a consideration for the contract, was held not to estop the maker of the note the collection of which was not enjoined, in a suit against him upon the note by the plaintiff in the original action, claiming to be the owner thereof, and not having delivered it to the defendant therein, from proving that he had paid the same to such defendant while he had the same in his possession, claiming to be its owner. Such maker was neither a party nor privy to the original action. Stoddard v. Burton, 41 Iowa, 5!

McKay v. Kilburn, 42 Mich. 614

<br>

### --- Page 0360 ---

§§ 236, 237.] BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 255

Sec. 236. Judgment may be impeached for fraud.— Judgments may be impeached for fraud in procuring them, by such third parties as would be prejudiced by them if given effect, in respect to some prior right, and this may be done in a collateral proceeding. The right, as hereinbefore stated, is restricted to such parties as may be thus prejudiced by them. Hence, subsequent vendees, or any person not injured by the fraud, cannot impeach the judgment on that ground. The fraud, however, must have been participated in by the plaintiff in the suit in which the judgment was obtained, or if property has been sold upon execution issued thereon by the vendee in such sale.

Sec. 237. No indebtedness or judgment for excess of amount due is ground of impeachment.— There is some controversy among courts as to whether third parties, such as creditors, may prove there was no debt due to the judgment creditor. It seems to have been held in several cases that this question was a proper question of inquiry and the judgment was not conclusive; but with respect to the admission of evidence to show that the judgment was for too large an amount, there is also much diversity of opinion; however, the courts are practically agreed, if the excessive amount was the result of collusion or fraud on the part of the parties, that the judgment is equally open to attack as where no debt in fact existed. When, however, it is simply the result of mistake, and there is an absence of fraudulent intent, then some courts hold that the judgment is conclusive with respect to the amount, not only as to the parties and privies, but also as to strangers; while others seem to

1 Webster v. Reed, 11 How. 437; Reeves v. Kennedy, 43 Cal. 643; Michaels v. Post, 21 Wall. 398; Hackett v. Manlove, 14 Cal. 85; Freyendall v. Baldwin, 103 Ill.; Bridgeport F. & M. Ins. Co. v. Wilson, 34 N. Y. 275; Biddle v. Tomlinson, 115 Pa. St. 209; Atkinson v. Allen, 12 Vt. 619.
2 Wilcher v. Robertson, 78 Va. 602.
3 Hogg v. Link, 90 Ind. 346; Johns v. Pattee, 55 Iowa, 665; Smith v. Keen, 26 Me. 411; Mosely v. Mosely, 15 N.Y.
4 Kirby v. Fitzgerald, 31 N.Y. 417.
5 Yarbell v. Jewett, 129 Mass. 457; Henderson v. Thornton, 37 Miss. 448; Bergman v. Hutchinson, 60 Miss. 872; Gottlieb v. Thatcher, 34 Fed. Rep. 435, and cases cited.
6 Willard v. Whitney, 49 Me. 235; Eureka Iron & Steel Works v. Bresnahan, 66 Mich. 489; Swihart v. Shaum, 24 Ohio St. 4; Alpine v. Sweetzer, 76 Ind. 78; Sidensparker v. Sidensparker, 52 Me. 431.

<br>

### --- Page 0361 ---

23

DH

200 IMPEACHMENT. [

fe

hold that the question of the actual amount of the debt may be examined. In Sufford v. Weare, however, the judgment appeared to be greater than that demanded.

Sec. 238. Instances of privies and strangers.— It is not our purpose to give at length illustrations where it has been determined that a party stood in the relation to the original judgment of privy or as a stranger, but simply to select some few instances which have special reference to designated classes of persons, having relation of some character to the parties to the original action.

Heirs.— The relation of an heir to proceedings by the administrator to sell land for the payment of debts, whether privy or not, is a question which has received much attention by the courts, and one as to which courts have come to different conclusions. In a former chapter, in discussing the effect of probate proceedings of the character stated as in rem, we concluded, upon the authority of the decision of the supreme court of the United States and those of some of the older states, that a judgment of sale could not be collaterally attacked by the heir (secs. 215, 217, 218). We there found an apparent exception in the Wisconsin court. Other courts have held in effect that an heir is neither a party nor privy to such proceedings. That while the allowance of a claim against the estate has the effect of a judgment in most respects, yet, in proceedings to sell the real estate to satisfy such claims, the judgment does not preclude the heir from attacking the claim or its allowance. The ground upon which this ruling is made to rest was stated to be, that judgments only bind parties and privies, and that the heir is neither. That at common law a judgment was no evidence against the heir, and that the petition to sell the real estate of an intestate is the substitute under our system for the action against the heir. That the proceeding to sell the real estate amounts simply to a re-examination of the claim to test its validity against the heir, and to produce or prevent a decree for the sale of the land. The case of Conway v. Duncan, 28

1 Safferd v. Weare, 142 Mass. 231; Tate of Hidden, 29 Cal. 363; Stone v. Warwick v. Pelty, 44 N. J. L. 542. Wood, 16 Ill. 177.  
2 Beckett v. Selover, 7 Cal. 216; Es-

<br>

### --- Page 0362 ---

§ 239.) BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 257

Ohio St. 102, is sometimes cited as supporting the doctrine above expressed. In that case, however, it was only held that the heir could attack such a judgment on the ground of fraud; that this might be done upon cross-petition in the same proceeding. This latter proposition was ruled upon the ground that such manner of attack was permissible under the code. That judgments were impeachable for fraud, and therefore the same relief could thus be obtained as if the proceeding was in chancery to restrain the administrator from proceeding to sell the land on the ground that the alleged debts and judgments upon them were fraudulent, and were obtained without the fault or negligence of the complainant. The heirs and devisees of a deceased person are bound by a judgment against him, and are entitled to the benefits of one in his favor.

Sec. 239. Sureties.—There exists much conflict in the courts with respect to the conclusive effect upon the sureties of judgments against their principal. A definite rule, which will be recognized in every jurisdiction, cannot be stated. In many courts, the doctrine is held with respect to official as well as other bonds that the judgment is at least prima facie evidence against the sureties. That when one is responsible by force of law or by contract for the faithful performance of the duty of another, a judgment against that other for a failure in the performance of such duty, if not conclusive, is prima facie evidence in a suit against the party so responsible for that other, to stand until impeached or controlled in whole or in part by countervailing proof. The principle governing, as stated by some courts, is, that the liability of the surety is dependent upon that of the principal. He covenants (in respect to official bonds) that his principal shall discharge certain official duties. When it is once fairly determined by a competent judicial tribunal that there has been a breach of the official undertaking, the liability of the surety prima facie attaches, whether he was a party to the action adjudging the breach or not. Though not a party, the surety is not a stranger to the

1 See also Sidener v. Hawes, 37 Ohio Met. 309; McLaughlin v. The Bank of
St. Potomac et al. 7 How. 220; Drum-
2 Boykin v. Cook, 61 Ala. 472.
3 City of Lowell v. Parker et al, 10 St. 532; Charles v. Hoskins, 14 Iowa, 471.

<br>

### --- Page 0363 ---

258 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 240, 241.

judgment. In other courts the reasoning is not the same, though the same conclusion is reached. The sureties are strangers to the judgment, and are bound by the judgment only because the condition of their bond makes them so. They agree that the principal shall faithfully execute his duty. The judgment proves a breach of the bond prima facie.

Sec. 240. Judgment against the principal prima facie evidence—Conclusive as to errors.—The use of the term prima facie does not completely express the doctrine of the rule. Such judgment may be only prima facie evidence of the liability of the surety, and at the same time may be conclusive evidence with respect to the fact of a breach of conditions of the bond upon which his liability is made to depend. The sureties are so far concluded by its terms that they cannot impeach it for error or irregularity by an appeal on their own motion nor collaterally. They may show in defense either that the bond was not made, or that the judgment was not made, or, if made, that the same has been obeyed; or that the same was obtained by fraud or collusion; or that there is a mistake in the amount; but they cannot show that the court has erred in making the judgment or other matters affecting the merits which was determined in the original action against the principal. It is stated by the California court that a remedy is afforded by appeal for errors in the original action, which would imply that the surety was a party or privy in such sense that he would have the right of appeal.

Sec. 241. Such judgments are conclusive in some courts.—There are other courts which hold the doctrine that judgments against the principal are conclusive upon the sureties and can only be impeached for fraud, and the rule applies to official bonds. The case of Tracy v. Goodwin et al., 5 Allen, 409, is often cited as sustaining the foregoing rule. It does to the

1 Charles v. Hoskins, 14 Iowa, 471. v. Terry, 35 N. Y. 256. See also Stovall  
2 Annett v. Terry, 35 N. Y. 256. v. Banks, 10 Wall. 583.  
3 Annett v. Terry, 35 N. Y. 256.  
4 Irwin v. Backus, 25 Cal. 214  
5 Cal. 214; 6 Masser v. Strickland, 17 S. & R  
Heard v. Lodge, 20 Pick. 538; Annett 354; Evans v. Commonwealth, 8 Watts (Pa.), 398

<br>

### --- Page 0364 ---

§§ 242, 243.] BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 259

extent that when the official bond is joint, not several, then the judgment is conclusive upon the principal and surety alike, on the ground that it is a guaranty to the party bringing action for such amount as he has legally established to be due to himself from the officer, and that in the absence of fraud or collusion the judgment against him conclusively settles against his sureties, as well as himself, not only the right of the plaintiff to recover against him, but the amount of the damages. The court say: "If the bond had been several as well as joint, there would have been less embarrassment in treating the evidence as prima facie, and permitting the sureties to offer rebutting evidence."

Sec. 242. In some other courts such judgments are no evidence at all against the surety.— There are still other courts which hold that such judgments are no evidence at all against the sureties, and that in an action upon the bond they are at liberty to prove facts which would have established a good defense in the action against the principal. That in such cases the general rule prevails that none but parties or privies are concluded by a judgment. An exception to the foregoing rule is, that the party entitled to be indemnified may throw upon the indemnitors the burden and risk of the primary litigation, by giving to them the opportunity of defending the original suit. It then becomes the defense of the indemnitors and they are concluded by its results as privies, at least in the absence of fraud or collusion, or collusion between the prosecuting party and him whom they are bound to defend.

Sec. 243. Distinction recognized between an undertaking general in its terms and one where the principal is to do a specific act to be ascertained in a given way.— A distinction, however, is recognized in those courts which hold that judgments against the principal are not conclusive upon the sureties,

1 Thomas v. Hubbell, 15 N.Y. 405; Governor v. Backus, 6 Ala. 826; Foxcraft v. Governor, 25 Cal. 214; Pico v. Webster, 14 Cal. 202; Irwin v. Nevens, 4 Greenl. 72; Beall v. Beck, 7 Greenl. 237; Dane v. Gilmore, 8 H. & McH. (Md.) 242; Carmichael v. Governor, 3 How. (Miss) 236; Slee v. Bloom, 20 John. 669; Kip v. Brigham, 6 John. 158; Lucas v. Thomas v. Hubbell, 15 N.Y. 405.

<br>

### --- Page 0365 ---

260 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 244.]

where the undertaking on the latter's part is that the principal shall do a specific act, to be ascertained in a given way, from those cases where the undertaking is general in its terms, that the principal will perform his official duties. The reasoning is, that in the former class of cases the obligation is express that the principal will do this thing, and the judgment is conclusive of the fact and extent of the obligation. As the surety in such case stipulates without regard to notice to him of the proceedings to obtain the judgment, his liability is of course independent of such fact. It is upon this ground that the liability of bail is fixed absolutely by the judgment against the principal.

This rule rests upon the terms of the contract. In the case of official bonds the sureties undertake, in general terms, that the principal will perform his official duties. They do not agree to be absolutely bound by any judgment obtained against him for official misconduct, nor to pay every such judgment. They are only held for a breach of their obligations, as to which the legal principle is that no party can be so held without an opportunity to be heard in defense. This right is not divested by the fact that another party has defended in the cause of action and been unsuccessful, as the sureties did not stipulate that they would abide by the judgment against the principal, or permit him to conduct the defense and be themselves responsible for the result of it. The fact that the principal has unsuccessfully defended has no effect on their rights. They have a right to contest with the plaintiff the question of their liability.

§ 244. Administrators’ bonds.—The effect of such judgments upon the sureties of administrators is not the same as in cases of official bonds. The courts in those jurisdictions which refuse to give conclusive effect to judgments against the principal, as against the sureties upon official bonds, evidently make an exception with respect to administrators’ bonds, classifying such sureties, in respect to their liability for the default of the principal, with such sureties as we have mentioned, as covenant that their principal shall do a particular act, such as sureties upon bail or appeal bonds, whose liability is fixed by the judg-

Pivo v. Webster, 14 Cal. 202,

<br>

### --- Page 0366 ---

§ 244.) BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 261  
ment against their principal. This distinction seems to be founded upon the terms of the obligation into which the sureties upon an administrator's bond enter, which are that their principal shall faithfully perform all the duties imposed upon him by the nature of his trust, and will account for and pay over all money which may come into his hands pursuant to the orders and decrees of the probate court. The account must be rendered and settled by the court, and the money must be paid out and distributed by and pursuant to the orders of the court, and the undertaking of the sureties is that their principal will do all this! In some of the states a contrary rule is maintained. Thus, in North Carolina it was held that a recovery against a guardian was no evidence against his sureties in an action upon his bond for the non-payment of the judgment. The same court held that a judgment obtained by a creditor of an intestate against his administrator could not be used as evidence by the creditor in an action against the sureties upon the administrator’s bond. In others that such judgments are prima facie evidence only.

The New York court of appeals has been classed as holding a similar rule. It was there simply held that the decree of the surrogate court could be impeached for fraud or collusion. It is true they use the term that the decree is prima facie evidence of the amount due from the administrator, but this is coupled with the statement that its recovery upon the admis-

v. Dekum, 52 Pac. Rep. 517; Meyer v. Barth (Wis.), 72 N. W. Rep. 74  
Kellar v. Bowell et al., 4 Hawks, 214; Stovall v. Banks, 10 Wall.;  
Baggott v. Boulger, 2 Duer, 160; People ex rel. v. Laws, 3 Abb. Pr.;  
Ulver v. State, 5 Port. (Ind.), 265  
Jobbs v. Middleton, 1 J.J. Marsh. (Ky.) 177; State v. Holt, 27 Mo. 310; Slagle v. Enterkin, 44 Ohio St. 637; Braiden v. Mercer, 44 Ohio St.;  
Weber v. North, 51 Iowa, 375; Rallston v. Wood, 15 Ill. 159; Williamson v. Howell, 4 Ala. 693; Shepard v. Pebbles, 38 W.;  
Botkin v. Kleinschmidt, 52 Pac. Rep. 563; Thompson  
McBride v. Choate, 2 Ired. Eq. 610.

Bemuth v. Graham, 71 Ga. 212; Fauntleroy v. Lyle, 5 T. B. Mon. (Ky.) 266; Verret v. Belanger, 6 La. Ann. 109; Lipscomb v. Postell, 88 Miss. 476, 77 Am. Dec. 651; Hayes v. Seaver, 7  
Me. 237; Inglehart v. State, 2 (Ma.) [illegible text] Caldwell,  
Cordos. Bird v. Mitchel, 101 Ga. 46; Seat v. Cannon, 1 Humph. (Tenn.) 471; Craddock v. Turner, 6 Leigh (Va.), 116; Bradwell v. Spencer, 16 Ga. 8,  

Annett v. Terry, 35 N.Y. 256.

<br>

### --- Page 0367 ---

245.

202 [§ sion of such principal, arising from his default, binds the sureties in the absence of fraud or collusion. Again, the court states that the sureties are strangers to the proceeding in which the decree was pronounced, and are bound by the decree only because the condition of their bond makes them so. In a later case it was distinctly held that the undertaking of the sureties upon an executor’s bond becomes fixed and operative by the decree of the surrogate. In the absence of fraud or collusion between the executor and the legatee, the decree of the surrogate is conclusive upon the sureties. It binds the principal and sureties alike, and cannot be impeached in a collateral proceeding. While the most solemn judgments do not conclude those who are neither parties nor privies, yet, when an obligee undertakes the payment of a judgment which may be recovered against his principal, he cannot escape the effect of such judgment when recovered. He has bound himself to pay, and is indebted for the amount of the judgment when recovered, without regard to its legal merits. The sureties cannot go back of the judgment. An exception to the rule last above stated is made by some courts where the administrator fails to insist upon the bar of the statute of limitations in a suit against him by a creditor, holding that the sureties are not liable, and that they may object to the effect of a judgment against their principal, when sued on their bond. Other courts, however, refuse to recognize the exception stated. It follows that the general rule stated as applicable to sureties upon administrators’ bonds applies as well to sureties upon all bonds, where the condition is that the surety will be bound by the result of litigation between the principal and other persons.

IMPEACHMENT.

Sec. 245. Bonds of indemnity.— With respect to bonds of indemnity, the rule quite generally adhered to is that the judgment against the principal is only prima facie evidence in a 3 Weber v. North, 51 Iowa, 

Schofield v. Churchill, 72 N.Y. 

54 Mo. 439, 

565.

2 Dawes v. Shed, 15 Mass. 6; Heard v. Lodge, 20 Pick. 53; Robinson v. Hodge, 117 Mass. 222; Gooker v. Sanborn, 3 N.H. 491.

Burroughs v. Farmer,

4 Riddle v. Baker, 13 Cal. 295; Way v. Lewis, 115 Mass. 26; Towle v. Towle, 46 N.H. 431; Methodist Church v. Barker, 18 N.Y. 463; Parkhurst v. Sumner, 23 Vt. 538.

<br>

### --- Page 0368 ---

§§ 246, 247.) BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 263
Suit upon the bond against the sureties. A distinction is recognized between covenants in a bond which stipulates against the consequences of a suit and those which contain no such undertaking. In the latter class the judgment is res inter alios acta, and proves nothing except res ipsa loquitur against the indemnitor, unless he had notice and an opportunity to defend. Covenants to indemnify against the consequences of a suit have been by the courts divided into two classes: (1) Where the covenantor expressly makes his liability depend on the event of a litigation to which he is not a party and stipulates to abide the result; and (2) where the covenant is one of general indemnity merely against claims or suits. In cases of the first class the judgment is conclusive evidence against the indemnitor, although he was not a party and had no notice, for its recovery is the event against which he covenanted. In those of the second class the want of notice does not go to the cause of action, but the judgment is prima facie evidence only against the indemnitor, and he may be let in to show that the principal had a good defense to the claim. Obviously the only effect of such judgments against the sureties in an action against them on the bond is to throw the burden of proof upon them.

Src. 246. Bail, injunction, appeal and replevin bonds.— The sureties on injunction, appeal, replevin and bail bonds are within the first class stated, and are concluded by a judgment against the principal.

Src. 247. Receivers’ bonds.— Sureties on receivers’ bonds seem to have been placed by some courts under the second class, to the extent that judgments against their principal are

Brown, 21 Kan. 171; Collins v. Mitchell, 5 Fla. 364; Jones v. Dole, 3 La. Ann. 588; Tracey v. Maloney, 105 Mass. 0; Binsse v. Wood, 37 N.Y. 526; Parkhurst v. Sumner, 23 Vt. 596; Hathaway v. Da, 38 Cal. 161; Richardson v. People’s Nat. Bank, 48 N. E. Rep. 1100, 57 Ohio St. 299.

### --- Page 0369 ---

264 IMPEACHMENT. §§ 248, 249.

prima facie evidence against them in a suit on a bond. It was said: “That the rule in the cases which makes the adjudication against the principal conclusive on the surety is always based upon the special covenant, which in terms submits the latter to the judgment or order of the court, indicates very plainly that the general words were not deemed sufficient in and of themselves to effect that result. In every decision which has made the adjudication against the principal conclusive upon the surety, it has been founded on apt words or phrases, other than a general promise to perform duty, and which indicated an understood promise to contract beyond the ordinary liability.” 

Sec. 248. Impeachment by sureties for fraud or collusion. The sureties in a suit upon the bond may impeach the judgment for fraud or collusion in obtaining it. It was stated by Justice Cowen in an early case, speaking with reference to a stipulation by sureties to abide the event of a suit: “It is plain from the nature of the agreement that the surety means to be concluded, always saving the right, as the law must in every case where suit is between third persons, to contest the proceeding on the ground of fraudulent collusion, for the purpose of charging the surety.” 

Sec. 249. Failure of jurisdiction.— The surety may always impeach such a judgment upon the ground of want of jurisdiction of the subject-matter or of the person of his principal. He is neither a party nor a privy to the judgment, and cannot, by rules of law, review or reverse it. In some states, however, it appears that a surety has the right of review. Some courts have held that such judgments could not be impeached for fraud only in a direct proceeding by the surety; but they are 

Whitehead v. Woolfolk, La. Ann. v. Backus, 25 Cal. 214; City of Lowell, 42; Comm. v. Gould, 118 Mass. 500; Bell v. Parker et al., 10 Met. 309; Thomson v. MacGregor, 81 N. Y. 592; Stimpson v. Malden, 109 Mass. 313; Charles v. Hoskins, 14 Iowa. 471.

Thomson v. MacGregor, 81 N. Y. 592. Fall River v. Riley, 140 Mass. 488.

Douglass v. Howland, 24 Wend. 356; People v. Downing, 4 Sand. 189;

Annett v. Terry, N.Y.; Hobbs v. Middleton, 1 J. J. Marsh; Heard v. Lodge, 20 Pick. 53; Tracy v. Goodwin et al., 5 Allen, 409; Irwin v. Goodwin et al., 177 Ky.

### --- Page 0370 ---

$§ 250, 251.] BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 265

exceptional in this respect. In some cases it has been held that the fraud which would impeach a settlement with the orphans’ court was collusion between the legatees or those interested in the settlement with the administrator.

Src. 250. Assignees and vendees.— Assignees and vendees are generally privies in estate, as we have seen, and are not only bound by, but may take advantage of, prior judgments against their assignors or vendors. But this rule only applies when the right or title of the assignee or vendee accrues during the pendency of proceedings against the vendor, or where the purchase is subject to liens or encumbrances. In the latter case, in order that the grantee shall be bound by the decree, he must be made a party. Thus, a grantee is not bound by a judgment rendered in an action commenced against his grantor subsequent to the conveyance. Only those are privies, within the meaning of the rule, who acquire their interest in the subject matter of the suit after its commencement. A judgment against an assignee for the benefit of creditors, as such, is binding upon the creditors of the assignor. However, the judgment is impeachable for fraud or collusion. A judgment for or against an assignee of a chose in action, where the statute directs that an action must be brought in his own name in respect thereto, is binding and conclusive upon the assignor in the absence of fraud or collusion. But ordinarily an assignee is not bound by proceedings against the assignor or those taken by him, when he is not properly a party thereto.

Sec. 251. Bailees.— Bailees are in general bound by prior judgments against the bailor; but a judgment against a bailee

1 Williamson v. Howell, 4 Ala. 693; 5 Field v. Flanders, 40 Ill. 470; Kerr v. Backus, 25 Cal. 214; Hobbs v. Blodgett, 48 N. Y. 62.

v. Middleton, 1 J. J. Marsh. (Ky.) 6 Wetmore v. San Francisco, 44 Cal. 177. 294; Castner v. Austin, 2 Minn. 44; Adams v. Barnes, 17 Mass. 365. Sheridan v. Mayor, etc., 68 N.Y. 30.

Dull v. Blackman, 169 U.S. 243; 7 Goodin v. Newcomb (Kan.), 49 Pac. Rep. 821; Miller & Co. v. Sloan (Mich.), 73 N. W. Rep. 123; Hunt v. Lucas,

Carroll v. Goldschmidt, 83 Fed. Rep. 5038, 27 C. C. A. 566. Green v. Clark, 12 N. Y. 343.

<br>

### --- Page 0371 ---

252, 253.

266 IMPEACHMENT. [§§ 252, 253]

is not a bar to an action against the judgment creditor by the bailor; but otherwise if the bailor defended the action.

Sec. 252. Executors and administrators.—Executors and administrators are bound by judgments against a deceased person to the extent to which they succeed to the property rights of such person; and creditors of a decedent's estate who have filed their claims in time to be entitled to any of the general assets are bound by the decree in an action by the administrator to set aside as fraudulent transfers of real estate by the deceased.

Sec. 253. Garnishees and garnishors.—The defendant in an action is bound by a judgment against the garnishee therein to the extent of the payments made by such garnishee, and cannot maintain an action against the latter. In some states the judgment itself operates as a bar; in other states it does not have such effect if the garnishee has not satisfied and may not be obliged to satisfy the judgment. But the judgment may be a ground for an abatement or stay of proceedings. In some of the states it operates until payment as a lien upon the fund, and not to actually transfer the fund. “Such a judgment, however, is not binding upon other creditors of the defendant; yet where the insured sued on a policy of insurance and the verdict was for the defendant, it was held that a judgment entered thereon was, in the absence of fraud or collusion, conclusive against a creditor of the insured who issued an attachment execution against the defendant as garnishee prior to the issue of the summons by the insured.”

2 Tarleton v. Johnson, 25 Ala. 300.

3 Ladd v. Durkin, 54 Cal. 395.

### --- Page 0372 ---

§§ 254-257.] BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 267

Sec. 254. Lessees.—Lessees are bound by judgments against their lessor where their leases are subsequent to such judgments. But where lessees are in possession under a lease at the time of the commencement of the action, they are not so bound unless the action is for the foreclosure of a mortgage or other lien upon the real estate, or involves some question which relates to a right or interest prior to that of the lessees, or paramount to that of the lessor.

Sec. 255. Officers and their successors.—Judgments rendered against officers, affecting the rights and privileges of the office, not personal, are binding upon their successors.

Sec. 256. Parties not of record.—Parties to an action, though not so of record, are bound by the judgment when the action is prosecuted in their behalf. But ordinarily this result follows only when they have the right to control the proceedings and of appeal from the judgment. The fact that they were real parties in interest may be shown by parol.

Sec. 257. Stockholders.—Judgments against a corporation are conclusive upon the stockholders. In proper cases such judgments may be impeached in proceedings brought by stockholders for fraud or collusion and sometimes mistake. The doctrine is elementary that the stockholders may sue both at law and in equity in their names in behalf of the company’s interest, where it cannot or will not do so in its corporate capacity. The suit is maintained on the ground that the rights of the corporation are involved; those rights the stockholder is allowed to assert because the directors decline to take proper steps to assert them. The proceedings should be so conducted as to bind the corporation. This can only be done by making

1 Bennett v. Couchman, 48 Barb. 73.

2 Satterlee v. Bliss, 36 Cal. 489;  
Georges v. Hufschmidt, 44 Mo. 179.

3 Vance v. Wesley, 85 Fed. Rep. 157, 29 C. C. A. 63; King v. Grimes, Buller's N. P. 231.

4 Valentine v. Mahoney, 37 Cal. 389; Hanna v. Read, 102 Ill. 596; Marsh v. Smith, 73 Iowa, 295; Montgomery v. Vickery, 110 Ind. 211; Elliott v. Hayden, 104 Mass. 180; Ver Planck v. Van Buren, 76 N. Y. 247; Robbins v. Chicago, 4 Wall. 658.

5 Palmer v. Hayes, 112 Ind. 289; Shirley v. Fearne, 33 Miss. 65; Bennett v. Wilmington, etc. Co., 119 Ill. 9; Corse v. Sandford, 14 Iowa, 235; Cole v. Butler, 43 Me. 401; Merchants’ Bank v. Chandler, 19 Wis. 434.

<br>

### --- Page 0373 ---

268 IMPROVEMENT, [$ 258.

The relief is on behalf of the corporation, not the individual shareholder. Ordinarily, redress for wrongs to corporate rights or property must, as before stated, be sought in the name of the corporation, and a stockholder cannot sue for damage to him individually, unless the corporate authorities refuse to act when applied to by him. But that rule does not apply where the officers or agents of a corporation, in whom authority to direct litigation is vested, are themselves guilty of the wrong against the corporation; for a demand would ordinarily be nugatory under such circumstances, and it would be wholly against established principles of justice to permit the authors of a wrong to conduct a litigation against themselves as agents of the injured complainant. So absurd a requirement cannot be imposed on a plaintiff as a condition of affording relief. The object of a request is to give the corporation the opportunity to prosecute or defend, but the reason for the rule fails when the managing officers are themselves guilty of the wrong. The stockholder’s remedy usually is, during the pendency of the suit, in that suit, upon leave obtained, to prosecute or defend, and not by an independent action. Judgments procured by fraud or collusion with such officers may be impeached by stockholders within the principles herein stated, but not collaterally, as they are parties to the action in which the judgments are obtained. Judgments against a corporation for debt, a receiver being appointed to collect unpaid stock subscriptions, is conclusive upon stockholders both as to the necessity of the assessment and the amount thereof, even though they were not personally served with process in the suit.

Sec. 258. Trustees and cestui que trust.—Ordinarily a cestui que trust is bound by a judgment against the trustee.

1 Davenport v. Dows, 18 Wall. 626; Dodge v. Woolsey, 18 How. 331; Bronson v. La Crosse R. R. Co., 2 Wall. 283; Morrill v. Little Falls Mfg. Co., 53 Minn. 387.

2 Rothwell v. Robinson, 39 Minn. 1, 38 N. W. Rep. 772; Jones v. Morrison Iron Co., 2 Black, 718; Bronson v. La Crosse R. R. Co., 2 Wall. 283.

3 Graham v. Boston, Hartford & Erie R. Co., 118 U.S. 161.

4 Castleman v. Templeman (Md.), 40 Atl. Rep. 275.

5 Whitford v. Cooks, 54 Mich. 361; Johnson v. Robertson, 31 Minn. 140; Heath v. Erie Ry. Co., 8 Blatch, 347. 

6 Graham v. Railroad Co., 118 U.S. 161; Kochler v. Black River Falls; Corcoran v. Chesapeake Canal Co., 64 U.S. T.A.L.

<br>

### --- Page 0374 ---

BY STRANGERS TO JUDGMENTS. 269

but is not bound where the judgment is against the trustee individually or in matters where he did not represent the cestui que trust. In a suit against a canal company to recover interest upon certain bonds, a decision adverse to the right was rendered, to which suit those holding adverse interests were made parties, including a trustee for certain bondholders. It was held in a subsequent suit, brought by such trustee in his own behalf and in behalf of all others similarly interested as holders of this class of bonds, that they were bound by such former decree. To the argument that in such suit he was only a party "in his representative character and therefore should not be affected as to his rights as an individual owner of the bonds," it was said it was his duty as trustee to represent and protect the holders of those bonds; and for that reason he was made a party. It would be a new and dangerous doctrine in equity practice to hold that the cestui que trust is not bound by the decree against his trustee in the very matter of the trust for which he was appointed. If he (the complainant) owned any of these bonds and coupons, then he is bound because he was representing himself; if he has bought them since, he is bound as privy to the person who was represented. Whether the cestui que trust is a necessary party in an action by the trustee against a stranger to enforce the trust, or to one by a stranger against him to defeat it, in whole or in part, depends to a large extent, if not wholly, upon the powers invested in the trustee in relation to the trust. Where he represents his beneficiaries in all things relating to their common interest in the trust property, then such trustee is in court for and in behalf of the beneficiaries; and they, though not parties, are bound by the judgment unless it is impeached by fraud or collusion between him and the adverse party.

Note to Sec. 255—A trustee deposited money with a firm without the consent of his cestui que trust. The partnership was dissolved by the death of a partner. An administrator was appointed. The trustee probated his

3 Kerrison, Assignee, v. Stewart et al., 03 U.S. 163. See also Shaw v. Campbell v. Watson, 8 Ohio, 500.

<br>

### --- Page 0375 ---

270 IMPEACHMENT. [§ 259.

claim for the money due him, in his own name and without the consent or knowledge of the cestui que trust. It was held that the latter was not bound by the decree in the probate court. Evangelical Synod, etc. v. Schoeneich, 45 S. W. Rep. 647.

Sec. 259. Defendants in chancery.—In chancery suits, where parties are often made defendants because they will not join as plaintiffs, who are yet necessary parties, adverse interests as between co-defendants may be passed upon and decided, and if the parties have had a hearing and an opportunity of asserting their rights, they are concluded by the decree as far as it affects rights presented to the court and passed upon by its decree. This rule does not apply to judgments against one defendant only in an ordinary action at law against two persons as tortfeasors, and such a judgment is not conclusive as to defendants' rights inter se, such an issue not being raised or submitted.

Corcoran v. Chesapeake Canal Co., O’Rourke v. Snidell Ry. Co. 142 Mo. 342. 94 U.S. 748.

<br>

### --- Page 0376 ---

CHAPTER 12.

DIVORCE— DECREES.

Where defendant is a citizen, though without the jurisdiction, substituted service may be sufficient to confer jurisdiction to affect his status.

Distinction between alimony and division of estates under statutes.

Non-resident defendant, over whom jurisdiction is not acquired, may commence proceedings for divorce and alimony.

Alimony is only incident to a suit for divorce.

Court has a continuing power to revise, but cannot allow where decree is silent.

A division of property decreed is final.

Decree cannot affect children domiciled out of the territorial limits.
