The only question that can possibly arise is, whether the matter or conduct alleged is contempt, and to determine this, the court cannot be governed by statutory definitions or classifications, but its duty and power alike is, to determine whether the matter or conduct was contempt at common law. If so, it remains such. If what are now termed indirect or constructive contempts were contempts at common law, they remain such; and a legislature has no more power to control the exercise of the power to punish them as such, than it has to control the exercise of such power with respect to direct contempts. As stated by an able court: “To say that it (the statute) is absolutely binding upon the courts would be to concede that the courts have no constitutional and inherent power to punish any class of contempts, but that the whole subject is under the control of the legislative department, because if the general assembly may deprive courts of the power to punish one class of contempts, it may go the whole length and divest them of power to punish any contempt.” And another: “It would seem to follow that if the legislature may curtail this jurisdiction and determine that in no other matter than those for which it may provide shall the courts have jurisdiction, it may take away the power to punish entirely.” “If this inherent power exists for the preservation

1 Ex parte Robinson, 19 Wall. 510.  
2 State v. Morrill, 16 Ark. 398.  
3 In re Chadwick (Mich.), 67 N.W. Rep. 107L

<br>

### --- Page 0433 ---

207,

312 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [

(7a)

of mere order, for how much greater reason should it exist to protect the fountains of justice from being poisoned and the courts themselves from being brought into contempt and disrepute?”

The supreme court of Virginia, in a very recent case not yet published (Carter v. Grubbs), rendered an able opinion distinctly asserting the inherent power of courts to punish summarily contempts, whether committed within or out of the presence of the court. The court holds that the Saunders law, passed at the last session of the legislature, is unconstitutional so far as it confines the power of punishing for contempt to direct contempts. In delivering the opinion, Keith, J., said that after a careful consideration of the authorities the court had arrived at the following conclusions: “That in the courts created by the constitution there is an inherent power of self-defense and self-preservation; that this power may be regulated, but cannot be destroyed, or so far diminished as to be rendered ineffectual by legislative enactment; that it is a power necessarily resident in and to be exercised by the court itself; and that the vice of an act which seeks to deprive the court of this inherent power is not cured by providing for its exercise by a jury; that while the legislature has the power to regulate the jurisdiction of circuit, county and corporation courts, it cannot destroy, while it may confine within reasonable bounds, the authority necessary to the exercise of the jurisdiction conferred.”

This enunciation of principle is extremely gratifying at this time. The court had the advantage of all the recent declarations of courts with respect to this question. It observed the weakness of some and the strength of others. It must have realized that there existed an undercurrent pervading the populace against the exercise of power and authority even by the courts; that restraint upon human action and human utterances was irksome; that the popular vein lay in indirectly giving it countenance. The court, however, was both able and strong. It gave no heed to such influences, but fearlessly, firmly and correctly declared the law.

The eloquent words of Chancellor Kent are appropriate here: “Whenever we subject the established courts of the land to

State v. Judge, 45 La. Ann. 1250,

<br>

### --- Page 0434 ---

§ 298.] CONTEMPTS. 313

the degradation of private prosecution, we subdue their independence and destroy their authority. Instead of being venerable before the public, they become contemptible; and we thereby embolden the licentious to trample upon everything sacred in society, and to overturn those institutions which have hitherto been deemed the best guardians of liberty.

Sec. 298. Contempts within the statute.— The provisions of the statutes of the different states are not exactly the same. As a rule they merely re-enact the common law with respect to direct contempts. The limitations attempted relate more particularly to constructive contempts, so called. In many states the provision with respect to publication is in substance that the publication of a false or grossly inaccurate report or copy of the proceedings is contempt; but that no court can punish as a contempt the publication of true, fair and full reports of any trial, argument, proceedings or decisions had in such court. Such is the statute in Michigan and Wisconsin. The supreme court of Michigan, in construing this statute, held that “it did not limit the power to causes still pending in the court, but extends to the criticism of past decrees,” and accordingly, where an article published reflected upon the conduct and motives of a judge in his official action, held that it constituted contempt within the statute. The supreme court of Wisconsin held that the power to punish for constructive contempt was limited to pending cases, but declined to determine whether the subject was within legislative control. The statutes of Ohio contain the following provision (sec. 5639): “A court or judge at chambers may punish summarily a person guilty of misbehavior in the presence of or so near the court or judge as to obstruct the administration of justice.” In Meyers v. Stute, 46 Ohio St. 473, it appears that an article was published in a paper located in a city distant from where a judge was holding court, reflecting upon the conduct and the motives of the judge in the drawing of a grand jury. It was said by the court that “the intention of the publication was to insult and intimidate the judge, degrade the court, destroy its power and influence, and thus bring

1 Yates v. Lansing, 5 Johns, 282.
2 State v. Circuit Judge, 72 N. W. Rep. 193.
3 In re Chadwick, 67 N. W. Rep. 107.

<br>

### --- Page 0435 ---

314 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 298.

it into contempt, to inflame the prejudice of the people against it, to lead them to believe that the trial then being conducted was a farce and an outrage, which had its foundation in fraud and wrong on the part of the judge, and if communicated to the jury to prejudice their minds, and thus prevent a fair and impartial trial. Besides, the tendency was, when read by the judge, to produce irritation, and to a greater or less extent render him less capable of exercising a clear and impartial judgment. It therefore tended directly to obstruct the administration of justice in reference to the case on trial, and its publication was a contempt of court. The publication came within the statute (sec. 5639). It is true the article was not written nor was it circulated by the respondent in the presence of the court. Indeed it was written in Cincinnati, though dated at Columbus, but the publication was in the courtroom as well as elsewhere. It was intended to have effect and did have effect in the courthouse at Columbus, and the writer was just as much responsible for that effect as though he had in the courtroom itself, and while the trial was in progress, circulated and read aloud the article and uttered the libelous words verbally.”

<br>

### --- Page 0436 ---

CHAPTER 14.

CIVIL CONTEMPTS,

Sec.

299. What are such.

300. Character of proceedings.

301. Power inherent in courts independent of statute.

302. Distinction between civil and criminal contempts.

303. The judgment or final order.

304. Striking out pleading.

304a. Denial of relief.

304b. Disobedience of order.

304c. Debs case — Government by injunction.

304d. Inspection of books and papers.

304e. Enjoining removal of officer.

304f. Judgment appealed from.

304g. Enjoining federal officers.

Sec.

304i. Mandamus — Writ allowed at chambers.

304j. California doctrine.

304k. Order respecting possession of realty.

304l. Injunction improperly granted.

304m. Orders made in other than judicial proceedings.

304n. Interference by third party.

304o. Decree of specific performance.

304p. Removal of child.

304q. Receivers — Interference with their possession.

304r. Non-payment of alimony.

304s. Non-payment of alimony — Ability to pay.

304t. Distinction between jurisdiction and judgment.

Sec. 299. Civil contempts, what are.—Where the disobedience of the person or his act or omission arises in a civil action or proceeding, and is of such a character as affects or impairs the rights or remedies of a party thereto, such conduct is termed a civil contempt, and when any such misconduct shall be committed in the immediate view and presence of the court it may be punished summarily as a contempt. Statutes have been enacted in most of the states defining, enumerating and classifying this species of contempt, prescribing to some extent the manner of procedure and the nature and extent of the punishment. It is within the discretion of the legislature to regulate the exercise of the power of the courts to punish such contempts, and this ordinarily is all that has been attempted. Such statutes usually embrace officers, either general or special, connected with the court or its process, defining misconduct on

1 People ex rel. Heckley v. Kelley, 24 N. Y. 74; In re Rosenburg, 90 Wis. 581.

<br>

### --- Page 0437 ---

316 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§§ 300.

their part, with respect to their duties, a contempt; also parties to actions for putting in fictitious bail or sureties, or for any deceit or abuse of the process or proceedings of the court, such misconduct constituting contempt. Also making it a contempt on the part of either such attorneys, parties, or other persons not to pay any money ordered to be paid by the court in cases where by law executions cannot be awarded, and any other disobedience of any lawful order, judgment, or process of the court. Such statutes also declare it to be contempt for persons to assume to be and act as officers or attorneys and counselors without such authority; to rescue any person or property which shall be in the custody of any officer by virtue of process issued by such court or judge thereof; to unlawfully detain any witnesses or party to an action while going to, remaining at or returning from the court, and for any other unlawful interference with the process or proceedings in any action. If summoned as witnesses or garnishees, to refuse or neglect to obey such summons, or to attend or to be sworn, or to answer as such witnesses or garnishees. If summoned as jurors in any court, to improperly converse with any party to an action tried at such court, or with any person in relation to the merits of such action, or to receive communication from any such party or from any other person in relation to the merits of such action without immediately disclosing the same to the court. For inferior magistrates, officers and tribunals to disobey any lawful order or process of a superior court, or for proceeding in any action or proceeding contrary to law after such action shall have been removed from their jurisdiction; and embraces all other cases where attachments and proceedings as for contempts have been usually adopted and practiced in courts of record to enforce the civil remedies of any party or to protect the rights of any such party.

Sec. 300. Character of proceedings.— Contempt of court is a specific criminal offense, and the imposition of a fine for a contempt is a judgment in a criminal case. Though there has been neither a final nor interlocutory decree on the merits

1 New Orleans v. Steamship Co., 20 Rep. 63; Haight v. Lucia et al., 36 Wall 387; Fischer v. Hayes, 6 Fed. Wis 355.

<br>

### --- Page 0438 ---

§ 300.) CIVIL CONTEMPTS, 317

in the suit, yet the order imposing punishment for contempt is a final order or judgment as to the matter of contempt. Such proceeding is no part of the process in the civil action. It is a special proceeding, criminal in character, in which the state is the real plaintiff or prosecutor, and it was held in Wisconsin that it is wrongly entitled in the civil action out of which it arises. Yet the Wisconsin court entertained an appeal from an order committing a party for contempt, entitled in the original action, examined the merits and reversed the order; and this would seem to be the proper practice, notwithstanding what was stated in Knight v. Lucia, supra. In proceedings in equity between parties to the suit, for contempt in not obeying the process of the court, or any order or decree in the cause, the proceedings on the attachment may be and usually are entitled as in the original suit, though it is not irregular to entitle them in the name of the people on the relation of the person prosecuting the attachment against the defendant or party proceeded against. Where the attachment proceeding is against a witness or a person not a party to the suit, the practice is to entitle the order for attachment, and all subsequent proceedings thereon, in the name of “the people on the relation,” etc. It would be foreign to the purpose of this work to include any considerable number of instances where the power authorized by the provisions of this chapter has been exercised. It is sufficient to state that neglect of duty on the part of an officer of the court is directly within the provisions of the statute, so that in case of such neglect, which also includes misconduct, the inquiry is limited to the facts. So is disobedience of a valid order or process of the court. The only questions present where such is the charge is the validity of the order or process, and the fact of disobedience without sufficient justification. The statute and the rule at common law includes the power to compel the production of books in equity proceedings and to make truthful discovery.

1 Fischer v. Hayes, 6 Fed. Rep. 63.  
2 Haight v. Lucia et al., 36 Wis. 355.  
3 Warren et al v. Rosenberg, 94 Wis. 523.  
4 People v. Craft, 7 Paige, 325; Stafford v. Brown, 4 Prize, 360; Fischer v. Hayes, 6 Fed. Rep. 63.

<br>

### --- Page 0439 ---

318 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§$§ 301, 302.

under oath and without evasion.’ And where it also appears that it is within the power of the party to comply with such an order, it is not necessary that the court should find or recite in the order that the misconduct was such as to defeat, impair, impede or prejudice the rights or remedies of the opposite party. Such a refusal is a direct disobedience and contempt of the authority of the court, which independently of any statute the courts have inherent power to punish. A party, however, cannot be imprisoned for refusal to comply with the terms of an order until he has been convicted of contempt.

Sec. 301. Power inherent in the court independent of statute.— Independently of the statute, an attorney of the court may be punished as for a contempt for misconduct such as is stated therein. If it were absolutely essential that it should appear that such misconduct, in order to be punished, affects the rights and interests of a party to an action, then there would remain no power in the court pursuant to statute to punish for many acts of misconduct on the part of such officers. Statutes which define criminal contempts do not provide for such cases. Whatever may be the rule with respect to publications made by other parties derogatory to the court, if made by an attorney of the court it is contempt.

Sec. 302. Distinction between civil and criminal contempts.— The abduction of a child by its mother, where in a divorce suit its custody has been ordered to be given to the father, is a criminal contempt, though arising out of a civil proceeding. And so it may be said that the several acts or omissions enumerated in the statute as being contempts and punishable as such, though arising in civil proceedings, are in a sense criminal contempts. Statutes quite generally contain pro—

1In re Rosenberg, 90 Wis. 681; 3In re Blair, 4 Wis. 522. People ex rel. Heckley v. Kelley, 24 N. Y. 74; Commerce Commission v. Brimson, 154 U. S. 447. 
2In re Rosenberg, 90 Wis. 581; In re Chiles, 22 Wall 157.

<br>

### --- Page 0440 ---

§ 302.) CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 319

visions, in effect, that if an actual loss or injury has been produced to any party by the misconduct of those mentioned (in the statute hereinbefore referred to), the court shall order a sufficient sum to be paid by the defendant to such party to indemnify him, etc., instead of imposing a fine. Where such is the punishment imposed, then the proceeding is termed a civil contempt, or a contempt civil in its nature; the distinguishing feature being that one is prosecuted by the state, outside of the action to vindicate the authority and dignity of the court, and results in fine or imprisonment or both, while the other is a proceeding in the cause in which the offense was committed or ancillary to it, which adjudges indemnity for the loss sustained by such misconduct. In the one case the fine goes to the school or other public fund. In the other, to the party. In the former, no appeal lies. In the latter, the order may be reviewed upon appeal.

We have before called attention to the fact that the court of appeals in New York was among those which implicitly, without argument or question, yielded to the assumption of authority by the legislature to declare and define what were contempts. That court was called upon to determine whether the act of a juror in personally and without leave of the court viewing the scene and surroundings of an alleged offense was a contempt, either civil or criminal, and was forced, by giving effect to the statute, to determine it was neither. In reaching this conclusion the court very ably, clearly and comprehensively distinguished civil from criminal contempts, declaring the power of the courts with respect to each. Thus they state: “In one class are grouped cases whose occasion is an injury or wrong done to a party who is a suitor before the court, and has established a claim upon its protection, and which results in a money indemnity to the litigant, or a compulsory act or omission enforced for his benefit. In these cases the authority of the court is indeed vindicated, but it is after a manner lent to the suitor for his safety and vindicated for his sole benefit. The authority is exerted in his behalf as a private individual, and the fine imposed is measured by his loss.

1 In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.

<br>

### --- Page 0441 ---

320 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 302.

and goes to him as indemnity; and imprisonment, if ordered, is awarded not as a punishment, but as a means to an end, and that end the benefit of the suitor in some act or omission compelled which are essential to his particular rights of person or of property. A fine may be imposed to indemnify his actual loss; when such is not shown, the fine must not exceed his costs and expenses, and $250 in addition thereto, and in both cases paid over to the suitor. The imprisonment where the act or duty can yet be performed must end with the performance of the act and payment of the fine; but if the act or duty cannot be performed, then the imprisonment must not exceed six months and until the fine is paid. In this last provision there is a trace of the element of punishment, but it is for the violation of the private right of the party and to check similar violations in the future, and has no respect to public offenses or the vindication of public wrongs.

These cases have been usually described as proceedings for the enforcement of civil remedies, and more briefly as civil contempts, and because the great volume of instances occur in the progress of civil actions; but they may also occur in criminal actions or proceedings. If we describe this first class of contempts as private contempts, because their occasion is primarily and in the main the vindication of private rights, we shall avoid confusion or misapprehension.

The second class of contempts consists of those whose cause and result are a violation of the rights of the public as represented by their constitutional legal tribunals, and a punishment for the wrong in the interest of public justice and not in the interest of an individual litigant. In these cases, if a fine is imposed, its maximum is limited by a fixed general law and not at all by the needs of individuals, and its proceeds, when collected, go into the public treasury and not into the purse of an individual suitor. The fine is punishment rather than indemnity, and, if imprisonment is added, it is in the interest of public justice and purely as a penalty, and not at all as a means of securing indemnity to an individual. Necessarily these contempts in their origin and punishment partake of the nature of crimes, which are violations of the public law, and end in the

<br>

### --- Page 0442 ---

§ 302.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 321

Vindication of public justice, and hence are named criminal contempts. As described in the statute, an element of wilfulness, or of evil intention, enters into and characterizes them. They are a disturbance of the court which interferes with the performance of duty as a judicial tribunal; wilful disobedience to its lawful mandate; resistance to such mandate wilfully offered; contumacious and unlawful refusal to be sworn as a witness or to answer a proper question; and publication of a false and grossly inaccurate report of its proceedings. But here again we find that they occur as well in civil as in criminal actions, and so, for convenience, we may speak of them, in view of the present classification, as public contempts, although the established nomenclature must remain unchanged. The court proceeds to state that the two classes of contempts were known to and recognized by the common law, and the courts were held to possess an inherent power of punishing by process of contempt any disregard of their authority, both for the benefit of their suitors and for the protection of their own order and dignity. They conclude that the statute has restricted criminal contempts, and no act can be punished as such that does not come within the enumerated classes. That the misconduct of the juror in question would have been a criminal contempt at common law. It was not a private contempt, for it invaded no right of an individual suitor before the court, and involved no question or duty of indemnity to an individual litigant. It was not a criminal contempt under the statute, and hence it was no contempt at all. Where a party is thus adjudged to be in contempt, the final order must be either for a criminal or civil contempt, either fine or imprisonment, or both, in the one case, or for indemnity in the other. The court cannot include both in the same order.

Note To Sec. 302.— The distinction between criminal and civil contempts is illustrated by a decision of the Wisconsin court. In re Ida Louisa Pierce, 44 Wis. 411. Section 2565 of the Revised Statutes classifies among criminal contempts “wilful disobedience of any process or order lawfully issued or made by the court.” Section 3477, under title of “Proceedings to punish contempts to protect the rights of parties to civil actions,” includes

1 People v. Court of Oyer and Terminer, 101 N.Y. 245.  
2 In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.

<br>

### --- Page 0443 ---

322 SPECIAL REMEDIES. § 303.

also “disobedience of any process of such court or any lawful order thereof, or of any lawful order of a judge of such court, or of any officer authorized to perform the duties of such judge.” In the case mentioned, the defendant, who was the mother of a child, was convicted of contempt for refusing to restore the custody of the child to the care and custody of her former husband, to whom it had been awarded by a decree of divorce, and the order was that she restore the child to the custody of the husband forthwith, to pay a fine of one dollar, and to stand committed until she should have complied with the order. It was stated that the difference between the two chapters with reference to criminal punishment for contempt was that, if the offense comes within the provisions of the latter, imprisonment may be imposed for a longer term than can be lawfully imposed under the former. That the act could be punished as a criminal contempt was also held; that the latter chapter does not provide for the punishment of all contempt, but only of those specifically enumerated in section 1, by which the rights or remedies of a party in a cause pending in the court inflicting the punishment, or triable therein, may be defeated, impaired, impeded or prejudiced. Under section 3849 the court may fine or imprison, and, if it does, such punishment is essentially for a criminal contempt, and the fine goes to the school fund. Section 3490 provides for awarding to the injured party compensation or indemnity for the loss or injury he has sustained by the misconduct, etc. The distinction is that when the proceeding results in criminal punishment under the provisions of either chapter, it is criminal; when it results in awarding indemnity to the injured party, the proceeding is civil in character. The one is prosecuted by the state outside the particular cause; the other is a proceeding in the cause or ancillary to it. One results in punishment by fine or imprisonment, or both, inflicted to vindicate the authority and dignity of the court; the other to indemnify the party. In the one the fine goes to the school fund; in the other the indemnity goes to the injured party. An appeal lies from the order or judgment in the latter, but not from the order imposing a fine or imprisonment. Hence the final order in contempt proceedings must be one thing or the other — must impose criminal punishment for misconduct or enforce the civil remedy by awarding indemnity. It cannot do both. It was further held in that case that the loss or injury referred to was pecuniary loss, one which would be the subject of an action; that the husband in the case could not bring an action and recover damages against the wife for the abduction and detention of the child. Hence the order that the defendant be imprisoned until she should comply with the order of the court was wholly unauthorized and beyond the jurisdiction of the court; that she could be punished for criminal contempt only.

Sec. 303. The judgment or final order.— The judgment or final order generally is regulated by statute. In criminal contempts proper, with the qualification hereafter noticed, it is imprisonment or payment of a fine, sometimes both, limited as to term and amount. When the misconduct proved consists of an

<br>

### --- Page 0444 ---

§ 303.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 323

Omission to perform some act or duty which is yet in the power of the defendant to perform, he shall be imprisoned only until he shall have performed such act or duty and paid such fine as shall be imposed, and the costs and expenses of the proceeding. In such case the order and warrant of commitment shall specify the act or duty to be performed and the amount of the fine or expense to be paid. It was said by the Wisconsin court that the provision with respect to imprisonment until compliance with the terms of the order was only applicable in cases where an actual loss or injury had been produced to a party by the misconduct alleged. In civil contempts proper, as has heretofore been stated, the judgment may be, instead of imposing a fine, the payment of a sufficient sum by the defendant to the party suffering loss or injury by his misconduct to indemnify him and to satisfy his costs and expenses, and imprisonment of such defendant until the order be complied with. Yet, in a later case, the Wisconsin court held that such provision was applicable to a commitment for refusal to produce books and papers or make discovery. Thus a witness refusing to answer questions may be committed until he shall be willing to answer; and generally it may be stated that fine or imprisonment, or both, is the penalty prescribed by law for misconduct, and the amount and term must be definitely fixed. Where the object is to enforce the performance of a duty, then the penalty, if it may be so called, is confinement until the party having it in his power is willing to perform the duty commanded. It is not really a punishment or penalty, but rather an exercise of the coercive powers of the court. A party cannot be committed until he performs an act of indemnity to the opposite party, unless it appear that the latter has sustained loss or injury by such misconduct, which must be ascertained, and that the party has it within his power to comply with the order and make such indemnity.

Poertner v. Russell, 33 Wis 193;  
Heymann v. Cunningham, 51 Wis. 506.

In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.

Poertner v. Russell, 33 Wis. 193.

In re Rosenberg, 90 Wis. 581. See also Fischer v. Hayes, 6 Fed. Rep. 63; In re Allen, 13 Blatch. 271; In re Yates, 4 Johns. 317; Warren et al. v. Rosenberg, 94 Wis. 522.

People v. Davidson, 35 Hun, 471; Williamson's Case, 26 Pa. St. 23.

People v. Davidson, 35 Hun, 471; Fischer v. Hayes, 6 Fed. Rep. 6; In re Rosenberg, 90 Wis. 581; In re Allen, 13 Blatch. 271; In re Chiles, 22 Wall. 157; Heymann v. Cunningham, 51 Wis. 506.

<br>

### --- Page 0445 ---

3824 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ dud.

indemnity. The loss or injury for which compensation may be awarded is a pecuniary loss or injury for which the injured party might recover damages. Hence it was held that indemnity could not be awarded a father for the contempt of the mother in abducting their child, the custody of which in a divorce suit had been awarded the former.

The New York court, construing the several provisions of the code of that state, make a clear distinction between criminal and civil contempts, evidently ruling that those which arise in civil proceedings, within the class enumerated in the statute, are civil contempts, and that the person charged therefor is not subject to indictment. A party not entitled to an injunction can suffer no legal damages for its violation, and while a court may, in vindication of its injunctional order, punish a party for a wilful violation thereof, notwithstanding such order ought not to have been granted, it may not in such case order the party disobeying to pay any sum as an indemnity to the opposite party. Actual service of an injunctional order is not a prerequisite to conviction for contempt in violating it. If the party or person has knowledge of its issue he is bound to observe it, and to prevent his agents and employees from violating it.

Sec. 304. Striking out pleading.— The doctrine has often been asserted and applied that a party while in contempt was in no position to ask the indulgence or favor of the court, and that the courts could deny him any such affirmative relief until he rendered obedience to its order. It has been assumed by some courts that the rule went to the extent of striking out or suppressing the answer of the defendant, and denying to him the right and opportunity of being heard. The question is

1 In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411; Warren & Kaehler v. Dobberpuhl, 56 Wis.; et al. v. Rosenberg, 94 Wis.; Heyman v. Cunningham, 51 Wis. 508; & Poertner v. Russell, 33 Wis. 193; Ex parte Silvia, 123 Cal. 203; In re Mead v. Norris, 21 Vt. 310; Rambler v. Blair, 4 Wis. 522. See, however, Stock v. Roth, 18 Wis.; Wright v. Wright, 74 Wis. 439. Daniell's Chancery Pr. & Pl. vol. 2 State ex rel. v. Lonsdale, 48 Wis. 1. p. 504; Hovey v. Elliott, 167 U.S. 348; In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411. 409. 

3 In re Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.  Walker v. Walker, 82 N. Y. 260; 

4 Sherwin v. People, 100 N. Y. 351. Pickett v. Ferguson, 45 Ark. 177.

<br>

### --- Page 0446 ---

§ 304.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 825

ably discussed and the authorities elaborately reviewed in a recent case by the Supreme Court of the United States. It appears that an order was made in that case to the effect that, unless the party paid into the registry a sum of money, his answer be stricken out, and that the cause proceed as if no answer therein had been interposed, and until compliance therewith all proceedings on his part be perpetually stayed. A final order was made striking out said answer, adjudging that the bill be taken pro confesso against him. The appellate court held that such order and decree was beyond the jurisdiction of the court. That the court had no power to disregard the answer, which was in all respects sufficient, or to deny the right of the party thus alleged to be in contempt from prosecuting his defense. In some of the states statutes have been enacted providing for the examination of parties before trial at the instance of the adverse party, which authorize, in case of refusal, the striking out of the pleading interposed by him. Such statutes have been held in the nature of a substitute for the old "bill of discovery." It would appear that under the decision of the Supreme Court of the United States, such penalty cannot be imposed nor the party deprived of his right to proceed with his defense. The same principle was applied where a court made an order disbarring an attorney for an alleged contempt. Attorneys hold their office during good behavior, and can only be deprived of it for misconduct ascertained and declared by the judgment of the court after opportunity to be heard has been afforded. Before a judgment disbarring an attorney is rendered he should have notice of the grounds of complaint against him and ample opportunity of explanation and defense. The Iowa court, following the dicta of another court, fell into the same error, but held, however, that the rule could not be applied in divorce proceedings on the ground of public policy.

It was stated by the New York Court of Appeals (47 N.Y. 40) that the Supreme Court of that state was not limited to punishment by fine and imprisonment in enforcing obedience to its

Hovey v. Elliott, 167 U.S. 409. 3 Bailey v. Bailey, 69 Iowa, 77.

Ex parte Robinson, 19 Wall. 505;

Ex parte Garland, 4 Wall. 383.

<br>

### --- Page 0447 ---

326 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 304.

orders, but it has control over its own proceedings and can refuse the benefit of them to the party in contempt when asked as a favor, and can prevent him from taking any aggressive proceedings against his adversary; but it has no power to stay him in his proceedings by motion or appeal where the object is to rid himself of the alleged contempt or show that the order which he did not obey was erroneous. This was said in a divorce action where the defendant was adjudged in contempt for non-payment of alimony and counsel fees. The order was to the effect that, unless the defendant complied with the former order of the court, the answer filed and served by him be stricken out, and that the action proceed as if no answer therein had been interposed. The order also stayed the defendant’s proceedings upon appeal. Later, in Wadher v. Walker, 82 N.Y. 260, the court of appeals, in referring to this case and the rule stated therein as above, took occasion to say that such rule has been held broader than that and enforced with much vigor, citing cases mainly in the courts of England. The argument is that the power in excess of the rule is confined to courts of equity, and is based on the familiar doctrine that the supreme court of that state, on the equity side, has all the power and authority that formerly existed in the courts of chancery in England and was continuously exercised by it. It was further stated that in preparing the sections relative to the production and discovery of books and papers (2 R. S. 199, sec. 21 et seq.), the revisers provided (sec. 26) that, in case of a party neglecting or refusing to obey an order, the court might strike out his plea and debar him from a defense; and they sought thus to assimilate the practice to that of the court of chancery. They conclude that there has long been exerted by the court of chancery in England the power to refuse to hear the defendant when he was in contempt of the court by disobeying its orders, and that that power was in the courts of chancery in this country. The same conclusion was reached by the supreme court of Michigan. It was stated that a complainant in a court of equity can always be compelled by that court to perform any conditions or make any disclosure which the court has a

1 Brinckley v. Brinckley, 47 N.Y. 40.

<br>

### --- Page 0448 ---

§ 304.]  CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 827

right to exact, by refusing to allow him to proceed in a cause until he does what he ought to do.

The supreme court of the United States (Hovey v. Elliott, 167 U.S. 409), in discussing the very question decided by the New York court of appeals, speaking of a decree being ordered pro confesso against a party in contempt for not paying money to a receiver as directed, state: “It is difficult to speak of a decree thus rendered with moderation; it was in fact a mere arbitrary edict, clothed in the form of a judicial sentence.” The court proceeds to discuss the grounds assumed by the New York court as the basis of their position, and reach the conclusion that they were untenable. They state: “But the argument is, that however plain may be the want of power in all other branches of the government to condemn a citizen without a hearing, both upon the elementary principles of justice and under the express language of the constitution, these principles do not limit the power of courts to punish for contempt, or as for contempt, because it is asserted from the earliest times the chancery court in England has possessed and exercised the power to refuse the right to be heard to one in contempt, and that a power so well established in England before the adoption of the constitution, and which has been so often exercised since, is not controlled by the principles of reason and justice stated. But this contention is without solid foundation to rest upon, and is based upon a too strict and literal rendering of general language to be found in isolated passages contained in the works of writers on ancient law and practice, and in loose statements as to the practice of the court of chancery to be found in a few decisions of English courts. Certain it is that in all the reported decisions of the chancery courts in England, no single case can be found where a court of chancery ordered an answer to be stricken from the files and denied to a party defendant all right of hearing because of a supposed contempt. And in the American adjudications, whilst there are two cases, one in New York and the other in Arkansas, asserting the existence of such power, an analysis of these cases and the authorities upon which they rely will conclusively show the erroneous character of the conclusions reached.” The learned

1 Atchison, T. & S. F. R. Co. v. Jennison, 60 Mich. 232,

<br>

### --- Page 0449 ---

323 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [88 304a, 304b.

court proceeds to review the English cases and expressions of writers upon the law at great length, which conclusively show, as the court states, that the New York court argued from false premises. It was held that the judgment rendered was void for want of jurisdiction and was subject to collateral attack. That when a decree passes upon questions not at issue in the cause, the decree is beyond the jurisdiction of the court. Citing Reynolds v. Stockton, 140 U.S. 254. And there is no distinction in principle between determining a cause upon issues not raised by the pleadings in the actual absence of the party, and rendering a decree by refusing to permit a defendant to be heard in his defense, and indeed by striking the pleading containing such defense from the files.

Sec. 304a. Denial of relief.—It has been urged that because a party was in contempt by reason of violating an injunctional order, or for other disobedience, he could not be heard to move to dissolve the injunction, or to insist upon his legal rights. But in the language of a learned court, “a party in contempt is not deemed by the courts an outlaw, nor do courts disregard his legal rights pending proceedings to punish him for such contempt. The rule is, that while in contempt the courts will not grant him any favors, but it will see that his legal rights are protected. A party against whom an injunction is issued upon an ex parte application has the legal right to demand a hearing upon the question of the regularity and propriety of issuing the same, and the fact that he may have violated such injunction does not deprive him of that right.”

Sec. 304b. Disobedience of order.—In the chapters treating of habeas corpus (sec. 348 et seq.) we have discussed the question of commitment for refusal to obey a void order with reference to review upon habeas corpus, and the conclusion is reached that when a court undertakes, by its process of contempt, to punish a man for refusing to comply with an order which the court had no authority to make, the order itself being without jurisdiction and void, that the order punishing

Smith v. Austin, 1 Code R. (N. S.) 187; Johnson v. Pinny, 1 Paige, 646; Kaeliler v. Dobberpuhl, 56 Wis. 497.

<br>

### --- Page 0450 ---

§ 304c.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 329

for contempt was equally void; that if the order is erroneous merely, then it cannot be collaterally attacked upon habeas corpus (sec. 349). Where, however, the order is one within the jurisdiction of the court, there is no question but that its disobedience may be punished as a contempt.

Sec. 304c. Debs case—Government by injunction—The power was exercised in the famous case of In re Debs, reported in 158 U.S. 564. The case excited great public interest, and out of it grew the expression “Government by injunction.” It was there contended that the order which it was alleged was violated was beyond the power of the court to make; that injunction would not lie to restrain the acts complained of in the bill. The Supreme Court of the United States, however, reached the conclusion which they state as follows: “Summing up our conclusions, we hold that the government of the United States is one having jurisdiction over every foot of soil within its territory, and acting directly upon each citizen. That while it is a government of enumerated powers, it has within the limits of those powers all the attributes of sovereignty; that to it is committed power over interstate commerce and the transmission of the mail; that the powers thus conferred upon the national government are not dormant, but have been assumed and put into practical exercise by the legislation of Congress; that in the exercise of these powers it is competent for the nation to remove all obstructions upon highways, natural or artificial, to the passage of interstate commerce or the carrying of the mail; that while it may be competent for the government (through the executive branch and in the use of the entire executive power of the nation) to forcibly remove all such obstructions, it is equally within its competency to appeal to the civil courts for an inquiry and determination as to the existence and character of any alleged obstructions, and if such are found to exist or threaten to occur, to invoke the powers of those courts to remove or restrain such obstructions; that the jurisdiction of the courts to interfere in such matters by injunction is one recognized from ancient times and by indubitable authority; that such jurisdiction is not ousted by the fact that the obstructions are accompanied by or consist of acts in them-

<br>

### --- Page 0451 ---

3830 SPECIAL REMEDIES. (§ 304d.

selves violations of the criminal law; that the proceeding by injunction is of a civil character, and may be enforced by proceedings in contempt; that such proceedings are not in execution of the criminal laws of the land; that the penalty for a violation of injunction is no substitute for and no defense to a prosecution for any criminal offenses committed in the course of such violation; that the complaint filed in this case clearly showed an existing obstruction of artificial highways for the passage of interstate commerce and the transmission of the mail—an obstruction not only temporarily existing, but threatening to continue; that under such complaint the circuit court had power to issue its process of injunction; that it having been issued and served on these defendants, the circuit court had authority to inquire whether its order had been disobeyed, and when they found that it had been, then to proceed under section 725, Revised Statutes, which grants power to punish by fine or imprisonment . . . disobedience . . . by any party . . . or other person to any lawful writ, process, order, rule, decree or command, and enter the order of punishment complained of; and finally, that, the circuit court having full jurisdiction in the premises, its finding of the fact of disobedience is not open to review on habeas corpus in this or any court.”

Sec. 304d. Inspection of books and papers.—Under Revised Statutes of Illinois, chapter 51, section 9, which empowers courts “to compel parties to produce books or writings: which contain evidence pertinent to the issue,” it was held that a court has no right to order a defendant to place his account books in the hands of the clerk of the court that they may be inspected by the plaintiff and his attorney so that they may prepare for trial, when no showing is made that such books are required for any purpose of evidence in the case, such an order being an infringement of the defendant's constitutional right to be secure against unreasonable searches and seizures, and the disobedience of such an order is not a contempt. It was said that the evident design and purpose of this statute was to furnish to a party litigant a speedy and summary mode by which,

1 Lester v. People (Ill.), 23 N. E. Rep. 387.

<br>

### --- Page 0452 ---

§ 304e.] : CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 331

under the order of the court, to obtain written evidence pertinent to the issue which might be in the possession and control of his adversary, and thus obviate the necessity of a bill of discovery seeking the same end. It is manifest that it contemplates the production of evidence on the trial of the cause which the party applying therefor is entitled to introduce in support of his case and which the other party withholds. It is only such books or writings as contain evidence pertinent to the issue that are required to be produced, and it is for the purpose of enabling the party demanding their production to introduce such pertinent matter in evidence on the trial. A defendant is not required to disclose matters of evidence relied upon in the defense and thus inform the plaintiff of his case further than the pleadings show. Matters purely of defense are the property rights of the defendant, which he may disclose or not upon the trial. It was further stated that under the statute quoted the court has power to compel the production of the books of a party to be used in evidence upon the trial by his adversary upon proper showing that they contain entries tending to prove the issues; but the statute cannot be construed as giving the court power and authority to take the books and papers of the party and impound them with an officer of the court for inspection or examination out of the presence of the court. The statute does not give the right to compel the submission of the books of a party to general inspection or examination for fishing purposes, or with a view to find evidence to be used in other suits or prosecutions.¹

Sec. 304e. Enjoining removal of officer.— The question which was decided by the United States supreme court in In re Sawyer, 124 U.S. 200, was one of considerable importance as bearing upon the equity jurisdiction of the federal courts. The common council of a city were restrained from proceeding to investigate charges filed against a police judge, and from removing him, at the instance and suit of such officer, upon the ground that the council were without authority in the premises. The members of the council disobeyed said order and proceeded with their investigation. They were adjudged in con-

¹ Opdyke v. Marble, 44 Barb. 64; Whitman v. Weller, 39 Ind. 515,

<br>

### --- Page 0453 ---

332 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 304f.

tempt and fined a large sum each, and were imprisoned for non-payment of the fine. They applied to the federal supreme court for a writ of habeas corpus, not controverting the disobedience of the order, but claiming that such order was void. It was held that a court of equity has no jurisdiction of a bill to stay criminal proceedings nor to restrain the removal of a public officer, and that an injunction issued upon such a bill, as well as an order committing the defendants for contempt in disregarding the injunction, is absolutely void, and they are entitled to be discharged on habeas corpus. In another case it was stated by the same court that it is established by the decisions of this court that while the exercise of the power of punishment for contempt of their orders by courts of general jurisdiction is not subject to review by writ of error or appeal to this court, yet when a court of the United States undertakes by its process of contempt to punish a man for refusing to comply with an order which the court had no authority to make, the order itself, being without jurisdiction, is void, and the order punishing for the contempt is equally void, and that when the proceeding in such a case results in imprisonment this court will by its writ of habeas corpus discharge the prisoner. The direct question was whether an order of the circuit court enjoining the attorney-general and various commonwealth attorneys from bringing and prosecuting suits in the name and for the use of the state against taxpayers reported to be delinquent, but who had tendered, in payment of their taxes, tax-receivable coupons cut from bonds of the state, was void, the defendants having disobeyed it and were imprisoned for contempt. It was held under the particular facts that the action was in fact against the state, and hence within the prohibition of the eleventh amendment to the constitution, which declares that “the judicial power of the United States shall not be construed to extend to any suit in law or equity commenced or prosecuted against one of the United States by citizens of another state, or by citizens or subjects of a foreign state.” 

Sec. 304f. Judgment appealed from.— Where directors of a railway company refused to recognize one duly declared elected

1 In re Ayres, 123 U.S. 443

<br>

### --- Page 0454 ---

8§ 8047, 304A.) CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 333

as one of such board by the judgment of the court, an appeal having been taken from such judgment, it was held that such directors were not in contempt. The superior court had no jurisdiction, after proceedings had been stayed by appeal from its judgment determining the contest for the election of directors, to punish any of the other directors for contempt for refusal to recognize the director adjudged to have been elected or to permit him to act as such director in disobedience to the judgment, and an order adjudging him guilty of contempt for such disobedience will be annulled upon writ of review. There is no reason why the same rule should not apply as is held applicable in contests for public office, which is that the one declared elected is entitled to the office pending the appeal. Instead, however, of the court finding support in such rule, which they recognize, they find reason, because it was incorporated into the statutes of that state, to regard it as authority sustaining the conclusion reached.

Sec. 304g. Enjoining federal officers.— It was not a contempt of court on the part of the register and receiver of one of the United States land offices to disobey an injunction restraining them from proceeding to advertise and sell certain lands. They were officers of the land department of the government, presumably acting under instructions from the department, and the courts of equity of the state had no jurisdiction to enjoin them in the performance of their duties; that the act of congress (5 Stats. at Large, p. 107) placed the supervision and control of the survey and sale of the public lands in the commissioner of the general land office under the direction of the president of the United States.

Sec. 8042. Distinction between jurisdiction and judgment. The decision of the court in Forest v. Price, 52 N. 3. L. 16, is important not only in its bearing upon the particular question under consideration, but also in the distinction which is made between jurisdiction and judgment. It was said: “It is no excuse in a proceeding for contempt that the orders contemned are erroneous in law. The method of correcting such error is by

Foster v. Superior Court, 115 Cal. 279. Brewer v. Kidd, 23 Mich. 440.

<br>

### --- Page 0455 ---

334 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 8047.

appeal, not by disobedience. When a person is proceeded against for disobedience to an order or judgment he cannot allege in defense that the court erred in that order or judgment. To be successful he must go further and make out there was in legal effect, no order, by showing that the court had no right to judge between the parties upon the subject. In Mundy v. Vail, 5 Vroom, 418, Chief Justice Beasley defined jurisdiction to be “the right to adjudicate the subject-matter in a given case, to constitute which it is essential that the court must have cognizance of the class of cases to which the one adjudged belongs; that the proper parties shall be before the court, and that the point decided must in substance and effect be within the issue made by the pleadings. Testing the present case by the definition thus given, we first ascertain that its subject-matter is the application of the defendant’s established and ascertained property in possession of the United States to the satisfaction of the complainant’s judgment. That this court has cognizance of this class of cases is not disputed. The defendant is regularly before the court, and the points to be decided are clearly within the issues presented by the pleadings. To urge that the particular money in question is exempt from the application desired is to present a defense upon the merits of the case, and to object that the temporary restraint of the endorsement of drafts will hinder and delay the fiscal operations of the United States is to offer a reason why the court should not continue its temporary restraint; and so the insistment that under the section of the United States Revised Statutes which has been referred to the assignment to a receiver would be a nullity may be a reason why the court should not order it to be made. None of these matters, however, go to the court’s jurisdiction. They are defenses properly belonging in the cause which the court has power to adjudicate upon. If the court err in that adjudication, the remedy is by appeal.”

Sec. 3047. Mandamus — Writ allowed at chambers.— It is decided in New York that a judge at chambers has no jurisdiction either in the city of New York or elsewhere in the state to issue a writ of mandamus, and hence disobedience of such a writ by an election board was held not punishable as a

<br>

### --- Page 0456 ---

§§ 304), 304). CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 335

contempt. The writ was without authority and absolutely void.

Sec. 3047. California doctrine.— We have before referred to decisions of the supreme court of California in effect not recognizing the distinction generally observed by other courts between jurisdiction and judgment, and which held that the court will upon habeas corpus determine whether the act complained of was in fact a contempt. This exceptional doctrine is well illustrated in Ex parte Jaynes, 70 Cal. 638. There an employee of a telegraph company having charge of messages transmitted by it was by subpoena duces tecum commanded to search for and produce all messages from and to a large number of persons therein named between specified dates. He refused obedience to the command and was adjudged in contempt. It was held upon habeas corpus that the service of the subpoena was an evident search after testimony. That the petitioner was not bound to respond, and committed no contempt in failing to examine the papers under his control to ascertain if any such messages had been sent or received. The subpoena should have identified the particular messages required.

Sec. 304k. Order respecting possession of realty.— When complainant, claiming to be in actual possession of certain premises, obtained a preliminary injunction enjoining respondent from trespasses of a nature likely to injure the buildings, and the writ also enjoined them from in any way interfering or meddling with the possession and from entering into the premises, and it appeared that respondents were actually in possession at the time the injunction was issued, it was held that they could not be punished as for contempt in maintaining this possession by force. That the injunction was not in fact violated. It was issued to preserve an actual possession against molestation and not to oust a possessor who may have been a tortious holder. That no court can, by a preliminary ex parte order or process, turn even a wrong-doer out of possession. In fact, any decree or order divesting possession or rights on a preliminary inquiry is illegal and void and no one need respect.

1 People v. Donovan, 133 N. Y. 76. 2 People v. Simonson, 10 Mich. 333.

<br>

### --- Page 0457 ---

336 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 3042.]

or obey it. Thus, in Michigan a court of chancery cannot take from the directors of a corporation or vest in a receiver the management and control of the corporate business except in proceedings under the statutes to dissolve the corporation.

Sec. 3042. Injunction improperly granted.— The rule is applied that when a court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter, and over the persons of the parties, it may enforce obedience to its orders by proceedings against them as for contempt. In such a proceeding for contempt in failing to obey an order of the court, the respondent may question the order which he is charged with refusing to obey, only in so far as he can show it to be absolutely void. Thus, where the assignee of an insolvent estate was ordered by the court to pay a certain claim, he could not be heard in a proceeding for contempt to claim that the order was erroneous because that order was not authorized by the allegations in the petition, etc., and because the order went beyond the scope and prayer of the petition. This was said to result from the well-settled rule that judgments of courts cannot be attacked collaterally for irregularities in the proceeding, however erroneous they may be. The supreme court of Kansas emphatically stated the rule that where the court has jurisdiction of the parties and of the subject-matter, the fact that an order of injunction has been erroneously granted affords no justification or excuse for its violation before it has been dissolved; and this is equally true whether the proceeding is reviewed upon error or by certiorari or habeas corpus. The same was declared by the supreme court of Wisconsin, and it was held, where one has been imprisoned upon attachment for a contempt in disobeying an injunctional order, he cannot, on an application for discharge by habeas corpus, avail himself of mere irregularities in the proceedings upon which the order was based, but must show lack of jurisdiction to make the order; and the court of appeals of New York declare the same doctrine. It was there stated that it is not an excuse for

Tawas Bay Co. R. Co. v. Iosco Judge, 31 Mich. 456. See Merchants’ Judge, 44 Mich. 479; McCombs v. Merryhew, 40 Mich. 721; Arnold v. Bright, 41 Mich. 207; Nat. Bank v. Kent, 43 Mich. 292; Clark v. Burke et al, 163 Ill. 334.
Billard v. Erhart, 35 Kan. 616.
Port Huron & G. R. W. Co. v. In re Perry, 80 Wis. 268.

<br>

### --- Page 0458 ---

§ 304m.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 337

the violation of an injunction that the order is more extensive in its restraints than the prayer of the complaint; the order, if irregular, is not void, and while it is in force it is the duty of the defendant to obey it. If there is legal evidence sufficient to call for the exercise of the judgment and discretion of the court upon motion to punish a party in contempt for violating an injunction, its decision is not reviewable. The expressions of the Iowa court are equally emphatic and to the point. It was stated, speaking with reference to a judicial order: “If they” (articles of discipline) “were called to the attention of the court in that proceeding, and, notwithstanding, the court erroneously ordered the trustees to do what is beyond their power, the order may upon proper proceedings be reversed. But the order of the court, even if erroneous, is not void. The court had jurisdiction of the parties and of the subject-matter, and its adjudication cannot be disregarded with impunity. So long as it remains unreversed it must be obeyed. There would be an end of all subordination and social order if parties could disregard judicial orders, and, when proceeded against for contempt, call in question the correctness of the order itself. In such a proceeding the only legitimate inquiry is, Did the court have jurisdiction, and did it make an order which has been violated?” The supreme court of Vermont is in full accord with the rule thus expressed. They say: “Although an injunction be irregularly obtained, it is still an order of the court, and must be discharged before it can be disobeyed.”

Sec. 304m. Orders made in other than judicial proceedings.— Statutes sometimes provide for the making of certain orders by courts or judges thereof, which, strictly speaking, are not within their ordinary duties, and the question is presented of the power of such court or judge to enforce obedience to such orders by proceedings in contempt. Thus courts ordinarily are clothed with the power to fix and order paid the compensation of phonographic reporters in criminal cases, and it is made the duty of the treasurer to pay the same upon the 

1 People v. Pendleton, 64 N. Y. 623. 
2 State v. Baldwin, 57 Iowa, 266. 
3 Howe v. Millard, 40 Vt. 654.

<br>

### --- Page 0459 ---

333 SPECIAL REMEDIES, L§ 3804:n.

order of the court. In a case where such power and duty were present, it was held that the act of the treasurer in disobeying an order of the court fixing the compensation of a reporter and directing its payment was a contempt and as such punishable by imprisonment. That the laws relating to such matter appertained to the judicial system of the state and constituted a part of it; that it did not pertain to the legislative branch; that the board of supervisors of a county in such matters cannot review or reverse or modify the action of the court. The same reasoning and rule apply with respect to orders for the payment of the fees of witnesses in criminal cases. In another and subsequent case in the same state the distinction was drawn between powers conferred upon the court and those to be exercised by the judge, holding that the orders of the court were enforceable by proceedings in contempt, those of the judge by proceedings in mandamus. The facts were that, under section 144 of the California code, it was provided that if suitable rooms and necessary furniture, stationery, etc., sufficient for the transaction of the business of the superior court be not provided by the supervisors of the county, the court or a judge thereof may direct the sheriff to provide the same, and that the expenses incurred, certified by the judge or judges to be correct, shall be a charge against the city and county treasury, and paid out of the general fund thereof. It was held that it was the duty of the judge of the court to audit the demand for expenses incurred in pursuance of said section of the code, and it was not necessary that the supervisors should also audit it; and when so audited the duty of the treasurer to pay it out of the general fund was a duty imposed upon him by law, a neglect or refusal to perform which rendered him liable to proceedings by writ of mandamus or other appropriate remedy. The power of the judge under that section is exhausted after his auditing the demand for expenses incurred, and the court has no inherent statutory power to order the treasurer, who is neither an officer of the court nor a party to the proceeding, to pay the certified demand out of the general fund, and a refusal to obey such order did not render the treasurer amenable to proceedings for contempt of court. The decision goes further than first stated. It

1 Ex parte Reis, 64 Cal. 233.

<br>

### --- Page 0460 ---

§ 3042.7 CIVIL CONTEMPTS, 339

was held that the language of the section did not clothe either the court or judge with power to order the payment. The judge was to certify the account as correct only, and in this respect it was distinguished from Ex parte Weeks, for there the statute was in express words, “to pay on the order of the court.”1

Sec. 304n. Interference by a third party.—Any person who interferes with the process, control or action of the court in a pending litigation, unlawfully and without authority, is guilty of a civil contempt if his act defeats, impairs, impedes or prejudices the rights or remedies of a party to such action or proceeding. Thus the advising and procuring disobedience of an order or judgment directing the transfer of stock upon the books of a corporation is such a contempt. The power of the court is not destroyed by the original party purging himself of contempt by compliance with the order, after proceedings are commenced against such third person, but the court could proceed and punish the offender. His offense is not in the omission to perform something the court had enjoined upon him and which it was in his power to do, but is an affirmative act of resistance to the process of the court—an active effort to defeat its orders and make its judgment nugatory; hence the power of punishment. The court also takes occasion to refer to the distinction between criminal and civil contempts, and states the conclusion which elsewhere appears herein; in fact, that what may be termed civil contempts may be punished criminally. That the distinction between civil and criminal contempts is not the test to determine the extent or character of the punishment. The offender in the above case was sentenced to imprisonment for a term of six months, and it was argued that as the party who directly disobeyed the order of the court had purged his contempt, the same result extended to him. The statute provided that where it is in the power of the party to perform the act required he shall be imprisoned only until he has performed it and paid his fine; but it further provided that in every other case the penalty shall be a fine of not more than $250 and imprisonment not to exceed six months. Such was the provision which covered the contempt of the offender.2

1 Ex parte Wilber, 91 Cal. 367.  
2 King et al. v. Barnes, 113 N.Y. 476.

<br>

### --- Page 0461 ---

340 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 3040, 3804].

Sec. 3040. Decree of specific performance—Refusal.—The wilful refusal of a party to comply with the decree of a court of competent jurisdiction directing him to execute a deed of conveyance constitutes a contempt of such court, and may be punished as such. It seems that the code of California expressly confers the power to thus enforce performance, though it is conceded that the power exists independent of such a statute. A defendant, however, could not be guilty of contempt until a deed had been presented to him for execution and he had refused to execute it.

Sec. 3041. Removal of child.—A state court adjudged the father of a child in contempt for removal of his child beyond the jurisdiction of the court while proceedings were pending to determine with respect to the care and custody of the child as between the father and mother in an action for divorce. More properly it should be stated that he was adjudged in contempt for failure to obey the order of the court to produce the child. It appeared that the child was beyond the jurisdiction of the court, but subject to his control and direction, and was so placed by him to avoid the effect of an adverse decision with respect to its custody. The petitioner sued out a writ of habeas corpus in the federal court, alleging as a ground for the writ that he was imprisoned without due process of law, the particular ground being that, as the child was without the state, the state court was without jurisdiction, which is the rule where it is simply sought to reach the person of the child upon habeas corpus. It was held that the state court evidently had jurisdiction, and hence the federal court could not interfere and review its judgment. It was further held that, while the child was not in the manual possession of the petitioner, it was in his custody and under his control, and he was within the jurisdiction of the court. The facts resembled those in the case of In re Jackson, 15 Mich. 416. There the writ was refused upon an equal division of the judges—Cooley and Christiancy in favor of the writ, the others contra. The opinion of Justice Cooley was to the effect that habeas corpus would issue.

Land and Water Co. v. Superior Court, 93 Cal. 139; Berry v. Innes, 35 Mich. 189.

<br>

### --- Page 0462 ---

§ 304g.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 341

when a citizen of the state is unlawfully held in custody elsewhere by another person who is himself within the jurisdiction of the court. If he is within the jurisdiction, the wrong is there being done, for the wrong is done wherever the power of control is exercised.

Sec. 304g. Receivers—Interference with their possession, etc.—Receivers are mere officers of the court, and their possession or right to possession of property is that of the court. Hence any interference with their lawful authority is a contempt of the court. Strictly speaking, it is a disobedience of the order appointing the receiver and clothing him with authority; and such contempt in interfering with property in the hands of a receiver may be committed by any person, whether an officer or employee of the receiver, or by a stranger. Any unlawful interference with the operation of a road in the hands of a receiver is a contempt of court, because it is a disobedience or resistance by a person to a lawful order of the court. It was declared that any wilful attempt by any one, with knowledge that the road is in the hands of the court, to prevent or impede the receiver from complying with the orders of the court in running the road, when the attempt is unlawful, and, as between private individuals, would give a right of action for damages, is a contempt of the order of the court. The rights of a receiver with reference to his business in conducting the railroad under orders of the court are not different in any respect from those of a private railway corporation. The only difference is in the remedy which the courts will apply to prevent or to punish a violation of them, when such violation prevents or impedes the operation of the road, and is intended to do so. It was further declared that maliciously inciting employees of a receiver who is operating a railroad under orders of the court to leave his employ, in pursuance of an unlawful combination to prevent the operation of the road, thereby inflicting injuries to its business for which damages would be recoverable if it were operated by a private corpora-

1 Ex parte Young, 50 Fed. Rep. 526.  
3 Secor v. Railroad Co., 7 Biss. 513;  
2 In re Acker, 66 Fed. Rep. 200; In United States v. Kane, 23 Fed. Rep., re Debs, 158 U. S. 564, 748,

<br>

### --- Page 0463 ---

d42 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 304.

tion, is a contempt of court. The rule extends not only to the taking possession of property in the charge of a receiver, but also in taking legal proceedings to obtain possession thereof, without the sanction of the court. Thus, it is a contempt for a sheriff to seize property in the hands of a receiver, with notice of his appointment and without leave of the court appointing him, even though the title of the person whose writ the sheriff executes is paramount to that of the receiver.

Sec. 3047, Non-payment of alimony.— The question whether proceedings in contempt will lie for the non-payment of alimony is one as to which courts are not agreed. The Wisconsin court are of the opinion that when the judgment is for a gross sum, payable at once, that it might undoubtedly be docketed as a money judgment and execution might issue to enforce it. They state in that case the argument might be strong that contempt proceedings could not be resorted to, and the position would not be without authority,—citing Lansing v. Lansing, 4 Lans. 377. When, however, alimony is payable in instalments, the same result does not follow. They reason that as execution can only issue on a judgment which has been docketed, and that a judgment is to be docketed at the time of filing the judgment roll, and that it would require the docketing of a separate judgment as each instalment became due, it is apparent that an execution cannot issue upon such an order or decree; and such is the reasoning of the New York court of appeals. Another sufficient reason might be stated, and that is, the question of alimony is always subject to revision by the court. In Michigan it seems that an order for temporary alimony is to be enforced by contempt proceedings. They concede, however, that under their statute the payment of money can only be enforced by attachment when by law execution cannot be awarded for the collection of such sum, but conclude that execution cannot be awarded for temporary alimony. That an execution is only proper after final decree in case of permanent

Thomas v. Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co., 62 Fed. Rep. 803.
Staples v. Staples, 87 Wis. 592.
Park v. Park, 80 N. Y. 156.
Richards v. People, 81 Ill. 551.
Haines v. Haines, 35 Mich. 138.
Commonwealth v. Young, 11 Phila. (Pa.) 606.

<br>

### --- Page 0464 ---

§ 3048. CIVIL CONTEMPTS. 343

alimony, or of an allowance in support of children? The supreme court of Maine are of the opinion, and so decide, with respect to the payment of temporary alimony payable in instalments, that it may be enforced by proceedings in contempt or by execution. In Massachusetts it is held that scire facias is not the exclusive remedy in case of non-payment of instalments. An action, however, will not lie for arrears, upon the ground that the court may from time to time, on the petition of either party, revise and alter its decree respecting the amount of alimony and the payment thereof. It is conceded that an execution is usually ordered when the decree is for the payment of money. They state, however, that there may be cases where an attachment for contempt could be ordered, but only where there has been a contemptuous disobedience of the order or decree. So in Louisiana the fact that a judgment for alimony is enforceable by fieri facias does not preclude a commitment for contempt for a wilful refusal to comply with its terms; and it seems such is the doctrine of the Minnesota court. In Nebraska, however, it is held that a decree awarding permanent alimony being enforceable in the same manner as judgments at law, the non-compliance with such order in the absence of fraud is not punishable as for contempt of court.

Sec. 3049. Non-payment of alimony — Ability to pay.— It is held by the California court that contempt in neglecting to pay alimony directed is a civil contempt, and state that there is a well-settled distinction between a civil and criminal contempt. The former consists generally in failing to do something ordered to be done by a court in a civil action for the benefit of the opposing party therein; the latter consists in acts of disrespect of the court, such as disorderly or violent conduct in its presence or immediate vicinity, or in the doing of a forbidden act, resistance to process, etc.; that there might be instances where the character of the contempt would be of difficult determination. They further state that all the author-

Taylor v. Gladwin, 40 Mich. 232; Slade v. Slade, 106 Mass. 499.  
Palmer v. Palmer, 45 Mich. 150; State v. King, 49 La. Ann. 1503.  
North v. North, 30 Mich. 67; State v. Jamison, 72 N. W. Rep.  
Russell v. Russell, 69 Me. 836; 451.  
Dwelly v. Dwelly, 46 Me. 377.  
Leeder v. State, 75 N. W. Rep. 541.

<br>

### --- Page 0465 ---

344 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 304s.

ities agree that in a case of civil contempt — that is, when a defendant in a civil action is ordered by the court to pay money generally to the plaintiff, and is committed until he shall have paid it — the prisoner is in custody under an execution; and it was so held in England. Such was the doctrine declared in New York, where it was said: “It is to all intents and purposes an execution. The order is a judgment rendered in favor of one party against the other for the payment of a sum certain, or which may be reduced to a certainty, if it be for costs, by taxation. The object of the precept is to execute the order, to give it effect and to enforce its performance; and when the party is imprisoned upon it, he is, within the letter and meaning of the law, imprisoned by virtue of an execution.” If these premises be correct, it follows that an attachment for contempt for refusal to pay alimony or imprisonment on such ground cannot be justified on any other ground than wilful disobedience of the order; and if it is made to appear that the defendant is without the ability to meet its requirements, the refusal cannot be wilful, otherwise the imprisonment would be for debt. Hence, it is the duty of the court to hear evidence touching the property and effects of such a defendant, and to discharge him if it appears therefrom that he is not of sufficient ability to pay. As stated by one court: “To hold that the petitioner, though penniless, must suffer a life imprisonment for debt because of the particular form in which the imprisonment was imposed, would be a gross violation of the plain intent of the code.”

It was stated by the Wisconsin court that the remedy by imprisonment was severe and harsh; that imprisonment should not be ordered where it appears that the default is the result of an honest inability to pay, on account of business misfortunes or lack of health or earning ability, or other circumstances which are not the fault of the defendant. But where the inability is wilfully brought about by the defendant himself, with intent to avoid payment, the refusal to pay becomes contumacious, and the inability so resulting will not purge the defendant.

1 Rex v. Stokes, Cowp. 186. See In re Wilson, 75 Cal. 580.  
2 Van Wezel v. Van Wezel, 3 Paige, 43.  
3 In re Wilson, 75 Cal. 580.

<br>

### --- Page 0466 ---

§ 304:.] CIVIL CONTEMPTS, 845

ant of contempt. The language used by the Wisconsin court is somewhat general in character, and would seem to imply, in connection with other language used, that the question of an honest inability to pay was one which the court had discretion to determine. It was held by the supreme court of Maine that the determination of that question by the trial court was conclusive upon the parties.
