It is elementary that jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person must exist to enable a court to render a valid judgment, and, if either is wanting, a judgment rendered is absolutely void and without authority of law; and the question of jurisdiction is always open to inquiry, either upon direct or collateral attack. Says Allen, Justice, in People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N. Y. 559, on page 566: “The remedy against illegal imprisonment afforded by this writ as it was known and used at common law is placed beyond the pale of legislative discretion, except that it may be suspended, when public safety requires, in either of the two emergencies named in the constitution.” In commenting upon a statute in terms similar to the one quoted, he further states: “To bar the applicant from a discharge from arrest by virtue of a judgment or decree, or an execution thereon, the court in which the judgment or decree is given must have had jurisdiction to render such judgment. The tribunal must be competent to render the judgment under some circumstances. The prohibition does not and cannot take from the court or officer the power or relieve him from the duty of determining whether the process, judgment, decree or execution emanated from a court of competent jurisdiction; and whether the court making the judgment or decree or issuing the process had the legal and constitutional power to give such judgment or send forth such process. It simply pro-

1 In re Boardman in behalf of Durant, 169 U. S. 39.

<br>

### --- Page 0613 ---

A64 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 318.

hibits the review of the decision of a court of competent jurisdiction. If the record shows that the judgment is not merely erroneous, but such as could not, under any circumstances or upon any state of facts, have been pronounced, the case is not within the exception of the statute, and the applicant must be discharged. If the judgment is merely erroneous, the court having given a wrong judgment when it had jurisdiction, the party aggrieved can only have relief by writ of error or other process of review. He cannot be relieved summarily by habeas corpus.”

Sec. 318. Judgment distinguished from jurisdiction.— It would seem that this clear and concise statement of the law ought to render it a matter of little difficulty to determine in a given case whether the writ of habeas corpus would lie. We find, however, in many cases the distinction between jurisdiction and judgment is so close that it is extremely difficult to draw the line by any test expressed in the statement of the rule. The general statement was made in Ex parte Watkins, 3 Pet. 193: “An imprisonment under a judgment cannot be unlawful unless that judgment be an absolute nullity; and that it is not a nullity if the court has a general jurisdiction of the subject, although it would be erroneous. To determine whether the offense charged in the indictment be legally punishable or not is among the most unquestionable of its (the court’s) powers and duties. The decision of this question is the exercise of jurisdiction, whether the judgment be for or against the prisoner.” It was seemingly conceded, in a subsequent case, that this language was too broad in asserting that, because every court must pass upon its own jurisdiction, such decision is itself the exercise of a jurisdiction which belongs to it, and cannot therefore be questioned in any other court; and it was said that it was not intended to say that, because a federal court tries a prisoner for an ordinary common-law offense, as burglary, assault and battery, or larceny, with no averment or proof of any offense against the United States, or any connection with a statute of the United States, and punishes him by imprisonment, he cannot be released by habeas corpus, because the court which tried him had assumed jurisdiction. In all

<br>

### --- Page 0614 ---

§ 319.] HABEAS CORPUS. 465

Such cases, when the question of jurisdiction is raised, the point to be decided is whether the court has jurisdiction of that class of offenses. If the statute has invested the court which tried the prisoner with jurisdiction to punish a well-defined class of offenses, as forgery of its bonds or perjury in its courts, its judgment as to what acts were necessary to constitute the crime is not reviewable on a writ of habeas corpus; and as the laws of Congress are only valid when they are within the constitutional powers of that body, the validity of a statute under which a prisoner is held in custody may be inquired into under a writ of habeas corpus, as affecting the jurisdiction of the court which ordered his imprisonment; and if their want of power appears on the face of the record of his condemnation, whether in the indictment or elsewhere, the court which has authority to issue the writ is bound to release him.

Sec. 319. Sentence in excess of law.— We have in a former chapter discussed the effect of excess of punishment upon the question of jurisdiction. Briefly it will be referred to again in connection with the subject under discussion. The principle, say the Supreme Court of the United States, is established, that where a court has jurisdiction of the person and offense, the imposition of a sentence in excess of what the law permits does not render the legal or authorized portion of the sentence void, but only leaves such portion of the sentence as may be in excess open to question and attack. In other words, the sound rule is that a sentence is legal so far as it is within the provisions of law and the jurisdiction of the court over the person and offense, and only void as to the excess when such excess is separable and may be dealt with, without disturbing the valid portion of the sentence. The excess in the sentence of the court beyond the provisions of law is only voidable in proceedings upon writ of error. This statement of the rule is not in conflict with those cases in the federal and other courts, cited in the former chapter, where the prisoner was discharged upon

1 In re Coy, 127 U.S. 731-758. Sennotti's Case, 146 Mass. 489; People v. Kelley, 97 N.Y. 2 parte Van  
2 See ch. 2, sec. 28.  
3 United States v. Pridgeon, 153 U.S. 48.  
4 Ex parte Lange, 18 Wall. 163; 44 Cal. 579.  
lagen, 25 Ohio St. 426; Elsner v. Shrigley, 80 Iowa, 30; Ex parte Max,

<br>

### --- Page 0615 ---

466 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 320.

the showing that he was held in custody under a sentence in excess of that prescribed by law. Those cases were decided upon the ground that the prisoner had performed or suffered the lawful part of the sentence, and was being held for the excess or unlawful part only, and where such is the case the writ of habeas corpus will issue.

Sec. 320. Former jeopardy.— From what was said, as well as from the conclusion reached in the cases cited in the former part of this chapter, it might possibly be inferred that the question of a second jeopardy was reviewable upon writ of habeas corpus, except where the question arises upon certain conditions. Such is not the rule. Such question does not go to the jurisdiction or authority of the trial court, but involves the judgment of the court, which, if wrongfully exercised, is but mere error, which is not reviewable upon habeas corpus. The language in Ex parte Bigelow, 113 U. S. 328, is very concise and fully expressive of the application of the general rule to the particular subject in question. It was said: “The trial court had jurisdiction of the offense described in the indictment on which the prisoner was tried. It had jurisdiction of the prisoner, who was properly brought before the court. It had jurisdiction to hear the charge and the evidence against the prisoner. It had jurisdiction to hear and to decide upon the defenses offered by him. The matter now presented is one of those defenses. Whether it was a sufficient defense was a matter of law on which the court must pass, so far as it was purely a question of law, and on which the jury under the instruction of the court must pass, if we can suppose any of the facts were such as required submission to the jury. If the question had been one of former acquittal, a much stronger case than this, the court would have had jurisdiction to pass upon the record, whether there had been a former acquittal for the same offense, and if the identity of the offenses was in dispute, it might be necessary on such a plea to submit the question to the jury on the issue raised by the plea. The same principle would apply to

1 In re Swan, 150 U. S. 637; People v. Snow, 126 U.S. 274; Ex parte Lange, ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N. Y. 18 Wall. 163; In re Coy, 127 U.S. 731.
3 Ex parte Bigelow, 113 U.S. 328;
2 In re Nielson, 131 U.S. 176; In re Ex parte Belt, 159 U. S. 95.

<br>

### --- Page 0616 ---

§ 321.] HABEAS CORPUS. 467

a plea of former conviction. Clearly in these cases the court not only has jurisdiction to try and decide the question raised, but it is its imperative duty so to do. If the court makes a mistake on such trial it is error, which may be corrected by the usual mode of correcting such errors; but that the court had jurisdiction to decide upon the matter raised by the plea, both as matter of law and of fact, cannot be doubted.” In Ex parte Lange, supra, the relator was sentenced to both fine and imprisonment when by the law the punishment could only have been fine or imprisonment, and the party had paid the fine, when the court afterwards modified the judgment by imposing imprisonment instead of the former sentence. It was held that, the judgment of the court having been executed so as to be a full satisfaction of one of the alternative penalties of the law, the power of the court as to that offense was at an end. The power of the court was tested by the writ, not the question of second jeopardy.

Sec. 321. Subject further discussed.— The Nielson case presented a question closer to the borderline, yet the court seems to have found no difficulty in arriving at a conclusion. There the prisoner was indicted for unlawful cohabitation, under an act of congress prescribing the offense, and also for adultery, the latter act being included in the act of unlawful cohabitation. He pleaded guilty to the first indictment. Being arraigned upon the other he pleaded not guilty, and, after he had suffered the penalty imposed by the sentence for unlawful cohabitation, the indictment for adultery came on for trial, whereupon by leave of court he entered a formal plea of former conviction, setting up the indictment for unlawful cohabitation and his sentence upon his plea of guilty thereto, claiming in substance that the unlawful cohabitation was continuous and constituted but one offense. The district attorney demurred to this plea, which was sustained. The defendant was found guilty of adultery and again sentenced; whereupon the next day he presented his petition for writ of habeas corpus, setting forth the indictments and proceedings in both cases. The court refused the application. The prisoner appealed from this judgment upon the writ to the supreme court of the United States. At the outset it was urged that no question of

<br>

### --- Page 0617 ---

jurisdiction was presented. The court held, first, that the double conviction appeared on the record, which was admitted to be true by the demurrer of the government, and that, it thus appearing, the court would inquire into the legality of the judgment, and if, as a matter of law, the conditions existing established that in fact there was a second conviction for the same offense, the court would order his discharge. It must be true that the question of the defense of former conviction was properly a question for the trial court to determine, as much so in this case as in the Bigelow case. It is also true that, in the Bigelow case, the application for the writ was made direct to the supreme court, while in the Nielson case it came before the supreme court upon appeal from the decision and judgment of the circuit court, pursuant to section 1909, Revised Statutes. Upon such appeal it was held in In re Neagle, 135 U.S. 1, that upon such an appeal the court will examine the whole record, and examine the case upon the law and facts as it was heard in the inferior court. In the Bigelow case the application was made before judgment. I hardly think that any of these questions were controlling as to the reasons for the judgment of the court. The reasoning proceeds upon other grounds; one is that in one respect the facts brought the case within the principle applied in the Lange case, to wit: that the accused had suffered the penalty that lawfully could be imposed; and as in that case the court determined the effect of the law under which the punishment was imposed, though it was the duty of the trial court so to do, so in this, the court could so far examine the law upon the admitted record as to ascertain and determine whether the punishment was legal or otherwise. In other words, it could review the judgment of the court below upon that question of law. This appears to follow from the language used by the court after affirming what was held in another case (In re Coy, 127 U.S. 731), in effect that the power of congress to pass a statute under which a prisoner is held in custody may be inquired into under a writ of habeas corpus as affecting the jurisdiction of the court. They say: “In the present case, it is true, the ground for habeas corpus was not the invalidity of the act of congress under which the defendant was indicted, but a second prosecution and trial.

<br>

### --- Page 0618 ---

§ 321.) HABEAS CORPUS. 469

for the same offense, contrary to an express provision of the constitution. In other words, a constitutional immunity of the defendant was violated by the second trial and judgment. It is difficult to see why a conviction and punishment under an unconstitutional law is more violative of a person’s constitutional rights than an unconstitutional conviction and punishment under a valid law. In the first case, it is true, the court has no authority to take cognizance of the case, but in the other it has no authority to render judgment against the defendants; and state again, that in such a case “it is not a case of mere error of law, but a case denying to a person a constitutional right.” This reasoning is far from conclusive. The answer to the argument is, that the accused was not denied a constitutional right so long as he was afforded a constitutional remedy. The question was not that he had been denied such right, but that the court had made an erroneous decision with respect to that right, which was subject to review upon writ of error. This question the court of necessity had to decide, and the fact that it made a wrong decision instead of a right one in no way affected its jurisdiction to decide.

Note To Sec. 321.—In Andrews v. Swartz, 156 U. S. 272, the contention on the part of the accused was that the statute of New Jersey, which in effect, in criminal cases punishable with death, provided that writs of error shall be considered as writs of grace and shall not issue but by the order of the chancellor, and prescribing certain conditions, was in violation of the constitution of the United States. The court, in reply, repeating what was said in a former case, say: “An appeal from a judgment of conviction is not a matter of absolute right independently of constitutional or statutory provisions allowing such appeal. A review by an appellate court of the final judgment in a criminal case, however grave the offense of which the accused is convicted, was not at common law and is not now a necessary element of due process of law. It is wholly within the discretion of the state to allow or not to allow such a review. It is therefore clear that the right of appeal may be accorded by the state to the accused upon such terms as in its wisdom may be proper: and whether an appeal should be allowed, and if so under what circumstances or on what conditions, are matters for each state to determine for itself.” It was further stated: “The repugnancy of a statute to the constitution of the state, by whose legislature it was enacted, cannot authorize a writ of habeas corpus from a court of the United States unless the petitioner is in custody by virtue of such statute, and unless, also, such statute is in conflict with the constitution of the United States.” The further contention on the part of the accused was that he was restrained of his liberty, in violation of the

<br>

### --- Page 0619 ---

470 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 321c.

constitution and laws of the United States, in that persons of his race were arbitrarily excluded, solely because of their race, from the panel of jurors summoned for the term of court at which he was tried, and because the state court denied him the right to establish that fact by competent proof. It was held that, conceding this to be true, it was but mere error in the conduct of the trial, and if it was to his prejudice his proper remedy was, after final judgment of conviction, to carry the case to the highest court of the state having jurisdiction to review that judgment, thence upon writ of error to the supreme court of the United States, if the final judgment of such state court denied any right, privilege or immunity specially claimed and which was secured to him by the constitution of the United States. Because of the denial of the right to establish by proof the fact alleged, it did not follow that the court lost jurisdiction of the case within the meaning of the well-established rule that a prisoner under conviction and sentence of another court will not be discharged on habeas corpus unless the court that passed the sentence was so far without jurisdiction that its proceedings must be regarded as void.

§ 321a. Indictment not charging an offense.— To determine whether the offense charged in the indictment be legally punishable or not is among the most unquestionable of the powers and duties of the trial court. The decision of this question is the exercise of jurisdiction. The real point to be decided in the case upon writ of habeas corpus is whether the court has jurisdiction of that class of offenses. If it is such jurisdiction as is conferred, its judgment as to what acts were necessary under the statute to constitute the crime is not reviewable upon a writ. Whether the indictment sets forth in comprehensive terms the offense which the statute describes and forbids, and for which it prescribes a punishment, is in every case a question of law which must necessarily be decided by the court in which the case originates, and is therefore clearly within its jurisdiction. Whether the indictment or information fails to show that any criminal offense has been committed is a question of law for determination by the trial court, as well as whether the act was or was not a crime within the statute. Whether an act charged in an indictment is or is not a crime by the law which the court administers is a ques-

1 In re Coy, 127 U.S. 781; Ex parte Yarbrough, 110 U. S. 651; Ex parte Watkins, 3 Pet. 193; Ex parte Parks, 93 U. S. 23; 
2 Hauser v. State, 33 Wis. 678; Evans, 88 Wis. 255; De Puy v. Evans, 88 Wis, 285, 
3 Hauser v. State, 33 Wis, 678; 
4 Ex parte Parks, 93 U. S. 18

<br>

### --- Page 0620 ---

§ 321a.] HABEAS CORPUS. 471

tion which has to be met at almost every stage of criminal procedure, on motions to quash the indictment, on demurrers, on motions to arrest judgment, etc. The court may err, but it has jurisdiction of the question. The rule is different where the indictment and the arrest thereunder are not warranted by the constitution, and are in violation of the rights of the prisoner. In such case the court upon habeas corpus will determine the question of law as to the constitutionality of the act under which the court proceeds, as well as whether the constitutional rights of the prisoner have been violated. There are cases which hold that habeas corpus will issue where the facts stated in the indictment do not constitute a crime. Hence, where a state provided a punishment for conducting the game of faro, it was held not to apply to those who merely bet at the game; and where one of the latter class was upon indictment convicted of the offense prescribed by the statute, he was released upon habeas corpus. Hence, where it appears from the record that there is a second conviction for the same offense, the prisoner will be discharged upon habeas corpus. And the same result will follow where it appears that the prisoner has not had a constitutional trial by a constitutional jury. The doctrine, however, does not extend to a sentence under an unconstitutional law relating simply to the character and extent of the sentence, so long as there exists a valid law which would authorize a sentence of the kind given; nor to proceedings regulating the time and manner in which the issue of insanity should be tried, though such manner should be different from the established method existing at common law, and the statute authorizing such new method should be invalid. Nor does the doctrine apply unless the prisoner is in custody under an unconstitutional law. Where it was alleged that a prisoner was restrained of his liberty in violation of the constitution and laws of the United States, in that persons of his race were arbitrarily excluded, solely because of their race, from the panel

1 Ex parte Parks, 93 U.S. 18.
2 In re Nielson, 131 U.S. 176.
3 Ex parte Virginia, 100 U.S. 339.
4 In re Staff, 63 Wis. 285.
5 In re Nielson, 131 U.S. 176.
6 In re Pikulik, 81 Wis. 158; In re Ex parte Boland, 11 Tex. App. 159.
7 Ex parte Ah Yem, 53 Cal. 246; Schuster, 82 Wis. 610.
8 In re French, 81 Wis. 597.
9 Ex parte Ah Yem, 53 Cal. 246.
10 Andrews v. Swartz, 156 U.S. 272.

<br>

### --- Page 0621 ---

A472 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 322.

of jurors, and because the state court denied him the right to establish that fact by competent proof, it could not be said that he was in custody under an unconstitutional law.

Sec. 322. Unconstitutional laws — Conviction under. — If the court which renders a judgment has no jurisdiction to render it, either because the proceedings or the law under which they are taken is unconstitutional, or for any other reason, the judgment is void, and may be questioned collaterally, and a defendant who is imprisoned under and by virtue of it may be discharged from custody on habeas corpus. The repugnancy of a state statute to the constitution of the state will not authorize a writ of habeas corpus from a court of the United States, unless the prisoner is in custody by virtue of such statute, and unless also the statute conflicts with the federal constitution. As the laws of congress are only valid when they are within the constitutional power of that body, the validity of the statute under which a prisoner is held in custody may be inquired into under a writ of habeas corpus as affecting the jurisdiction of the court which ordered his imprisonment. The court has no authority to take cognizance of a case under an unconstitutional law, and it has no authority to proceed to judgment in an unconstitutional method, or where the court violates in its judgment the constitutional rights of the prisoner, or where it violates such rights in the method of procedure. It was said, however, that where the state court had entered upon the trial, the proper remedy after final judgment of conviction was, if such court committed error to the prejudice of the accused, to carry the case to the highest court of the state having jurisdiction to review that judgment, thence upon writ of error to the supreme court of the United States, if the final judgment of such state court denied any right, privilege or immunity specially claimed, and which was secured to him by the constitution of the United States.

1 In re Nielson, 131 U.S. 176. 4 In re Nielson, 131 U.S. 176.

2 Andrews v. Swartz, 156 U.S. 272; 5 In re Staff, 63 Wis. 285.

3 In re Coy, 127 U.S. 731. 6 Andrews v. Swartz, 156 U.S. 272.

63 Wis. 235. See also Bergeman v. Backer, 157 U.S. 655.

<br>

### --- Page 0622 ---

§ 322u.] HABEAS CORPUS. 473

Sec. 322a. Municipal ordinances in conflict with personal rights.— There is a growing tendency on the part of municipal governments, under the guise of the exercise of the police power, to encroach upon the rights and liberties of the citizen or inhabitant. It is manifested by creating unreasonable restrictions upon the carrying on of lawful business pursuits, as well as imposing unreasonable restraint upon personal action and conduct and in the enjoyment of property. Penalties are prescribed for the violation of ordinances of such character which may result in imprisonment. Where a person has been thus imprisoned, the federal courts are open to him to test the constitutionality of such laws or regulations by habeas corpus. Definitions of the police power must, in the language of the supreme court of the United States, “be taken subject to the condition that the state cannot in its exercise, for any purpose whatever, encroach upon the powers of the general government or rights granted or secured by the supreme laws of the land. Objects not within its scope cannot be secured at the expense of the protection afforded by the federal constitution.” The same court has many times of late taken occasion to express its views upon such legislation in clear and forcible terms. The following is language uttered by Mr. Justice Swayne in the famous Slaughter House Cases, 16 Wall., on p. 127: “Life is a gift of God, and the right to preserve it is the most sacred of the rights of man. Liberty is freedom from all restraints but such as are justly imposed by law. Beyond that line lies the domain of usurpation and tyranny. Property is everything which has an exchangeable value, and the right of property includes the power to dispose of it according to the will of the owner. Labor is property, and as such merits protection. The right to make it available is next in importance to the rights of life and liberty. It lies to a large extent at the foundation of most other forms of property.” In the same connection, Justice Bradley gave utterance to the following: “In my view a law which prohibits a large class of citizens from adopting a lawful employment, or from following a lawful employment previously adopted, does deprive them of liberty as well as property without due process of law. Their right of choice is a portion of their liberty.

1 New Orleans Gas Light Co. v. La. Light & Heat Co., 6 Sup. Ct. Rep. 252.

<br>

### --- Page 0623 ---

404 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 322a.

Their occupation is their property. Such a law also deprives those citizens of the equal protection of the laws, contrary to the last clause of the section.” Page 122. The words “privileges and immunities,” as used in the constitution, say the court in Ward v. Maryland, 12 Wall. 430, “are words of very comprehensive meaning; but it will be sufficient to say that the clause plainly and unmistakably secures and protects the right of a citizen of one state to pass into any other state of the Union for the purpose of engaging in lawful commerce, trade or business without molestation; to acquire personal property; to take and hold real estate; to maintain actions in the courts of the state, and to be exempt from any higher taxes or excises than are imposed by the state upon its own citizens.” Applying this principle to the facts of the case under consideration it was further said: “And inasmuch as the constitution provides that the citizens of each state shall be entitled to all privileges and immunities of citizens in the several states, it follows that the defendant might lawfully sell, or offer or expose for sale, within the district described in the indictment, any goods which the permanent residents of the state might sell, or offer or expose for sale, in that district, without being subjected to any higher tax or excise than that exacted by law of such permanent residents.”

With respect to ordinances declaring structures or other matter or thing to be a nuisance, it was said by the supreme court of the United States in Yates v. Milwaukee, 10 Wall. 505: “The mere declaration by a city council that a certain structure was an encroachment or obstruction did not make it so; nor could such declaration make it a nuisance unless it in fact had that character. It is a doctrine not to be tolerated in this country that a municipal corporation, without any general laws either of the city or the state, within which a given structure can be shown to be a nuisance, can, by its mere declaration that it is one, subject it to removal by any person supposed to be aggrieved, or even by the city itself. This would place every house, every business, and all the property of the city, at the uncontrolled will of the temporary local authorities.” It was held on habeas corpus that an ordinance was void, under the fourteenth amendment to the national constitution, on the ground that as a condition of obtaining a license the party de-

<br>

### --- Page 0624 ---

§ 322a.] HABEAS CORPUS. 475

Siring to carry on the business of laundry must obtain the consent of the board of supervisors, which could only be granted upon the recommendation of not less than twelve citizens and taxpayers in the block in which the laundry was to be carried on. It was held that licenses could not be required as a means of prohibiting any avocations of life which are not injurious to public morals, nor offensive to the senses, nor dangerous to the public health or safety, nor could conditions be attached to their issue which would tend to such prohibition. The ordinance was thereafter amended by striking out the clause requiring the consent of twelve resident taxpayers of the block, and again it came before the same court in a similar manner. The court did not recede from its former opinion, but as the ordinance had been before the supreme court of the state, which held it valid, the federal court, in deference to the opinion of such state court, would not overrule it, but adopted a course of policy. It was stated: “This court has no appellate power over the courts of the state, and the writ of habeas corpus cannot be used to perform the functions of a writ of error. Upon the question of habeas corpus there is only a concurrent jurisdiction with the supreme court of the state.” While it was announced that by statute it was made the duty of the federal court to investigate the particular matter, yet they did not conclude that it thus became their duty to override the decision of the highest court of the state under the peculiar circumstances. The statute having given a right of appeal from their decision, and a writ of error to the state court would lie from the supreme court of the United States, they would let that mode be pursued.

In New York v. Eno, supra, as has been stated, the supreme court of the United States seem to advise that the better course is to let the supreme court of the state pass upon such questions, and then bring the matter before the federal supreme court by writ of error directed to that court. In some jurisdictions, however, it has been held that the constitutionality of a law or ordinance cannot be tested on habeas corpus. In so far as the question relates to the provisions of the constitu-

1 Laundry Ordinance Case, 13 Fed. Rep. 229. See also Mayor of Baltimore v. Radecke, 49 Md. 217.
2 In re Wo Lee, 26 Fed. Rep. 471.

<br>

### --- Page 0625 ---

476 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 322a.

tion of the state, the decision of the court of that particular state is binding; but if the particular law or ordinance is violative of provisions of the federal constitution, they are of no force. In Plutt v. Harrison, 6 Iowa, 79, the constitutionality of the ordinance was not involved, but rather the question was the power and authority of the city, under its charter, to enact the ordinance. If there existed such power, then, as I understand, no question was involved respecting its constitutionality. The court were of the opinion, and so decided, that the question of the authority of the common council in the premises was one which it had the power and which it was its duty to decide, and hence was within its jurisdiction. In the Matter of Underwood, 30 Mich. 502, the case was somewhat different. There no question was involved as to the power to enact the law, if in fact the exercise of such power was not violative of some constitutional provision. The court, however, did not recognize any distinction, but simply held that the question could only be reviewed upon writ of error.

That the validity of legislative acts under which indictments are found is a proper subject of inquiry was emphatically stated in another case by the supreme court of the United States. They state that such question lies at the foundation of the whole proceedings. An unconstitutional law is void, and is no law; an offense created by it is not a crime; a conviction under it is not merely erroneous, but is illegal and void, and cannot be a legal cause of imprisonment. It is true, if no writ of error lies, the judgment may be final in the sense that there may be no means of reversing it. But personal liberty is of so great moment in the eyes of the law that the judgment of an inferior court affecting it is not deemed so conclusive but that the question of the court’s authority to try and imprison the party may be reviewed on habeas corpus by a superior court or judge having authority to issue the writ. This is not only the doctrine of the federal courts, but that recognized and declared by the courts of many states as well. The federal courts will

¹ In Matter of Underwood, 30 Mich. 502; In Matter of Harris, 47 Mo. 164; Ex parte Siebold, 100 U. S. 371.
² Darrah v. Westerlage, 44 Tex. 388; Ex parte Boenninghausen, 91 Mo. 301; Platt v. Harrison, 6 Iowa, 79.
³ Cropper’s Case, 2 Rob. (Va.) 842; In re O'Connor, 6 Wis. 288; Ex parte

<br>

### --- Page 0626 ---

§ 322a.] HABEAS CORPUS. 477

thus inquire upon habeas corpus, though the prisoner may be held under state process, if in violation of the federal constitution.

The jurisdiction of the courts has been exercised where the prisoner was held for violation of a state law which was invalid by reason of an unlawful attempt to interfere with the regulation of interstate commerce; and also where the state law provided for the licensing of drummers, and attached penalties for violation thereof.

Where an ordinance making it an offense for persons who carry on the business of laundrying to wash clothes within the habitable portions of a city was violated, and persons imprisoned as a punishment therefor, such an ordinance was held to be unconstitutional and in violation of the fourteenth amendment to the constitution. It was condemned by the court in very emphatic terms. It was said: “No decision of any court in this state (California) or elsewhere has been brought to the notice of the court holding an ordinance so sweeping and exclusive in its provisions to be valid, since or even before the adoption of the fourteenth amendment to the national constitution. This ordinance does not regulate, it extinguishes. It absolutely destroys, at its chosen location, an established ordinary business, harmless in itself and indispensable to the comfort of civilized communities, and which cannot be so conveniently, advantageously or profitably carried on elsewhere. . . . Indeed, if this ordinance be valid, it is difficult to perceive what right the people of California have which a municipal corporation is bound to respect. Of course no one can doubt the purpose of this ordinance. It means, ‘the Chinese must go;’ and, in order that they shall go, it is made to encroach upon one of the most sacred rights of citizens of the state of California — of the Caucasian race as well as upon the rights of the Mongolian. It should be remembered that

Rosenblat (Nev.), 14 Pac. Rep. 298; 1 Ex parte Royall, 117 U.S. 241,  
Brown v. Dufties, 66 Iowa, 193; Whit—? Ex parte Rollins, 80 Va. 314  
combs’s Case, 120 Mass. 118; Ex parte 3 Ex parte Rosenblatt, 19 Nev. 439.  
Burnett, 30 Ala. 461; In re Payson, 4 In re Tie Loy, 26 Fed. Rep. 611.  
23 Kan. 757; Ex parte Delaney, 43 al. 478; Ex parte Rollins, 80 Va. 314,

<br>

### --- Page 0627 ---

475 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§§ 323, 324.

the same clause of our constitution which protects the rights of every native citizen of the United States, born of Caucasian parents, equally protects the rights of the Chinese inhabitant who is lawfully in the country. When this barrier is broken down as to the Chinese, it is equally swept away as to every American citizen, and, in this instance, the ordinance reaches American citizens as well as Chinese residents. . . . This occupation is not a nuisance per se, nor one that is prima facie a nuisance, like a slaughter-house, a house for the manufacture or storage of gunpowder or dynamite. It can only become a nuisance by gross carelessness, or gross imperfections in the arrangements and appliances by means of which it is carried on. . . . The right to labor in this or any other honest, necessary, and, in itself, harmless calling, where it can be the most conveniently, advantageously and profitably carried on, without injury to others, is one of the highest privileges and immunities secured by the constitution to every American citizen and to every person residing within its protection.”

Sec. 323. Sentence without indictment.— A person sentenced to imprisonment for an infamous crime in the federal courts without having been presented or indicted by a grand jury, as required by the fifth amendment of the constitution, is entitled to be discharged upon habeas corpus. Yet, notwithstanding such provision of the federal constitution, the supreme court of the United States, as well as the courts of last resort of several of the states, have held that it is within the power of the states to provide punishment of all crimes without indictment by a grand jury.

Sec. 324. Amendment of indictments or informations.— It has been held by the United States supreme court and some others that an indictment cannot be changed by order of the court. “It is of no avail,” say the supreme court of the United States, “under such circumstances, to say that the court still has jurisdiction of the person and of the crime; for though it has

1 Ex parte Wilson, 114 U. S. 417; Ex parte Bain, 121 U.S. 1.
2 Ex parte Bain, 121 U. S. 1; Hurado v. People, 110 U.S. 516; Rowan v. State, 30 Wis. 129; Kallock v. Superior Court, 56 Cal. 229.

<br>

### --- Page 0628 ---

§§ 325, 326.] HABEAS CORPUS. 479

possession of the person, and would have jurisdiction of the crime if it were properly presented by indictment, the jurisdiction of the offense is gone, and the court has no right to proceed further in the progress of the case for want of indictment. If there is nothing before the court which the prisoner, in the language of the constitution, can be held to answer, he is then entitled to be discharged so far as the offense originally presented to the court by the indictment is concerned. The power of the court to proceed to try the prisoner is as much arrested as if the indictment had been dismissed or a nolle prosequi had been entered. There was nothing before the court on which it could hear evidence or pronounce sentence. Yet amendments to an information, if there be not a substantial change in the offense charged, is provided for by state statutes in many states, and the validity of such statutes sustained by the courts.

Sec. 325. Grand jury—Competency of, not open to inquiry.—The court, however, cannot go behind the indictment and inquire into the competency of a grand jury, or illegality in the manner in which they were summoned, or the validity of the law which authorized their being impaneled. It is always sufficient, upon habeas corpus, that the body acted under color of lawful authority. It would put an end to all judicial proceedings if the legal title and qualifications of all judicial officers could be contested in collateral proceedings at the instance of aggrieved parties.

Sec. 326. Jurisdictional questions must be determined from the record.—Where the inquiry is upon a writ of habeas corpus issued after judgment, the question of jurisdiction can only be determined from the record. The inquiry is necessarily, in every case, whether the process is void, and the officer or the court having jurisdiction of the writ must pass upon it. It therefore is no justification for the detention of the prisoner.

1 Ex parte Bain, 121 U.S. 1; Commonwealth v. Drew, 3 Hawks, 184 (N.C.); People v. Campbell, 4 Parker's Cr. Cases, 386; State ex rel. Dunn v. Noyes, 87 Wis. 340.

2 Carpenter v. People, 64 N.Y. 483; People v. Jewett, 3 Wend. 314; Cox v. People, 80 N.Y. 500; People v. Petrea, 92 N.Y. 128; In re Gannon, 69 Cal. 541; Ex parte Haymont, 91 Cal. 545; State v. Belvel, 56 N.W. Rep. 545 (Iowa); State ex rel. Dunn v. Noyes, 87 Wis. 34.

<br>

### --- Page 0629 ---

480 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 397.

that the process is good in form. The court will determine from the whole record whether the judgment was warranted by law and within the jurisdiction of the court. The record imparts a verity and cannot be impeached by extrinsic evidence. Hence, if there is a question raised as to whether the crime charged was committed within the territorial limits of the jurisdiction of the court, and that question is submitted to the jury, who find that it was so committed within such jurisdiction, their finding cannot be impeached by showing that the crime was committed without such jurisdiction.

Sec. 327. Record must show jurisdiction.—And of course every matter that was actually involved in the issue tried and embraced in the decision of the court and the finding of the jury is conclusive upon habeas corpus. In fact it is quite difficult to imagine a necessity for resort to extrinsic evidence where there has been a conviction. It is sometimes said that in courts of general jurisdiction, though the record fails to show jurisdiction of the person and subject-matter, their jurisdiction will be presumed where the imprisonment is, under process, valid upon its face, and the prisoner must assume the burden of proving its invalidity by showing a want of jurisdiction. If this broad assertion is true, it extends no farther than to an impeachment by the record, and if the record fails to disclose jurisdiction, or discloses an excess of jurisdiction, the prisoner will be discharged. The presumptions that apply to such courts in civil suits will not be indulged in criminal cases, but the record of such a court must affirmatively show not only jurisdiction, but a trial according to established constitutional methods. Hence, it has often been held that no presumption would be indulged that the prisoner was present during the trial where the record fails to show it; and again, that it is essential that the record should show that the prisoner was present and was asked before sentence whether

People ex rel. Tweed v. Liscomb, 60 N. Y. 559; Ex parte Lange, 18 Wall. 163; Deckard v. State, 38 Md. 186; State v. Fenderson, 28 La. 82; Ex parte Phillips, 57 Miss. 357; French v. State, 85 Wis. 400, and cases cited.

49 Ency. of Law, 224, note.

<br>

### --- Page 0630 ---

§ 328.] HABEAS CORPUS. 481

he had anything to say why sentence should not be pronounced against him.

Note To Sec. 327.— In England an omission to state that the defendant was asked if he had any legal cause to show against the judgment would be fatal on a writ of error. 1 Chit. Crim. Law. 701. In the United States this question has been decided in various ways, some courts holding that such an omission is error merely, and others that it is not error, even in capital cases. Grady v. State, 11 Ga. 253; Sarah v. State, 28 Ga. 576; West v. State, 2 Zab. 212; State v. Ball, 27 Mo. 324; Hamilton v. Commonwealth, 4 Harris (Pa.), 129; Safford v. People, 1 Park. Cr. 474; Dyson v. State, 26 Miss. 362; People v. Stuart, 4 Cal. 218. It was stated by the California court that they had been unable to find a single case in England or America where such an omission had been held sufficient to render the judgment absolutely void. Ex parte Gibson, 31 Cal. 619.

It was held, however, with respect to a proceeding in an inferior court, where a person was held on a mittimus issued by a justice of the peace on a conviction of vagrancy, where the jurisdiction of the justice was denied, that it was competent to show that the affidavit set out in the record and purporting to be that on which the warrant issued for petitioner, and on which he was tried, was in fact not filed with the justice until after the trial and imprisonment of petitioner; that the exclusion of such evidence was reversible error. And again, where a return set forth a commitment under a conviction before a court of special sessions, it was held that the relator might impeach it by showing that only two justices were in fact present, and that the proceedings were therefore coram non judice.

Sec. 328. Final judgments of inferior courts—Presumptions.—The courts, in reviewing upon habeas corpus the proceedings of inferior courts, often use the expression that, being inferior courts, whose powers are conferred and whose duties and mode of procedure are prescribed by statute, the evidence of their proceedings must affirmatively show jurisdiction of the person of the defendant and over the subject-matter. This, as we have seen, is the established rule in such courts with respect to civil actions or proceedings, and it is true with

1 Ball v. United States, 140 U. S. 3; People v. Devine, 5 Park. 62; People v. Devine, 21 How. Pr. 80.  
2 Smith v. Clausmeier (Ind.), 35 N. E. Rep. 904.  
3 1

<br>

### --- Page 0631 ---

482 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§ 329.

respect to criminal trials and proceedings. But the use of the expression would naturally seem to imply that there was a substantial difference in this respect between criminal proceedings in such a court and like proceedings in a court of general jurisdiction. I do not think any such distinction prevails. On the preceding page was stated the rule in respect to proceedings in such superior courts, and that the presumptions which attached to their proceedings in civil actions did not apply in criminal actions. It was said by the California court (Ex parte Gibson, 31 Cal. 620, 626); “What the entry which contains the judgment against a defendant in a criminal action ought regularly and properly to state to satisfy the requirements of the law was that all the steps or acts required by the statute which regulate the proceedings in criminal cases to be done at that stage of the case were performed. In felonies it ought to show that the defendant was present in person; that he was informed by the court, or by the clerk under its direction, of the nature of the indictment, of his plea, and if his plea was ‘not guilty,’ of the nature of the verdict; and that he was asked whether he had any legal cause to show why judgment should not be pronounced against him. If cause be shown, the entry should show what it was and what disposition was made of it; and lastly, the punishment or sentence imposed by the court, which should be stated with sufficient certainty to enable the officer to execute it.” It is true these several matters are required by statute, but it is but a re-enactment of the common law. It was further said by the court: “It does not follow that the omission of some of them, or any of them, will render the judgment erroneous, much less void.” The court was speaking with reference to what the clerk should embrace in his entry of judgment of conviction. If it should be inferred that some of these matters, not appearing in the record, would not constitute reversible error, this conclusion would evidently be a mistaken one. That the judgment might not be void, for want of jurisdiction, may be correct.

Sec. 329. Subject further discussed — Regularity of proceedings.— Of course, in so far as the record of the proceedings is concerned, as to manner of proceeding, the calling of the

<br>

### --- Page 0632 ---

§ 330.] HABEAS CORPUS. 483

case, adjournments and the like, the same requirements, in order to acquire or retain jurisdiction, must be kept as in civil actions; and to this extent, perhaps, it may be said that presumptions of regularity in the proceedings of the superior court will be indulged in that do not apply to inferior courts, and that the failure to make of record certain steps in the proceedings in inferior courts will deprive them of jurisdiction, while such omission in superior courts will not affect the question of their jurisdiction. It is not of presumptions of this class that we referred to at the beginning of the discussion of this branch of our subject, but rather that class of presumptions which relate to proceedings in criminal cases, not to the conduct of the court. The only real difference is that matters which do not constitute a want of jurisdiction in the one case may in the other. The principle is the same in both; that is, that after judgment in criminal cases only jurisdictional questions will be determined. Thus, it is held in Wisconsin that a criminal trial in a justice’s court can lawfully be adjourned only for cause, and a failure of a record of conviction which shows such an adjournment to show that it was for cause is fatal to the jurisdiction of the justice and of the circuit court on appeal. Again, it was held that the record of conviction of a criminal offense in a justice’s court must show that he had jurisdiction; and therefore, if it shows that more than one day intervened between the return and the conviction, it must show a continuance for cause, and perhaps the cause therefor.

§ 330. Erroneous sentence.— With respect to erroneous sentences, we find the same rule applied to those rendered by inferior courts as is applied to those given by superior courts, to wit: That in general they are not void, but merely voidable. It would be strange indeed if the omission to comply with the positive provisions of law, in a criminal proceeding, should in a superior court be held error merely, and the failure to comply with the same provisions in a similar proceeding in

1 Ex parte Sam, 51 Ala. 34; Matter of O'Connor, 6 Wis. 288; Hepler v. State, 43 Wis. 479; Ex parte Jackson v. Boyd, 53 Iowa, 536; Kearney, 55 Cal.; People v. Markham, 7 Cal. 208; Harrington v. State, 50 Wis. 63; Sennott’s Case, 146 Mass. 489.

<br>

### --- Page 0633 ---

484 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 830.

an inferior court should deprive the latter of jurisdiction.  
The Massachusetts court, conceding that there has been diversity of opinion among different courts as to sentences which are not authorized by law, state: “The better rule seems to be that when a court has jurisdiction of the person and of the offense, the imposition by mistake of a sentence in excess of what the law permits is within the jurisdiction, and does not render the sentence void, but only voidable, by proceedings upon a writ of error.”

Sennott’s Case, 146 Mass. 489,

CHAPTER 19.
