Sec. 331. The rule where writ is applied for or issued prior to judgment.—In most of the states the rule is the same whether the writ is applied for or issues prior to the judgment.  

<br>

### --- Page 0635 ---

486 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 332.

or subsequent thereto, with respect to the extent of the inquiry. The writ in either case cannot be resorted to for the purpose of reviewing and correcting orders or judgments which are erroneous merely. It deals with more radical defects which ‘go to the jurisdiction of the court or officer, and which render the proceeding or judgment void.’ This doctrine was expressed by courts where the statute required the courts to discharge the prisoner if upon full hearing it should appear that law and justice should require it.

Sec. 332. Sufficiency of indictment or information.—Hence a prisoner will not be discharged upon habeas corpus on the ground that the indictment or information is insufficient to charge him with an offense, and that the court erred in refusing to quash it for that reason. The reasoning of the courts is that the sufficiency of the indictment, even with respect to charging a criminal offense, is committed to the judgment of the trial court, and its decision, if erroneous, does not affect its jurisdiction to decide. The supreme court of California, while it recognizes the general rule stated, makes an apparent exception to it where the warrant or indictment does not charge any crime or offense, and holds that in such a case the prisoner may be discharged on habeas corpus. The reasoning is that a court, whether superior or inferior, derives its jurisdiction from the law, and its criminal jurisdiction extends only to such matters as the law declares to be criminal. The supreme court of Wisconsin, in In re Semler, 41 Wis. 517, after stating the rule substantially as here given, and quoting in its support several cases, proceeds to state: “Now the inquiry is, in the light of these adjudications, Did the circuit court act without jurisdiction or in excess of its jurisdiction in the matter complained of, or did it make merely a wrong decision? There can be no

1 Petition of Semler, 41 Wis. 517; 2 Ex parte Tracy, 25 Vt. 93; In re Petition of Crandall, 34 Wis. 177; Ex parte Hosley, 22 Vt. 363; Zelle v. McHenry, 35 Cal. 97; In re Milburn, 59 Wis. 24; Selz v. Presburger, 49 N. J. L. 296; Ex parte Davis, 17 Neb. 436; Jackson v. Boyd, 53 Iowa, 536; Zelle v. McHenry, 51 Iowa, 572; Ex parte Tracy, 25 Vt. 93; In re Hosley, 22 Vt. 363.

<br>

### --- Page 0636 ---

88 333, 334.] HABEAS CORPUS. 431

Doubt that the circuit court had jurisdiction of the person of the petitioner and of the offense charged in the information. But it is claimed that the first and second counts in the information charged no offense; in other words, that the information is insufficient, and that the motion to quash, for that reason, should be sustained. This may be at once conceded; but what follows? Manifestly this: that the circuit court gave a wrong decision, where it clearly had jurisdiction, in holding a defective information good. The court committed an error, but there is no ground for saying it acted without jurisdiction in rendering its decision. If a demurrer had been filed to the information and overruled by the court, precisely the same question would have been presented. It is a case of error for which the petitioner can only have relief on writ of error or some other appropriate process of review. He cannot have relief on a writ of habeas corpus without making such writ perform all the office of a writ of error. This seems very obvious. Nor does the fact that this court, under the constitution, has appellate jurisdiction over circuit courts, in any way affect the question before us, for this court can only exert revisory or appellate jurisdiction on proper process, proceeding according to the rules of law.

Sec. 333. Change of venue — Denial of right.—The rule has been applied to the extent that where an inferior court denied an application for a change of venue authorized by law, the writ would not lie. Yet it is held in some of the states, where the statute directs a change of venue on such an application, that upon the filing of the affidavit prescribed, the court having control of the proceedings is deprived of jurisdiction.

Sec. 334. Statute — Effect of — Guilt or innocence of the accused.—In most of the states it is provided by statute that the return of the officer or other person to whom the writ is directed may be traversed, and this may be done by a denial of the material facts set forth in the return, or by alleging facts to show that the prisoner’s imprisonment or detention is unlawful or that he is entitled to his discharge, and the court or

See Ex parte Parks, 93 U.S. 18; v. Daniel Shaw Lumber Co., 93 Wis.
Zelle v. McHenry, 51 Iowa, 572.
Taft v. Taft, 78 Wis. 635; Peterson

<br>

### --- Page 0637 ---

483 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 335.

Officer issuing the writ shall proceed in a summary manner to examine into the facts contained in the return and to hear the allegations and proofs of the parties in support of such imprisonment or detention, or against the same, whether the same shall have been upon commitment for any criminal or supposed criminal matter or not. The language of such statutes is very broad and seemingly comprehensive, and for this reason courts have been perplexed in determining the nature and extent of the inquiry that may thus be made where there is a traverse of the return. The courts, however, seem to be pretty well agreed that, upon such inquiry, the question of the guilt or innocence of the accused cannot be determined. “Nothing is better settled,” said Mr. Justice Cowen, in the famous McLeod case, “on English authority than that, on habeas corpus, the examination as to guilt or innocence cannot under any circumstances extend beyond the depositions and proofs upon which the prisoner was committed. This would be so, even on habeas corpus, before an indictment found, however loosely the charge might be expressed in the warrant of commitment.”

Sec. 335. Statute construed.—The learned judge proceeds to determine the effect of the statute, and, after quoting it, states that “under this statute the prisoner’s counsel claim the right of going behind the indictment and proving that he is not guilty, by affidavit, as he may by oral testimony before the jury. We have already seen the absurdity of such a proposition in practice and its consequent repudiation by the English criminal courts, and we are not disposed to admit its adoption by our legislature without clear words or necessary construction. We think its object entirely plain, without a resort to the rules of construction. Its words are satisfied by being limited to the lawfulness of the authority under which the prisoner is detained, without being extended to the force of the evidence upon which the authority was exerted, or which it may be in the prisoner’s power to adduce at the trial. This, if necessary, is rendered still more plain by considering

1 People v. McLeod, 25 Wend. 483, 37 Am. Dec. 328; Farmer v. Lewis, 92 Ind. 444; People v. Ruloff, 47 Am. Rep. 153, 5 Park. C. C. 77; Ex parte Sprail- Ryan, 44 Cal. 555; Ex parte
2 People v. McLeod, 25 Wend. 483.

<br>

### --- Page 0638 ---

diction to commit him under such circumstances, and this case illustrates the application of the statute allowing extrinsic proof. 

In a similar vein, it is important to recognize that the principle behind habeas corpus is to ensure that no individual is unlawfully deprived of their liberty. This fundamental right serves as a safeguard against arbitrary detention and provides a mechanism for individuals to challenge their imprisonment. The evolution of habeas corpus reflects the ongoing balancing act between the state’s interest in maintaining order and the individual's right to personal freedom. As such, the courts have grappled with the nuances of what constitutes lawful imprisonment and the mechanisms available for individuals to challenge their incarceration.

The ongoing discussion about the use of extrinsic evidence in habeas corpus proceedings highlights the complexities of the law in this area. The lack of a universally applicable rule illustrates the necessity for flexible approaches tailored to the specifics of individual cases. This adaptability is crucial for aligning legal practices with the overarching goal of preventing unlawful detentions and ensuring justice is upheld. The dialogue surrounding these issues remains vital in the context of protecting civil liberties and navigating the intricacies of the judicial system.

<br>

### --- Page 0639 ---

490  SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 336.]

diction to make the order because of the illegality of the proceeding. Where a prisoner asked for his discharge upon writ of habeas corpus, being under indictment for murder, and the court decided that there could be no conviction, as the prisoner had been once put in jeopardy by a former trial, which had been arbitrarily terminated by the court on the supposition that it could not continue, as the time fixed by law for the term of court had expired, yet, notwithstanding there could be no conviction, it was held there was no error on the part of the subordinate court in refusing to discharge the prisoner.¹

Sec. 336. In Indiana and in the federal courts—Evidence examined to determine question of jurisdiction.—In Indiana, where one charged with having obtained property under false pretenses was committed by the magistrate in default of bail and applied for a writ of habeas corpus, upon the hearing thereon, it seems from the report of the case, evidence was introduced with respect to the place where the alleged crime was committed. The court below remanded the prisoner, but the supreme court reversed the judgment of the court below, upon the ground that it appeared from the evidence that the alleged crime was committed in another state. The proceedings before the magistrate were attached to the return, and the testimony of the prosecuting witness may have disclosed that the crime was committed in another jurisdiction. However, the decision is based upon the evidence adduced before the court. It would thus appear, in that state at least, that before indictment the court will determine from extrinsic evidence questions that after indictment would be held proper for the determination of the trial court in the first instance, as the place where an alleged crime is committed is charged in an indictment (as well as a warrant), and if in dispute is to be determined by the jury.²

In Re Neagle, 135 U.S. 1, it appears that upon habeas corpus issued to a state officer, who had in control a person charged with murder, the circuit court of the United States determined from extrinsic evidence that, at the time of the commission of

¹ Wright v. State, 5 Ind. 290; Stewart v. Jessup, 51 Ind. 413, 19 Am. Rep. 739.  
² Wentworth v. Alexander, 66 Ind. 39.

<br>

### --- Page 0640 ---

§ 337.] HABEAS CORPUS. 491

The act of killing the defendant acted in discharge of his duty as an officer of the United States, and that having so acted in that capacity he could not be guilty of murder under the laws of California, nor held to answer to its courts for an act for which he had the authority of the laws of the United States. The question whether the act was committed in the discharge of his duty, of necessity had to be determined from the evidence, as the mere fact that he was an officer of the United States was not conclusive upon the question. The defendant might have committed the act without provocation and with intent to murder. These questions were also involved. The section of the statute which the court held justified the ruling declares “that when by the writ of habeas corpus the petitioner is brought up for a hearing, the court or justice or judge shall proceed in a summary way to determine the facts of the case by hearing the testimony and arguments, and thereupon to dispose of the party as law and justice require.” “This, of course,” say the court, “means that if he is held in custody in violation of the constitution or a law of the United States, or for an act done or omitted in pursuance of a law of the United States, he must be discharged.” It thus appears that in the federal courts the court will determine from extrinsic evidence jurisdictional facts upon habeas corpus.

§ 337. Review of proceedings of committing magistrate. The rule seems to have been relaxed in some cases, where the writ issued to review the proceedings of the committing magistrate and prior to the finding of an indictment or the filing of an information. It would be difficult to give a satisfactory reason for this departure. Several of the states have enacted statutes, by the provisions of which a more extended inquiry into the merits of the accusation is permitted than otherwise would be allowed. Thus it has been held that the evidence upon which a person charged with crime was committed may be examined on habeas corpus for the purpose, first, of determining whether it fairly and reasonably tends to show the commission of the offense charged; second, whether it fairly and reasonably tends to make out probable cause for the charge made against the prisoner. Such, however, is the extent of the inquiry. The

<br>

### --- Page 0641 ---

492 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 338, 339.

Judgment of the magistrate, if there is competent evidence to sustain it, should not be rejudged by an inquiry into the right of the evidence.

Sec. 338. Where complaint shows want of jurisdiction, prisoner will be discharged.—A somewhat similar question was before the supreme court of Wisconsin. There it was urged that upon a traverse of the return, which also traversed the recitals of the complaint in the warrant, the court should pass upon the merits as raised by the demurrer to the traverses. It was held that this could not be done. That the only question that could be raised was that of jurisdiction. That when a prisoner is held by legal process the writ of habeas corpus does not operate, so to speak, by way of change of venue from the court or officer issuing the process of arrest to the court or officer issuing the habeas corpus. The latter writ, in such a case, raises only the question of jurisdiction of the court or officer to issue the process of arrest. It appeared from the complaint that the dam creating the alleged nuisance was maintained in a county other than where the complaint was issued and where the injury complained of was done. The court held further, that it appeared that the magistrate was without jurisdiction. This court evidently makes no departure from the general rule where the proceedings are reviewed before indictment. However, it should be stated that in that state the statute (sec. 3428) provides: “But no such court or officer on the return of any such writ (habeas corpus) shall have power to inquire into the legality of any judgment, order or execution.”

Sec. 339. Effect of statutory enactment regulating scope of inquiry.—The Iowa statute provides (sec. 3482) that in a proceeding upon habeas corpus the petitioner may deny the sufficiency of the testimony to justify the action of the committing magistrate, and that upon the trial the testimony taken before the magistrate may be given in evidence in connection

1 In re Snell, 31 Minn. 110. See also 2 In re Eldred, 46 Wis. 530. See also Jones v. Darnell, 103 Ind. 569; People v. Van Horn, 8 Barb. 163; People v. Tompkins, 1 Park. C. C.; People v. Biegler, 3 Park. C. C. 316; State v. Noyes, 87 Wis. 340, 58 N. W. Rep. 386.

<br>

### --- Page 0642 ---

[S§ 340-342.] HABEAS CORPUS. 493

with any other testimony which may there be produced. Under this statute the real question to be tried is whether the petitioner ought to be held in view of all the testimony that may be produced; and it is clear that the petitioner should be discharged in view of such testimony if it appear that he ought not to be held, even if the testimony taken simply before the magistrate showed otherwise. The fact that the prisoner waived a preliminary examination is not a waiver of his right to introduce testimony at the hearing upon a writ of Habeas corpus. But the officer holding him in custody cannot admit the allegations in the petition to be true, and thus deprive the court of its duty and power to act upon all the evidence.

Sec. 340. Courts ordinarily will not interfere pending examination.—Courts ordinarily will not interfere pending an examination before a magistrate. To deny inquiry is to hinder the discovery of such facts as lawful inquiry is alone competent to reveal. And it is not a fit exercise of jurisdiction to interpose merely because in the progress of the inquiry and before it is finished the purpose is yet unaccomplished, and it is not ascertained that any crime has been committed or that there is any ground for holding the prisoner.

Sec. 341. Federal courts will not determine whether facts proved constitute a crime.—The federal courts are fully committed to the doctrine that only jurisdictional questions are the subject of inquiry with respect to proceedings of committing magistrates; hence the writ of habeas corpus will not lie to determine whether the facts proved before a commissioner on preliminary hearing are sufficient to constitute the crime for which the prisoner has been committed. Such a question is merely one of law committed to the determination of the commissioner.

Sec. 342. Custody under civil process.—The position was early taken that the writ of Habeas corpus ought not to lie.

1 Cowell v. Patterson, 49 Iowa, 514. See also Hamilton's Case, 51 Mich. 65 Iowa, 382.
2 Same case.
3 State ex rel. Hart v. Rosecrans; Ex parte Rickett, 61 Fed. Rep. 203; United States v. Horner, 143 U.S. 570.
4 Matter of Peoples, 47 Mich. 626.

<br>

### --- Page 0643 ---

494 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 843.

where the party was in custody under civil process. The power of the court to relieve against all illegal imprisonments, either in civil or criminal cases, was conceded as one derived from the common law; but its exercise in civil cases was to a great extent discretionary, and ordinarily would not be exercised where the party had a remedy by motion or other proceeding in the original action. An exception appears to have been made where the process was not warranted by the judgment.

Sec. 343. Statutes regulating the use of the writ.— The use of the writ in such cases has in subsequent years quite generally, in many of the states, been regulated by statute, which provides (the provision may not be uniform in the different states) that if it appears that the person is in custody by virtue of civil process of any court legally constituted, or issued by any officer in the course of judicial proceedings before him authorized by law, such prisoner can only be discharged in one of the following cases:

1. Where the jurisdiction of such courts or officer has been exceeded, either as to matter, place, law or person.

2. Where, although the original imprisonment was lawful, yet by some act, omission or event, which has taken place afterward, the party has become entitled to be discharged.

3. Where the process is defective in some matter of substance required by law, rendering such process void.

4. Where the process, though in the proper form, has been issued in a case not allowed by law.

5. Where the person having the custody of the prisoner under such process is not the person empowered by law to detain him; or

6. Where the process is not authorized by any judgment or order of any court, nor by any provision of law.

This power to discharge is limited by a general provision relating to civil and criminal cases, in effect that no court or officer upon such writ shall have power to inquire into the legality or justice of any final judgment or order of any court.

1 Bank of the United States v. Jenkins, 18 Johns. 305; Cable v. Cooper, 15 Johns. 157. 
2 People v. Willett, 26 Barb. 78; People v. Kelley, 35 Barb. 44.

<br>

### --- Page 0644 ---

§§ 344-347. HABEAS CORPUS. 495  
petent court of civil or criminal jurisdiction, or of any execution issued upon such judgment or order.  

Sec. 344. Extent of the inquiry.— It is held that, where a prisoner is in custody under civil process, the only inquiry is that of the validity of the process and the jurisdiction of the court, with the exception of those matters which are embraced in the second subdivision or statement of grounds for discharge.  

Sec. 345. Contempt proceedings — Rule — The question of jurisdiction only open.— The general rule may be stated to be that, where the prisoner is held in custody by virtue of a judgment or order committing him for contempt, the only questions open to inquiry upon a writ of habeas corpus are those of jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person. The sufficiency of the cause of commitment will not be inquired into. See discussion of this question under “Contempt,” “Certiorari,” and “Prohibition.”  

Sec. 346. Adjudication is a conviction — Commitment is an execution.— When a court commits a party for contempt, their adjudication is a conviction, and their commitment in consequence is execution. The sole adjudication of contempt and the punishment thereof belongs exclusively, and without interfering, to each respective court.  

Sec. 347. Statutes — Effect of.— In some states, however, it is provided by statute that no order of commitment for any alleged contempt or upon proceedings as for contempt to enforce the rights or remedies of any party shall be deemed a judgment or order, within the provisions of the statute, which deny the right to prosecute the writ to persons who shall have been committed or are detained by virtue of the final judgment or order of any competent tribunal of civil or criminal jurisdiction, or by virtue of any execution issued upon such judgment or order.  

1 In re Ida Louisa Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.  

2 Tinsley v. Anderson, 171 U.S. 101; Ex parte Kearney, 7 Wheat. 38; Ex parte Henshaw, 73 Cal. 486; In Matter of Tyler, 64 Cal. 484; Ex parte Grace, 12 Iowa, 203; Watson v. Williams, 36 Miss. 331; In re Cooper, 32 Vt. 253; In re Blair, 4 Wis. 2; Holman v. Mayor, 34 Tex. 668; People v. 24 N. Y. 74; In re Ida Louise Pierce, 44 Wis. 411.  

3 Ex parte Kearney, 7 Wheat. 33.  

4 Same case.  

<br>

### --- Page 0645 ---

496 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 348.

extent by statutes enacted in many of the states, in effect that, upon habeas corpus, the court or officer, on the return of the writ, shall not have the power to inquire into the legality or justice of any judgment, order or execution, for any contempt specially and plainly charged in the commitment, by some court, officer or body having authority to commit for the contempt so charged. The one universal exception to the rule is that, if the court was without jurisdiction to make the judgment or order, then the prisoner will be discharged upon habeas corpus.¹

Sec. 348. Judgment distinguished from jurisdiction—Commitment for refusal to obey void order.—Here again we meet the same difficulty that attends the discussion of this subject throughout, and that is the wide difference that exists among the courts as to what is or what is not jurisdiction, as applied to particular conditions or facts. It is the doctrine of many courts that where a court undertakes, by its process of contempt, to punish a man for refusing to comply with an order which the court had no authority to make, the order itself, being without jurisdiction, is void, and the order punishing for contempt is equally void; and that where the proceeding for contempt in such a case results in imprisonment, the court will, by its writ of habeas corpus, discharge the prisoner.² Hence, it was held that an order of the circuit court of the United States, under which a marshal held the petitioners in custody, having been made in a proceeding for contempt in disobeying a writ of mandamus, which writ was in excess of the jurisdiction of the court, in that it commanded to be done that which by law the petitioners had no power to do, was for such reason a nullity, and the petitioners were entitled to be discharged upon writ of habeas corpus.³ This doctrine was further illustrated in a somewhat notable case (Ex parte Fisk, 113 U.S. 713). There in an action pending in the state court,

¹ Ex parte Rowland, 104 U.S. 604; Ex parte Fisk, 113 U.S. 713; In re Grace, 12 Iowa, 208; In re Ida Louisa Ayres, 123 U.S. 443; Dudley v. McPierce, 44 Wis. 411; Ex parte Hollis, Cord, 65 Iowa, 671; Tyley v. Connelly, 65 Cal. 28; Tinsley v. Anderson, 171 U.S. 101.
² In re Ayres, 123 U.S. 443; Ex parte Rowland, 104 U.S. 604.
³ Ex parte Rowland, 104 U.S. 604.

<br>

### --- Page 0646 ---

§ 349.] HABEAS CORPUS. 497

under a statute which authorized it, a party to the action was sought to be examined before trial. The action was removed into the federal court, and there the examination was sought to be continued, and in fact an order of the federal court required it. The party refused to submit and was punished as for contempt in so refusing. He sought release upon habeas corpus, where it was held that, so far as the practice affected proceedings in the federal courts, it was contrary to a federal statute upon the subject; that the circuit court had no authority to make the order; that for that reason it was void, and being void there could be no contempt in disobeying or in refusing to comply with it. Where a judgment for divorce awarded the custody of a child to the father, and thereafter the mother abducted the child and removed it from the jurisdiction of the court, and the court ordered the mother to restore the child to the custody of the father and pay forthwith a fine of $1, and to stand committed to the county jail until she should fully comply with the order, it was held that the order was in excess of the jurisdiction of the court, and the warrant of commitment issued in a case not allowed by law, and that relief would be granted upon writ of habeas corpus; that the act of the mother in disobeying the order was a criminal, not a civil, contempt, and the punishment could not be made continuing, but only as for the criminal act. The rule extends to a case where the prisoner is committed for refusing to give evidence in a case over which the court has no jurisdiction; yet it was held in other cases that whether the question which the petitioner refused to answer was a proper one, or whether he was privileged from answering it, could not be the subject of inquiry.

Sec. 349. Whether commitment voidable for error, or facts insufficient, not subject of inquiry.— But generally, where there is jurisdiction, it is of no consequence how erroneous the judgment or order of the court may be, if not absolutely void, it cannot be attacked on habeas corpus by a party who has been committed for failure to comply with it. Whether the facts

1 In re Ida Louisa Pierce, 44 Wis. 3 People v. Fancher, 2 Hun, 226.
All, 4 Ex parte Grace, 12 Iowa, 208; Ex

2 In Matter of Morton, 10 Mich. 208. parte Cottrell, 79 Am. Dec. 529; Ex

<br>

### --- Page 0647 ---

498 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 350.

justified punishment as for contempt for acts in the presence of the court cannot be inquired into on such a writ; nor the sufficiency of the affidavits in supplementary proceedings; nor whether items of costs and expenses which the party is required to pay, imposed as a fine, are proper items to be included?

Sec. 350. Whether the matter charged is a contempt—Extent of inquiry.—Whether the court will determine that the matter alleged is or is not a contempt upon such a writ is a question upon which the courts are not agreed. In speaking of the exercise of the power of courts to punish for contempt, Chief Justice Kent, in Yates v. Lansing, 5 John. 282, very forcibly and accurately states the law as follows: “It is sufficient that the court which commits has jurisdiction of the cause of commitment; and as the cause in the present case was an alleged malpractice and contempt, the court of chancery most undoubtedly had jurisdiction over the subject-matter. It is decisive of the point that the court considered the act of which it complained to be a contempt and malpractice, by being an unauthorized interference with the practice of the court. Every court judges exclusively for itself of its own contempts; no other court, much less a single judge out of court, can undertake to judge on the question. The plaintiff was recommitted, to use the language of the order, ‘for contempt and malpractice,’ and whether the court of chancery was right or wrong in considering that the plaintiff’s conduct amounted to a contempt, and whether it took the proper steps to ascertain the contempt, is perfectly immaterial as to the point of jurisdiction. It had authority to punish contempts. It must judge what are contempts.

... Judicial exercise of power is imposed upon the courts. They must decide and act according to their judgment, and therefore the law will protect them. The chancellor, in the case of the plaintiff, was bound in duty to imprison and reimprison him if he considered his conduct as amounting to a contempt of his court. The obligations of his office left him no volition. He was as much bound to punish a contempt...

comparte Cohn, 55 Cal. 198; Matter of Ex parte Smithurst, 4 How. Pr. Bissell, 40 Mich. 63. 369.

In re Terry, 128 U. S. 289; People v. Jacobs, 66 N. Y. & People v. Kelley, 24 N. Y. 74.

<br>

### --- Page 0648 ---

§§ 351, 352.] HABEAS CORPUS. 499

mitted in his court as he was bound in any other case to exercise his power. He may possibly have erred in judgment in calling an act a contempt which did not amount to one, and in regarding a discharge as null when it was binding. This court may have erred in the same way; still it was but error of judgment, for which neither the chancellor nor the judges of this court are or can be responsible in a civil suit.

Sec. 351. Views of the New York court.—In another and more recent case the facts were that a person was committed for contempt for disobedience of an order made in a proceeding therein, which commitment was reversed by the court of appeals as being error. Thereafter an action was brought against the attorneys for the defendant to recover damages for false imprisonment. The simple question presented in the first proceeding was whether such disobedience of the order of the court “defeated, impaired or prejudiced a right or remedy of the defendants.” It was said by the court: “A simple question of law was thus presented to the court, as to whether all the elements constituting the offense of contempt appeared on the application of commitment. Whether they did or did not, in no sense constituted a jurisdictional question. The court concededly had jurisdiction of the parties and the subject-matter of the application, and we think authority to determine whether the facts of the case brought it within the statutory definition of a contempt. An erroneous decision of that question in no sense affected the jurisdiction of the court over the subject-matter of the application. The disobedience of its order gave the court jurisdiction of the subject-matter, and called upon it to determine whether a contempt had been committed or not. The right to adjudicate upon this question did not depend upon the fact whether the plaintiff was guilty of a contempt, but whether a case had been made calling for an adjudication upon that question.” 

Sec. 352. Views of the Wisconsin court.—In a recent case in another state, the relator obtained an order from the supreme court directed to the sheriff of a county, to show cause why a

1 Fischer v. Langbien, 103 N. Y. 84

<br>

### --- Page 0649 ---

500 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 353, 354.]

Writ of habeas corpus should not issue to inquire into the legality of the custody wherein he held the petitioner. On the hearing of the motion it appeared that such sheriff held him in custody by virtue of an order of commitment for contempt made by the circuit court. It was held by the supreme court that, on habeas corpus, only jurisdictional questions will be inquired into. It was said there is no question but that the court had jurisdiction of the cause of action and the parties. If then it had power to make the order of commitment in any supposable circumstances which might arise in the progress of the cause, then the order is valid until reversed, however erroneous it may be in the particular circumstances.

Sec. 353. Subject discussed.— So long as the courts announce the rule to be that habeas corpus will only issue to inquire into jurisdictional defects, it follows that the sufficiency of the application for an order of commitment for contempt cannot be determined upon the application for or the hearing upon the return of the writ of habeas corpus. The courts, as we have seen, are practically a unit upon the proposition that upon such a proceeding the sufficiency of an indictment as to charging an offense cannot be the subject of inquiry. For similar reasons and upon similar grounds, the averments in the preliminary proceedings charging or attempting to charge an alleged contempt cannot be reviewed for the purpose of determining whether or not matter constituting a contempt is actually charged. It is within the power, and by law is made the duty, of the court before whom the proceedings are pending to determine the question, and whether that determination be right or wrong does not in any manner affect the jurisdiction or power of the court to decide.

Sec. 354. Views of the California court.— There are courts, however, which assume to determine the question of the sufficiency of the application upon habeas corpus. It is, however, but an arbitrary exercise of power, and not justified by the rules or reasons by which the question of jurisdiction is tested in other cases. The California court is one among others which

1 In re Rosenburg, 90 Wis. 681

<br>

### --- Page 0650 ---

§§ 355, 356.] HABEAS CORPUS. 501

contends that the question whether the matter alleged is a contempt or not is reviewable upon habeas corpus, but the ruling of the court is placed upon the provisions of a statute which the court claims authorized a review of the proceedings, not only with respect to jurisdictional questions, but also as to matters of law which it may be alleged were erroneously charged. It would seem that in most cases in the court where such ruling has been made it was predicated upon the fact that the court had no authority to make the order the disobedience of which was the basis of the commitment for contempt.

Sec. 355. Views of the Iowa court.—The Iowa court is often cited as authority in favor of the proposition above stated, but the rule as lately declared in that state is to the effect that upon habeas corpus the court cannot review the order of imprisonment for contempt and reverse it, unless the act constituting the alleged contempt was such that the court could pronounce as matter of law that it was not a contempt.

Sec. 356. Federal circuit and district courts.—There appears a statement in the opinion of the supreme court of the United States (In re Cuddy, 131 U.S. 280, 286), which, unless obiter, would seem to indicate that upon habeas corpus the petitioner may show, so long as it does not contradict the record, that the misbehavior with which he was charged and for which he was committed was not such as, under the statute (the power in the district court of the United States to punish for contempt being limited by statute), made him liable to fine and imprisonment. The contention there was that it did not appear by the record that the misconduct charged occurred in the presence of the court; that there was a want of jurisdiction, such court being a court of limited jurisdiction. It was held, however, that the rule of presumption, as to the jurisdiction of such courts, had no application where their judgments were attacked collaterally, and therefore, unless want of jurisdiction appears, it would be presumed that jurisdiction was possessed. The record not disclosing that the alleged contempt

1 Ex parte Rowe, 7 Cal. 181.
2 Brown v. Moore, 61 Cal. 432; Williams v. Dwinnelle, 51 Cal. 442.
3 State ex rel. Whitcomb v. Seaton, 61 Iowa, 563.

<br>

### --- Page 0651 ---

502 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§ 3562.

was committed in the presence of the court, or at such place or in such manner as to come within the power of the court, that fact would be presumed, unless the petitioner alleged facts showing otherwise, which he might do, and, upon proving such facts, would be entitled to be discharged. If the record had disclosed that the misconduct was within the power conferred, evidently proof would not be received to contradict the record. What the rule might have been declared to be in case the proceeding was had in a court of general jurisdiction, the record being silent, of course we cannot anticipate. (See discussion of subject under title “Prohibition.”) In the more recent case of Tinsley v. Anderson, 171 U. S. 101, it is distinctly stated that “if the committing court had jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person, and power to make the order for disobedience to which the judgment in contempt was rendered, and to render that judgment, then the court could not do otherwise than discharge the writ upon habeas corpus and remand the petitioner. The writ cannot be availed of as a writ of error or an appeal, and if the commitment was not void petitioner was not deprived of his liberty without due process of law. The general rule, in substance, that the writ only lies to reach jurisdictional defects, applies here in contempt proceedings as well as in criminal proceedings. The only question is, to determine whether the defect is of that character as to be beyond the power or jurisdiction of the court. We have seen what some courts classify as error merely, others hold to be jurisdictional. In fact the only difference in the courts is the distinction they make between judgment and jurisdiction in particular cases.”

Sec. 356a. View of the Michigan court.— It is stated by the supreme court of Michigan that under article 6, section 3, of the constitution of that state, and a statute of that state, such court has authority to inquire into the justice or propriety of proceedings in contempt, whether before or after commitment, only so far as to ascertain whether the court or officer has jurisdiction and is proceeding according to law. If the court or officer has jurisdiction and is proceeding according to law, we can go no farther. Commitments for contempts cannot be impeached for mere irregularity. The attack upon a

<br>

### --- Page 0652 ---

§ 357.] HABEAS CORPUS. 503

Judgment for contempt can go no farther than the power of the court to act in a given case. But the question of jurisdiction necessarily involves an inquiry whether the conduct alleged was in fact a contempt of the court committed under circumstances which authorize the court to proceed to punishment therefor; and it also involves the question whether the court is proceeding in the manner prescribed by law. The inquiry is thus naturally suggested, where in that state is the distinction between jurisdiction and judgment—what error can the court commit that is not jurisdictional? A wrong decision as to the effect of misconduct, or as to disobedience of an order, deprives the court of jurisdiction, for it has, undoubtedly, jurisdiction to determine that question, where it depends upon particular acts or facts. So proceeding in an irregular manner—that is, not observing in procedure the statutory or other requirements—deprives the court of jurisdiction, for it has, undoubtedly, jurisdiction to proceed in some manner. If the court intends to apply the rule that is applicable to a court when proceeding out of the course of the common law, or that of its general jurisdiction, and to so apply as to proceedings subsequent to the acquiring of jurisdiction, its conclusion may not be incorrect. It cannot be defended, however, on any other ground, and even then the judgment is not subject to collateral attack by habeas corpus, but upon direct attack by certiorari.

Sec. 357. Admitting prisoner to bail—Use of writ.— Where a person has been committed charged with the commission of a crime, if the offense be bailable the order to admit him to bail may be procured by habeas corpus. By the use of such means he may be brought before the court or judicial officer having authority in the premises, where the evidence and the proceedings upon which his commitment was ordered may be examined, and the amount and sufficiency of the bond will be determined by such court or officer, due regard being had to the character of the crime charged and the attendant circumstances, so far as they appear from the evidence.

1 In re Wood, 82 Mich. 75. Rep. 144; People v. Tinder, 19 Cal.

2 Ex parte Bridewell, 57 Miss. 89; 539. In re Madison, 36 Kan. 725, 14 Pac.

<br>

### --- Page 0653 ---

504 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 357a.]

Sec. 357a. Discretion of the court in capital cases.—In most of the states it is the constitutional right of one charged with crime to be admitted to bail prior to conviction, except in capital cases. With respect to capital cases the provision generally is, “except for capital offenses where the proof is evident or the presumption great.” In some states the right to bail for murder or treason is expressly prohibited by their constitution. In others it is held that there is a want of power to admit to bail where the proof is evident and the presumption great; while in still others it is held that a defendant charged with a capital crime may be admitted to bail, in the discretion of the court, even where the proof is evident and the presumption great. Where, in such cases, the proof is not evident or the presumption great, it is held that the right of admission to bail is a constitutional right. The greatest difficulty is in determining the question of evident proof and great presumption. The very statement of the proposition recognizes that it involves a matter of discretion. The judicial power of the court must be invoked, and it must determine as matter of law and fact the weight of the proof or the presumption. It is stated that an indictment of itself furnishes a presumption of the guilt of the defendant too great to entitle him to bail as a matter of right under the constitution, or as a matter of discretion under the legislation of the state. That it creates a presumption of guilt for all purposes, except on trial before a petit jury. One court which pronounced this rule reasoned that if it did not create this presumption the defendant held under it or by virtue of the warrant based upon it, without other evidence of his guilt, would be entitled to a discharge absolutely. If it furnished no such presumption, it would not justify the exaction of bail or the detention of the defendant. This view is sustained by the decisions of other states, some declaring that an indictment furnishes the very strongest possible presumption of guilt, and the finding of a grand jury of a true bill is conclusive so far as to put the defendant upon his trial and control all the intermediate proceedings.

1 Comm. v. Kieper, 2 Ashm. 227; People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539; Ex parte Hay, 23 Tex. App. 585.

2 Ex parte Bridewell, 57 Miss. 39. 4 People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539.

3 In re Secrest (Kan.), 14 Pac. Rep. 5 Hight v. United States, 1 Morris,

<br>

### --- Page 0654 ---

§ 3570. HABEAS CORPUS. 505

Sec. 3570. Whether extrinsic evidence admissible.—The important question is whether extrinsic evidence can be received to overcome the force of such presumption. We find with respect to this proposition a diversity of opinion among the courts. In some it is the doctrine that the effect of the presumption is so far conclusive that it cannot be overcome or resisted by extrinsic evidence as to the guilt or innocence of the accused, unless where conditions or circumstances extraordinary in their character exist which seem to render such a proceeding necessary to the furtherance of justice. All the cases practically agree that no inquiry can be had as to the evidence taken before the grand jury, as the deliberations of that body are secret, and the law does not permit the testimony received by them to be disclosed. It is contended that the effect of allowing extrinsic evidence would in effect convert the proceeding upon habeas corpus into a trial upon the merits. In other states the doctrine is that the court will receive testimony extrinsic to the record in determining the question of evident proof and great presumption of guilt. Thus, in Mississippi the rule was stated to be that the officer upon the writ should require the production of all the evidence available for the prosecution, and should, if necessary, postpone the hearing until it can be obtained. If upon the whole testimony adduced before him he entertains a reasonable doubt whether the relator committed the act, or whether in so doing he was guilty of a capital crime, he should admit him to bail. The Indiana court contends that a proper construction of the statute of that state authorizes an examination of witnesses and a full investigation of the case. It also maintained that the indictment should not have the force of conclusive presumption, as the accused might be convicted of murder in the first or second degree or of manslaughter. Such reasoning did not

410 (Iowa); State v. Mills, 2 Dev. 421 (N. C.); People v. McLeod, 1 Hill, 394; People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539.

People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539; People v. McLeod, 1 Hill, 895; People v. Huyler, 2 Park. Crim. Rep. 571; State v. Mills, 2 Dev. 420 (N. C.); Ex parte Taylor, 5 Cow. 56; United States v. Hill, 3 Jones, 3 Wash. 224.

Comm. v. Rutherford, 5 Rand. 646 (Va); Lumm v. State, 8 Port. 293 (Ind.); Ex parte Halpine, 30 Ind. 254; Street v. State, 43 Miss. 1; State v. Brev. 89 (S. C.).

Ex parte Bridewell, 57 Miss. 39.

Lumm v. State, 3 Ind. 204.

<br>

### --- Page 0655 ---

506 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 357c.

impress itself upon the California court and they ignored it. The practice of allowing extrinsic evidence is recognized in Texas by the provisions of their statute.²

Sec. 357c. Circumstances extraordinary in character which will create an exception to the rule.— What circumstances will be deemed of a sufficient and extraordinary character to come within the exception to the rule is a question that does not admit of a definite answer. Where the defendant was under indictment for a capital offense, it was held that affidavits tending to show that the prosecution was instituted from malice or mistake, and that the inveigling a slave from his master, which constituted the offense charged, was not felonious or fraudulent, but in consequence of a fair claim of property, should be received and considered on the application.³ This determination did not find favor with the California court. They state that “it is difficult to perceive how malice in the institution of the prosecution can affect the case. That the accused is not entitled to any more leniency or consideration because improper motives may have led to the exposure of his crime and to proceedings against him. Where a mistake in the institution of the prosecution is alleged, the proper course is to make a representation to the public prosecutor, who will undoubtedly consent to the reception of bail if satisfied of the truth of the representation.”⁴ Several instances are stated by the California court which are supposed to be of such a special and extraordinary character as to justify the consideration of evidence offered against the presumption of guilt created by the indictment, as follows: The existence at the time the indictment was found of great popular excitement with reference to the prisoner or the offense charged against him, likely to bias or warp the judgment of the grand jurors. It seems that such a ground has been determined to constitute an exception to the general rule stated, and the court considered the fact that while the indictment was pending against him the accused was elected a member of the legislature by the citizens of the county. The existence of the party charged to have

¹ People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539. ³ Satte v. Hill, 3 Brev. 89 (S. C.).  
² Yarbrough v. State, 2 Tex. 523. ⁴ People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539.

<br>

### --- Page 0656 ---

§ 357d.) HABEAS CORPUS. 507

been murdered will constitute an extraordinary circumstance in any case; and the allegation, under oath, of his existence in the application for bail would justify, if not require, a careful examination of the evidence offered. So too would a clear confession by another of the commission of the offense for which the defendant is indicted. The same court, though stating in the same case that extraneous evidence cannot be received unless special and extraordinary circumstances exist, also state that bail may be taken after indictment found where no such special and extraordinary circumstances exist as they have mentioned. That it may be taken upon the admission of the public prosecutor that the evidence which he can produce will not warrant a conviction of a capital offense, or upon his admission of facts from which it is evident no such conviction can take place. Other grounds have been stated by other courts, some of which were also stated by the California court. Thus, the non-existence of a secure place to confine the prisoner until the day of his trial has been held a sufficient ground for admitting a prisoner to bail. So also where the accused has been once tried and acquitted for the same offense, as well as where the alleged homicide was committed in a war between two nations. If there has been unreasonable delay in bringing the prisoner to trial, bail should not be refused even where the trial has been postponed for what appeared to be sufficient reasons, or where some event has transpired which operates to indefinitely postpone the further prosecution of the action. The rule does not extend to authorizing the letting to bail for every postponement.

Sec. 357d. Where there has been a trial and disagreement of jury.—If there has been a trial and evidence has been produced for and against the accused, and upon its consideration the jury have failed to agree, this is a strong circumstance against the presumption of guilt created by the fact of indictment found, and oftentimes is sufficient of itself to take the case without the general rule. It may not be sufficient to jus-

1 People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539.  
2 Smith v. Smith, 1 Cal. 9.  
3 Finch v. State, 15 Fla. 633; People v. McLeod, 1 Hill, 328.  
4 In re Peoples, 47 Mich. 626; Street v. State, 43 Miss. 1.  
5 Regina v. Andrews, 2 Dowl. & L. 10; Logan v. State, 3 Brev. 415 (S.C.).

<br>

### --- Page 0657 ---

5uS SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 856e, 857fh.]

tify the letting to bail as a matter of course. Where, however, there have been two disagreements, and it appears the accused voluntarily surrendered himself and refused to escape when the opportunity was present, these are strong circumstances to overcome the presumption that attaches to an indictment found. Where a jury have disagreed, the affidavits of jurors are competent evidence to show that fact; and it seems that where application has been refused before trial, a second application, where there has been a mistrial, may be entertained.

Src. 357e. Where confinement will endanger life.—It has frequently been determined that a prisoner should be admitted to bail where it is satisfactorily made to appear that continued confinement will endanger his life, or has produced, or is likely to produce, serious results; and this condition may be shown in like manner as other extraneous facts.

Src. 3577. Rule where application is made before indictment.—The rule with respect to the character of the evidence that may be received and considered, where the application is made before indictment found, is different than where the application is made after indictment. The application may be based upon the testimony taken before the committing magistrate. In such case it has been stated that “It is a safe rule to refuse bail in cases where a judge would sustain a capital conviction if pronounced by a jury on such evidence of guilt as was exhibited to him on the hearing of the application to admit to bail, and to admit to bail where the evidence would not sustain such conviction.” The judicial officer should hear such testimony as may be offered respecting the commission of the homicide, for the purpose of determining what crime has been committed and the probable guilt of the accused, and if he is

1 People v. Cole, 6 Park. Crim. R. 4; Ex parte Pattison, 56 Miss. 161; People v. Tinder, 19 Cal. 539; Commonwealth v. Semmes, 11 State v. Simmons, 19 Ohio, 139; Ex parte Pattison, 56 Miss. 161.  
2 Ex parte McLaughlin, 41 Cal. 211; People v. Cole, 6 Park. Crim. Rep. 695; In Matter of Alexander, 59 Mo. 599.  
3 People v. Cole, 6 Park. Crim. Rep. 605; In Matter of Trola, 54 Cal. 152, 665.

<br>

### --- Page 0658 ---

satisfied therefrom that the offense is one not capital he should admit him to bail. If the evidence is of such character and weight as to create a strong presumption of guilt, bail should be denied.

Sec. 357g. Rule where there has been a conviction for felony pending appeal.—It is generally held that one charged with the commission of a felony is not entitled to be admitted to bail as a matter of right pending an appeal to the court of last resort. It then becomes a matter within the discretion of the court in cases not capital. This question has been in many states regulated by statute. So has the question of the effect of an appeal. In some an appeal stays sentence and execution. In others it has no such effect. There is much of force in the position that one accused of crime should not be required to suffer the punishment prescribed by law upon conviction, where the matter is undetermined and awaits the action of the appellate court. Yet the courts hold otherwise, and such punishment must be entered, unless the court in its discretion determine otherwise, by admitting the defendant to bail. This discretion, however, will not be exercised in favor of such a course where the conviction is for a capital offense. In felonies other than capital offenses, the discretion will be cautiously exercised, and application will be denied unless extraordinary circumstances have intervened. When reference is made to a conviction, it is meant a finding that the accused is guilty either by the verdict of a jury or some other recognized mode. It does not refer to the final act of judgment and sentence. After sentence and commitment, a prisoner cannot ordinarily be bailed.

Sec. 357h. Excessive bail cannot be required.—Among the constitutional provisions of the several states is one, in substance, that excessive bail shall not be required. I understand

1 Finch v. State, 15 Fla. 63; Ex parte Marks, 49 Cal. 680; Ex parte Smith, 23 Tex. App. 100; Ex parte Smallman, 54 Cal. 15; Ex parte Harfound, 16 Fla. 283; State v. Best, 7 Blackf. 641; Ex parte Taylor, 5 Cowen, 39; Ex parte Bridewell, 57 Miss. 44; Ex parte Brown, 68 Cal. 171; Corbett v. State, 24 Ga. 391; In Matter of Percy, 2 Daly, 530; Davis v. State, 6 How. 399 (Miss.).

<br>

### --- Page 0659 ---

510 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 3572.

that the constitution of Illinois is the only exception. A provision of the federal constitution is to the effect that excessive bail shall not be required, nor excessive fines imposed, nor cruel and unusual punishment inflicted.1 It has been determined by the supreme court of the United States that such provision, and others which contain no expression indicative of an intention to apply them to the state governments, are restrictions upon the government of the United States only.2 The question of the amount of the bail is necessarily one addressed to the discretion of the court or judge, and it must be that such discretion, when exercised, will be interfered with only in cases where it is clear it has been abused. To require excessive bail is an abuse of discretion as well as a violation of fundamental law.3 It has been stated that the discretion of the magistrate in taking bail is to be guided by the compound consideration of the ability of the prisoner to give bail and the atrocity of the offense.4 The nature and character of the duty thus imposed upon the judicial officer suggests a large discretion in its exercise, and so long as there are different minds there will remain a difference in judgment. What one might deem, under particular circumstances and conditions, a reasonable bail, another might deem large and excessive. It is therefore evident that, in order to justify a reduction of the amount of the bail, it must clearly appear to the reviewing officer that the bail is disproportionate to the enormity of the offense, and to such a degree that it may be assumed that it was the result of prejudice.5 It was stated by an able court “that to require larger bail than the prisoner could give would be to require excessive bail, and to deny bail in a case clearly bailable by law.” This statement is often quoted, but it will be observed at a glance is not a full statement of the rule. If it was, then in a majority of cases of criminal procedure the accused would be relieved from restraint of imprisonment upon nominal bail only.

1 See Story on Constitution, sec.; McConnell v. State, 18 Tex. App. 1903 and notes, 390.

2 Barron v. Mayor, etc. of Baltimore, 7 Pet. 243. See also Commonwealth v. Hitchings, 5 Gray, 481; Barker v. People, 8 Cowen, 686; James v. Commonwealth, 12 S. & R. 221.

3 United States v. Brawner, 4 Fed. Rep., 86; Ex parte Ryan, 44 Cal. 555.

4 United States v. Lawrence, 4 Cranch, 518.

<br>

### --- Page 0660 ---

§ 8577, 3577.  HABEAS CORPUS. 511

Sec. 3572. Reduction of bail.— Upon an application for reduction of bail the guilt of the prisoner is assumed, where indictment has been found, and if the crime charged is one of particular enormity, or one where the punishment prescribed is great, the ability of the prisoner to furnish bail, though an element to be considered, cannot have the effect to reduce the amount to a sum less than reasonably may seem proper, under all the circumstances, to require his appearance for trial when demanded. Where, therefore, it appears from the evidence that the defendant is unable to give such bail as the court believes sufficient to insure his appearance, the court will not, merely for the sake of reduction, reduce the amount of bail. Bail in the sum of $15,000 was held not to be excessive where the accused was under indictment for assault with intent to commit murder. One who stands charged with the commission of a crime may, before conviction, apply upon habeas corpus for a reduction of bail, where the amount is believed to be too large, and after commitment for failure to give bail, he may make such an application if the bail fixed appears excessive.

Sec. 3577. Right of appeal from order.—In some of the states an appeal or writ of error may be prosecuted from an order refusing bail made in habeas corpus proceedings. The order for such purposes may be considered a final judgment. In other states the order may be brought up for review by certiorari, and in at least one other the proceeding may be reviewed upon habeas corpus issued from the supreme court. The record in all cases should contain all the evidence, as the appellate court is confined exclusively to such record in its review. It is only upon the ground that the order is in effect a final judgment that an appeal or writ of error will lie. The nature of the order, therefore, is important. Thus it has been held that

1 People v. Town, 8 Scam. 19; Ex parte Walker, 8 Tex. App. 669;  
Ex parte Ryan, 44 Cal. 555, Lumley v. State, 8 Port. 293 (Ind.);

2 Ex parte Ryan, 44 Cal. 555. Ex parte Halpin, 39 Ind. 254.

3 Ex parte Ryan, 44 Cal. 555; Bethuram v. Block, 11 Bush, 628 (Ky.);  
Evans v. Foster, 1 N. H. 878; Miller v. State, 43 Tex. 579.

4 Evans v. Foster, 1 N. H. 878.

5 Ex parte Bridewell, 57 Miss. 89;

6 Ex parte Good, 19 Ark. 410.

7 Ex parte Croom, 19 Ala. 561

8 People v. Hessing, 28 Ill. 410.

9 Ex parte Rothschild, 2 Tex. App. 560.

<br>

### --- Page 0661 ---

512 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 357.

no appeal lies from an order refusing a writ of habeas corpus; nor from an order dismissing a motion to reduce the amount of bail; nor from a refusal to hear extraneous evidence upon the application for bail where the party stands indicted for murder. It is equally the law that no appeal on the part of the state lies from an order admitting a party to bail; nor from an order admitting to bail, which has been complied with, where the particular ground is that the prisoner should have been discharged.

1 Ex parte Foster, 5 Tex. App. 525.  
2 Miller v. State, 43 Tex. 579.

CHAPTER 20.  
HABEAS CORPUS—Continuing,  

Custody of CHILDREN,  

Sro. Sec.  

358. Purpose of writ not to recover possession, but to discharge from illegal restraint.  

359. Character of proceedings in such cases.  

360. Child necessarily need not be in the territorial jurisdiction if wrong-doer is.  

361. Whether demand should first be made, which is always open.  

362. Petitioner must affirmatively show his right — Order of the court.  

363. Paramount right is in the father.  

364. Rule stated by the New Jersey court.  

365. Rule stated by the Michigan court that neither party has any right that militates against welfare of child.  

366. Rule generally is that the father’s right is limited by the welfare of the child.  

367. Rule extends to cases where parent seeks custody of child from third persons.  

368. Rule does not go to the extent that the child can dictate his custodian — Writ in cases of discretion merely discharges.  

369. Fitness of the parties a question.  

370. Surrender of custody having been made by parents.  

371. Contracts surrendering children — Validity of.

374. Age of discretion to express a choice.  

375. Illegitimate children—Custody.  

Src. 358. Purpose of writ not to recover possession, but to discharge from illegal restraint.— The writ of habeas corpus is frequently resorted to to obtain the custody of children, and oftentimes to determine the rights of parties to their custody. Strictly speaking, the office of the writ is not to recover the possession of the persons restrained, but simply to discharge from illegal restraint. Therefore it ought to follow that, when such restraint is disproved, the writ will be discharged? The  

1 State v. Baird et al, 18 N. J. Eq. (Miss.); Armstrong v. Stone, 9 Gratt. 194; Foster v. Alston, 6 How. 406 102 (Va.).  
2 People v. Porter, 1 Duer, 709.  

<br>

### --- Page 0663 ---

§ 359, 360.

7A)  
7a

514 SPECIAL REMEDIES.  

Detention of a child of tender years from one entitled to its custody is an illegal restraint within the law.

Sec. 359. Character of proceedings in such cases.—Yet, although denominated proceedings in habeas corpus, and possessing elements that are incident thereto, they are in some respects unlike the ordinary proceedings for the release of a party held upon a criminal charge. It is really, in most cases, a controversy between the father and mother (where that relation exists), and the real question is, which of the contestants is the more suitable person to have the control and custody of the child?

Sec. 360. Child necessarily need not be within the territorial jurisdiction if wrong-doer is.—While it is true that the writ will not lie when the child is not in the custody of the alleged respondent, yet the mere fact that the child is in a foreign jurisdiction is not itself sufficient to defeat the application for the writ. The place of confinement is not important to the relief if the guilty party is within reach of process, so that, by the power of the court, he can be compelled, to use the expression of the court, “to release his grasp.” The important question is, Where is the power of control exercised? It is incumbent, therefore, upon a respondent to show good cause for not producing a child in court in obedience to the writ. If a parent, whom the proceedings show, had the custody or control of the child, then the presumption remains that it is within his control, and the mere fact that he maintains him in another state, or that he procured him to be sent there, or gave him in custody to another who removed him out of the jurisdiction of the court, will afford him no excuse. He must show that he is without the power to exercise control over the child.

1 Williamson's Case, 26 Pa. St. 23; 4 Rivers v. Mitchell, 57 Iowa, 193; 67 Am. Dec. 874; People v. Cooper; See also United States v. Davis, 5 How. Pr. 288; Cranch C. C. 622; In Matter of Stacey.

2 Rivers v. Mitchell, 57 Iowa, 193. 10 Johns, 827.

3 In Matter of Jackson, 15 Mich. 416; Rivers v. Mitchell, 57 Iowa, 193.

<br>

### --- Page 0664 ---

§§ 361-363.] HABEAS CORPUS — CUSTODY OF CHILDREN. 515

Sec. 361. Whether demand should first be made.— It has been stated that a demand for the custody of the child must precede institution of proceedings to recover it or release it from restraint. In most cases it is required that this should be done. But I apprehend that the demand in such a case is but evidence of the unlawful restraint, as it is in cases at law of the unlawful detention of the property where the taking was lawful, and that where it appears that the respondent willfully and unlawfully obtained custody of the child, and especially without an apparent right to such custody, no demand is necessary to precede the application for the writ.

Sec. 362. Petitioner must affirmatively show his right — Order of the court.—The party applying for the writ must affirmatively show that he is entitled to the custody of the child, whether it be a parent, individual, or a corporation organized for charitable purposes; and the order of the court will be, unless the child has been legally bound or legally committed to the care and custody of another or of some institution, either penal, reformatory or charitable, if of the age of discretion, that he be freed from the restraint alleged, if found to exist, and, if not of the age of discretion, then assigned by the court to the lawful or a proper custodian, according as the facts may warrant.

Sec. 363. Paramount right is in the father.— When parents have separated and are living apart from each other, the courts are often called upon to adjudicate and determine with respect to the custody of the children. It is often asserted, and sometimes held, that, other conditions being equal, the paramount right to their custody rests with the father, and such undoubtedly was the law of England. One of the leading cases

-where the question was directly involved was that of People v. Mercein. It first appears in the court of chancery (8 Paige, 47), again in the supreme court (25 Wend. 64), again in the court

Speer v. Davis, 38 Ind. 271; Bullen’s Petition, 28 Kan. 781. In re Waldron, 13 Johns, 418; Mercein v. People, 3 Hill, 399, 38 Am. Dec. 644.  
People v. Mercein, 3 Hill, 399.  
State v. Baird, 18 N. J. Eq. 194; Corrie v. Corrie, 42 Mich. 509; Gishwiler v. Dodez, 4 Ohio St. 615,  
Milligan v. Children's Home, 97 Ind. 355.

<br>

### --- Page 0665 ---

516 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§§ 364, 365.]

of errors (25 Wend. 83), and finally in the supreme court again (3 Hill, 400). After different conclusions reached by the different courts, the supreme court sustained the former ruling of that court, which was in favor of the proposition stated that the paramount right to the custody of the child rested in the father. A majority of the supreme court of Ohio were also of the opinion that the law was correctly stated by the supreme court of New York,¹ and it has been affirmed by courts of other states.

Sec. 364. Rule stated by the New Jersey court.—The New Jersey court, however, state a rule which they claim, and cite authorities to sustain, is the settled rule in this country, as follows: “Upon habeas corpus brought by the father for his children, the court will not as a matter of course order them to be delivered up to him, but only in case they are improperly restrained of their liberty. The office of the writ is not to recover the possession of the persons detained, but to free them from all illegal restraints upon their liberty, and therefore, in cases where infant children are not in the custody of improper persons, and are not restrained against their will, they will not, on habeas corpus, be delivered to their father. If the infants are of sufficient years or discretion to judge for themselves they will be examined, and if they are satisfied and wish to remain the court will hold that they are not unduly deprived of their liberty, and will permit them to remain with which of the parties they may select. When the infants are too young to exercise any discretion, the court will determine for them, and adjudge the custody to such of the parties as may be considered most advantageous to the infants. Such, it must be presumed, would be the choice of the infants had they the capacity to elect; but the custody in all such cases will be given to the father, if not an improper person, unless it clearly appears that it is for the advantage of the infant to remain where found.”²

Sec. 365. Rule stated by the Michigan court that neither party has any right that militates against welfare of child. In speaking of contests of this kind between parents, it was

¹ Gishweiler v. Dodez, 4 Ohio St. 615.  
² State ex rel Baird et al., 18 N. J. Eq. 194.

<br>

### --- Page 0666 ---

§ 366.] HABEAS CORPUS— CUSTODY OF CHILDREN. 517

stated by the Michigan court that “the opinion is now nearly universal that neither of the parties has any rights that can be allowed to seriously militate against the welfare of the child. The paramount consideration is, What is really demanded by its interests? It is doing no violence to what is taught by judicial experience to assume that the parties will be more alive to the satisfaction of their own feelings and interests than to the true end of the inquisition; while the innocent subject of the contention is utterly unable to speak or act for itself, and is in danger of being lost sight of in the strife for its possession.”

Sec. 366. Rule generally is that the father’s right is limited by the welfare of the child.— Some of the earlier cases recognized the rule that the custody of the child should be awarded to the father unless he was an unfit and improper person to retain its custody; but the rule of the courts at the present day is far more extended, and his right to such control is limited by humane considerations, that of the welfare of the child. The pathetic words of an able jurist are here repeated: “The reputation of a father may be as stainless as crystal; he may not be afflicted with the slightest mental, moral or physical disqualifications from superintending the general welfare of the infant; the mother may have separated from him without the shadow of a pretense of justification; and yet the interests of the child may imperatively demand the denial of the father’s right and its continuance with the mother. The tender age and precarious state of its health make the vigilance of the mother indispensable to its proper care; for, not doubting that paternal anxiety would seek for and obtain the best substitute which could be procured, yet every instinct of humanity unerringly proclaims that no substitute can supply the place of her whose watchfulness over the sleeping cradle, or waking moments of her offspring, is prompted by deeper and holier feelings than the most liberal allowance of nurses’ wages could possibly stimulate.”

1 Corrie v. Corrie, 42 Mich. 509.  
2 Barton, P. J., in Commonwealth v. Hauteville, decided in 1840 in the court of general sessions, city and county of Philadelphia,

<br>

### --- Page 0667 ---

515 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 367.

Sec. 367. Rule extends to cases where parent seeks custody from third persons.— The rule is now carried to the extent that the welfare of the child is the paramount consideration, where the parent seeks its custody from third parties who obtained lawful custody thereof. Hence, where the father was convicted of a crime and sent to the penitentiary, and the mother, being destitute and unable to properly care for the child, gave its custody, a girl six years old, to the home for destitute children, and she was given by such institution to a respectable family, who properly cared for the child, the court refused to award its custody to her parents after the release of the father from imprisonment. In another case where a child five years of age was left unprovided for by the death of its mother, while its father was absent as a sailor on a long voyage, and was placed in an asylum and thereafter adopted by the respondent and his wife as their own child, the court refused to award its custody to the father on his return. The court certainly took advanced ground. The court stated in the opinion: “There remains the question as to the condition of the child itself. In a decision of the case it must be considered how the father’s right is affected by the condition of the child. Suppose by a pure misfortune, as insanity, or being cast away and being compelled to live among savages, a father has left his child destitute and dependent upon charity; does that give the child the right to form such new relations as to take from the father the right to the custody of the child? Upon reflection I am satisfied it does. When by misfortune the father is compelled to leave the child utterly helpless, the child ought to be considered as emancipated by the father. If by misfortune the child has made new relations in life, so deep and strong as to change its whole character and nature, the father has no right to reclaim it.” Other courts declare the same doctrine. The father’s right is not absolute under all circumstances. He may relinquish it by contract, forfeit it by abandonment, or lose it by being in a condition of total inability to afford his minor children necessary care and support.

1 Dumaine v. Gwynne, 10 Allen, 578 (Mass.). See also Commonwealth v. Hammond, 27 Mass.
2 In Matter of O'Neal, 8 Am. Law R. 270. Clark v. Bayer, 32—

<br>

### --- Page 0668 ---

§ 368.] HABEAS CORPUS — CUSTODY OF CHILDREN. 519

Sec. 868. Rule does not go to the extent that child can dictate his custodian — Writ, in cases of children of discretion, generally discharged.— It is not to be assumed from what has been stated that a child, capable of exercising a discretion as to his custody, can dictate his custodian. The cases, generally, only go to the extent that the wishes of the child may be consulted, and this for the purpose of enabling the court to exercise its discretion more properly and wisely, not that the court shall adopt its choice. The court exercises its discretion only where it appears that, either by reason of the tender years of the child or other conditions, it should be placed in the custody of a proper person, whether it be the parent or another. In other cases the court simply discharges the writ, and permits the minor to go free and exercise his own choice. If necessary for the welfare of the child, its custody will be taken from the parents or refused to them. Where a father, under a written agreement, surrendered the custody of his child to third parties until she should have arrived at the age of twelve years, and at the expiration of such period sought to reclaim her, and it appeared that the foster parents had become greatly attached to the child, and kindly and properly cared for her, and that the child in turn had formed a deep attachment for them and an aversion to her father, the latter being a man of means and of high moral and social standing, and she expressed a desire to remain with her foster parents and a great aversion to being returned to her natural parent, it was held that the child should be delivered to her father; that while the wishes and preferences of the child should not be ignored, yet they were not of controlling influence; that, in the opinion of the court, the permanent interests of the child, her intellectual culture and social position, would be much promoted by placing her under her father’s control.

A remark of the supreme court of Wisconsin, to the effect that a child twelve years old should have been allowed to choose

State ex rel. Lynch v. Bratton (Del.), Dumaine et ux. v. Gwynne, 10 Allen, 15 Am. Law Reg. 359; Jones v. Darnell, 103 Ind. 569; Sturdevant v. State, 2 Shaw ex rel. v. Nachtwey, 43 Iowa, 15 Neb. 459, 653.

Clark v. Bayer, 82 Ohio St. 299;

<br>

### --- Page 0669 ---

520 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 368.

for herself whether she would remain with those who had nurtured and cared for her under void indentures, or go to her father, who, it appeared, had been dissolute and convicted of crime, and who had permitted his family to become inmates of the county poor-house, from which the child was taken by the respondents, is neither warranted by law nor sanctioned by the dictates of humanity. Though the indenture was void, yet that fact did not give to the father the absolute right to the control of the child, as we have seen. The court still had a discretion, not controlled either by the whims or caprice of the child or her affection for her natural parent, to dispose of the custody of the child in such manner as would be most conducive to its welfare, and if that result lay in remanding her to the respondents, as the court seems to indicate, it would seem such should have been the judgment of the court. The judgment of the county court which remanded the child to the respondents was reversed, on the ground that, as the indentures were void, they had no legal claim to the custody and services of the child, and hence it ought not, against the will of the child, have remanded her to them. The court demonstrates to a certainty that the custody of the child should not have been awarded to the father, who was an unfit person. The court indicates that the child should have been given the privilege of determining for herself whether she would go to her father or remain with the respondent.

Note To Sec. 368.—It was said by the court in In re Goodenough, 19 Wis. 274, “That the father has a legal and paramount right to the custody and services of his child will not in general be denied; and that such right will sometimes be enforced upon this writ seems settled by the later and better-considered decisions. (Citing several English cases and State v. Richardson, 40 N. H. 272.) Formerly the rule was different, and it was held that the court was not bound in any case to deliver the infant over to any particular person, though such person was the natural or legally appointed guardian, (Citing Rex v. Deleval, 3 Burr. 1434; In Matter of Waldron, 13 Johns. 418; In Matter of McDowle, 8 Johns. 328; Commonwealth v. Hammond, 10 Pick. 274.) All that the court was bound to do was to see that the infant was not wrongfully imprisoned or detained against his will. If he was they would set him at liberty, and if of sufficient age leave him to go where he pleased. But if he was of such tender years that he could not form a proper judgment, the court would exercise its judgment for his

1 In re Goodenough, 19 Wis. 274
