<br>

### --- Page 0670 ---

§ 369.] HABEAS CORPUS— CUSTODY OF CHILDREN, 521

benefit, and do what it thought most for his interest and welfare. It was in the sound discretion of the court to change the custody of the infant or not. By the later decisions, those first above cited and others, this doctrine seems to have been somewhat modified. If the father apply, the child should, if within the age of nurture, which is fixed at fourteen years, be delivered over to him, unless there be something in his situation or conduct which renders him unfit for the trust. If the father be dead and the mother still a widow, the same rule applies to her. But if the mother be married again and her husband living, the old rule prevails. The infant, if of sufficient age, must choose for himself; if not, the court will choose for him. In such case the mother is considered by her subsequent marriage as having lost her right of guardianship for nurture. But when the infant is above the age of fourteen years, he must, it seems, in every case, choose for himself. The court will not compel him upon habeas corpus to submit to parental authority.

Sec. 369. Fitness of the parties a question which is always open.—The question is always present and open to inquiry as to the fitness of the parties to have the custody of the child, and the inquiry extends not only to the ability to properly maintain and care for it, but also as to the habits and character of the parties. Hence, if it appears that the father is immoral or intemperate, and these habits are likely to affect the child to its prejudice, the court will, in exercising its discretion, give to them proper consideration and declare him unfit to have the custody of the child; and so with the mother: if her habits, character or conduct have been such that the morals of the child are likely to be injured by her bad example, and the child has arrived at that age where it no longer needs those attentions which a mother alone can bestow, then the court will declare her to be an unfit person to have the care and custody of the child. In other words, where these elements exist, then the court is called upon to exercise its discretion without regard to the paramount rights at law of either, and dispose of the custody of the child as its best interests seem to demand. Hence, where both the father and mother of the infant were persons of immoral character, the court refused to award her custody to

1 Commonwealth v. Briggs, 16 Pick. 203; Commonwealth v. Addicks, 5 Binn. 520; Commonwealth v. Nutt, 197; Farnham v. Pierce, 141 Mass. 203; Gwynne, 10 Allen, 270; House of Refuge v. Ryan, 87 Ohio St. 1; Milwaukee Industrial School v. Supervisors, 40 Wis. 328; Dumaine v. Browne, 148; Cincinnati v. P. A. Browne, 148.

<br>

### --- Page 0671 ---

522 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 370.

either, but placed her in charge of her sister, who was a suitable person and who consented to take her.

Sec. 370. Surrender of custody having been made by parents.— There are many cases where parents have either by act or word transferred the custody of their minor children to others, and afterwards have sought to reclaim them upon habeas corpus. In such cases the modern rule seems to be, though there are some exceptions, that where the custodian appears in every way to be a proper person to have the care, training and education of the infant, and the court is satisfied that its social, moral and educational interests will be best promoted by remaining in the custody of the person to whom it was transferred or received when surrendered or abandoned, the new custody will be treated as lawful and exclusive. “After the affections of both child and adopted parent become engaged, and conditions exist which cannot be changed without risk to the happiness of the child, and the parent desires to reclaim it, he is not in a position to ask a court to interfere in his favor. His parental rights must yield to the feelings, interests and rights of other parties acquired with his consent. Hence, where the father and mother, living apart by agreement, transferred the care and custody of their infant children to the grandfather of such children, in consideration that he would receive, care and provide for them, and in pursuance of such agreement he did take them in charge, his custody was held to be lawful, and the parties afterwards without sufficient cause could not reclaim them, and such grandparent could maintain an action for damages against one who wrongfully took or caused them to be taken from his custody.” Where the father and mother of children were divorced, and the custody of the children was awarded to their mother, and the mother and children went to live with her mother, with whom they were living when the mother of the children died, committing at her death the care and custody of the children to their grandmother, who ac-

1 Commonwealth v. Nutt, 1 P. A. Browne, 143; Note to State v. Smith, 20 Am. Dec. 335.

2 Clark v. Bayer, 32 Ohio St. 299.

3 Clark v. Bayer, 32 Ohio St. 299.

<br>

### --- Page 0672 ---

§ 371 HABEAS CORPUS — CUSTODY OF CHILDREN

Excepted the charge, it was held that the father’s right to reclaim the children was forfeited.

Sec. 371. Contracts surrendering children — Validity of. — A case involving facts somewhat similar to the foregoing was decided by the Massachusetts court, and there it was declared with reference to the particular facts that “if a married woman who has been compelled to live separate from her husband, by reason of his intemperance and crime, is unable to provide for her children, and therefore voluntarily gives them up to a charitable institution, established for the purpose of furnishing homes to destitute children, under a written contract by which the children are to be placed out or adopted in a good family, and she is not to seek to discover them or deprive such family of them, the contract is valid. The court, however, on habeas corpus, will inquire whether the welfare of the children is properly attended to.” Another case involving the question of the validity of a parol surrender of a child by its parents was decided by the supreme court of Iowa. The court found that when the child was two months old its mother gave it to her (the mother’s) parents to raise, and to remain with the latter until the grandmother's death; that they accepted the gift and had the care and custody of the child at the time of the proceedings on the part of the mother to reclaim her. The court approved of the statement of the rule declared in Clark v. Bayer, 32 Ohio St. 299, and added, that “when a parent has either by abandonment or contract surrendered his present legal right to the custody of a child, in all controversies subsequently arising respecting its custody the matter of primary importance is the interest and welfare of the child. To this the right of the parent must yield.” The writ was discharged. An agreement made by the father, surrendering the custody of his child, would not operate of itself to deprive the mother of the custody of the child after its father’s death.

1 State ex rel. Lynch v. Bratton Bonnett v. Bonnett et al., 61 Iowa, (Del), 15 Am. Law Reg. 359. See 199. 
Also State v. Smith, 6 Greenl. 462. 
‘State v. Rueff, 29 W. Va. 751. 
4 Dumaine et ux. v. Gwynne, 10 Allen, 270.

<br>

### --- Page 0673 ---

524 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 872, 3872.

Sec. 372. Child may be reclaimed is the doctrine of some courts.—There are cases which, while acknowledging the doctrine stated to prevail in extreme cases, yet hold more strongly to the old rule, that the parent, being the natural guardian of the child, has the paramount right to its custody, even though he has voluntarily surrendered its control for an indefinite time, or in fact abandoned it,1 and others which hold that the parent may reclaim the child where it is shown that the former objections to his having the custody of his child no longer exist.2

Sec. 373. Statutes respecting placing of abandoned or destitute children in charitable institutions—Effect of.—In a number of the states it is provided by statute for the placing of abandoned, destitute or indigent children in charitable institutions under control of the state. In most cases the parents are not made parties to the proceedings. Where such is the case, it is quite generally held that the proceedings as authorized are valid, and that the legal custody of the children properly follows; yet these proceedings do not operate to deprive the parents of the right to reclaim the custody of their children. In fact they are not bound by the proceedings. But the inquiry as to the fitness of the parents to resume control of their children, and the best interests of the children, is still open. It is probable, however, that the wishes of the children in respect to their custodian would not prove as weighty a consideration with the court in the exercise of its discretion as they might in other cases.2 “Such statutes are not considered penal, and the commitment by the public officers is not in the nature of punishment. They are provisions by the state, as parens patriae, for the custody and care of neglected children, and are intended only to supply to them the parental custody which they have lost. They do not punish the infant by con-

1 Wishard v. Medaris, 34 Ind. 168; Milwaukee Industrial School v. Supervisors, 40 Wis. 328; Taylor v. Jeter, 33 Ga. 195, 81 Am. Dec. 202; House of Refuge v. Ryan, 87 Ohio St. 197;

2 Farnham v. Pierce, 141 Mass. 203; Roth v. House of Refuge, 31 Md. 329; Ex parte Murphy, 75 Ala. 409; In re Farnham v. Pierce, 141 Mass. 203; Goodenough, 19 Wis. 291; Ferrier’s Petition, 108 Ill. 367.

<br>

### --- Page 0674 ---

§ 373.] HABEAS CORPUS — CUSTODY OF CHILDREN. 525

finement nor deprive him of his liberty. They only recognize and regulate, as in providing for guardianship and apprenticeship, the parental custody, which is an incident of infancy.

Note to Sec. 373.—In Farnham v. Pierce, 141 Mass. 203, the purpose and scope and effect of such statutes is fully discussed and determined. At the outset the court states that if the statute is to be construed as authorizing a final adjudication upon the rights of the father, taking from him the care and custody of his child in a summary manner and without notice, it would be a grave question whether it could be sustained. But they say: "We do not so construe the statute. It provides custody for a child who is suffering for the need of it, in consequence of the death or unfitness of its parent. The fact of the death or neglect or crime or vice of the parent shows the condition of the child, that he is in need of parental custody. The fact that he is suffering morally for want of parental restraint calls for immediate and appropriate relief, as would the want of food or shelter. The inability or failure of the parent to furnish the relief is intended to show the need of the child, not to be the basis of a decree against the parent. The proceeding is intended to be summary. Any magistrate is authorized to act when it shall be made to appear to him, etc. No complaint or written application to the magistrate is required, and no notice to any one except to the state board of health, lunacy and charity after it shall have been made to appear. No trial is required, and it might be made to appear by inspection of the child and his surroundings, without any further proceeding. The statute not only requires no notice to the parent, but does not make him a party, and gives him no right to be heard even if present; and it does not prescribe a fact as constituting the unfitness of the parent — as support as a pauper, or sentence to the state’s prison, for instance — but leaves the question of unfitness in the respects specified to the summary determination of any magistrate, without revision or appeal. The finding of the district court must be taken to be that the child was in the condition which required the custody of the overseers of the poor, according to the statute, and she was given into their custody for that reason, and not because the father was adjudged to have forfeited his right. The commitment is valid, and the custody in which the child is held is lawful and subject to the rights of the father. The statute does not provide any way in which the father can maintain his rights. He can apply to the overseers of the poor to discharge the child for the reason that the object of the commitment has been accomplished, and on showing his ability and fitness to take charge of the child she should be discharged by them. The statute leaves that in their discretion, it is true, and, as to matters other than the right of the parent, their discretion may be absolute; but the right of the parent can be protected by habeas corpus by this court. . . . The parent has a right to show that the cause stated for the commitment does not now exist, that he is competent and fit to have the care of his child,

Farnham v. Pierce, 141 Mass. 203,

<br>

### --- Page 0675 ---

526 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 374, 375.

and that the welfare of the child will permit of her removal from her present custody.”

Sec. 374. Age of discretion to express a choice.— It is often said that, when the child has arrived at sufficient age to exercise his discretion, his wishes will be consulted; and again, when he is of sufficient age, the court will not remand, but allow him to choose as he pleases. With respect to the period in life when a minor is capable of exercising the discretion to choose there is no arbitrary rule. After all, the determination of this preliminary question is left with the court. It is often said that the child must be able to make an intelligent choice; but what is an intelligent choice, to a great extent, depends upon the choice which he makes. In one case it was held that a child nine years old was incapable of assenting to the transfer of his custody from his father to his mother, who was living apart from her husband,¹ and in another it was said that a girl twelve years old ought to have been consulted as to her choice, and inferentially, at least, that her choice should have determined the action of the court.² Quite generally, the courts hold that when a minor arrives at the age of nurture (fourteen) his wishes not only should be consulted, but that he has the absolute freedom of choice.³

Sec. 375. Illegitimate children—Custody of.—With respect to illegitimate children the mother has the paramount right to their custody over their putative father. But it has been held that the father has the paramount right of custody as against all other persons than the mother. In Texas, however, after the child has reached the age of seven years, the father and mother stand on an equal footing with respect to such custody.⁴ In all respects the rule as to their custody is practically the same as is applied to children who are legitimate, and the courts are actuated and controlled by the same considerations. The wel-

¹ Commonwealth v. Nickerson, 87 Mass. 518; People v. Landt, 2 Johns. 374; Mitchell, 44 Barb. 245, 5 People v. Cooper, 8 How. Pr. 288; In Matter of Doyle, 1 Clark's Ch. (N. Y.) 154.  
² In re Goodenough, 19 Wis. 291.  
³ Alfred v. McKay, 36 Ga. 440; Byrne v. Love, 14 Tex. 81.  
⁴ Byrne v. Love, 14 Tex. 81.

<br>

### --- Page 0676 ---

§ 375. HABEAS CORPUS — CUSTODY OF CHILDREN. 527

Welfare of the child is the paramount question where the legal custody has been given to another. If the mother is unfit for any reason to have their custody, such custody will be awarded to another, if a proper and fit custodian. Such children may exercise their choice to the same extent and with the same effect as those who are legitimate.

1 Simmons v. Bull, 21 Ala. 501, 56 Am. Dec. 259; Fullilove v. Banks, 62 Miss. 11; People v. Landt, 2 Johns. 875.

<br>

### --- Page 0677 ---

CHAPTER 21.  
HABEAS CORPUS—CONTINUED.  

COURTS-MARTIAL—BOARDS OF HEALTH—COMMISSIONERS OF IMMIGRATION, ETC.—PERSONS ENTITLED TO THE WRIT.  

Sec.   Sec.  
376. Courts-martial—Review of proceedings.  
377. Enlistments in federal service—Party in custody of federal authorities—State courts cannot release.  
378. Boards of health—Persons restrained by direction of.  
379. Commissioners of immigration and other like bodies—Review of proceedings.  
380. Persons entitled to the writ.  
381. Master may not have writ for his apprentice.  
382. Right of guardianship will not be tried.  

Sec. 376. Courts-martial—Review of proceedings.—The question of the jurisdiction of a court-martial may always be inquired into upon the application of any party aggrieved by its proceedings, as may that of every other tribunal, and the scope and range of the inquiry upon habeas corpus is controlled by the same rules and limitations in both cases. There must be jurisdiction to hear and determine and to render the particular judgment. If this exists, however erroneous the proceedings may be, they cannot be reviewed collaterally upon habeas corpus. In order to have the writ lie, it is essential that the person be in actual confinement or have the present means of enforcing it. Mere moral restraint is not sufficient. A mere order of the secretary of the navy addressed to and notifying a medical officer that he was placed under arrest, and to confine himself within the limits of the city of Washington, was held not such an actual confinement as would justify the issuing of the writ. Courts-martial and delinquency courts are tribunals of special and limited powers, having jurisdiction only of offenses against military discipline committed by persons belonging to the particular branch of the service.  

1 Ex parte Kearney, 7 Wheat. 38; Davison, 21 Fed. Rep. 618; Wales v.  
Ex parte Reed, 100 U.S. 18; In re Whitney, 114 U.S. 564.  
2 Wales v. Whitney, 114 U.S. 564.

<br>

### --- Page 0678 ---

376. HABEAS CORPUS — COURTS-MARTIAL, ETC. 529

for which such courts are organized. The existence of a valid enlistment constitutes a part of the subject-matter of the jurisdiction of such a court, and in its absence the court has no authority whatever over the offense or the person proceeded against. Their judgments are of no force when attacked in a collateral proceeding, unless accompanied by proof of the jurisdictional facts upon which the authority of the court to render them depends. Unlike courts of general jurisdiction, the recital of jurisdictional facts in their records does not furnish prima facie evidence even of their existence, but they must be affirmatively proved in order to establish the validity of their judgments. Such proof is always subject to be controverted by the person affected by such judgment, and he may establish, even by extrinsic evidence, a want of jurisdiction. Hence, where a person detained by virtue of the judgment of such a court applied for his discharge upon habeas corpus on the ground that he was a minor, and there was no proof (though recited in the judgment) that the consent of his father had been obtained to such enlistment, it was held that the judgment was not even prima facie evidence of the essential jurisdictional fact of a valid enlistment. It was stated, however, that when a court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person, it is competent for it to try and determine all questions within the issue arising during the course of the trial, and its decision therein can be reviewed only on appeal in a direct proceeding having that object in view. The evident purpose of this statement was to recognize the rule, as applicable to such cases, that where the court, upon proof before it, has determined the existence of the facts upon which its jurisdiction depends, this is conclusive upon collateral attack, and with reference to the particular case to state that, if the court had before it competent proof upon the question of the validity of such enlistment, then its judgment as to its legal effect, though in effect it was determining its own jurisdiction, could only be reviewed upon appeal.

Note To Sec. 376.— Among the many important as well as interesting cases which grew out of the disturbed condition of the country during the

People ex rel. Fry v. The Warden, etc., 100 N. Y. 20.

<br>

### --- Page 0679 ---

530 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 376.

civil war was that of Ex parte Milligan, 4 Wall. 2. Milligan, who was a citizen and resident of the state of Indiana, was tried before a military commission, convicted, and condemned to suffer the penalty of death. Upon the petition for habeas corpus the circuit court for Indiana, the judges of the circuit court being opposed in opinion, certified the points of difference to the United States supreme court. It was held that military commissions organized during the civil war in a state not invaded and not engaged in rebellion, in which the federal courts were open and in the proper and unobstructed exercise of their judicial functions, had no jurisdiction to try, convict or sentence for any criminal offense a citizen who was neither a resident of a rebellious state, nor a prisoner of war, nor a person in the military or naval service, and congress could not invest them with any such power. That the guaranty of trial by jury contained in the constitution was intended for a state of war as well as a state of peace, and is equally binding upon rulers and people at all times and under all circumstances. That the federal authority having been unopposed in the state of Indiana, and the federal courts open for the trial of offenses and the redress of grievances, the usages of war could not, under the constitution, afford any sanction for the trial there of a citizen in civil life, not connected with the military or naval service, by a military tribunal, for any offense whatever. That cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia, in time of war or public danger, are excepted from the necessity of presentment or indictment by a grand jury; and the right of trial by jury, in such cases, is subject to the same exceptions. That neither the president nor congress, nor the judiciary, can disturb any one of the safeguards of civil liberty incorporated into the constitution, except so far as the right is given to suspend, in certain cases, the privilege of habeas corpus. That a citizen not connected with the military service, and resident in a state where the courts are open and in the proper exercise of their jurisdiction, cannot, even when the privilege of the writ of habeas corpus is suspended, be tried, convicted or sentenced otherwise than by the ordinary courts of law. That suspension of the privilege of the writ of habeas corpus does not suspend the writ itself. The writ issues as a matter of course, and on its return the court decides whether the applicant is denied the right of proceeding any further. That a person who is a resident of a loyal state, where he was arrested, who was never a resident of any state engaged in rebellion, nor connected with the military or naval service, cannot be regarded as a prisoner of war. These questions may never arise again. Whether they do or do not, they gave occasion for the determination of the most important of principles which may affect the liberty of the subject. Some extracts from the language of the court may not be out of place here. They open up a wide field for thought, and impress us with the great importance, at all times and under all circumstances, of adhering to the provisions of the constitution as the supreme law of the land. Thus it was said: “It is claimed that martial law covers with its broad mantle the proceedings of this military commission. The proposition is this: that in time of war the commander of an armed force (if in his opinion the exigencies of the country demand it, and of which he is to judge) has the power within

<br>

### --- Page 0680 ---

§ 377.) HABEAS CORPUS — COURTS-MARTIAL, ETC. 531

the lines of his military district to suspend all civil rights and their remedies, and subject citizens as well as soldiers to the rule of his will, and in the exercise of his lawful authority cannot be restrained except by his superior officer or the president of the United States. If this position is sound to the extent claimed, then when war exists, foreign or domestic, and the country is subdivided into military departments for mere convenience, the commander of one of them can, if he chooses, within his limits, on the plea of necessity, with the approval of the executive, substitute military force for and to the exclusion of the laws, and punish all persons as he thinks right and proper without fixed or certain rules. The statement of this proposition shows its importance; for if true, republican government is a failure, and there is an end of liberty regulated by law. Martial law established upon such a basis destroys every guarantee of the constitution, and effectually renders the military independent of and superior to the civil power; an attempt to do which by the king of Great Britain was deemed by our fathers such an offense that they assigned it to the world as one of the causes which impelled them to declare their independence. Civil liberty and this kind of martial law cannot endure together; the antagonism is irreconcilable, and in the conflict one or the other must perish. The court concedes that in a great crisis, like that occasioned by the civil war, there should be a power somewhere of suspending the writ of habeas corpus. That in the emergency of the times an immediate public investigation according to law may not be possible; and yet the peril to the country may be too imminent to suffer such persons to go at large. “Unquestionably,” they say, “there is then an exigency which demands that the government, if it should see fit, in the exercise of a proper discretion, to make arrests, should not be required to produce the persons arrested in answer to a writ of habeas corpus. The constitution goes no further. It does not say, after a writ of habeas corpus is denied a citizen, that he shall be tried otherwise than by the course of the common law. If it had intended this result, it was easy by the use of direct words to have accomplished it.” Subsequently they state in the opinion: “The suspension of the privilege of the writ of habeas corpus does not suspend the writ itself. The writ issues as a matter of course; and on the return made to it, the court decides whether the party applying is denied the right of proceeding any further with it.” The court is speaking with reference to an act of congress approved March 3, 1863, which it is determined gave the court jurisdiction to inquire upon habeas corpus to the extent of determining whether proceedings should be continued under it. If the law was applicable to all cases, then the military was subject to the direction of the court in the matter.

Sec. 377. Enlistments in federal service — Jurisdiction of federal courts exclusive if party in custody of federal authorities.— The federal courts have jurisdiction to inquire upon habeas corpus into the validity of the enlistment of soldiers in the military service of the United States, and to discharge them.

<br>

### --- Page 0681 ---

532 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 378.

from such service when in their judgment their enlistment had not been made in conformity to law. But the state courts, while they may issue a writ of habeas corpus in such cases, cannot proceed, when it is made to appear that the petitioner is in custody under the authority of the United States. Under such conditions the jurisdiction of the federal court is exclusive.

Sec. 378. Boards of health — Persons restrained by direction of.— Where the right is claimed by boards of health to confine persons or restrain them of their liberty by reason of supposed danger to the public welfare, as in cases of infectious diseases, it must appear very clearly and satisfactorily not only that such right has been conferred by the law, but also that in its exercise the facts were present which justified it. The validity of the law generally is not so much called in question as the right to enforce its provisions. The courts therefore will, upon habeas corpus, notwithstanding such a board has determined that the facts of a particular case bring the case within the law, review such question. Hence, where a person was thus restrained of his liberty, which restraint was sought to be justified on the ground that he had been exposed to an infectious disease (the statute authorizing such restraint under such conditions, and the only evidence was merely to the effect that, as a driver of an express wagon in the affected district, he might have been exposed, and had refused to be vaccinated, there being no proof that he was actually exposed), it was held that the facts did not bring the case within the provisions of the law. The power conferred upon such boards is peculiar, and made so by reason of the necessity that may exist for immediate and summary action in many cases. As stated, the validity of the law under which they proceed is ordinarily not so much called in question as the right in particular cases to enforce its provisions. Where such a right is claimed, it must appear very clearly and satisfactorily not only that it has been conferred by law, — and, where a method has been provided, that such method was pursued, — but also that the facts were

In re Tarble, 13 Wall. 397; Robb v. Connolly, 111 U.S. 624; In re Davison, 21 Fed. Rep. 618. See, however, McConologue’s Case, 107 Mass. 154. Matter of Smith et al., 146 N. Y. 68.
Robb v. Connolly, 111 U.S. 624.

<br>

### --- Page 0682 ---

§ 379. HABEAS CORPUS — COURTS-MARTIAL, ETC. 

present which justified it. Such officers act upon their own inspection and knowledge. They are not required to give any person a hearing, and are not required and have no power to call and swear witnesses. Their determinations are not final and conclusive; if they were, then the exercise of such summary power could not be upheld. Thus, their determination that certain conditions existing constitute a nuisance does not make them a nuisance. It is the actual existence of a nuisance which gives them jurisdiction to act, and this ultimately is to be determined by the court, whenever the proceedings are brought before it, as they may be by certiorari and upon habeas corpus, where persons are restrained of their liberty. “The board acts at its peril.”

Sec. 379. Commissioners of immigration and other like bodies — Review of proceedings. — The general doctrine of the law is that, where the determination of the facts is lodged in a particular officer or tribunal, the decision of that officer or tribunal is conclusive and cannot be reviewed except as authorized by law. Hence, applying this rule to boards of immigration, and the further rule that the writ of habeas corpus only issues to reach jurisdictional defects or errors of law, it was held that the determination of such tribunals cannot be reviewed by means of such a writ. The distinction between the determinations of such a board and those of a board of health is thus clearly made. The former are more nearly classed with boards of review and the like, who act upon evidence. In such case, in the absence of statutes providing otherwise, the court issuing the writ must inquire and adjudge whether the commissioner acquired jurisdiction of the matter, whether he exceeded his jurisdiction, and whether he had any competent or legal evidence of facts before him on which to exercise his judgment, but not to inquire as to the sufficiency of such legal evidence; while in the latter it is stated, as we have seen, that the board acts summarily, and not upon evidence, and hence the proceeding cannot be upheld unless, as matter of fact, and so determined. 

1 People v. Board of Health, 140 N.Y. 1. 
2 In re Day, 27 Fed. Rep. 678.

<br>

### --- Page 0683 ---

534 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 380.

by a court or magistrate, their determination is correct. Yet they make up their conclusions from physical evidence, statements of persons, and in so doing proceed no more summarily than boards of immigration who refuse to allow passengers from foreign ports to land. It is true the latter act upon evidence and determine the fact, but they do so upon their own inspection and the unsworn testimony of others. In one important particular there is similarity, if not identity — they summarily condemn. The hearing given to a party does not seem to be much broader in the one case than the other. The statement of the parties as to exposure to contagious disease, and the statements with reference to becoming a public charge, are of equal force, and with reference to each the board are to determine, in the one case, the fact as to exposure, and in the other, the fact as to becoming a public charge. The determination in each case should be subject to review to the same extent. In either case the person may be denied the right of liberty: in the one, restraint by confinement; in the other, restraint of living in a free country, of becoming a citizen. Boards of immigration are full as likely to act arbitrarily and without sufficient reason as boards of health. It was held that the determinations of boards of health were not final or conclusive; that if they were their authority could not be upheld. In the case cited it was held that the decision of the board of immigration was conclusive, and hence not subject to review.

Sec. 380. Who is entitled to the writ.— It may be stated as a general proposition that where it satisfactorily appears that the party restrained of his liberty is, either by reason of the restraint under which he is detained, or from youth, feebleness of mind or other causes, prevented from or unable to apply for the writ, the application may be made on his behalf by some other person. Of course a person entitled to the custody of another may apply for the writ in all cases. Hence, a daughter may apply for the writ to relieve her aged mother from restraint, where she is forcibly taken from her care, against her will, and without authority of law. A sister may

1 Commonwealth v. Curly (Pa.), 2 Brewst. 610.

<br>

### --- Page 0684 ---

§§ 381, 382.] HABEAS CORPUS— COURTS-MARTIAL, ETC. 535

have this remedy to remove an orphan under fourteen years of age from an asylum, where access to her is denied. So a husband may by such writ regain the custody of his wife, where, against her will, she is kept from him, and the wife may apply on behalf of her husband, and in her own behalf, and against her husband, when she is improperly restrained by him.

Sec. 381. Master may not have writ for his apprentice.—The master may not have the writ to restore the custody of his apprentice, where he is unlawfully detained from him. The purpose of the writ, as we have seen ante, is to discharge persons from actual restraint, and except in the cases of young children who are unable to choose for themselves, and those where peculiar conditions exist, and where persons are not held under process, the person will be restored to liberty, and be left free to choose for himself as to the person in whose custody he shall remain. Its purpose and object is not primarily to restore to custody, but to relieve from restraint. Therefore, if one be entitled to the custody and service of another as an apprentice, and he is unlawfully detained by a third person, the court will discharge him. After his discharge, whether the writ issues at the instigation of the master or the apprentice, the court will leave the master to pursue his appropriate remedy at law, if any such he may have. The writ may be used, however, to discharge an apprentice from custody, where the indentures under which he is held are invalid.

Sec. 382. Right of guardianship will not be tried.—The writ will issue upon the application of a guardian to relieve

1 In re Daley, 2 F. & F. 258.

2 Ex parte Newton, 2 Smith, 617; Ex parte Sandelands, 12 Eng. L. & Eq. 463; Sanders v. Rodway, 13 Eng. L. & Eq. 463.

3 Cobbett v. Hudson, 15 Ad. & EL Q. B. 988; Matter of Ferrens (U. S.), 3 Ben. 445.

4 Lister’s Case, 8 Mod. 22.

5 Lea v. White, 4 Sneed, 73 (Tenn.), 67 Am. Dec. 599.

6 Commonwealth v. Harrison, 11 Mass, 63; Matter of McDowle, 8 John. 828; People v. Pillow, 1 Sand. 672 (N. Y.); Ballinger v. McLean, 54 Ga. 159. Contra, Commonwealth v. Beck, 1 Brown, 277 (Pa.).

7 Musgrave v. Kornegay, 7 Jones, 71 (N. C.); Commonwealth v. Hamilton, 6 Mass. 273; Ballenger v. McLean, 54 Ga. 159; People v. Weisenbach, 60 N. Y. 385; Matter of Goodenough, 19 Wis, 274

<br>

### --- Page 0685 ---

536 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 382.

his ward from illegal restraint, but the right of guardianship will not thus be tried. The rule with respect to disposing of the custody of the child is the same as in other cases. If the child be of sufficient age, it will be merely relieved from the illegal restraint and permitted to go where it pleases. If not, the court will exercise its judgment in the interest of the welfare of the child.

1 Townsend v. Kendall, 4 Minn. 412; Matter of Heather Children, 50 Pick. 274; Wilcox v. Wilcox, 14 N.Y. Commonwealth v. Hammond, 10 Mich. 261.

<br>

### --- Page 0686 ---

CHAPTER 22.

HABEAS CORPUS—Continued,

Fugitives From Justice.

Sec. 383. Fugitive from justice — Who is such. — A fugitive from justice is one who, having within a state committed that which by its laws constitutes a crime, leaves its jurisdiction when it is sought to subject him to its criminal process to answer for his offense and is found in the jurisdiction of another state or country. It is not necessary that he should have left after indictment found, or for the purpose of avoiding a 

384. Jurisdiction of federal and state courts. State courts cannot proceed when it appears petitioner held under federal authority.

386. State courts not prohibited simply because federal rights or the federal constitution and laws are involved. The agent selected to convey a fugitive not an officer of the United States. Nature and extent of the inquiry. Affidavit or indictment may be examined.

390. The affidavit — Sufficiency of.

391. Statement merely that a designated crime has been committed ordinarily not sufficient.

392. The indictment — Sufficiency of.

393. Test of sufficiency.

394. Determination of the governor that the accused is a fugitive, how far conclusive.

395. The certificate of authenticity of affidavit — How far conclusive.

396. The executive warrant — Effect of. Determination whether act charged constitutes a crime in state where committed. Identity of alleged fugitive. Guilt or innocence — Question of. Arrest prior to demand. Illegal extradition—Prosecution for another offense. Fugitive under charge in state where found. Foreign extradition. Proceedings of federal commissioners subject to review. Federal statute — Evidence under. Fugitive entitled to process at expense of government. Proceedings before commissioner analogous to those before examining magistrates. Complaint — Sufficiency of.

<br>

### --- Page 0687 ---

538 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 384.

prosecution anticipated or begun. If one goes into a state and commits a crime and then returns to the state which he left, he is as much a fugitive from justice as though he had committed a crime in the state of his residence and then had fled to another. But one who was not actually present in the state where the crime charged was committed, though his acts of participation in the commission thereof, while in another state, might subject him to criminal prosecution in such former state, cannot be said to be a fugitive from justice. The words in the constitution, “who shall flee,” cannot include a person who never was in the country from which he is said to have fled. In order to charge one as a fugitive from justice he must have been in the state, committed the crime, and fled.

Src. 384. Jurisdiction of federal and state courts.— The general statement is often made that the federal and state courts have concurrent jurisdiction in extradition proceedings, and this statement is true to the extent that the jurisdiction is concurrent to inquire upon habeas corpus as to the cause of detention. But the jurisdiction of state courts in such cases to proceed with the inquiry is limited by the same provisions and rule of law that limits their jurisdiction generally upon habeas corpus. This limitation was clearly and concisely stated by the supreme court of the United States. It was said: “Congress has not undertaken to invest the judicial tribunals of the United States with exclusive jurisdiction of issuing writs of habeas corpus in proceedings for the arrest of fugitives from justice, and their delivery to the authorities of the state in which they stand charged with crime. When a demand has been made in accordance with the constitution of the United States, by the state from which the fugitive has fled, upon the executive authority of the state in which he is found, that instrument indeed makes it the duty of the latter to cause his arrest and surrender to the executive authority of the demanding state, or to the agent of such authority. Subject, then, to the

1 Roberts v. Reilly, 116 U.S. 80; Ex parte Mohr, 73 Ala. 503, 49 Fed. Rep. 653. Am. Rep. 63.   
2 In re Roberts, 24 Fed. Rep. 132.   
3 In re Roberts, 24 Fed. Rep. 132; Joes et al. v. Leonard, 50 Iowa; Ex parte Coy, 32 Fed. Rep. 911; Robb v. Connolly, 111 U.S. 624.

<br>

### --- Page 0688 ---

§ 355.] | HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 539

exclusive and paramount authority of the national government, by its own judicial tribunals, to determine whether persons held in custody by authority of the United States, or by the commissioners of such courts, or by the officers of the general government acting under its laws, are so held in conformity to law, the states have the right by their own courts, or by the judges thereof, to inquire into the grounds upon which any person within their respective territorial limits is restrained of his liberty, and to discharge him if it be ascertained that such restraint is illegal; and this, notwithstanding such illegality may arise from a violation of the constitution or the laws of the United States.”1

Sec. 385. State courts do not proceed when it appears petitioner is held under federal authority.— State courts and state judges, authorized by laws of their states to issue writs of habeas corpus, have undoubtedly the right to issue the writ in any case where a party is alleged to be illegally confined within their limits, unless it appear upon his application that he is confined under the authority or claim and color of the authority of the United States by an officer of that government. If such fact appear upon the application, the writ should be refused. If the court, after the return is made, is judicially apprised that the party is in custody under the authority of the United States, they can proceed no further. They then know that the prisoner is within the domain and jurisdiction of another government, and that neither the writ of habeas corpus, nor any other process issued under the state authority, can pass over the line of division between the two sovereignties. He is then within the domain and exclusive jurisdiction of the United States. If he has committed an offense against their laws, their tribunals alone can punish him. If he is wrongfully imprisoned, their judicial tribunals can release him and afford him redress.2 If it does not appear that he is held under the authority of the United States, the state judge or court issuing the writ has a right to inquire into the cause of the imprisonment, and ascertain by what authority the prisoner is held.

1 Robb v. Connolly, 111 U. S. 624.  
2 United States v. Booth, 21 How. 506; In re Tarble, 13 Wall. 389.

<br>

### --- Page 0689 ---

540 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 386-388.

within the limits of the state, and it is the duty of the marshal or other federal officer having the custody of the person to give, by a proper return, information in respect to the person being in federal custody.)

Sec. 386. State court not prohibited because federal rights or laws are involved.— The recognition, therefore, of the authority of a state court, or of one of its judges, upon a writ of habeas corpus, to pass upon the legality of the imprisonment within the territory of that state of a person held in custody otherwise than under the judgment or orders of the judicial tribunals of the United States, or by the order of a commissioner of a circuit court, or by officers acting under their laws, cannot be denied merely because the proceedings involve the determination of rights, privileges or immunities derived from the nation, or require a construction of the constitution and laws of the United States.?

Sec. 387. The agent selected to convey a fugitive not an officer of the United States.— A state court will not release one arrested and in custody of the agent selected to convey him from the jurisdiction where arrested to the jurisdiction where the alleged crime was committed, on the ground that he is in custody of one acting under the authority and executing the power of the United States, because such agent is not an officer of the federal government nor acting under the authority of the United States, but is an agent of the state?

Sec. 388. Nature and extent of the inquiry.— The court will examine the extradition papers to determine the lawfulness of the arrest. The act of congress (Rev. Stat., sec. 5278) prescribes that, when a demand upon the executive authority of the state where a fugitive has fled has been made for such fugitive, there shall be produced a copy of an indictment or affidavit made before a magistrate charging the person demanded with having committed a crime, which shall be certified as authentic by the governor or chief magistrate of the

1 In re Tarble, 13 Wall. 397; Robb v. Connolly, 111 U. S. 624; Roberts v. Reilly, 116 U. S. 80.  
2 Robb v. Connolly, 111 U. S. 624

<br>

### --- Page 0690 ---

§§ 389, 390.]  HABEAS CORPUS— FUGITIVES FROM Justice. 541

state from whence the person so charged has fled. It must therefore appear to the governor of the state upon whom such demand is presented that the person demanded is substantially charged with a crime against the laws of the state from whose justice he is alleged to have fled, by an indictment or affidavit certified as authentic by the governor of the state making the demand; and second, that the person demanded is a fugitive from justice of the state from which proceeds the demand.

The first of these prerequisites is a question of law, and is always open on the face of the papers to judicial inquiry on an application for a discharge upon a writ of habeas corpus. The second is a question of fact, which the governor of the state upon whom the demand is made must decide upon such evidence as he deems satisfactory. How far his decision may be viewed judicially in proceedings on habeas corpus, or whether it is not conclusive, are questions which the supreme court of the United States said were not settled by harmonious judicial decisions, nor by any authoritative judgment of that court. The determination of that fact, however, by the governor is sufficient to justify the removal until the presumption in its favor is overthrown.

Sec. 389. Affidavit or indictment may be examined.— The indictment or affidavit produced before the governor therefore may be examined in order to determine whether it charges a crime against the laws of the state, the authorities of which demand the alleged fugitive, because it is elementary that these documents must charge a crime to have been committed in such latter state by the accused, to entitle its authorities to make the demand for his arrest, or to authorize the governor of the state upon whom the demand is made to issue his warrant.

Sec. 390. The affidavit — Sufficiency of.— With respect to the affidavit, it must be so explicit and certain that if it were laid before a magistrate he would be justified in committing the accused to answer the charge. The allegations thereof

1 Roberts v. Reilly, 116 U. S. 80. Davis’ Case, 122 Mass. 324; State v.  
2 Same case. Richardson, 34 Minn. 115.  
3 Ex parte Smith, 56 N. Y. 182;  
4 Ex parte Morgan, 20 Fed. Rep. 298.

<br>

### --- Page 0691 ---

542 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 391.

must be positive and direct, not upon information and belief, and sufficient must appear therein or in the accompanying papers to affirmatively show that the tribunals of the state which produce it have jurisdiction to try the person demanded as a fugitive for the particular offense charged. But charging one with the commission of the crime of murder, larceny, and the like, ordinarily will be sufficient when committed within the boundaries of a state, because it is a matter of common knowledge that for the commission of such offenses each state has provided tribunals for the trial of the offender, and also for his punishment, if his guilt be established; yet there is a class of offenses and crimes which is exclusively within the jurisdiction of the federal courts, and such latter courts have also exclusive jurisdiction of common-law and statutory offenses committed upon territory ceded to the United States, though such territory may be within the territorial limits of a state.

The courts of the Cherokee nation have jurisdiction and can only try for the crime of murder when the person murdered is an Indian and the one charged with the murder is also an Indian. Extradition papers which proceed from that nation, where the charge is murder, must affirmatively show that the accused and his victim were Indians in order to confer jurisdiction upon the governor of the state to which one charged has fled, to honor the requisition and issue his warrant. It has been stated that it cannot be held that any less degree of certainty is admissible in an affidavit than is required in an indictment for the same offense. If any distinction exists in this respect, the affidavit should be more full and explicit.

Sec. 391. Statement merely that a designated crime has been committed ordinarily not sufficient.— It is not sufficient ordinarily to state the conclusion that a designated crime or offense was committed, but the essential elements constituting the offense or crime must be stated with as much particularity as is required in an indictment. For instance, where the offense

1 Ex parte Morgan, 20 Fed. Rep. 298; Ex parte Smith, 8 McLean, 121 (U.S.); People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182; Kentucky v. Dennison, 24 How. 66; Davis’ Case, 122 Mass. 824; Ex parte Morgan, 20 Fed. Rep. 298.

<br>

### --- Page 0692 ---

§§ 392, 393.] HABEAS CORPUS—FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 543

alleged is the obtaining of property by false pretenses, the pretenses must be alleged. And where conspiracy is charged to cheat or defraud another of his property by false tokens or pretenses, it is not sufficient to aver the combination and the object without setting out the means or the pretenses by which the end was to be obtained. And where one is charged as an accessory before the fact of the crime of assault with intent to kill, it is not sufficient to aver that the person was shot with intent to kill, and the person charged was accessory before the fact. In California, however, it was held that it was not necessary that the affidavit upon which the requisition issued should set forth the crime with all the legal exactness necessary to be observed in an indictment. If it distinctly charges an offense, it is all that is necessary.

Sec. 392. The indictment—Sufficiency of.—With respect to the indictment when this form of evidence is adopted, if it does not charge a crime, it is insufficient to confer jurisdiction to issue the warrant, and the judge or court, on habeas corpus, can go behind the warrant and examine the indictment to ascertain whether the executive had jurisdiction to issue it, and if it appears that no crime is charged, the alleged fugitive is entitled to his discharge. It is, however, only matters of substance that the court will consider; mere matters of form will be disregarded. If, however, the indictment appears to have been returned by a grand jury, and is certified by the governor of the demanding state as authentic, and substantially charges a crime, the court cannot, on habeas corpus, discharge the prisoner because of formal defects in the indictment. Its sufficiency, as a matter of technical pleading, is to be tried and determined in the state in which it was found.

Sec. 393. Test of sufficiency.—It would seem that the proper test in such cases is whether the indictment was suffi-

1 People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182.
2 Lambert v. People, 9 Cow. 578; Davis' Case, 122 Mass. 324; People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182.
3 Ex parte Sheldon, 34 Ohio St. 319; Ex parte Smith, 3 McLean, 121 (U.S.).
4 In Matter of Manchester, 5 Cal. 237.

<br>

### --- Page 0693 ---

544 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 394.

sufficient to justify, under the law of the state where found, a conviction for the offense charged. If it was so defective in matters of substance, or wanting in essential averments of time, place or intent, that it would be quashed on motion, then it is that no crime is charged; in other words, there must appear a legal indictment. This position may not accord with that taken by the supreme court of Massachusetts in the Davis Case, supra, but is certainly in accord with the weight of authority. In that case, from the meager report of the facts, I assume that the indictment, which was for the offense of obtaining money under false pretenses, was lacking in proper averments that the prosecuting witness was induced by the false pretenses alleged to part with his money, or that he was cheated or defrauded in any manner, and that the court held, notwithstanding such defects, it substantially charged the offense; that such defects were matters of form only, to be passed upon by the court wherein the indictment was found. I am aware, also, that the position here taken is not in accord with what was held in In re Greenough, 31 Vt. 279, and as well aware that what was there said is not in accord in another respect with any well-considered adjudication in the states. The particular question to which I refer is the power of the courts upon habeas corpus to go behind the warrant and examine the indictment or affidavit with respect to jurisdictional defects. It was stated that if the warrant is duly issued, the court, upon habeas corpus, can go behind it only so far as to entertain a question as to the identity of the alleged fugitive, citing two cases decided by the Delaware court as sustaining such contention.

Sec. 394. Determination of the governor that the accused is a fugitive, how far conclusive.— The other proposition, heretofore stated, is whether the determination of the governor, upon the evidence submitted, that the person demanded as a fugitive from justice is such in fact, can be reviewed upon habeas corpus. It has heretofore been stated that the federal supreme court has said that this question has not been authoritatively decided by that court, though it was there said

1 Roberts v. Reilly, 116 U.S. 80. It was also an open question in a prior case. Ex parte Reggel, 114 U.S. 642.

<br>

### --- Page 0694 ---

§ 595.] HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 545

that if the determination of that fact by the governor, upon evidence introduced before him, is subject to judicial review upon habeas corpus, the accused should not be discharged merely because in the judgment of the court the evidence as to his being a fugitive from justice was not so full as might properly have been required, or because it was so meager as perhaps to admit of a conclusion different from that reached by him. It is quite generally held that the governor in such cases acts ministerially, not judicially, though the Vermont court asserts that he acts both ministerially and judicially. It is quite difficult to understand why a distinction should be made as to the determination of the governor in respect to the questions necessarily submitted to him upon the application. Necessarily both of such questions may present questions of law. Such was the case in Jones et al. v. Leonard, 50 Iowa, 206. The affidavit there presented to the governor merely stated the conclusion that the person demanded was a fugitive from justice. The facts were that the person demanded had never been in the state where the alleged offense was committed. It was sought to extradite him on the ground of a constructive presence in the state, arising from his being in some manner a party to the crime. The question was therefore directly presented upon the facts, if the governor had before him the facts, whether under such circumstances the accused was a fugitive from justice. This was purely upon the facts, when ascertained, a question of law, and the court so held; it being determined that it was not consistent to say that a man had fled from a jurisdiction into which he had never been; and this latter statement is in accord with the statement of the supreme court of the United States. It was stated by the New York court of appeals that a rule which would make the determination of the executive final, even though the papers produced clearly showed that the essential preliminaries of the law were unfulfilled, did not commend itself to their judgment.

Sec. 895. The certificate of authenticity of affidavit — How far conclusive.— Statutes generally require the copy of the in-

Jones et al. v. Leonard, 50 Iowa, 206. Ex parte Reggel, 114 U. S. 642. People v. Donahue, 84 N. Y. 438.  
In re Greenough, 31 Vt. 279.

<br>

### --- Page 0695 ---

546 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 396.

dictment found or affidavit made to be duly certified as authentic by the demanding governor. The reason for this requirement has been stated to be, that otherwise the executive of the state upon whom the demand is made might be imposed upon by what purported to be a true copy of such an indictment, but which in fact might be a spurious copy. The genuineness of the copy is not to be ascertained by a resort to any technical rule for ascertaining the fact, nor need the fact be made to appear in any set form of words, or even in the words of the statute requiring the authentication. All that can be required is that the language employed by the demanding governor in the requisition, understood in its ordinary meaning, shows that the copy of the indictment upon which the requisition is made is genuine. The governor may rely upon the clerk’s certificate that the copy of the indictment is genuine, the genuineness of which, it is presumed, he has the means of knowing and has knowledge. The mere fact that the clerk has omitted to attach his seal to such certificate, or has omitted to place his file-mark upon the indictment, will not be noticed in proceedings by habeas corpus. The certificate of the governor that the indictment is authentic is conclusive of such questions. The governor of the state, who makes the demand for the alleged fugitive, is the only judge of the authenticity of the affidavit; and where that appears by his proper certificate, it is conclusive, and no question can be raised upon habeas corpus, impeaching the affidavit, upon the ground that it was a forgery, or for other cause. If, however, it appears upon the face of the affidavit that it was not made before a proper officer, authorized in the particular case to administer the oath or sign the jurat, there is no authority for the governor to issue his warrant.

Sec. 396. The executive warrant — Effect of. — The warrant of the governor is at least prima facie evidence that all required precedent conditions have been complied with, and of the right to remand the alleged fugitive; and it is incumbent

1 Ex parte Sheldon, 34 Ohio St. 319; 4 Matter of Manchester, 5 Cal. 237; Kingsbury’s Case, 106 Mass. 223. Kurtz v. State, 22 Fla. 36, 1 Am. St.

2 Ex parte Sheldon, 34 Ohio St. 319. Rep. 173.

3 Hibler v. State, 43 Tex. 196; 5 Ex parte Powell, 20 Fla. 806.

<br>

### --- Page 0696 ---

§ 396.] HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 547

upon the petitioner to show that under the law no such right has been established. We have seen that, when the preliminary papers upon which a warrant of extradition has been granted are produced and are before the court, it is not only the right, but the duty, of the court to examine them and judge and determine whether they are sufficient under the law to justify the warrant of extradition. Where, however, the papers upon which the executive warrant is founded are not produced on the return, but are withheld by the executive in the exercise of official discretion and authority, then the court can look only to the warrant itself and its recitals for the evidence that the essential conditions of its issue have been fulfilled; and if therein be recited a representation of the governor of a state that the prisoner stood charged with a crime, designating it, in said state; that said governor has demanded his arrest and extradition; that the demand was accompanied by affidavits or indictment (where both are not required by statute), whereby the prisoner is charged with said crime and with having fled from the state; and that such papers were certified by the governor to be duly authenticated, the warrant fully complies with the statute and sufficiently establishes the conditions necessary to its issue. It is not necessary in such a case to state in the warrant the facts constituting the alleged crime. Whether the warrant is conclusive, and whether it is competent for the prisoner to prove that the papers presented to the governor were defective, the New York court did not determine, as the question was not in issue, no proof being offered. It seems to be established by authority that a copy of the indictment or affidavit need not accompany the executive warrant. But it is the rule in some jurisdictions, where copies of the affidavit or other records are not annexed to the warrant, that they must be produced, if the warrant be called in question, unless the jurisdic-

1 Ex parte Sheldon, 34 Ohio St. 819; 4 People v. Pinkerton, 77 N. Y. 245. Ex parte Watson, 2 Cal. 59; Davis’ Case, 122 Mass. 324; Taylor v. Tainter, Ex parte Thornton, 9 Tex. 635.

16 Wall. 366. 5 People v. Donahue, 84 N. Y. 438;

2 People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182; People v. Donahue, 84 N. Y. 438.

3 People v. Donahue, 84 N. Y. 438; People v. Pinkerton, 77 N. Y. 245.

Nichols v. Cornelius, 7 Ind. 611; Matter of O'Leary, 10 Ben. 197 (U. So; State v. Richardson, 34 Minn. 115.

<br>

### --- Page 0697 ---

548 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§§ 397, 398.

tional facts are recited on the face of the warrant; and if the evidence upon which the warrant issues is either an affidavit or an indictment, the warrant must recite such affidavit or indictment.

Sec. 397. Determination whether act charged constitutes a crime in state where committed.—If it be the rule that the governor must determine from the evidence that the party under arrest is charged with the commission of a crime, and if it also be true that the court, on habeas corpus, may review his determination in this respect, a question is oftentimes presented as to whether certain acts charged to have been done constitute a crime in the particular demanding states as well as the manner in which that question shall be determined. How is the governor or the court to be informed whether such acts are made a crime by law? There is no difficulty in this respect with the acknowledged common-law offenses. It arises, generally, when the statute makes that a crime which was not such at common law. Can the governor or the court assume that the acts charged constitute a crime? These questions are oftentimes important, and different courts have not reached the same conclusion. It may be said that there are three different positions:

First. That the fact that it appears an indictment has been found, or the demand made, is at least prima facie sufficient.

Second. That in the absence of any proof upon the subject, the law of the demanding state will be considered the same as the law of the state where the alleged fugitive is found.

Third. In some jurisdictions it is enacted that the printed statutes of another state shall be admitted as presumptive evidence of such laws, and therefore a construction can be placed upon statutes defining a crime.

Sec. 398. Identity of alleged fugitive.—The question of identity of the party in custody by virtue of the warrant is

1 State v. Richardson, 34 Minn. 115, 409; Opinion of Judges of Maine, 24 Am. Jur. 233.  
2 Ex parte Thornton, 9 Tex. 635; In re Doo Woon, 18 Fed. Rep. 898.  
3 In re Van Sceiver (Neb.), 60 N. W. Rep. 1037; Brown's Case, 112 Mass.  
4 People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182.  
5 Ex parte Sheldon, 34 Ohio St. 319.

<br>

### --- Page 0698 ---

§§ 399, 400.] HABEAS CORPUS—FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 549.

always open to inquiry. But one case has come to my knowledge holding a contrary view. This question of necessity involves the hearing of evidence extrinsic to the record. The conclusion of the court upon the facts is not reviewable in the appellate court.

Sec. 399. Guilt or innocence—Question of.—The rule is that upon habeas corpus the question of the guilt or innocence of the accused is not the subject of inquiry. That question is for the determination of the tribunals of the state where the crime is alleged to have been committed.

Sec. 400. Arrest prior to a demand.—The law is well settled that a fugitive from justice may be arrested before a demand is actually made, and that he may be detained in custody a reasonable time to enable the executive of the state from which he fled to issue a requisition. In many of the states the authority is found in the statute, which also provides the manner of proceeding. Where such is the case the provisions must be strictly pursued, and the prisoner cannot be detained for a longer period than that prescribed. Such statutes usually provide that a justice, upon complaint made setting forth the offense and such other matters as are necessary to bring the case within the provisions of the law, may issue his warrant to arrest such person. As the federal constitution and laws enacted in reference thereto only authorize or direct that an alleged fugitive charged with crime shall be surrendered, it is held that this relates to a charge made in the state where the offense was committed. That this charge must be made to some court, magistrate or officer in the form of an indictment, information or accusation known to the law of the state in which the of-

1 In re Greenough, 31 Vt. 279; In re Pinkerton, 77 N. Y. 243; O'Leary's Case, 10 Ben. (U. S.) 197; People v. Byrnes, 33 Hem. 97.

2 Robinson v. Flanders, 29 Ind. 10.

3 In re Pinkerton, 77 N. Y. 243.

4 Matter of Clark, 9 Wend. 212; People v. Brady, 56 N. Y. 182; People v. Donahue, 84 N. Y. 438; Tullis v. Fleming, 69 Ind. 15; In re Greenough, 31 Vt. 279; Kingsbury’s Case, 106 Mass. 223.

5 People v. Goodhue, 2 Johns. Ch. 198; Commonwealth v. Deacon, 10 Serg. & R. 125; State v. Hafford, 28 Iowa, 391.

6 Ex parte Rosenbelt, 51 Cal. 285.

7 State v. Hafford, 28 Iowa, 391. Ex parte White, 49 Cal. 434.

<br>

### --- Page 0699 ---

550 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 401.

fense was committed, and therefore that unless such a person arrested as a fugitive is so charged in the proceeding before the magistrate of the state wherein he is found, the magistrate has no jurisdiction. The warrant must specify the offense alleged to have been committed, and it must appear by the affidavit that he is a fugitive from justice. The proceedings are similar to those provided by law against persons charged with crime in the state where the alleged fugitive is arrested.

Sec. 401. Illegal extradition — Prosecution for another offense.— Where an alleged fugitive from justice is extradited from a foreign country into the United States, the rule is authoritatively stated by the highest court in the land, that under the conditions of the treaty, if similar to that existing between this country and Great Britain, he is clothed with exemption from trial for any other offense until he has an opportunity to return to the country from which he was taken, for the purpose alone of trial for the offense specified in the demand for his surrender. The subject is elaborately and exhaustively treated in a most able opinion by Justice Miller in the case cited as authority for the foregoing proposition, and from what is there stated, and what has been written by learned lawyers and jurists upon the subject there quoted, it is strongly intimated that, independent of treaties, the same rule will prevail. There are some cases where it is stated that the doctrine thus declared by the federal court does not prevail in the absence of a treaty stipulation. These statements are unimportant if the law declared by the supreme court of the United States is otherwise. In some of the states the doctrine is declared that this exemption of the accused is his right, where he is extradited from one state of the Union into the other; while in other states the doctrine is, that he can be arrested and tried

1 State v. Hafford, 51 Iowa, 391.  
2 See also Ex parte Donaghey, 2 Pitts. U.S. 407.  
3 United States v. Rauscher, 119 U.S. 407.  
4 Ex parte McKean, 3 Hughes, 23; See also People v. Gray, 66 Cal. 271; State v. Vanderpool, 39 Ohio St. 49 Cal. 433.  
5 State v. Swope, 72 Mo. 399; Ex parte Lorraine, 16 Nev. 63; Ex parte White, 273.  
6 State ex rel. Brown v. Stewart, 60 Wis. 587; Adriance v. La Grave, 59 N. Y. 110.  
7 Ex parte Cubreth, 49 Cal. 435.  
8 Cannon's Case, 47 Mich. 481; Kerr  


<br>

### --- Page 0700 ---

§ 401.] HABEAS CORPUS— FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 551

on criminal process; that the interstate extradition of the constitution was never intended for the benefit of fugitives, nor to enable them to escape just punishment for their offenses. However, the courts will not permit an individual who was instrumental in extraditing the alleged offender to thereby take an advantage of a presence thus secured to obtain a civil reparation or to permit the accused to be imprisoned on civil process, though it seems that one not taking any such part is not precluded from his remedy. This exemption has no application in cases where the alleged fugitive has been brought within reach of the process of the court by means wholly without legal authority; nor where he has been induced by stratagem, falsehood, artifice or fraud to come within the territory where he could be arrested.

Note to Sec. 401.— The decision of the supreme court of Wisconsin was criticised in a recent case by the supreme court of Ohio (Ex parte McKnight, 48 Ohio St. 588), and the distinction attempted to be made by the former between foreign and interstate extradition is considered by the latter not well taken. The position of the Ohio court is that a person surrendered to the authorities of a state by another state or territory on extradition proceedings cannot, while held in custody thereunder, be lawfully tried for any other crime than the one upon which his extradition was obtained, unless he voluntarily waives his privilege. It is further held that failure to plead in abatement of the indictment for such different crime, or by entering a plea of not guilty thereto, does not constitute such a waiver; yet the accused should object in some manner, as proceeding to trial without objection would be a waiver, the privilege being personal. The court is simply without power to try the accused on such indictment against his objection. It was also held that habeas corpus was a proper remedy. It was conceded that the writ cannot be properly employed for the review and correction of mere errors committed by courts while acting within the sphere of their authority. However, it is the appropriate remedy to obtain a discharge from imprisonment under an order or process of a court which it was without jurisdiction to make or issue: that the imprisonment of the petitioner in the penitentiary was of such a character, and he was entitled to be discharged therefrom. The view taken by the Ohio court, and those

v. People, 110 Ill. 627; Compton v. Wilder, 40 Ohio St. 130.

1 State ex rel. Brown v. Stewart, 60 Wis. 587; Adriance v. La Grave, 59 N. Y. 110; In re Miles, 52 Vt. 609; Dow's Case, 18 Pa. St. 37; State v. Ross, 21 Iowa, 467; Kerr v. People, 110 Ill.

2 Adriance v. La Grave, 59 N. Y. 629.

110; State ex rel. Brown v. Stewart, 60 Wis. 587; In re Brown, 28 Fed. Rep. 633,

<br>

### --- Page 0701 ---

holding similar views, commends itself strongly to the man who dislikes any proceeding which appears to favor either trickery, deception or fraud. The average American resents the taking of an undue advantage on the part of the state or its tribunals. In fact, the spirit of our laws is directed against it. Thus, in many states, when the law compels a person to attend at a place away from where he is abiding, it at the same time usually protects him from having undue advantage taken of his unwilling and enforced presence. Everyone in legal custody has a right to legal protection. This doctrine was carried to the extent that an arrest in a state court of a person attending under process of a federal court was held unlawful and should not be permitted, whether the federal court interfered or not. Watson v. Judge, 40 Mich. 729. See also Commonwealth v. Hawes, 13 Bush, 697. The doctrine of comity is repudiated by many courts. As stated by one, “the constitutional safeguards on this subject concern the individual's liberty, and not merely the state’s dignity; and no one holds his liberty subject to state comity or on any less tenure than constitutional right.” In Matter of Cannon, 47 Mich. 481.

Sec. 402. Fugitive under charge in state where found.—When a demand is properly made by the governor of one state upon the governor of another, the duty to surrender is not absolute and unqualified. It depends upon the circumstances of the case. If the laws of the latter state have been put in force against the fugitive, and he is imprisoned there, the demands of those laws may be first satisfied. The duty of obedience then arises and not before. And this rule extends to persons imprisoned on civil process. This latter proposition was not recognized by the supreme court of California. It was there stated that an officer armed with process of arrest of a person in a civil suit cannot take the defendant from the hands of another officer who holds him on another warrant in a criminal case; nor can he hold him as against one armed with a criminal warrant; and that the same rule applies to a proceeding under a requisition from the governor of another state asking for his return as a fugitive from justice.

Sec. 403. Foreign extradition.—By force of treaties entered into between the United States and other foreign countries quite generally, persons charged with crime may be extradited. Such treaties usually designate the class of crimes, and

1 Taylor v. Taintor, 16 Wall. 866; Matter of Briscoe, 51 How. Pr. 422; Taylor v. Taintor, 16 Wall. 366.  
2 Matter of Troutman, 24 N. J. L. 634.  
3 Ex parte Rosenbelt, 51 Cal. 235.

<br>

### --- Page 0702 ---

§ 409.] HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 553

In some instances the particular crime, which are within its provisions. Formerly it was questioned whether the surrender of fugitives from justice by one government to another was a duty or obligation imposed by the law of nations, or depended upon courtesy or comity, which might or might not be exercised at the pleasure of each government, without cause of complaint. In this country and in England it has been substantially settled that no such duty exists, and in practice it is believed that in nearly all countries neither demand nor surrender is now made, except in obedience to treaty stipulations. If, however, an alleged fugitive has been surrendered by a foreign government who is charged with a crime not embraced within those covered by the treaty, the fact that the crime is not thus included therein is no ground for the discharge of the prisoner. The government has power to surrender an alleged fugitive from justice for any offense, and, even though such government has been deceived and defrauded with respect to such extradition, the accused cannot interfere in behalf of such government. The question of good faith is for the two governments.

The question whether arrest and judicial inquiry could precede a demand made upon the president of the United States was elaborately discussed by several of the judges of the supreme court of the United States, and it was decided that judicial authority under the act of August 12, 1848, was vested in magistrates to arrest and commit preparatory to a surrender. There was a strong dissent on the part of Taney, C. J., and Nelson and Daniels, JJ. The statute referred to provided, among other things, that the determination of such judicial officer, together with a copy of all the testimony taken before him, shall be certified to the secretary of state, so that a warrant may issue by the executive on the requisition of the foreign government, through its proper authorities, for the surrender of the fugitive. The determination of the judicial officer is not conclusive upon the president, and he may decline to de-

1 Kent’s Com., 38, note d, 11th ed.; 2 Adriance et al. v. La Grave, 59 N.Y. 110; Story’s Conflict of Laws, sec. 626; Caldwell’s Case, 8 Blatch. C. 131.

3 In re Kaine, 14 How. 103.

<br>

### --- Page 0703 ---

554 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§ 404.

liver the party accused, and this though the courts may have refused to discharge him upon habeas corpus. Nor does the fact of granting the executive warrant preclude an inquiry thereafter, upon habeas corpus, to ascertain and determine whether the case was such as to authorize it, as well as to inquire into the competency and authority of the committing magistrate. The authority to determine what officers shall be clothed with the power and authority to hold such examinations is vested in congress, and they have exercised it by designating judges of the federal courts and certain commissioners. A commissioner, simply as such, has no such authority. In order to possess it he must be within the class upon whom such special authority is conferred, and the complaint and warrant must show, upon their face, that the commissioner issuing the warrant is duly empowered to act.

Sec. 404. Proceedings of federal commissioners subject to review.—The proceedings of commissioners in such cases are subject to review upon writ of habeas corpus, which may issue out of the district and circuit courts of the United States. The questions which may thus be reviewed relate to the jurisdiction of the examining magistrate. The power conferred upon him being special and statutory, the rule with respect to such tribunals of course is applied to their proceedings. The authority conferred must appear in the record, and no presumptions are indulged in that that was done which the law makes essential to the exercise of the power conferred. The inquiry upon the writ will go to the extent of determining whether there is any competent evidence before him on which to base the judgment or order made, but not to determine the weight and sufficiency of such evidence as may be competent.
