If the committing magistrate has jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the accused, and the offense charged is within the terms of the treaty of extradition, and the magistrate, in arriving at a decision to hold the accused, has before him competent legal

1 In re Shepp, 12 Blatch. 501.
2 Ex parte Lane, 6 Fed. Rep. 34.
3 In re Kaine, 3 Blatch. 1; Ex parte Smith, 3 McLean, 121.
4 In re Kaine, 14 How. 103.
5 In re Fowler, 4 Fed. Rep. 303; In re Shepp, 12 Blatch. 501; In re Vandervelpen, 14 Blatch. 137.

<br>

### --- Page 0704 ---

§ 405.] HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 555

evidence on which to exercise his judgment as to whether the facts are sufficient to establish the criminality of the accused for the purpose of extradition, such decision cannot be reviewed on habeas corpus.1

Sec. 405. Federal statute — Evidence under.— The federal statute, section 5271, as amended by the act of June 19, 1876, provided that “in every case of complaint and of a hearing, upon the return of a warrant of arrest, any depositions, warrants or other papers offered in evidence shall be admitted and received for the purpose of such hearing, if they shall be properly and legally authenticated so as to entitle them to be received as evidence of the criminality of the person so apprehended by the tribunals of the foreign country from which the accused party shall have escaped, and copies of any such depositions, warrants or other papers shall, if authenticated according to the law of such foreign country, be in like manner received as evidence, and the certificate of the principal diplomatic or consular officers of the United States, resident in such foreign country, shall be proof that any such deposition or other paper, or copy thereof, is authenticated in the manner required by this section.” It was held that the foregoing provisions provide for two classes of documentary evidence: First. Depositions, warrants and other papers, which means original depositions, original warrants, and original other papers themselves, and not copies of them. Second. Copies of any such depositions, warrants or other papers. Under the first class, the originals must be documents which would be entitled to be received in the tribunals of the foreign country as evidence of the criminality of the person in respect to the offense charged against him, committed there, if the inquiry as to his criminality in respect of such offense were being had in such foreign tribunals, and such originals must be authenticated in such a proper and legal manner as would entitle them to be received as such evidence in such foreign tribunals. The second class must be copies of the originals, which originals would be entitled to be received in the tribunals of the foreign country as

1 Bryant v. United States, 167 U. S. 10; Ornelas v. Ruiz, 161 U. S. 502; In re Oteiza, 136 U. S. 330.

<br>

### --- Page 0705 ---

556 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 405.

evidence of such criminality, as if the offense were committed there, if an inquiry as to his criminality were pending there, and such copies must be authenticated according to the law of such foreign country; that is, authenticated as true copies of such originals, the authentication being made according to the law of the foreign country. The copies themselves are not competent evidence. They can only become such by proper proof that the originals would be in the foreign country. It must appear also that such copies are true copies, and their authentication as such must be made according to the law of the foreign country. Such authentication may be made in writing or by oral proof. With respect to such proof of authenticity, a witness may be permitted to swear here as to the verity and identity of the original, and also that from his knowledge and experience, if he have such, they would be received in the tribunals of the foreign country as evidence of criminality in respect to the offense charged. This will be sufficient under the statute when the originals are offered. The certificate of the principal diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, resident in that foreign country, shall be conclusive proof of proper authentication. The certificate of such diplomatic officer must, however, conform substantially to the provisions of the statute. If defective in this respect the originals or copies cannot be received. The court is without jurisdiction to proceed unless by resort to the other mode of proof. Hence, where the certificate of such diplomatic officer recited that the documents referred to therein were authenticated in the manner required by the statutes of the United States, it was said: “It ought to certify in respect to the original depositions offered that they are properly and legally authenticated so as to entitle them to be received as evidence of the criminality of the person apprehended by the tribunals of Great Britain; and it ought to certify in respect to the copies offered that the originals, of which they are copies, would be received as such evidence, and that such copies are authenticated according to the laws of Great Britain.”

This act, approved June 19, 1876, amending section 5271

1 In re Fowler, 4 Fed. Rep. 303, 3 In re Fowler, 4 Fed. Rep. 803.

<br>

### --- Page 0706 ---

§ 405.] HABEAS CORPUS — FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 557

of the Revised Statutes, was repealed by the act of congress approved August 3, 1882, and so much of said section 5271 as is inconsistent with said act. Section 5 of said act of 1882 provides: “That in all cases where any deposition, warrant or other papers, or copies thereof, shall be offered in evidence upon the hearing of any extradition case under title 66 of Revised Statutes of the United States, such deposition, warrant and other papers, or copies thereof, shall be received and admitted as evidence on such hearing, if they shall be properly and legally authenticated so as to entitle them to be received for similar purposes by the tribunals of the foreign country from which the accused party shall have escaped, and the certificate of the principal diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, resident in such foreign country, shall be proof that any deposition, warrant or any other paper, or copies thereof, so offered are authenticated in the manner required by this act.” Article 10 of the treaty with Great Britain (St. at Large, Public Treaties, etc., 320) provides for the surrender of the person accused “upon such evidence of criminality as according to the law of the place where such fugitive or person so charged shall be found would justify his apprehension and commitment for trial, if the crime or offense had been there committed.” An authentication of the documents, made in the language of the statute (Act of 1852) by the proper officers, the signature of the police magistrate being verified by oral proof, was held to be sufficient and proper evidence that the documents were such as would be received in similar proceedings in the demanding country.¹ The act of congress makes no provision upon this subject, except as it respects the admissibility of a species of evidence. The laws of the particular state where the alleged fugitive is found, therefore, are to govern and regulate the judge or commissioner in hearing and determining the criminality of the prisoner, and this would be so even without the specific provision of the treaty, as the only mode of proceeding, in summary criminal proceedings before the federal magistrates, is according to the practice before the state magistrates in analogous cases.²

¹ In re Waidge, 15 Fed. Rep. 864  
² In re Kaine, 14 Tow. 103,

<br>

### --- Page 0707 ---

558 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§§ 496, 407.

Sec. 406. Fugitive entitled to process at expense of government.—According to the practice here before committing magistrates, as well as by the provisions of section 3 of the act of 1882, it is the duty of the magistrate before whom extradition proceedings are pending to take such evidence as may be offered on the part of the accused, to grant him, if he presents the proper affidavit of his inability to pay for his witnesses, process to obtain them at the expense of the government, and to give him reasonable time to have them produced. The right does not extend to an indefinite postponement of the proceedings for the purpose of obtaining testimony upon commission or deposition, as regards the commission of the crime alleged, from foreign countries. The foreign government cannot be compelled to go into a full trial here before the magistrate upon the merits. The practice and all the provisions of the law contemplate the production of the defendant's witnesses in person before the magistrate for examination by him. The phrase in section 3 of the act of 1882 “that he (the accused) cannot safely go to trial without them” (the witnesses) cannot be construed as giving a right to a full trial in violation of treaty stipulations; but it must be confined to such a preliminary hearing only as was already allowable under the existing practice, viz.: such as is appropriate to a hearing, having reference only to a commitment for future trial. Section 3, act of 1882, provides that “in the hearing of any case under a claim of extradition by any foreign government, upon affidavit being filed by the person charged, setting forth that there are witnesses whose evidence is material to the defense, that he cannot safely go to trial without them, what he expects to prove by each of them, and that he is not possessed of sufficient means, and is actually unable to pay the fees of such witnesses, the judge or commissioner before whom such claim for extradition is heard may order that such witnesses be subpoenaed, and in such cases the expense incurred by the process and the fees of witnesses shall be paid in the same manner that similar fees are paid in the case of witnesses subpoenaed in behalf of the United States.

Sec. 407. Proceedings before commissioner analogous to those before examining magistrate.—The proceeding before

1 In re Wadge, 15 Fed. Rep. 864

<br>

### --- Page 0708 ---

§ 408.] HABEAS CORPUS— FUGITIVES FROM JUSTICE. 559

The commissioner is not to be regarded as in the nature of a final trial, by which the prisoner could be convicted or acquitted of the crime charged against him, but rather of the character of those preliminary examinations which take place before examining magistrates for the purpose of determining whether a case is made out which will justify the holding of the accused, either by imprisonment or under bail, to ultimately answer to an indictment or other proceeding in which he shall be finally tried upon the charge made against him. In other words, whether according to our laws there is probable cause to believe him guilty of the crime charged.

Sec. 408. Complaint— Sufficiency of.— Under section 5270 of the Revised Statutes, which requires a complaint to be made under oath charging a person, etc., with having committed “one of the treaty crimes,” it must affirmatively appear upon the face of the complaint that the offense charged is one of that character. The complaint, however, is not required to be as precise, technical and formal as an indictment. It is only necessary that the substance of the offense be clearly set forth, so that the court can see that one or more of the crimes enumerated in the treaty is alleged to have been committed. Extradition is the right of foreign governments only, not of individuals; therefore it must appear upon the complaint or in the proceedings that the complaint is made by some one authorized to represent the executive department of the foreign government. If the complaint be made by a private individual, his authority to act in behalf of the foreign government should be made to appear before the proceedings are closed. The allegations and statements in the complaint should be positive and direct, and not upon information and belief, where made by a private individual, though his representative character is disclosed. Where, however, if depositions have been taken in a foreign country tending to show the accused guilty of the crime, or if an indictment has been found against him, or if the

1 Bryant v. United States, 167 U.S. 3; In re Farez, 7 Blatch. 34; In re Roth, 15 Fed. Rep. 506.

2 In re Roth, 15 Fed. Rep. 506.

3 In re Ferrelle, 28 Fed. Rep. 873; In re Kelley, 26 Fed. Rep. 852; In re Herres, 33 Fed. Rep. 163.

4 In re Ferrelle, 28 Fed. Rep. 873.

<br>

### --- Page 0709 ---

560 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 408.

A representative of the foreign government demanding his extradition has fully informed himself, with regard to the particular events, by conversations with persons who witnessed them, he may make a complaint upon information and belief; but in such case he should set forth with some particularity the sources and details of his information or the grounds for supposing the defendant to be guilty. Where the treaty contains no provisions respecting a previous charge being officially made against the prisoner in the foreign government, it is not a prerequisite to proceedings here.

Ex parte Lane, 4 Fed. Rep. 34; In re Roth, 15 Fed. Rep. 506; In re Ex parte Smith, 3 McLean, 133; McDonnell, 11 Blatch. 88,
